explanation: According to the question, {tex}I = \int \frac { d x } { \sin x + \sin 2 x } {/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { d x } { \sin x + 2 \sin x \cos x }[ \because \sin 2 x = 2 \sin x \cos x ]{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { d x } { \sin x ( 1 + 2 \cos x ) }{/tex} Multiplying numerator and denominator by {tex}sinx{/tex} {tex} = \int \frac { \sin x } { \sin ^ { 2 } x ( 1 + 2 \cos x ) } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { \sin x } { \left( 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } x \right) ( 1 + 2 \cos x ) } d x{/tex} Put {tex}cos x = t{/tex} , {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-sin\ x\ dx = dt{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}sin x\ dx = -dt{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = \int \frac { - d t } { \left( 1 - t ^ { 2 } \right) ( 1 + 2 t ) }{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { - d t } { ( 1 - t ) ( 1 + t ) ( 1 + 2 t ) } [\because a^2-b^2 = (a+b)(a-b)]{/tex} Now, using partial fractions, let {tex}\frac { 1 } { ( 1 - t ) ( 1 + t ) ( 1 + 2 t ) }= \frac { A } { 1 - t } + \frac { B } { 1 + t } + \frac { C } { 1 + 2 t }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = (1 + t) (1 + 2t) A + (1 - t) (1 + 2t) B+ (1 - t) (1 + t) C ... (i) Putting t = -1 in Eq. (i), {tex}\Rightarrow1 = ( 2 ) ( - 1 ) B \Rightarrow B = - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} Putting t = 1 in Eq. (i), {tex}\Rightarrow 1 = 2 \cdot ( 3 ) A \Rightarrow A = \frac { 1 } { 6 }{/tex} Putting {tex}t = - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), {tex}\Rightarrow1 = \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) \left( 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 = \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = \frac { 4 } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \int\frac { -1 } { ( 1 - t ) ( 1 + t ) ( 1 + 2 t ) } dt= -\Bigg[\int \frac { A } { 1 - t } d t + \int \frac { B } { 1 + t } d t + \int \frac { C } { 1 + 2 t } d t\Bigg]{/tex} {tex}=-\Bigg[ \frac { 1 } { 6 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 - t } + \frac {- 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 + t } + \frac { 4 } { 3 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 + 2 t } \Bigg]{/tex} {tex}=\frac { -1 } { 6 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 - t } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 + t } + \frac {- 4 } { 3 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 + 2 t }{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 6 } \frac { \log | 1 - t | } { - 1 } +\frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | 1 + t | - \frac { 4 } { 3 } \frac { \log | 1 + 2 t | } { 2 } +C{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 6 } \log | 1 - t | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | 1 + t | - \frac { 2 } { 3 } \log | 1 + 2 t | + C{/tex} put t = cos x {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 6 } \log | 1 - \cos x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | 1 + \cos x |{/tex} {tex}- \frac { 2 } { 3 } \log | 1 + 2 \cos x | + C{/tex} {tex}={ \log | 1 - \cos x | + 3 \log | 1 + \cos x |- 4 \log | 1 + 2 \cos x | \over 6} + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \int \frac { d x } { \sin x + \sin 2 x } ={ \log | 1 - \cos x | + 3 \log | 1 + \cos x |- 4 \log | 1 + 2 \cos x | \over 6} + C{/tex}

(i), {tex}\Rightarrow 1 = 2 \cdot ( 3 ) A \Rightarrow A = \frac { 1 } { 6 }{/tex} Putting {tex}t = - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), {tex}\Rightarrow1 = \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) \left( 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 = \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = \frac { 4 } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \int\frac { -1 } { ( 1 - t ) ( 1 + t ) ( 1 + 2 t ) } dt= -\Bigg[\int \frac { A } { 1 - t } d t + \int \frac { B } { 1 + t } d t + \int \frac { C } { 1 + 2 t } d t\Bigg]{/tex} {tex}=-\Bigg[ \frac { 1 } { 6 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 - t } + \frac {- 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 + t } + \frac { 4 } { 3 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 + 2 t } \Bigg]{/tex} {tex}=\frac { -1 } { 6 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 - t } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 + t } + \frac {- 4 } { 3 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 + 2 t }{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 6 } \frac { \log | 1 - t | } { - 1 } +\frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | 1 + t | - \frac { 4 } { 3 } \frac { \log | 1 + 2 t | } { 2 } +C{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 6 } \log | 1 - t | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | 1 + t | - \frac { 2 } { 3 } \log | 1 + 2 t | + C{/tex} put t = cos x {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 6 } \log | 1 - \cos x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | 1 + \cos x |{/tex} {tex}- \frac { 2 } { 3 } \log | 1 + 2 \cos x | + C{/tex} {tex}={ \log | 1 - \cos x | + 3 \log | 1 + \cos x |- 4 \log | 1 + 2 \cos x | \over 6} + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \int \frac { d x } { \sin x + \sin 2 x } ={ \log | 1 - \cos x | + 3 \log | 1 + \cos x |- 4 \log | 1 + 2 \cos x | \over 6} + C{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}I = \int \frac { d x } { \sin x + \sin 2 x } {/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { d x } { \sin x + 2 \sin x \cos x }[ \because \sin 2 x = 2 \sin x \cos x ]{/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { d x } { \sin x ( 1 + 2 \cos x ) }{/tex}
Multiplying numerator and denominator by {tex}sinx{/tex}

 {tex} = \int \frac { \sin x } { \sin ^ { 2 } x ( 1 + 2 \cos x ) } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { \sin x } { \left( 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } x \right) ( 1 + 2 \cos x ) } d x{/tex}
Put {tex}cos x = t{/tex},
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-sin\ x\ dx = dt{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}sin x\ dx = -dt{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I = \int \frac { - d t } { \left( 1 - t ^ { 2 } \right) ( 1 + 2 t ) }{/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { - d t } { ( 1 - t ) ( 1 + t ) ( 1 + 2 t ) } [\because a^2-b^2 = (a+b)(a-b)]{/tex}
Now,

using partial fractions,
let{tex}\frac { 1 } { ( 1 - t ) ( 1 + t ) ( 1 + 2 t ) }= \frac { A } { 1 - t } + \frac { B } { 1 + t } + \frac { C } { 1 + 2 t }{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = (1 + t) (1 + 2t) A + (1 - t) (1 + 2t) B+ (1 - t) (1 + t) C ... (i)
Putting t = -1 in Eq. (i), 
{tex}\Rightarrow1 = ( 2 ) ( - 1 ) B \Rightarrow B = - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
Putting t = 1 in Eq. (i), 
{tex}\Rightarrow 1 = 2 \cdot ( 3 ) A \Rightarrow A = \frac { 1 } { 6 }{/tex}
Putting {tex}t = - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), 
{tex}\Rightarrow1 = \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) \left( 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 = \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = \frac { 4 } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \int\frac { -1 } { ( 1 - t ) ( 1 + t ) ( 1 + 2 t ) } dt= -\Bigg[\int \frac { A } { 1 - t } d t + \int \frac { B } { 1 + t } d t + \int \frac { C } { 1 + 2 t } d t\Bigg]{/tex}{tex}=-\Bigg[ \frac { 1 } { 6 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 - t } + \frac {- 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 + t } + \frac { 4 } { 3 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 + 2 t } \Bigg]{/tex}

{tex}=\frac { -1 } { 6 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 - t } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 + t } + \frac {- 4 } { 3 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 + 2 t }{/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 6 } \frac { \log | 1 - t | } { - 1 } +\frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | 1 + t | - \frac { 4 } { 3 } \frac { \log | 1 + 2 t | } { 2 } +C{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 6 } \log | 1 - t | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | 1 + t | - \frac { 2 } { 3 } \log | 1 + 2 t | + C{/tex}

put t = cos x
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 6 } \log | 1 - \cos x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | 1 + \cos x |{/tex}{tex}- \frac { 2 } { 3 } \log | 1 + 2 \cos x | + C{/tex}

{tex}={ \log | 1 - \cos x | + 3 \log | 1 + \cos x |- 4 \log | 1 + 2 \cos x | \over 6} + C{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \int \frac { d x } { \sin x + \sin 2 x } ={ \log | 1 - \cos x | + 3 \log | 1 + \cos x |- 4 \log | 1 + 2 \cos x | \over 6} + C{/tex}

x \cos \pi x , } & { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \leq x \leq \frac { 3 } { 2 } } \end{array} \right.{/tex} Now, {tex}I =\int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 / 2 } | x \cos \pi x | d x + \int _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { 3 / 2 } |x \cos \pi x | d x{/tex} {tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 / 2 } x \cos \pi x d x - \int _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { 3 / 2 } x \cos \pi x d x{/tex} ..........(i) Let I 1 = {tex}\int_{} {\mathop x\limits_I } \mathop {\cos \pi x}\limits_{II} dx{/tex} By Using integration by parts , we get {tex}= x \frac { \sin \pi x } { \pi } - \int \frac { \sin \pi x } { \pi } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { x \sin \pi x } { \pi } + \frac { \cos \pi x } { \pi ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Now, From Eq.(i), we have {tex}I =\left[ x \frac { \sin \pi x } { \pi } + \frac { \cos \pi x } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 / 2 } - \left[ \frac { x \sin \pi x } { \pi } + \frac { \cos \pi x } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \right] _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { 3 / 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) - \left( 0 + \frac { \cos 0 } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}- \left[ \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 \pi } \sin \frac { 3 \pi } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \cos \frac { 3 \pi } { 2 } \right)\right.{/tex} {tex}- \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) ]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } - \frac { 1 } { \pi ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 5 \pi - 2 } { 2 \pi ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore I = \frac { 5 \pi - 2 } { 2 \pi ^ { 2 } }{/tex} sq units.
solution_html:
According to the question ,  {tex} I =\int _ { 0 } ^ { 3 / 2 } | x \cos \pi x | d x{/tex}
consider, x cos {tex} \pi{/tex}x = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 0 \text { or } \cos \pi x = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 0 \text { or } \pi x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } , \frac { 3 \pi } { 2 } , \frac { 5 \pi } { 2 }{/tex}[{tex} \because \cos \frac { n \pi } { 2 } = 0{/tex}, n being an odd integer]
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 0 \text { or } x = \frac { 1 } { 2 } , \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 0 , \frac { 1 } { 2 } , \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex}
So, let us divide the integral by x = {tex} \frac 12{/tex} , we get 
Now ,
{tex} | x \cos \pi x | = \left\{ \begin{array} { c c } { x \cos \pi x , } & { 0 \leq x \leq \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \\ { - x \cos \pi x , } & { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \leq x \leq \frac { 3 } { 2 } } \end{array} \right.{/tex}
Now, {tex}I =\int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 / 2 } | x \cos \pi x | d x + \int _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { 3 / 2 } |x \cos \pi x | d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 / 2 } x \cos \pi x d x - \int _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { 3 / 2 } x \cos \pi x d x{/tex}..........(i)
Let I1 = {tex}\int_{} {\mathop x\limits_I } \mathop {\cos \pi x}\limits_{II} dx{/tex}
By Using integration by parts  , we get 
{tex}= x \frac { \sin \pi x } { \pi } - \int \frac { \sin \pi x } { \pi } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { x \sin \pi x } { \pi } + \frac { \cos \pi x } { \pi ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
Now, From Eq.(i), we have
{tex}I =\left[ x \frac { \sin \pi x } { \pi } + \frac { \cos \pi x } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 / 2 } - \left[ \frac { x \sin \pi x } { \pi } + \frac { \cos \pi x } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \right] _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { 3 / 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) - \left( 0 + \frac { \cos 0 } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \right) \right]{/tex}{tex}- \left[ \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 \pi } \sin \frac { 3 \pi } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \cos \frac { 3 \pi } { 2 } \right)\right.{/tex}{tex}- \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) ]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } - \frac { 1 } { \pi ^ { 2 } } - \left( \frac { - 3 } { 2 \pi } - \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \right){/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } - \frac { 1 } { \pi ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 3 } { 2 \pi } + \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 5 } { 2 \pi } - \frac { 1 } { \pi ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 5 \pi - 2 } { 2 \pi ^ { 2 } }{/tex}

{tex}\therefore I = \frac { 5 \pi - 2 } { 2 \pi ^ { 2 } }{/tex} sq units.

explanation: Let, {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} By partial fractions {tex}\frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{A}{x+2}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} x 2 + x + 1 = A(x 2 + 1) + Bx + C(x + 2) =Ax 2 + A + Bx 2 + 2Bx + Cx + 2Cx 2 + x + 1 = x 2 (A + B) + x(2B + C) + A + 2C On comparing coefficients of equation (i), we get 1 = A + B and 1 = 2B + C On solving above three equations, we get {tex}A=\frac{3}{5}{/tex} , {tex} B=\frac{2}{5} {/tex} and {tex}C=\frac{1}{5}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{3}{5(x+2)}+\frac{\frac{2}{5} x+\frac{1}{5}}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{\left(x^{2}+x+1\right) d x}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{3}{5} \int \frac{1}{x+2} d x+\frac{2}{5} \int \frac{x}{x^{2}+1} d x +\frac{1}{5} \int \frac{1}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{5} \log |x+2|+\frac{1}{5} \int \frac{2 x}{x^{2}+1}+\frac{1}{5} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{5} \log |x+2|+\frac{1}{5} \log \left|x^{2}+1\right|+\frac{1}{5} \tan ^{-1} x+c{/tex}
solution: Let, {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} By partial fractions {tex}\frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{A}{x+2}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} x 2 + x + 1 = A(x 2 + 1) + Bx + C(x + 2) =Ax 2 + A + Bx 2 + 2Bx + Cx + 2Cx 2 + x + 1 = x 2 (A + B) + x(2B + C) + A + 2C On comparing coefficients of equation (i), we get 1 = A + B and 1 = 2B + C On solving above three equations, we get {tex}A=\frac{3}{5}{/tex} , {tex} B=\frac{2}{5} {/tex} and {tex}C=\frac{1}{5}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{3}{5(x+2)}+\frac{\frac{2}{5} x+\frac{1}{5}}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{\left(x^{2}+x+1\right) d x}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{3}{5} \int \frac{1}{x+2} d x+\frac{2}{5} \int \frac{x}{x^{2}+1} d x +\frac{1}{5} \int \frac{1}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{5} \log |x+2|+\frac{1}{5} \int \frac{2 x}{x^{2}+1}+\frac{1}{5} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{5} \log |x+2|+\frac{1}{5} \log \left|x^{2}+1\right|+\frac{1}{5} \tan ^{-1} x+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let, {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex}
By partial fractions
{tex}\frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{A}{x+2}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+1}{/tex}
x2 + x + 1 = A(x2 + 1) + Bx + C(x + 2)
=Ax2 + A + Bx2 + 2Bx + Cx + 2Cx2 + x + 1
= x2(A + B) + x(2B + C) + A + 2C
On comparing coefficients of equation (i), we get
1 = A + B and 1 = 2B + C
On solving above three equations, we get
{tex}A=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}, {tex} B=\frac{2}{5} {/tex} and {tex}C=\frac{1}{5}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{3}{5(x+2)}+\frac{\frac{2}{5} x+\frac{1}{5}}{x^{2}+1}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{\left(x^{2}+x+1\right) d x}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{3}{5} \int \frac{1}{x+2} d x+\frac{2}{5} \int \frac{x}{x^{2}+1} d x +\frac{1}{5} \int \frac{1}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3}{5} \log |x+2|+\frac{1}{5} \int \frac{2 x}{x^{2}+1}+\frac{1}{5} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+1}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3}{5} \log |x+2|+\frac{1}{5} \log \left|x^{2}+1\right|+\frac{1}{5} \tan ^{-1} x+c{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\int_{a}^{b}{/tex} x3 dx = 0 and {tex}\int_{a}^{b}{/tex} x2 dx = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}, then find the values of a and b.
question_text: If {tex}\int_{a}^{b}{/tex} x 3 dx = 0 and {tex}\int_{a}^{b}{/tex} x 2 dx = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} , then find the values of a and b.
explanation: Here we have, {tex}\int_{a}^{b} x^{3} d x=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{4}\left(b^{4}-a^{4}\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b 4 - a 4 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b 2 - a 2 ) (b 2 + a 2 ) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b 2 - a 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = -a [ {tex}\because{/tex} b {tex}\neq{/tex} a] Now, {tex}\int_{a}^{b} x^{2} d x=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right)_{a}^{b}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{3}\left(b^{3}-a^{3}\right)=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} b 3 - a 3 = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} (-a) 3 - a 3 = 2 [ {tex}\because{/tex} b = -a] {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} -2 a 3 = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} a 3 = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} a = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} b = 1 Hence, a = -1 and b = 1
solution: Here we have, {tex}\int_{a}^{b} x^{3} d x=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{4}\left(b^{4}-a^{4}\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b 4 - a 4 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b 2 - a 2 ) (b 2 + a 2 ) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b 2 - a 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = -a [ {tex}\because{/tex} b {tex}\neq{/tex} a] Now, {tex}\int_{a}^{b} x^{2} d x=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right)_{a}^{b}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{3}\left(b^{3}-a^{3}\right)=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} b 3 - a 3 = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} (-a) 3 - a 3 = 2 [ {tex}\because{/tex} b = -a] {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} -2 a 3 = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} a 3 = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} a = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} b = 1 Hence, a = -1 and b = 1
solution_html:
Here we have,
{tex}\int_{a}^{b} x^{3} d x=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{4}\left(b^{4}-a^{4}\right)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b4 - a4 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b2 - a2) (b2 + a2) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 - a2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = -a   [{tex}\because{/tex} b {tex}\neq{/tex} a]
Now, {tex}\int_{a}^{b} x^{2} d x=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right)_{a}^{b}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{3}\left(b^{3}-a^{3}\right)=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} b3 - a3 = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} (-a)3 - a3 = 2 [{tex}\because{/tex} b = -a]
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} -2 a3 = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} a3 = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} a = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} b = 1
Hence, a = -1 and b = 1

question_text: {tex}\int \frac{(\cos 2 x-\cos 2 \alpha)}{(\cos x-\cos \alpha)} dx{/tex} is equal to:

options.html: 2 sin x + 2x cos {tex}\alpha{/tex} + C
options.explanation: Given {tex}\int \frac{(\cos 2 x-\cos 2 \alpha)}{(\cos x-\cos \alpha)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{-2 \sin \left(\frac{2 x+2 \alpha}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{2 x-2 \alpha}{2}\right)}{-2 \sin \left(\frac{x+\alpha}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{x-\alpha}{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{\sin (x+\alpha) \sin (x-\alpha)}{\sin \left(\frac{x+\alpha}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{x-\alpha}{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{2 \sin \left(\frac{x+\alpha}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{x+\alpha}{2}\right) \times 2 \sin \left(\frac{x-\alpha}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{x-\alpha}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{x+\alpha}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{x-\alpha}{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=2 \int 2 \cos \left(\frac{x+\alpha}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{x-\alpha}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=2 \int \cos \left(\frac{x+\alpha}{2}+\frac{x-\alpha}{2}\right)+\cos \left(\frac{x+\alpha}{2}-\frac{x-\alpha}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=2 \int(\cos x+\cos \alpha) d x{/tex} {tex}=2[\sin x+x \cos \alpha]+c{/tex} where c is the integrating constant.

explanation: Given {tex}\int \frac{(\cos 2 x-\cos 2 \alpha)}{(\cos x-\cos \alpha)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{-2 \sin \left(\frac{2 x+2 \alpha}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{2 x-2 \alpha}{2}\right)}{-2 \sin \left(\frac{x+\alpha}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{x-\alpha}{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{\sin (x+\alpha) \sin (x-\alpha)}{\sin \left(\frac{x+\alpha}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{x-\alpha}{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{2 \sin \left(\frac{x+\alpha}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{x+\alpha}{2}\right) \times 2 \sin \left(\frac{x-\alpha}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{x-\alpha}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{x+\alpha}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{x-\alpha}{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=2 \int 2 \cos \left(\frac{x+\alpha}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{x-\alpha}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=2 \int \cos \left(\frac{x+\alpha}{2}+\frac{x-\alpha}{2}\right)+\cos \left(\frac{x+\alpha}{2}-\frac{x-\alpha}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=2 \int(\cos x+\cos \alpha) d x{/tex} {tex}=2[\sin x+x \cos \alpha]+c{/tex} where c is the integrating constant.

question_html: Evaluate: {tex}\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} e^{x}\left(\frac{1-\sin x}{1-\cos x}\right) d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate: {tex}\int_{\pi / 2}^{\pi} e^{x}\left(\frac{1-\sin x}{1-\cos x}\right) d x{/tex}
explanation: Let the given integral is denoted by I, then {tex}I=\int_{\frac{\pi}{2}}^{x} e^{x} \frac{1-\sin x}{1-\cos x} d x=\int_{\frac{\pi}{2}}^{x} e^{x} \frac{1-2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}{2 \sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}\left[1-\cos x=2 \sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=-\int_{\frac{\pi}{2}}^{x} e^{x}\left(-\frac{1}{2} \ cosec ^{2} \frac{x}{2}+\cot \frac{x}{2}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=-\left.e^{z} \cot \frac{x}{2}\right|_{\frac{\tau}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\int e^{x}\left(F(x)+F^{\prime}(x)\right) d x=e^{x} F(x)\right]{/tex} {tex}=e^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex}
solution: Let the given integral is denoted by I, then {tex}I=\int_{\frac{\pi}{2}}^{x} e^{x} \frac{1-\sin x}{1-\cos x} d x=\int_{\frac{\pi}{2}}^{x} e^{x} \frac{1-2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}{2 \sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}\left[1-\cos x=2 \sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=-\int_{\frac{\pi}{2}}^{x} e^{x}\left(-\frac{1}{2} \ cosec ^{2} \frac{x}{2}+\cot \frac{x}{2}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=-\left.e^{z} \cot \frac{x}{2}\right|_{\frac{\tau}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\int e^{x}\left(F(x)+F^{\prime}(x)\right) d x=e^{x} F(x)\right]{/tex} {tex}=e^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the given integral is denoted by I, then
{tex}I=\int_{\frac{\pi}{2}}^{x} e^{x} \frac{1-\sin x}{1-\cos x} d x=\int_{\frac{\pi}{2}}^{x} e^{x} \frac{1-2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}{2 \sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}} d x{/tex}  {tex}\left[1-\cos x=2 \sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=-\int_{\frac{\pi}{2}}^{x} e^{x}\left(-\frac{1}{2} \ cosec ^{2} \frac{x}{2}+\cot \frac{x}{2}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}=-\left.e^{z} \cot \frac{x}{2}\right|_{\frac{\tau}{2}}{/tex}  {tex}\left[\int e^{x}\left(F(x)+F^{\prime}(x)\right) d x=e^{x} F(x)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=e^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex}

options.explanation: Since {tex}\int e^{x}\left(f(x)+f'(x)\right) d x{/tex} {tex}= e^x f(x) + C{/tex} Here {tex}f(x) = cosx,~~ f' (x) = -sin x.{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\int e^{x}\left(cosx-sinx\right) d x=e^xcosx+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: {tex} \int\limits_0^{\pi / 4} \frac{d x}{\cos ^3 x \sqrt{2 \sin 2 x}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \int\limits_0^{\pi / 4} \frac{d x}{\cos ^4 x \sqrt{\tan x}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \int\limits_0^{\pi / 4} \frac{\left(1+\tan ^2 x\right) \sec ^2 x}{\sqrt{\tan x}} d x {/tex} Put {tex}\tan x=t \Rightarrow \sec ^2 x d x=d t{/tex} {tex} \therefore I =\frac{1}{2} \int\limits_0^1 \frac{1+t^2}{\sqrt{t}} d t {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \int\limits_0^1\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{t}}+t^{3 / 2}\right) d t {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2}\left[2 \sqrt{t}+\frac{2}{5} t^{5 / 2}\right]_0^1 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{6}{5} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \int\limits_0^{\pi / 4} \frac{d x}{\cos ^3 x \sqrt{2 \sin 2 x}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \int\limits_0^{\pi / 4} \frac{d x}{\cos ^4 x \sqrt{\tan x}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \int\limits_0^{\pi / 4} \frac{\left(1+\tan ^2 x\right) \sec ^2 x}{\sqrt{\tan x}} d x {/tex} Put {tex}\tan x=t \Rightarrow \sec ^2 x d x=d t{/tex} {tex} \therefore I =\frac{1}{2} \int\limits_0^1 \frac{1+t^2}{\sqrt{t}} d t {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} \int\limits_0^1\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{t}}+t^{3 / 2}\right) d t {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2}\left[2 \sqrt{t}+\frac{2}{5} t^{5 / 2}\right]_0^1 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{6}{5} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \int\limits_0^{\pi / 4} \frac{d x}{\cos ^3 x \sqrt{2 \sin 2 x}} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{2} \int\limits_0^{\pi / 4} \frac{d x}{\cos ^4 x \sqrt{\tan x}} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{2} \int\limits_0^{\pi / 4} \frac{\left(1+\tan ^2 x\right) \sec ^2 x}{\sqrt{\tan x}} d x {/tex}
Put {tex}\tan x=t \Rightarrow \sec ^2 x d x=d t{/tex}
{tex} \therefore I =\frac{1}{2} \int\limits_0^1 \frac{1+t^2}{\sqrt{t}} d t {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{2} \int\limits_0^1\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{t}}+t^{3 / 2}\right) d t {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{2}\left[2 \sqrt{t}+\frac{2}{5} t^{5 / 2}\right]_0^1 {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{6}{5} {/tex}

explanation: Put {tex}x=a \tan ^2 \theta, d x=2 a \tan \theta \sec ^2 \theta d \theta{/tex} {tex} \int \sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{x}{a+x}} d x =2 a \int \sin ^{-1} {/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{\tan ^2 \theta}{1+\tan ^2 \theta}}\left(\tan \theta \cdot \sec ^2 \theta\right) d \theta {/tex} {tex} =2 a \int \theta \cdot\left(\tan \theta \sec ^2 \theta\right) d \theta {/tex} {tex} =2 a\left[\theta \frac{(\tan \theta)^2}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \int(\tan \theta)^2 d \theta\right] {/tex} {tex} =a\left[\theta \tan ^2 \theta-(\tan \theta-\theta)\right]+C {/tex} {tex} =a\left[\tan ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{x}{a}}\right) \cdot \frac{x}{a}-\sqrt{\frac{x}{a}}+\tan ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{x}{a}}\right)\right]+C {/tex} {tex} \text { or }(a+x) \tan ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{x}{a}}\right)-\sqrt{a x}+C {/tex}
solution: Put {tex}x=a \tan ^2 \theta, d x=2 a \tan \theta \sec ^2 \theta d \theta{/tex} {tex} \int \sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{x}{a+x}} d x =2 a \int \sin ^{-1} {/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{\tan ^2 \theta}{1+\tan ^2 \theta}}\left(\tan \theta \cdot \sec ^2 \theta\right) d \theta {/tex} {tex} =2 a \int \theta \cdot\left(\tan \theta \sec ^2 \theta\right) d \theta {/tex} {tex} =2 a\left[\theta \frac{(\tan \theta)^2}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \int(\tan \theta)^2 d \theta\right] {/tex} {tex} =a\left[\theta \tan ^2 \theta-(\tan \theta-\theta)\right]+C {/tex} {tex} =a\left[\tan ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{x}{a}}\right) \cdot \frac{x}{a}-\sqrt{\frac{x}{a}}+\tan ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{x}{a}}\right)\right]+C {/tex} {tex} \text { or }(a+x) \tan ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{x}{a}}\right)-\sqrt{a x}+C {/tex}
solution_html:
Put {tex}x=a \tan ^2 \theta, d x=2 a \tan \theta \sec ^2 \theta d \theta{/tex}
{tex} \int \sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{x}{a+x}} d x =2 a \int \sin ^{-1} {/tex}{tex}\sqrt{\frac{\tan ^2 \theta}{1+\tan ^2 \theta}}\left(\tan \theta \cdot \sec ^2 \theta\right) d \theta {/tex}
{tex} =2 a \int \theta \cdot\left(\tan \theta \sec ^2 \theta\right) d \theta {/tex}
{tex} =2 a\left[\theta \frac{(\tan \theta)^2}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \int(\tan \theta)^2 d \theta\right] {/tex}
{tex} =a\left[\theta \tan ^2 \theta-(\tan \theta-\theta)\right]+C {/tex}
{tex} =a\left[\tan ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{x}{a}}\right) \cdot \frac{x}{a}-\sqrt{\frac{x}{a}}+\tan ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{x}{a}}\right)\right]+C {/tex}
{tex} \text { or }(a+x) \tan ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{x}{a}}\right)-\sqrt{a x}+C {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: {tex} |x-1|+|x-2|+|x-5|{/tex} {tex}=(1-x)+(2-x)+(5-x)=8-3 x . {/tex} For {tex}1 \leq x \leq 2{/tex} : {tex} (x-1)+(2-x)+(5-x)=6-x {/tex} For {tex}2 \leq x \leq 5{/tex} : {tex} (x-1)+(x-2)+(5-x)=x+2 {/tex} Now integrate on each interval: {tex} \int\limits_0^1(8-3 x) d x=\left[8 x-\frac{3}{2} x^2\right]_0^1=8-\frac{3}{2}=\frac{13}{2} {/tex} {tex} \int\limits_1^2(6-x) d x=\left[6 x-\frac{1}{2} x^2\right]_1^2{/tex} {tex}=(12-2)-\left(6-\frac{1}{2}\right)=10-5.5=\frac{9}{2} {/tex} {tex} \int\limits_2^5(x+2) d x=\left[\frac{1}{2} x^2+2 x\right]_2^5{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{25}{2}+10\right)-(2+4)=\frac{33}{2} {/tex} Sum: {tex} \frac{13}{2}+\frac{9}{2}+\frac{33}{2}=\frac{55}{2} {/tex}
solution: {tex} |x-1|+|x-2|+|x-5|{/tex} {tex}=(1-x)+(2-x)+(5-x)=8-3 x . {/tex} For {tex}1 \leq x \leq 2{/tex} : {tex} (x-1)+(2-x)+(5-x)=6-x {/tex} For {tex}2 \leq x \leq 5{/tex} : {tex} (x-1)+(x-2)+(5-x)=x+2 {/tex} Now integrate on each interval: {tex} \int\limits_0^1(8-3 x) d x=\left[8 x-\frac{3}{2} x^2\right]_0^1=8-\frac{3}{2}=\frac{13}{2} {/tex} {tex} \int\limits_1^2(6-x) d x=\left[6 x-\frac{1}{2} x^2\right]_1^2{/tex} {tex}=(12-2)-\left(6-\frac{1}{2}\right)=10-5.5=\frac{9}{2} {/tex} {tex} \int\limits_2^5(x+2) d x=\left[\frac{1}{2} x^2+2 x\right]_2^5{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{25}{2}+10\right)-(2+4)=\frac{33}{2} {/tex} Sum: {tex} \frac{13}{2}+\frac{9}{2}+\frac{33}{2}=\frac{55}{2} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} |x-1|+|x-2|+|x-5|{/tex}{tex}=(1-x)+(2-x)+(5-x)=8-3 x . {/tex}
For {tex}1 \leq x \leq 2{/tex}:
{tex} (x-1)+(2-x)+(5-x)=6-x {/tex}
For {tex}2 \leq x \leq 5{/tex}:
{tex} (x-1)+(x-2)+(5-x)=x+2 {/tex}
Now integrate on each interval:
{tex} \int\limits_0^1(8-3 x) d x=\left[8 x-\frac{3}{2} x^2\right]_0^1=8-\frac{3}{2}=\frac{13}{2} {/tex}
{tex} \int\limits_1^2(6-x) d x=\left[6 x-\frac{1}{2} x^2\right]_1^2{/tex}{tex}=(12-2)-\left(6-\frac{1}{2}\right)=10-5.5=\frac{9}{2} {/tex}
{tex} \int\limits_2^5(x+2) d x=\left[\frac{1}{2} x^2+2 x\right]_2^5{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{25}{2}+10\right)-(2+4)=\frac{33}{2} {/tex}
Sum:
{tex} \frac{13}{2}+\frac{9}{2}+\frac{33}{2}=\frac{55}{2} {/tex}

explanation: {tex}\frac{5 x}{(x+1)\left(x^{2}+9\right)}=\frac{A}{x+1}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+9}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {A}=-\frac{1}{2}, {~B}=\frac{1}{2}, {C}=\frac{9}{2}{/tex} Given integral {tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{x+9}{x^{2}+9} d x{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x+\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{2 x}{x^{2}+9} d x+\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{18}{x^{2}+9} d x{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \log |{x}+1|+\frac{1}{4} \log \left({x}^{2}+9\right)+\frac{3}{2} \tan ^{-1} \frac{{x}}{3}+{C}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{5 x}{(x+1)\left(x^{2}+9\right)}=\frac{A}{x+1}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+9}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {A}=-\frac{1}{2}, {~B}=\frac{1}{2}, {C}=\frac{9}{2}{/tex} Given integral {tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{x+9}{x^{2}+9} d x{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x+\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{2 x}{x^{2}+9} d x+\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{18}{x^{2}+9} d x{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \log |{x}+1|+\frac{1}{4} \log \left({x}^{2}+9\right)+\frac{3}{2} \tan ^{-1} \frac{{x}}{3}+{C}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{5 x}{(x+1)\left(x^{2}+9\right)}=\frac{A}{x+1}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+9}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {A}=-\frac{1}{2}, {~B}=\frac{1}{2}, {C}=\frac{9}{2}{/tex}
Given integral
{tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{x+9}{x^{2}+9} d x{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x+\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{2 x}{x^{2}+9} d x+\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{18}{x^{2}+9} d x{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \log |{x}+1|+\frac{1}{4} \log \left({x}^{2}+9\right)+\frac{3}{2} \tan ^{-1} \frac{{x}}{3}+{C}{/tex}

solution: Given definite integral {tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \sqrt{(\sin x+\cos x)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}(\sin x+\cos x) d x{/tex} {tex}=[-\cos x+\sin x]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}{/tex} {tex}=1{/tex}

question_text: Evaluate: {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{5 \sin x+3 \cos x}{\sin x+\cos x} {~d} x{/tex}
explanation: {tex} I=\int\limits_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{5 \sin x+3 \cos x}{\sin x+\cos x} d x {/tex} ...(i) {tex} I=\int\limits_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{5 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)+3 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)+\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int\limits_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{5 \cos x+3 \sin x}{\cos x+\sin x} d x {/tex} ...(ii) Adding (i) and (ii), we get {tex} \left.2 I=\int\limits_0^{\pi / 2} 8 d x \Rightarrow I=4 x\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}=2 \pi {/tex}
solution: {tex} I=\int\limits_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{5 \sin x+3 \cos x}{\sin x+\cos x} d x {/tex} ...(i) {tex} I=\int\limits_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{5 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)+3 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)+\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int\limits_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{5 \cos x+3 \sin x}{\cos x+\sin x} d x {/tex} ...(ii) Adding (i) and (ii), we get {tex} \left.2 I=\int\limits_0^{\pi / 2} 8 d x \Rightarrow I=4 x\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}=2 \pi {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} I=\int\limits_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{5 \sin x+3 \cos x}{\sin x+\cos x} d x {/tex} ...(i)
{tex} I=\int\limits_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{5 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)+3 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)+\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int\limits_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{5 \cos x+3 \sin x}{\cos x+\sin x} d x {/tex} ...(ii)
Adding (i) and (ii), we get
{tex} \left.2 I=\int\limits_0^{\pi / 2} 8 d x \Rightarrow I=4 x\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}=2 \pi {/tex}

question_text: Find: {tex}\int \frac{\cos 2 x}{(\sin x+\cos x)^{2}} d x{/tex}
explanation: {tex}\int \frac{\cos 2 x}{(\sin x+\cos x)^{2}} d x =\int \frac{\cos ^{2} x-\sin ^{2} x}{(\sin x+\cos x)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{\cos x-\sin x}{\sin x+\cos x} d x{/tex} {tex}=\log |\sin x+\cos x|+C{/tex}
solution: {tex}\int \frac{\cos 2 x}{(\sin x+\cos x)^{2}} d x =\int \frac{\cos ^{2} x-\sin ^{2} x}{(\sin x+\cos x)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{\cos x-\sin x}{\sin x+\cos x} d x{/tex} {tex}=\log |\sin x+\cos x|+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: {tex}I=\int \frac{\sqrt{x}}{1+\sqrt{x^{3 / 2}}} d x{/tex} Put {tex}x^{3 / 2}=t \Rightarrow \frac{3}{2} \sqrt{x} d x=d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{2}{3} \int \frac{d t}{1+\sqrt{t}}{/tex} {tex}\operatorname{Put} \sqrt{t}=z \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{t}} d t=d z{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{2}{3} \int \frac{2 z}{1+z} d z{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{3}\left[\int 1 d z-\int \frac{1}{1+z} d z\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{3}[z-\log |1+z|]+C{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{3}[\sqrt{t}-\log |1+\sqrt{t}|]+C{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{3}\left[\sqrt{x^{3 / 2}}-\log \left|1+\sqrt{x^{3 / 2}}\right|\right]+C{/tex}
solution: {tex}I=\int \frac{\sqrt{x}}{1+\sqrt{x^{3 / 2}}} d x{/tex} Put {tex}x^{3 / 2}=t \Rightarrow \frac{3}{2} \sqrt{x} d x=d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{2}{3} \int \frac{d t}{1+\sqrt{t}}{/tex} {tex}\operatorname{Put} \sqrt{t}=z \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{t}} d t=d z{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{2}{3} \int \frac{2 z}{1+z} d z{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{3}\left[\int 1 d z-\int \frac{1}{1+z} d z\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{3}[z-\log |1+z|]+C{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{3}[\sqrt{t}-\log |1+\sqrt{t}|]+C{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{3}\left[\sqrt{x^{3 / 2}}-\log \left|1+\sqrt{x^{3 / 2}}\right|\right]+C{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}I=\int \frac{\sqrt{x}}{1+\sqrt{x^{3 / 2}}} d x{/tex}
Put {tex}x^{3 / 2}=t \Rightarrow \frac{3}{2} \sqrt{x} d x=d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{2}{3} \int \frac{d t}{1+\sqrt{t}}{/tex}
{tex}\operatorname{Put} \sqrt{t}=z \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{t}} d t=d z{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{2}{3} \int \frac{2 z}{1+z} d z{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4}{3}\left[\int 1 d z-\int \frac{1}{1+z} d z\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4}{3}[z-\log |1+z|]+C{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4}{3}[\sqrt{t}-\log |1+\sqrt{t}|]+C{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4}{3}\left[\sqrt{x^{3 / 2}}-\log \left|1+\sqrt{x^{3 / 2}}\right|\right]+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: f and g are continuous functions on interval {tex}[{a}, {b}]{/tex}. Given that {tex}{f}({a}-x)={f}(x){/tex} and {tex}g(x)+g(a-x)=a{/tex}, show that {tex}\int_{0}^{a} f(x) g(x) d x=\frac{a}{2} \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x{/tex}.
question_text: f and g are continuous functions on interval {tex}[{a}, {b}]{/tex} . Given that {tex}{f}({a}-x)={f}(x){/tex} and {tex}g(x)+g(a-x)=a{/tex} , show that {tex}\int_{0}^{a} f(x) g(x) d x=\frac{a}{2} \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}I=\int_{0}^{a} f(x) g(x) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) g(a-x) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{{a}} {f}({x})[{a}-{g}({x})] {dx}{/tex} {tex}I=a \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x-\int_{0}^{a} f(x) g(x) d x{/tex} Adding, we get {tex}{I}=\frac{{a}}{2} \int_{0}^{{a}} {f}({x}) {dx}{/tex}
solution: {tex}I=\int_{0}^{a} f(x) g(x) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) g(a-x) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{{a}} {f}({x})[{a}-{g}({x})] {dx}{/tex} {tex}I=a \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x-\int_{0}^{a} f(x) g(x) d x{/tex} Adding, we get {tex}{I}=\frac{{a}}{2} \int_{0}^{{a}} {f}({x}) {dx}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}I=\int_{0}^{a} f(x) g(x) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) g(a-x) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int_{0}^{{a}} {f}({x})[{a}-{g}({x})] {dx}{/tex}
{tex}I=a \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x-\int_{0}^{a} f(x) g(x) d x{/tex}
Adding, we get {tex}{I}=\frac{{a}}{2} \int_{0}^{{a}} {f}({x}) {dx}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let {tex}{I}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{{x}}{\cos {x}+\sin {x}} {dx} \Rightarrow {I}={/tex} {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\frac{\pi}{2}-{x}}{\sin {x}+\cos {x}} {dx} \Rightarrow 2 {I}=\frac{\pi}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{1}{\cos {x}+\sin {x}} {dx}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{\pi}{4 \sqrt{2}} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{1}{\cos (x-\pi / 4)} d x=\frac{\pi}{4 \sqrt{2}} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sec (x-\pi / 4) d x{/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow I=\frac{\pi}{4 \sqrt{2}} \log [\sec (x-\pi / 4)+\tan (x-\pi / 4)]\right]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4 \sqrt{2}}[\log (\sqrt{2}+1)-\log (\sqrt{2}-1)]{/tex} {tex}\text { Or }, I=\frac{\pi}{4 \sqrt{2}} \log \left(\frac{\sqrt{2}+1}{\sqrt{2}-1}\right){/tex}
solution: Let {tex}{I}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{{x}}{\cos {x}+\sin {x}} {dx} \Rightarrow {I}={/tex} {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\frac{\pi}{2}-{x}}{\sin {x}+\cos {x}} {dx} \Rightarrow 2 {I}=\frac{\pi}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{1}{\cos {x}+\sin {x}} {dx}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{\pi}{4 \sqrt{2}} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{1}{\cos (x-\pi / 4)} d x=\frac{\pi}{4 \sqrt{2}} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sec (x-\pi / 4) d x{/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow I=\frac{\pi}{4 \sqrt{2}} \log [\sec (x-\pi / 4)+\tan (x-\pi / 4)]\right]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4 \sqrt{2}}[\log (\sqrt{2}+1)-\log (\sqrt{2}-1)]{/tex} {tex}\text { Or }, I=\frac{\pi}{4 \sqrt{2}} \log \left(\frac{\sqrt{2}+1}{\sqrt{2}-1}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}{I}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{{x}}{\cos {x}+\sin {x}} {dx} \Rightarrow {I}={/tex}{tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\frac{\pi}{2}-{x}}{\sin {x}+\cos {x}} {dx} \Rightarrow 2 {I}=\frac{\pi}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{1}{\cos {x}+\sin {x}} {dx}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{\pi}{4 \sqrt{2}} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{1}{\cos (x-\pi / 4)} d x=\frac{\pi}{4 \sqrt{2}} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sec (x-\pi / 4) d x{/tex}
{tex}\left.\Rightarrow I=\frac{\pi}{4 \sqrt{2}} \log [\sec (x-\pi / 4)+\tan (x-\pi / 4)]\right]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4 \sqrt{2}}[\log (\sqrt{2}+1)-\log (\sqrt{2}-1)]{/tex}
{tex}\text { Or }, I=\frac{\pi}{4 \sqrt{2}} \log \left(\frac{\sqrt{2}+1}{\sqrt{2}-1}\right){/tex}

explanation: Using Partial fractions, {tex}\int \frac{3 x+1}{(x-2)^{2}(x+2)} d x =\frac{5}{16} {/tex} {tex}\int \frac{1}{x-2} d x+\frac{7}{4} \int \frac{1}{(x-2)^{2}} d x-\frac{5}{16} \int \frac{1}{x+2} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5}{16} \log |x-2|-\frac{7}{4(x-2)}-\frac{5}{16} \log |x+2|+C{/tex} {tex}\text { or } =\frac{5}{16} \log \left|\frac{x-2}{x+2}\right|-\frac{7}{4(x-2)}+C{/tex}
solution: Using Partial fractions, {tex}\int \frac{3 x+1}{(x-2)^{2}(x+2)} d x =\frac{5}{16} {/tex} {tex}\int \frac{1}{x-2} d x+\frac{7}{4} \int \frac{1}{(x-2)^{2}} d x-\frac{5}{16} \int \frac{1}{x+2} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5}{16} \log |x-2|-\frac{7}{4(x-2)}-\frac{5}{16} \log |x+2|+C{/tex} {tex}\text { or } =\frac{5}{16} \log \left|\frac{x-2}{x+2}\right|-\frac{7}{4(x-2)}+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Using Partial fractions,
{tex}\int \frac{3 x+1}{(x-2)^{2}(x+2)} d x =\frac{5}{16} {/tex}{tex}\int \frac{1}{x-2} d x+\frac{7}{4} \int \frac{1}{(x-2)^{2}} d x-\frac{5}{16} \int \frac{1}{x+2} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{5}{16} \log |x-2|-\frac{7}{4(x-2)}-\frac{5}{16} \log |x+2|+C{/tex}
{tex}\text { or } =\frac{5}{16} \log \left|\frac{x-2}{x+2}\right|-\frac{7}{4(x-2)}+C{/tex}

solution: {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\sin 2 p x}{\sin x} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\sin 2 p(\pi-x)}{\sin (\pi-x)} d x{/tex} {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{-\sin 2 p x}{\sin x} d x{/tex} Adding, we get {tex}2 {I}=0{/tex} {tex}\therefore {I}=0{/tex}
solution_html:

{tex}=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\sin 2 p(\pi-x)}{\sin (\pi-x)} d x{/tex}
{tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{-\sin 2 p x}{\sin x} d x{/tex}
Adding, we get
{tex}2 {I}=0{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate: {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\sin x \cos x}{\cos ^{4} x+\sin ^{4} x} d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate: {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\sin x \cos x}{\cos ^{4} x+\sin ^{4} x} d x{/tex}
explanation: {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\sin x \cos x}{\cos ^{4} x+\sin ^{4} x} d x{/tex} dividing numerator and denominator by {tex}\cos ^{4} x{/tex} , {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\tan x \sec ^{2} x}{1+\tan ^{4} x} d x{/tex} Put {tex}\tan ^{2} x=t \Rightarrow 2 \tan x \sec ^{2} x d x=d t{/tex} when {tex}x=0, t=0{/tex} ; when {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}, t=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_{0}^{1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{8}{/tex}
solution: {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\sin x \cos x}{\cos ^{4} x+\sin ^{4} x} d x{/tex} dividing numerator and denominator by {tex}\cos ^{4} x{/tex} , {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\tan x \sec ^{2} x}{1+\tan ^{4} x} d x{/tex} Put {tex}\tan ^{2} x=t \Rightarrow 2 \tan x \sec ^{2} x d x=d t{/tex} when {tex}x=0, t=0{/tex} ; when {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}, t=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_{0}^{1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{8}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\sin x \cos x}{\cos ^{4} x+\sin ^{4} x} d x{/tex}
dividing numerator and denominator by {tex}\cos ^{4} x{/tex},
{tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\tan x \sec ^{2} x}{1+\tan ^{4} x} d x{/tex}
Put {tex}\tan ^{2} x=t \Rightarrow 2 \tan x \sec ^{2} x d x=d t{/tex}
when {tex}x=0, t=0{/tex}; when {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}, t=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_{0}^{1}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{8}{/tex}

solution: {tex} I =\int\limits_0^\pi \frac{d x}{a^2 \cos ^2 x+b^2 \sin ^2 x} {/tex} {tex} =2 \int\limits_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{d x}{a^2 \cos ^2 x+b^2 \sin ^2 x} {/tex} {tex} =2 \int\limits_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sec ^2 x}{a^2+b^2 \tan ^2 x} d x {/tex} {tex} \tan x =t \text { gives } {/tex} {tex} I =2 \int\limits_0^{\infty} \frac{d t}{a^2+b^2 t^2} {/tex} {tex} \left.=\frac{2}{b^2} \cdot \frac{b}{a} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{b t}{a}\right)\right]_0^{\infty} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\pi}{a b} {/tex}

explanation: {tex} I =\int \frac{\cos x}{\left(4+\sin ^2 x\right)\left(5-4 \cos ^2 x\right)} d x {/tex} {tex} =\int \frac{\cos x}{\left(4+\sin ^2 x\right)\left(1+4 \sin ^2 x\right)} d x {/tex} {tex} \sin x=t \text { gives } {/tex} {tex} I =\int \frac{d t}{\left(4+t^2\right)\left(1+4 t^2\right)} {/tex} {tex} =-\frac{1}{15} \int \frac{d t}{4+t^2}+\frac{4}{15} \int \frac{d t}{1+4 t^2}{/tex} {tex} (\because \text { using Partial Fraction }) {/tex} {tex} =-\frac{1}{30} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{t}{2}\right)+\frac{2}{15} \tan ^{-1}(2 t)+C {/tex} {tex} =-\frac{1}{30} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin x}{2}\right)+\frac{2}{15} \tan ^{-1}(2 \sin x)+C {/tex}
solution: {tex} I =\int \frac{\cos x}{\left(4+\sin ^2 x\right)\left(5-4 \cos ^2 x\right)} d x {/tex} {tex} =\int \frac{\cos x}{\left(4+\sin ^2 x\right)\left(1+4 \sin ^2 x\right)} d x {/tex} {tex} \sin x=t \text { gives } {/tex} {tex} I =\int \frac{d t}{\left(4+t^2\right)\left(1+4 t^2\right)} {/tex} {tex} =-\frac{1}{15} \int \frac{d t}{4+t^2}+\frac{4}{15} \int \frac{d t}{1+4 t^2}{/tex} {tex} (\because \text { using Partial Fraction }) {/tex} {tex} =-\frac{1}{30} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{t}{2}\right)+\frac{2}{15} \tan ^{-1}(2 t)+C {/tex} {tex} =-\frac{1}{30} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin x}{2}\right)+\frac{2}{15} \tan ^{-1}(2 \sin x)+C {/tex}

solution: {tex}I =\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} ...(i) {tex}=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \sin (\pi-x)}{1+\cos ^{2}(\pi-x)} d x{/tex} {tex}I =\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} ...(ii) Adding (i) and (ii) {tex}2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\pi \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{\pi}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} Put {tex}\cos x=t \Rightarrow-\sin x d x=d t{/tex} {tex}I =-\frac{\pi}{2} \int_{1}^{-1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\pi\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_{0}^{1}=\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}I =\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \sin (\pi-x)}{1+\cos ^{2}(\pi-x)} d x{/tex}
{tex}I =\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} ...(ii)
Adding (i) and (ii)
{tex}2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\pi \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{\pi}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
Put {tex}\cos x=t \Rightarrow-\sin x d x=d t{/tex}
{tex}I =-\frac{\pi}{2} \int_{1}^{-1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\pi\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_{0}^{1}=\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex}

solution: {tex}I=\int \frac{\sin x+\cos x}{\sqrt{\sin x \cos x}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2} \int \frac{\sin {x}+\cos {x}}{\sqrt{\sin 2 {x}}} {dx}{/tex} Put {tex}\sin {x}-\cos {x}={t} \Rightarrow(\cos {x}+\sin {x}) {dx}={dt}{/tex} On squaring both sides, we get {tex}1-\sin 2 {x}={t}^{2}{/tex} {tex}I=\sqrt{2} \int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-t^{2}}} d t{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2} \sin ^{-1} {t}+{C}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2} \sin ^{-1}(\sin {x}-\cos {x})+{C}{/tex}

question_html: Evaluate: {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \tan x}{\sec x+\tan x} d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate: {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \tan x}{\sec x+\tan x} d x{/tex}
explanation: {tex}\text {Let }{/tex} {tex}I =\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \tan x}{\sec x+\tan x} d x{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Rightarrow I =\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \tan x}{\sec x+\tan x} d x{/tex} ...(ii) Adding (i) & (ii), we get {tex}2 I=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\tan x}{\sec x+\tan x} d x {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 I =\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\tan x(\sec x-\tan x)}{\sec ^{2} x-\tan ^{2} x} d x{/tex} {tex}=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi}\left(\sec x \tan x-\sec ^{2} x+1\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=\pi(\sec x-\tan x+x)]_{0}^{\pi}{/tex} {tex}=\pi(-1+\pi-1)=\pi(\pi-2){/tex} {tex}\therefore I =\frac{\pi}{2}(\pi-2) \text { or } \pi\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-1\right){/tex}
solution: {tex}\text {Let }{/tex} {tex}I =\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \tan x}{\sec x+\tan x} d x{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Rightarrow I =\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \tan x}{\sec x+\tan x} d x{/tex} ...(ii) Adding (i) & (ii), we get {tex}2 I=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\tan x}{\sec x+\tan x} d x {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 I =\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\tan x(\sec x-\tan x)}{\sec ^{2} x-\tan ^{2} x} d x{/tex} {tex}=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi}\left(\sec x \tan x-\sec ^{2} x+1\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=\pi(\sec x-\tan x+x)]_{0}^{\pi}{/tex} {tex}=\pi(-1+\pi-1)=\pi(\pi-2){/tex} {tex}\therefore I =\frac{\pi}{2}(\pi-2) \text { or } \pi\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-1\right){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\text {Let }{/tex}{tex}I =\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \tan x}{\sec x+\tan x} d x{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow I =\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \tan x}{\sec x+\tan x} d x{/tex} ...(ii)
Adding (i) & (ii), we get
{tex}2 I=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\tan x}{\sec x+\tan x} d x {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 I =\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\tan x(\sec x-\tan x)}{\sec ^{2} x-\tan ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi}\left(\sec x \tan x-\sec ^{2} x+1\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\pi(\sec x-\tan x+x)]_{0}^{\pi}{/tex}
{tex}=\pi(-1+\pi-1)=\pi(\pi-2){/tex}
{tex}\therefore I =\frac{\pi}{2}(\pi-2) \text { or } \pi\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-1\right){/tex}

question_html: Evaluate the definite integral  {tex}\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin 2 x \tan ^{-1}(\sin x) d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate the definite integral {tex}\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin 2 x \tan ^{-1}(\sin x) d x{/tex}
explanation: According to the question , {tex}I =\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } 2 \sin x \cos x \tan ^ { - 1 } ( \sin x ) d x{/tex} Put , sin x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x dx = dt Lower Limit , when x= 0, then t = sin 0 =0 Upper Limit , when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , then t = sin {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} = 1. {tex}\therefore \quad I = 2 \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } t \times \tan ^ { - 1 } t d t{/tex} Applying integration by parts,we get {tex}I = 2 \left[ \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \times \tan ^ { - 1 } t \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } - 2 \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { 1 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } \times \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } d t{/tex} {tex}= 2 \left[ \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \times \tan ^ { - 1 } t \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } - \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } d t{/tex} {tex}= 2 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \tan ^ { - 1 } ( 1 ) - \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { 1 + t ^ { 2 } - 1 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } d t{/tex} {tex}= 1 \times \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \left( \frac { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } \right) d t{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \left( 1 - \frac { 1 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } \right) d t{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \left[ t - \tan ^ { - 1 } t \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 } - 1 + \tan ^ { - 1 } 1{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 } - 1 + \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { 2 \pi } { 4 } - 1{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 1 {/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 1 {/tex}
solution: According to the question , {tex}I =\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } 2 \sin x \cos x \tan ^ { - 1 } ( \sin x ) d x{/tex} Put , sin x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x dx = dt Lower Limit , when x= 0, then t = sin 0 =0 Upper Limit , when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , then t = sin {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} = 1. {tex}\therefore \quad I = 2 \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } t \times \tan ^ { - 1 } t d t{/tex} Applying integration by parts,we get {tex}I = 2 \left[ \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \times \tan ^ { - 1 } t \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } - 2 \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { 1 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } \times \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } d t{/tex} {tex}= 2 \left[ \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \times \tan ^ { - 1 } t \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } - \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } d t{/tex} {tex}= 2 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \tan ^ { - 1 } ( 1 ) - \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { 1 + t ^ { 2 } - 1 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } d t{/tex} {tex}= 1 \times \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \left( \frac { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } \right) d t{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \left( 1 - \frac { 1 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } \right) d t{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \left[ t - \tan ^ { - 1 } t \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 } - 1 + \tan ^ { - 1 } 1{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 } - 1 + \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { 2 \pi } { 4 } - 1{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 1 {/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 1 {/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question , {tex}I =\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } 2 \sin x \cos x \tan ^ { - 1 } ( \sin x ) d x{/tex}
Put , sin x = t
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x dx = dt
Lower Limit , when x= 0, then t = sin 0 =0
Upper Limit , when x ={tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , then t = sin{tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} = 1.
{tex}\therefore \quad I = 2 \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } t \times \tan ^ { - 1 } t d t{/tex}
Applying integration by parts,we get 
{tex}I = 2 \left[ \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \times \tan ^ { - 1 } t \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } - 2 \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { 1 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } \times \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } d t{/tex}
{tex}= 2 \left[ \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \times \tan ^ { - 1 } t \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } - \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } d t{/tex}
{tex}= 2 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \tan ^ { - 1 } ( 1 ) - \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { 1 + t ^ { 2 } - 1 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } d t{/tex}
{tex}= 1 \times \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \left( \frac { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } \right) d t{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \left( 1 - \frac { 1 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } \right) d t{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \left[ t - \tan ^ { - 1 } t \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 } - 1 + \tan ^ { - 1 } 1{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 } - 1 + \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { 2 \pi } { 4 } - 1{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 1 {/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 1 {/tex}

explanation: Let I = {tex}\int _ \limits{ 0 } ^ { \frac {\pi} 4 } \frac { \sin x + \cos x } { 16 + 9 \sin 2 x } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int _ \limits{ 0 } ^ {\frac {\pi} 4 } \frac { \sin x + \cos x } { 9 ( 2 \sin x \cos x ) + 16 } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int _ \limits{ 0 } ^ { \frac{\pi} 4 } \frac { \sin x + \cos x } { - 9 ( - 2 \sin x \cos x ) + 16 } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int _ \limits{ 0 } ^ { \frac {\pi} 4 } \frac { \sin x + \cos x } { - 9 \left(1 - 2 \sin x \cos x - 1 \right) + 16 } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int _ \limits{ 0 } ^ {\frac {\pi} 4 } \frac { \sin x + \cos x } { - solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int _ \limits{ 0 } ^ { \frac {\pi} 4 } \frac { \sin x + \cos x } { 16 + 9 \sin 2 x } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int _ \limits{ 0 } ^ {\frac {\pi} 4 } \frac { \sin x + \cos x } { 9 ( 2 \sin x \cos x ) + 16 } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int _ \limits{ 0 } ^ { \frac{\pi} 4 } \frac { \sin x + \cos x } { - 9 ( - 2 \sin x \cos x ) + 16 } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int _ \limits{ 0 } ^ { \frac {\pi} 4 } \frac { \sin x + \cos x } { - 9 \left(1 - 2 \sin x \cos x - 1 \right) + 16 } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int _ \limits{ 0 } ^ {\frac {\pi} 4 } \frac { \sin x + \cos x } { - 9 \left( \sin ^ { 2 } x + \cos ^ { 2 } x - 2 \sin x \cos x - 1 \right) + 16 } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int _\limits{ 0 } ^ { \frac {\pi} 4 } \frac { \sin x + \cos x } { 25 - 9 ( \sin x - \cos x ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
Put {tex}sinx -cosx = t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (cosx + sinx)dx = dt
Also, when, x = 0, then t = -1
and when, x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}, then t = 0
{tex}\therefore I = \int _ \limits{ - 1 } ^ { 0 } \frac { d t } { 25 - 9 t ^ { 2 } } {/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 9 } \int _ \limits{ - 1 } ^ { 0 } \frac { d t } { \left( \frac { 5 } { 3 } \right) ^ { 2 } - t ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 9 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 \times \frac { 5 } { 3 } } \left[ \log \left| \frac { \frac { 5 } { 3 } + t } { \frac { 5 } { 3 } - t } \right| \right] _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 }{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { a ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 a } \log \left| \frac { a + x } { a - x } \right| + c \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 30 } \left[ \log \left| \frac { 5 + 3 t } { 5 - 3 t } \right| \right] _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 30 } \left[ \log 1 - \log \left( \frac { 2 } { 8 } \right) \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 30 } \left[ 0 - \log \frac { 1 } { 4 } \right]{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex}log 1 = 0]
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 30 } \left[ - \log 4 ^ { - 1 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 30 } \log 4{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 30 } \log 2^2{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 2 } { 30 } \log2{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 15 } \log2{/tex}

explanation: {tex}I = \int_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3} {\frac{{\sin x + \cos x}}{{\sqrt {\sin 2x} }}dx}{/tex} put sin x - cos x = t (cos x + sin x)dx = dt (sin x - cos x) 2 = t 2 sin 2 x + cos 2 x - 2 sin x cos x = t 2 1 - sin 2x = t 2 sin 2x = 1 - t 2 When {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{6},{/tex} then {tex}t = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}{/tex} When {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{3},{/tex} then {tex}t = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} - \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\int_{\frac{{1 - \sqrt 3 }}{2}}^{\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}} {\frac{{dt}}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}} {/tex} {tex}= \left[ {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t} \right]_{\frac{{1 - \sqrt 3 }}{2}}^{\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}}{/tex} {tex}= {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}} \right) - {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{1 - \sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right){/tex} {tex}= {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}} \right) + {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}} \right){/tex} {tex}= 2{\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}} \right){/tex}
solution: {tex}I = \int_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3} {\frac{{\sin x + \cos x}}{{\sqrt {\sin 2x} }}dx}{/tex} put sin x - cos x = t (cos x + sin x)dx = dt (sin x - cos x) 2 = t 2 sin 2 x + cos 2 x - 2 sin x cos x = t 2 1 - sin 2x = t 2 sin 2x = 1 - t 2 When {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{6},{/tex} then {tex}t = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}{/tex} When {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{3},{/tex} then {tex}t = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} - \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\int_{\frac{{1 - \sqrt 3 }}{2}}^{\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}} {\frac{{dt}}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}} {/tex} {tex}= \left[ {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t} \right]_{\frac{{1 - \sqrt 3 }}{2}}^{\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}}{/tex} {tex}= {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}} \right) - {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{1 - \sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right){/tex} {tex}= {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}} \right) + {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}} \right){/tex} {tex}= 2{\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}I = \int_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3} {\frac{{\sin x + \cos x}}{{\sqrt {\sin 2x} }}dx}{/tex}

put sin x - cos x = t

(cos x + sin x)dx = dt

(sin x - cos x)2 = t2

sin2x + cos2x - 2 sin x cos x = t2

1 - sin 2x = t2

sin 2x = 1 - t2

When {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{6},{/tex} then {tex}t = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}{/tex}

When {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{3},{/tex} then {tex}t = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} - \frac{1}{2}{/tex}

{tex}\int_{\frac{{1 - \sqrt 3 }}{2}}^{\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}} {\frac{{dt}}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}} {/tex}

{tex}= \left[ {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t} \right]_{\frac{{1 - \sqrt 3 }}{2}}^{\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}}{/tex}

{tex}= {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}} \right) - {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{1 - \sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right){/tex}

{tex}= {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}} \right) + {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}} \right){/tex}

{tex}= 2{\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}} \right){/tex}

question_html: {tex}\int_\limits0^\frac{\pi}{2} \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{1+\sin x \cos x} d x{/tex} is equal to:
question_text: {tex}\int_\limits0^\frac{\pi}{2} \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{1+\sin x \cos x} d x{/tex} is equal to:

options.explanation: The integral {tex} I=\int_\limits0^\frac{\pi}{2} \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{1+\sin x \cos x} d x{/tex} equals zero. By symmetry, {tex}I+I^{\prime}=0{/tex} , where {tex}I^{\prime}{/tex} is the integral with sin and cos swapped. Thus, the answer is zero.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}\int_\limits0^{\mathrm{a}} \frac{1}{4+\mathrm{x}^2} \mathrm{dx}=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} , then the value of ‘a’ is:

options.explanation: The given integral is: {tex}\int_\limits0^{\mathrm{a}} \frac{1}{4+\mathrm{x}^2} \mathrm{dx}=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} To solve this integral, we can use the substitution x = 2 tan {tex}\theta{/tex} . Then, dx = 2 sec 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} d {tex}\theta{/tex} . Substituting these into the integral, we get: {tex} \int_0^a \frac{1}{4+x^2} d x=\int_0^{\arctan (a / 2)} \frac{2 \sec ^2 \theta}{4+4 \tan ^2 \theta} d \theta{/tex} Simplifying the expression inside the integral: {tex} \int_0^{\arctan (a / 2)} \frac{1}{2} d \theta=\frac{1}{2}[\theta]_0^{\arctan (a / 2)}{/tex} Evaluating the integral at the limits of integration: {tex} \frac{1}{2}[\arctan (a / 2)-0]=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} Solving for a: {tex} \arctan (a / 2)=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} Taking the tangent of both sides: {tex} a / 2=\tan (\pi / 3)=\sqrt{3}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}a=2 \sqrt{3}{/tex} .

explanation: The given integral is: {tex}\int_\limits0^{\mathrm{a}} \frac{1}{4+\mathrm{x}^2} \mathrm{dx}=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} To solve this integral, we can use the substitution x = 2 tan {tex}\theta{/tex} . Then, dx = 2 sec 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} d {tex}\theta{/tex} . Substituting these into the integral, we get: {tex} \int_0^a \frac{1}{4+x^2} d x=\int_0^{\arctan (a / 2)} \frac{2 \sec ^2 \theta}{4+4 \tan ^2 \theta} d \theta{/tex} Simplifying the expression inside the integral: {tex} \int_0^{\arctan (a / 2)} \frac{1}{2} d \theta=\frac{1}{2}[\theta]_0^{\arctan (a / 2)}{/tex} Evaluating the integral at the limits of integration: {tex} \frac{1}{2}[\arctan (a / 2)-0]=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} Solving for a: {tex} \arctan (a / 2)=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} Taking the tangent of both sides: {tex} a / 2=\tan (\pi / 3)=\sqrt{3}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}a=2 \sqrt{3}{/tex} .

explanation: {tex} \int_\limits1^3(|x-1|+|x-2|+|x-3|) d x {/tex} {tex} = \int_\limits1^3(x-1) d x+\int_\limits1^2-(x-2) d x{/tex} {tex}+\int_\limits2^3(x-2) d x-\int_\limits1^3(x-3) d x {/tex} {tex} =\int_\limits1^3 2 d x+\int_\limits1^2(2-x) d x+\int_\limits2^3(x-2) d x {/tex} {tex} =[2 x]_1^3+\left[\frac{(2-x)^2}{-2}\right]_1^2+\left[\frac{(x-2)^2}{2}\right]_2^3 {/tex} {tex} =4+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}=5 {/tex}
solution: {tex} \int_\limits1^3(|x-1|+|x-2|+|x-3|) d x {/tex} {tex} = \int_\limits1^3(x-1) d x+\int_\limits1^2-(x-2) d x{/tex} {tex}+\int_\limits2^3(x-2) d x-\int_\limits1^3(x-3) d x {/tex} {tex} =\int_\limits1^3 2 d x+\int_\limits1^2(2-x) d x+\int_\limits2^3(x-2) d x {/tex} {tex} =[2 x]_1^3+\left[\frac{(2-x)^2}{-2}\right]_1^2+\left[\frac{(x-2)^2}{2}\right]_2^3 {/tex} {tex} =4+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}=5 {/tex}

question_html: Find: {tex}\int \frac{(3 \cos x-2) \sin x}{5-\sin ^2 x-4 \cos x} d x{/tex}
question_text: Find: {tex}\int \frac{(3 \cos x-2) \sin x}{5-\sin ^2 x-4 \cos x} d x{/tex}
explanation: {tex} \int \frac{(3 \cos x-2) \sin x}{5-\sin ^2 x-4 \cos x} d x, \text { Put } \cos {x}={t} {/tex} {tex}\text { so that, }-\sin {x} {dx}={dt} {/tex} {tex} =\int \frac{2-3 t}{5-\left(1-t^2\right)-4 t} d t {/tex} {tex} =\int \frac{2-3 t}{(t-2)^2} d t {/tex} {tex} \int \frac{2-3 t}{(t-2)^2} d t=-3 \int \frac{1}{t-2} d t-4 \int \frac{1}{(t-2)^2} {dt} {/tex} {tex} =-3 \log |t-2|-4\left(\frac{-1}{t-2}\right)+C {/tex} {tex} =-3 \log |\cos x-2|+\frac{4}{\cos x-2}+C {/tex}
solution: {tex} \int \frac{(3 \cos x-2) \sin x}{5-\sin ^2 x-4 \cos x} d x, \text { Put } \cos {x}={t} {/tex} {tex}\text { so that, }-\sin {x} {dx}={dt} {/tex} {tex} =\int \frac{2-3 t}{5-\left(1-t^2\right)-4 t} d t {/tex} {tex} =\int \frac{2-3 t}{(t-2)^2} d t {/tex} {tex} \int \frac{2-3 t}{(t-2)^2} d t=-3 \int \frac{1}{t-2} d t-4 \int \frac{1}{(t-2)^2} {dt} {/tex} {tex} =-3 \log |t-2|-4\left(\frac{-1}{t-2}\right)+C {/tex} {tex} =-3 \log |\cos x-2|+\frac{4}{\cos x-2}+C {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \int \frac{(3 \cos x-2) \sin x}{5-\sin ^2 x-4 \cos x} d x, \text { Put } \cos {x}={t} {/tex}{tex}\text { so that, }-\sin {x} {dx}={dt} {/tex}
{tex} =\int \frac{2-3 t}{5-\left(1-t^2\right)-4 t} d t {/tex}
{tex} =\int \frac{2-3 t}{(t-2)^2} d t {/tex}
{tex} \int \frac{2-3 t}{(t-2)^2} d t=-3 \int \frac{1}{t-2} d t-4 \int \frac{1}{(t-2)^2} {dt} {/tex}
{tex} =-3 \log |t-2|-4\left(\frac{-1}{t-2}\right)+C {/tex}
{tex} =-3 \log |\cos x-2|+\frac{4}{\cos x-2}+C {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: {tex} {I}=\int_\limits{-2}^2 \frac{x^3+|x|+1}{x^2+4|x|+4} d x{/tex} {tex} = \int_\limits{-2}^2 \frac{x^3}{x^2+4|x|+4} d x+\int_\limits{-2}^2 \frac{|x|+1}{x^2+4|x|+4} d x {/tex} {tex} = I_1+I_2(\text { say }){/tex} ...(1) {tex} I_1 =0\left(\because \frac{x^3}{x^2+4|x|+4} \text { is an odd function }\right) {/tex} {tex} =2 \int_\limits0^2 \frac{x+1}{x^2+4 x+4} d x{/tex} {tex}\left(\because \frac{|x|+1}{x^2+4|x|+4} \text { is an even function. }\right) {/tex} {tex} =2 \int_\limits0^2 \frac{x+1}{(x+2)^2} d x {/tex} {tex} \text { Put } {x}+2={t}, \text { so that } {dx}={dt} {/tex} {tex} =2 \int_\limits2^4 \frac{t-1}{t^2} d t {/tex} {tex} =2\left[\int_\limits2^4\left(\frac{1}{t}-\frac{1}{t^2}\right) d t\right] {/tex} {tex} =2\left[\log |t|+\frac{1}{t}\right]_2^4 {/tex} {tex} =2\left[\log 4+\frac{1}{4}-\log 2-\frac{1}{2}\right] {/tex} {tex} =2 \log 2-\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
solution: {tex} {I}=\int_\limits{-2}^2 \frac{x^3+|x|+1}{x^2+4|x|+4} d x{/tex} {tex} = \int_\limits{-2}^2 \frac{x^3}{x^2+4|x|+4} d x+\int_\limits{-2}^2 \frac{|x|+1}{x^2+4|x|+4} d x {/tex} {tex} = I_1+I_2(\text { say }){/tex} ...(1) {tex} I_1 =0\left(\because \frac{x^3}{x^2+4|x|+4} \text { is an odd function }\right) {/tex} {tex} =2 \int_\limits0^2 \frac{x+1}{x^2+4 x+4} d x{/tex} {tex}\left(\because \frac{|x|+1}{x^2+4|x|+4} \text { is an even function. }\right) {/tex} {tex} =2 \int_\limits0^2 \frac{x+1}{(x+2)^2} d x {/tex} {tex} \text { Put } {x}+2={t}, \text { so that } {dx}={dt} {/tex} {tex} =2 \int_\limits2^4 \frac{t-1}{t^2} d t {/tex} {tex} =2\left[\int_\limits2^4\left(\frac{1}{t}-\frac{1}{t^2}\right) d t\right] {/tex} {tex} =2\left[\log |t|+\frac{1}{t}\right]_2^4 {/tex} {tex} =2\left[\log 4+\frac{1}{4}-\log 2-\frac{1}{2}\right] {/tex} {tex} =2 \log 2-\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} {I}=\int_\limits{-2}^2 \frac{x^3+|x|+1}{x^2+4|x|+4} d x{/tex}
{tex} = \int_\limits{-2}^2 \frac{x^3}{x^2+4|x|+4} d x+\int_\limits{-2}^2 \frac{|x|+1}{x^2+4|x|+4} d x {/tex}
{tex} = I_1+I_2(\text { say }){/tex} ...(1)
{tex} I_1 =0\left(\because \frac{x^3}{x^2+4|x|+4} \text { is an odd function }\right) {/tex}
{tex} =2 \int_\limits0^2 \frac{x+1}{x^2+4 x+4} d x{/tex} {tex}\left(\because \frac{|x|+1}{x^2+4|x|+4} \text { is an even function. }\right) {/tex}
{tex} =2 \int_\limits0^2 \frac{x+1}{(x+2)^2} d x {/tex}
{tex} \text { Put } {x}+2={t}, \text { so that } {dx}={dt} {/tex}
{tex} =2 \int_\limits2^4 \frac{t-1}{t^2} d t {/tex}
{tex} =2\left[\int_\limits2^4\left(\frac{1}{t}-\frac{1}{t^2}\right) d t\right] {/tex}
{tex} =2\left[\log |t|+\frac{1}{t}\right]_2^4 {/tex}
{tex} =2\left[\log 4+\frac{1}{4}-\log 2-\frac{1}{2}\right] {/tex}
{tex} =2 \log 2-\frac{1}{2} {/tex}

explanation: {tex} \text { Given integral }{/tex} {tex}=\log \left(x^2-1\right) \times \frac{x^3}{3}-\int \frac{2 x}{x^2-1} \times \frac{x^3}{3} d x {/tex} {tex} =\log \left(x^2-1\right) \times \frac{x^3}{3}-\frac{2}{3} \int \frac{x^4-1+1}{x^2-1} d x {/tex} {tex} =\log \left(x^2-1\right) \times \frac{x^3}{3}{/tex} {tex}-\frac{2}{3}\left[\int\left(x^2+1\right) d x+\int \frac{1}{x^2-1} d x\right] {/tex} {tex} =\log \left(x^2-1\right) \times \frac{x^3}{3}{/tex} {tex}-\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{x^3}{3}+x+\frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right|\right]+C {/tex}

options.explanation: To find the anti-derivative of {tex}\sqrt{1+\sin 2 x}{/tex} , we use the identity {tex}\sin 2 x={/tex} {tex}2 \sin x \cos x{/tex} , giving {tex}\sqrt{1+2 \sin x \cos x}=\sqrt{(\sin x+\cos x)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sin x+\cos x{/tex} in {tex}\left[0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right]{/tex} . The anti-derivative is {tex}-\cos x+\sin x+C{/tex} . Thus, the answer is {tex}-\cos x+{/tex} {tex}\sin x{/tex}

explanation: Let {tex}x^{\frac{3}{2}}=t{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{3}{2} x^{\frac{1}{2}} d x=d t{/tex} The given integral becomes {tex}\frac{2}{3} \int t \sin ^{-1} t d t{/tex} {tex} =\frac{2}{3}\left[\sin ^{-1} t \times \frac{t^2}{2}-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-t^2}} \times \frac{t^2}{2} d t\right] {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{3}\left[\sin ^{-1} t \times t^2+\int \frac{1-t^2-1}{\sqrt{1-t^2}} d t\right] {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\left[\sin ^{-1} t \times t^2+\right.{/tex} {tex}\left.\int \sqrt{1-t^2} d t-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-t^2}} d t\right]{/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\left[t^2 \sin ^{-1} t+\frac{t}{2} \sqrt{1-t^2}\right.{/tex} {tex}\left.+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1} t-\sin ^{-1} t\right]+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3}\left[t^2 \sin ^{-1} t+\frac{t}{2} \sqrt{1-t^2}\right.{/tex} {tex}\left.-\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1} t\right]+C{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3}\left[x^3 \sin ^{-1}\left(x^{\frac{3}{2}}\right)+\right.{/tex} {tex}\left.\frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{2} \sqrt{1-x^3}-\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(x^{\frac{3}{2}}\right)\right]+C{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}x^{\frac{3}{2}}=t{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{3}{2} x^{\frac{1}{2}} d x=d t{/tex} The given integral becomes {tex}\frac{2}{3} \int t \sin ^{-1} t d t{/tex} {tex} =\frac{2}{3}\left[\sin ^{-1} t \times \frac{t^2}{2}-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-t^2}} \times \frac{t^2}{2} d t\right] {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{3}\left[\sin ^{-1} t \times t^2+\int \frac{1-t^2-1}{\sqrt{1-t^2}} d t\right] {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\left[\sin ^{-1} t \times t^2+\right.{/tex} {tex}\left.\int \sqrt{1-t^2} d t-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-t^2}} d t\right]{/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\left[t^2 \sin ^{-1} t+\frac{t}{2} \sqrt{1-t^2}\right.{/tex} {tex}\left.+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1} t-\sin ^{-1} t\right]+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3}\left[t^2 \sin ^{-1} t+\frac{t}{2} \sqrt{1-t^2}\right.{/tex} {tex}\left.-\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1} t\right]+C{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3}\left[x^3 \sin ^{-1}\left(x^{\frac{3}{2}}\right)+\right.{/tex} {tex}\left.\frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{2} \sqrt{1-x^3}-\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(x^{\frac{3}{2}}\right)\right]+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}x^{\frac{3}{2}}=t{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{3}{2} x^{\frac{1}{2}} d x=d t{/tex}
The given integral becomes {tex}\frac{2}{3} \int t \sin ^{-1} t d t{/tex}
{tex} =\frac{2}{3}\left[\sin ^{-1} t \times \frac{t^2}{2}-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-t^2}} \times \frac{t^2}{2} d t\right] {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{3}\left[\sin ^{-1} t \times t^2+\int \frac{1-t^2-1}{\sqrt{1-t^2}} d t\right] {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{3}{/tex}{tex}\left[\sin ^{-1} t \times t^2+\right.{/tex}{tex}\left.\int \sqrt{1-t^2} d t-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-t^2}} d t\right]{/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{3}{/tex}{tex}\left[t^2 \sin ^{-1} t+\frac{t}{2} \sqrt{1-t^2}\right.{/tex}{tex}\left.+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1} t-\sin ^{-1} t\right]+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{3}\left[t^2 \sin ^{-1} t+\frac{t}{2} \sqrt{1-t^2}\right.{/tex}{tex}\left.-\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1} t\right]+C{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{3}\left[x^3 \sin ^{-1}\left(x^{\frac{3}{2}}\right)+\right.{/tex}{tex}\left.\frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{2} \sqrt{1-x^3}-\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(x^{\frac{3}{2}}\right)\right]+C{/tex}

explanation: {tex}\int {{{\cos }^3}x.{e^{\log \sin x}}dx} {/tex} {tex}\because {e^{\log \theta }} = \theta {/tex} {tex}\therefore {e^{\log \sin x}} = \sin x{/tex} {tex}= \int {{{\cos }^3}x.\sin x}{/tex} put {tex}\cos x = t{/tex} {tex}- \sin xdx = dt{/tex} {tex}\sin xdx = - dt{/tex} {tex} = \int { - {t^3}dt} {/tex} {tex} = - \frac{{{t^4}}}{4} + c = - \frac{{{{\cos }^4}x}}{4} + c{/tex}
solution: {tex}\int {{{\cos }^3}x.{e^{\log \sin x}}dx} {/tex} {tex}\because {e^{\log \theta }} = \theta {/tex} {tex}\therefore {e^{\log \sin x}} = \sin x{/tex} {tex}= \int {{{\cos }^3}x.\sin x}{/tex} put {tex}\cos x = t{/tex} {tex}- \sin xdx = dt{/tex} {tex}\sin xdx = - dt{/tex} {tex} = \int { - {t^3}dt} {/tex} {tex} = - \frac{{{t^4}}}{4} + c = - \frac{{{{\cos }^4}x}}{4} + c{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\int {{{\cos }^3}x.{e^{\log \sin x}}dx} {/tex}

{tex}\because {e^{\log \theta }} = \theta {/tex}

{tex}\therefore {e^{\log \sin x}} = \sin x{/tex}

{tex}= \int {{{\cos }^3}x.\sin x}{/tex}

put {tex}\cos x = t{/tex}

{tex}- \sin xdx = dt{/tex}

{tex} = \int { - {t^3}dt} {/tex}

{tex} = - \frac{{{t^4}}}{4} + c = - \frac{{{{\cos }^4}x}}{4} + c{/tex}

question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { \cos x } } { e ^ { \cos x } + e ^ { - \cos x } } d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { \cos x } } { e ^ { \cos x } + e ^ { - \cos x } } d x{/tex}
explanation: According to the question , {tex}I=\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { \cos x } } { e ^ { \cos x } + e ^ { - \cos x } } d x{/tex} ........(i) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { \cos ( \pi - x ) } } { e ^ { \cos ( \pi - x ) } + e ^ { - \cos ( \pi - x ) } } d x{/tex} {tex}[\because \int_0^af(x) = \int _0^a f(a-x)]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { - \cos x } } { e ^ { - \cos x } + e ^ { \cos x } } d x{/tex} .....(ii) On adding Equation (i) and (ii), we get {tex}2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { \cos x } + e ^ { - \cos x } } { e ^ { \cos x } + e ^ { - \cos x } } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } 1 d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2 I = [ x ] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi }{/tex} {tex}=\pi - 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2I=\pi {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \pi / 2{/tex} {tex}\therefore I = \pi / 2{/tex} sq units.
solution: According to the question , {tex}I=\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { \cos x } } { e ^ { \cos x } + e ^ { - \cos x } } d x{/tex} ........(i) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { \cos ( \pi - x ) } } { e ^ { \cos ( \pi - x ) } + e ^ { - \cos ( \pi - x ) } } d x{/tex} {tex}[\because \int_0^af(x) = \int _0^a f(a-x)]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { - \cos x } } { e ^ { - \cos x } + e ^ { \cos x } } d x{/tex} .....(ii) On adding Equation (i) and (ii), we get {tex}2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { \cos x } + e ^ { - \cos x } } { e ^ { \cos x } + e ^ { - \cos x } } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } 1 d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2 I = [ x ] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi }{/tex} {tex}=\pi - 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2I=\pi {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \pi / 2{/tex} {tex}\therefore I = \pi / 2{/tex} sq units.
solution_html:
According to the question , {tex}I=\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { \cos x } } { e ^ { \cos x } + e ^ { - \cos x } } d x{/tex} ........(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { \cos ( \pi - x ) } } { e ^ { \cos ( \pi - x ) } + e ^ { - \cos ( \pi - x ) } } d x{/tex} {tex}[\because \int_0^af(x) = \int _0^a f(a-x)]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { - \cos x } } { e ^ { - \cos x } + e ^ { \cos x } } d x{/tex}.....(ii)
On adding Equation (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { \cos x } + e ^ { - \cos x } } { e ^ { \cos x } + e ^ { - \cos x } } d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } 1 d x{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow 2 I = [ x ] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi }{/tex}
{tex}=\pi - 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 2I=\pi {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = \pi / 2{/tex}

{tex}\therefore I = \pi / 2{/tex} sq units.

question_html: The value of {tex}\int_{1}^{{e}} \log x {~d} x{/tex} is :
question_text: The value of {tex}\int_{1}^{{e}} \log x {~d} x{/tex} is :

options.explanation: {tex}\int_{1}^{e} \log x d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{1}^{e} x^{0}\log x d x{/tex} {tex}=[x \log x]_{1}^{e}-\int_{1}^{e} \frac{1}{x} x d x{/tex} {tex}=[x \log x]_{1}^{e}-[x]_{1}^{e}{/tex} = (e - 0) - (e - 1) = e - e + 1 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int_{1}^{e} \log x d x{/tex} = 1

explanation: {tex}\int_{1}^{e} \log x d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{1}^{e} x^{0}\log x d x{/tex} {tex}=[x \log x]_{1}^{e}-\int_{1}^{e} \frac{1}{x} x d x{/tex} {tex}=[x \log x]_{1}^{e}-[x]_{1}^{e}{/tex} = (e - 0) - (e - 1) = e - e + 1 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int_{1}^{e} \log x d x{/tex} = 1

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find: {tex}\int \frac{2+\sin 2 x}{1+\cos 2 x} e^{x} d x{/tex}
question_text: Find: {tex}\int \frac{2+\sin 2 x}{1+\cos 2 x} e^{x} d x{/tex}
explanation: We are given {tex} \int \frac{2+\sin 2 x}{1+\cos 2 x} e^x d x {/tex} Step 1: Simplify the trigonometric part Use identities: {tex} 1+\cos 2 x=2 \cos ^2 x, \sin 2 x=2 \sin x \cos x {/tex} {tex} \frac{2+\sin 2 x}{1+\cos 2 x}=\frac{2+2 \sin x \cos x}{2 \cos ^2 x}=\frac{1}{\cos ^2 x}+\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}=\sec ^2 x+\tan x {/tex} Step 2: Substitute {tex} \int e^x\left(\sec ^2 x+\tan x\right) d x {/tex} Observe that: {tex} \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^x \tan x\right)=e^x \tan x+e^x \sec ^2 x {/tex} Step 3: Write the result {tex}\int e^x\left(\sec ^2 x+\tan x\right) d x=e^x \tan x+C{/tex} {tex}=e^x \cdot \tan x+c{/tex}
solution: We are given {tex} \int \frac{2+\sin 2 x}{1+\cos 2 x} e^x d x {/tex} Step 1: Simplify the trigonometric part Use identities: {tex} 1+\cos 2 x=2 \cos ^2 x, \sin 2 x=2 \sin x \cos x {/tex} {tex} \frac{2+\sin 2 x}{1+\cos 2 x}=\frac{2+2 \sin x \cos x}{2 \cos ^2 x}=\frac{1}{\cos ^2 x}+\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}=\sec ^2 x+\tan x {/tex} Step 2: Substitute {tex} \int e^x\left(\sec ^2 x+\tan x\right) d x {/tex} Observe that: {tex} \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^x \tan x\right)=e^x \tan x+e^x \sec ^2 x {/tex} Step 3: Write the result {tex}\int e^x\left(\sec ^2 x+\tan x\right) d x=e^x \tan x+C{/tex} {tex}=e^x \cdot \tan x+c{/tex}
solution_html:
We are given
{tex} \int \frac{2+\sin 2 x}{1+\cos 2 x} e^x d x {/tex}
Step 1: Simplify the trigonometric part
Use identities:
{tex} 1+\cos 2 x=2 \cos ^2 x, \sin 2 x=2 \sin x \cos x {/tex}
{tex} \frac{2+\sin 2 x}{1+\cos 2 x}=\frac{2+2 \sin x \cos x}{2 \cos ^2 x}=\frac{1}{\cos ^2 x}+\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}=\sec ^2 x+\tan x {/tex}
Step 2: Substitute
{tex} \int e^x\left(\sec ^2 x+\tan x\right) d x {/tex}
Observe that:
{tex} \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^x \tan x\right)=e^x \tan x+e^x \sec ^2 x {/tex}
Step 3: Write the result
{tex}\int e^x\left(\sec ^2 x+\tan x\right) d x=e^x \tan x+C{/tex}
{tex}=e^x \cdot \tan x+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: {tex} I=\int \frac{x^2+1}{\left(x^2+2\right)\left(x^2+4\right)} d x {/tex} Let {tex}x^2=y{/tex} , then {tex}\frac{x^2+1}{\left(x^2+2\right)\left(x^2+4\right)}=\frac{y+1}{(y+2)(y+4)}{/tex} Let {tex}\frac{y+1}{(y+2)(y+4)}=\frac{A}{y+2}+\frac{B}{y+4}{/tex} this gives {tex}A=-\frac{1}{2}, B=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} {tex} \therefore I=-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x^2+2} d x+\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{1}{x^2+4} d x {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I=-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}+\frac{3}{4} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+c {/tex}
solution: {tex} I=\int \frac{x^2+1}{\left(x^2+2\right)\left(x^2+4\right)} d x {/tex} Let {tex}x^2=y{/tex} , then {tex}\frac{x^2+1}{\left(x^2+2\right)\left(x^2+4\right)}=\frac{y+1}{(y+2)(y+4)}{/tex} Let {tex}\frac{y+1}{(y+2)(y+4)}=\frac{A}{y+2}+\frac{B}{y+4}{/tex} this gives {tex}A=-\frac{1}{2}, B=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} {tex} \therefore I=-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x^2+2} d x+\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{1}{x^2+4} d x {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I=-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}+\frac{3}{4} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+c {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} I=\int \frac{x^2+1}{\left(x^2+2\right)\left(x^2+4\right)} d x {/tex}
Let {tex}x^2=y{/tex}, then {tex}\frac{x^2+1}{\left(x^2+2\right)\left(x^2+4\right)}=\frac{y+1}{(y+2)(y+4)}{/tex}
Let {tex}\frac{y+1}{(y+2)(y+4)}=\frac{A}{y+2}+\frac{B}{y+4}{/tex}
this gives {tex}A=-\frac{1}{2}, B=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
{tex} \therefore I=-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x^2+2} d x+\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{1}{x^2+4} d x {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow I=-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex}{tex}+\frac{3}{4} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+c {/tex}

solution: {tex} F(x)=\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 x-x^2}} d x {/tex} {tex} =\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-(x-1)^2}} d x {/tex} {tex} =\sin ^{-1}(x-1)+c {/tex} {tex} \text { when } x=1, {/tex} {tex}y=0 \text { gives } c=0 {/tex} {tex} \therefore F(x)=\sin ^{-1}(x-1) {/tex}

explanation: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}}{x^{2}+6 x+12} d x{/tex} Therefore by long division we have, Therefore, {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{x^{2}+6 x+12}=1-\frac{(6 x+12)}{x^{2}+6 x+12}{/tex} ...(i) Let 6x + 12 = {tex}A \frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (x 2 + 6x + 12) + B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6x + 12 = A(2x + 6) + B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6x + 12 = (2A)x + 6A + B Equating Coefficients of like terms 2A = 6 A = 3 6A + B = 12 18 + B = 12 B = -6 {tex}\therefore \frac{x^{2}}{x^{2}+6 x+12}=1-\frac{3(2 x+6)-6}{x^{2}+6 x+12}{/tex} {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2} d x}{x^{2}+6 x+12}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} dx - 3 {tex}\int \frac{(2 x+6) d x}{x^{2}+6 x+12}+6 \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+6 x+12}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} dx - 3 {tex}\int \frac{(2 x+6) d x}{x^{2}+6 x+12}+6 \int \frac{d x}{(x+3)^{2}+(\sqrt{3})^{2}}{/tex} = x - 3 log |x + 6x + 12| + {tex}\frac{6}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+3}{\sqrt{3}}\right)+C{/tex} = x - 3 log |x + 6x + 12| + {tex}2 \sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+3}{\sqrt{3}}\right)+C{/tex}
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}}{x^{2}+6 x+12} d x{/tex} Therefore by long division we have, Therefore, {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{x^{2}+6 x+12}=1-\frac{(6 x+12)}{x^{2}+6 x+12}{/tex} ...(i) Let 6x + 12 = {tex}A \frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (x 2 + 6x + 12) + B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6x + 12 = A(2x + 6) + B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6x + 12 = (2A)x + 6A + B Equating Coefficients of like terms 2A = 6 A = 3 6A + B = 12 18 + B = 12 B = -6 {tex}\therefore \frac{x^{2}}{x^{2}+6 x+12}=1-\frac{3(2 x+6)-6}{x^{2}+6 x+12}{/tex} {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2} d x}{x^{2}+6 x+12}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} dx - 3 {tex}\int \frac{(2 x+6) d x}{x^{2}+6 x+12}+6 \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+6 x+12}{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} dx - 3 {tex}\int \frac{(2 x+6) d x}{x^{2}+6 x+12}+6 \int \frac{d x}{(x+3)^{2}+(\sqrt{3})^{2}}{/tex} = x - 3 log |x + 6x + 12| + {tex}\frac{6}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+3}{\sqrt{3}}\right)+C{/tex} = x - 3 log |x + 6x + 12| + {tex}2 \sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+3}{\sqrt{3}}\right)+C{/tex}
