explanation: Let I = {tex}=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \sin (\pi-x)}{1+\cos ^{2}(\pi-x)} d x{/tex} By using the properties of definite integrals = {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\pi \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x-I{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} Put cos x = t for {tex}x=\pi \Rightarrow t=-1, x=0 \Rightarrow t=+1{/tex} and {tex}-\sin x d x=d t{/tex} Therefore {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=\pi \int_{1}^{-1} \frac{-d t}{1+t^{2}}=\pi \int_{-1}^{1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\pi\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_{-1}^{1}=\pi\left[\tan ^{-1}(+1)-\tan ^{-1}(-1)\right]{/tex} = {tex}\pi\left[\frac{\pi}{2}\right]=\frac{\pi^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex}
solution: Let I = {tex}=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \sin (\pi-x)}{1+\cos ^{2}(\pi-x)} d x{/tex} By using the properties of definite integrals = {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\pi \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x-I{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} Put cos x = t for {tex}x=\pi \Rightarrow t=-1, x=0 \Rightarrow t=+1{/tex} and {tex}-\sin x d x=d t{/tex} Therefore {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=\pi \int_{1}^{-1} \frac{-d t}{1+t^{2}}=\pi \int_{-1}^{1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\pi\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_{-1}^{1}=\pi\left[\tan ^{-1}(+1)-\tan ^{-1}(-1)\right]{/tex} = {tex}\pi\left[\frac{\pi}{2}\right]=\frac{\pi^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \sin (\pi-x)}{1+\cos ^{2}(\pi-x)} d x{/tex}

By using the properties of definite integrals
= {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\pi \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x-I{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
Put cos x = t for {tex}x=\pi \Rightarrow t=-1, x=0 \Rightarrow t=+1{/tex} and {tex}-\sin x d x=d t{/tex}
Therefore {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=\pi \int_{1}^{-1} \frac{-d t}{1+t^{2}}=\pi \int_{-1}^{1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\pi\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_{-1}^{1}=\pi\left[\tan ^{-1}(+1)-\tan ^{-1}(-1)\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\pi\left[\frac{\pi}{2}\right]=\frac{\pi^{2}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex}

question_html: If {tex} \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } \frac { 1 } { 4 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { \pi } { 8 },{/tex} then find the value of a.
question_text: If {tex} \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } \frac { 1 } { 4 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { \pi } { 8 },{/tex} then find the value of a.
explanation: If {tex} \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } \frac { 1 } { 4 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { \pi } { 8 },{/tex} then we have to find the value of a. Given {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { a } \frac { 1 } { 4 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { \pi } { 8 }{/tex} ...........(i) Now, consider {tex}I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \left[ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { x } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { a }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { a ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { a } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { x } { a } + C \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { a } { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } ( 0 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { a } { 2 }{/tex} ...(ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { a } { 2 } = \frac { \pi } { 8 } \Rightarrow \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { a } { 2 } = \frac { \pi } { 4 } \Rightarrow \frac { a } { 2 } = \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { a } { 2 } = 1 \Rightarrow a = 2 \quad \left[ \because \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } = 1 \right]{/tex}
solution: If {tex} \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } \frac { 1 } { 4 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { \pi } { 8 },{/tex} then we have to find the value of a. Given {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { a } \frac { 1 } { 4 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { \pi } { 8 }{/tex} ...........(i) Now, consider {tex}I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \left[ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { x } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { a }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { a ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { a } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { x } { a } + C \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { a } { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } ( 0 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { a } { 2 }{/tex} ...(ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { a } { 2 } = \frac { \pi } { 8 } \Rightarrow \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { a } { 2 } = \frac { \pi } { 4 } \Rightarrow \frac { a } { 2 } = \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { a } { 2 } = 1 \Rightarrow a = 2 \quad \left[ \because \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } = 1 \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
If {tex} \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } \frac { 1 } { 4 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { \pi } { 8 },{/tex} then we have to find the value of a.

Given {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { a } \frac { 1 } { 4 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { \pi } { 8 }{/tex}...........(i)
Now, consider {tex}I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \left[ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { x } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { a }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { a ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { a } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { x } { a } + C \right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { a } { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } ( 0 ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { a } { 2 }{/tex} ...(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { a } { 2 } = \frac { \pi } { 8 } \Rightarrow \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { a } { 2 } = \frac { \pi } { 4 } \Rightarrow \frac { a } { 2 } = \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { a } { 2 } = 1 \Rightarrow a = 2 \quad \left[ \because \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } = 1 \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate the definite integral: {tex}\int_{1}^{2} e^{2 x}\left(\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{2 x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate the definite integral: {tex}\int_{1}^{2} e^{2 x}\left(\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{2 x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex}
explanation: We have, I = {tex}\int_{1}^{2} e^{2 x}\left(\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{2 x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} I = {tex}\int_{1}^{2} \frac{1}{x} \cdot e^{2 x}{/tex} - {tex}\int_{1}^{2} \frac{1}{2 x^{2}} \cdot e^{2 x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = I 1 - I 2 Now, I 1 = {tex}\int_{1}^{2} \frac{1}{x} e^{2 x}{/tex} (By parts we have) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 = {tex}[\frac{1}{x}]_1^2 \cdot \int _1^2 e^{2 x} d x{/tex} - {tex}\int_1^2-\frac{1}{x^{2}} \frac{e^{2 x}}{2} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 = {tex}\left[\frac{1}{x} \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}\right]_1^2{/tex} + {tex}\int _1^2\frac{1}{2 x^{2}} e^{2 x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 = {tex}\left[\frac{1}{2 x} e^{2 x}\right]_{1}^{2}{/tex} + I 2 As, I = I 1 - I 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\left[\frac{1}{2 x} e^{2 x}\right]_{1}^{2}{/tex} - I 2 + I 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\left[\frac{1}{2 x} e^{2 x}\right]_{1}^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{2} e^{4}-e^{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} e 2 (e 2 - 1)
solution: We have, I = {tex}\int_{1}^{2} e^{2 x}\left(\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{2 x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} I = {tex}\int_{1}^{2} \frac{1}{x} \cdot e^{2 x}{/tex} - {tex}\int_{1}^{2} \frac{1}{2 x^{2}} \cdot e^{2 x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = I 1 - I 2 Now, I 1 = {tex}\int_{1}^{2} \frac{1}{x} e^{2 x}{/tex} (By parts we have) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 = {tex}[\frac{1}{x}]_1^2 \cdot \int _1^2 e^{2 x} d x{/tex} - {tex}\int_1^2-\frac{1}{x^{2}} \frac{e^{2 x}}{2} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 = {tex}\left[\frac{1}{x} \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}\right]_1^2{/tex} + {tex}\int _1^2\frac{1}{2 x^{2}} e^{2 x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 = {tex}\left[\frac{1}{2 x} e^{2 x}\right]_{1}^{2}{/tex} + I 2 As, I = I 1 - I 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\left[\frac{1}{2 x} e^{2 x}\right]_{1}^{2}{/tex} - I 2 + I 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\left[\frac{1}{2 x} e^{2 x}\right]_{1}^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{2} e^{4}-e^{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} e 2 (e 2 - 1)
solution_html:
We have,
I = {tex}\int_{1}^{2} e^{2 x}\left(\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{2 x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex}  
I = {tex}\int_{1}^{2} \frac{1}{x} \cdot e^{2 x}{/tex} - {tex}\int_{1}^{2} \frac{1}{2 x^{2}} \cdot e^{2 x} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = I1 - I2 
Now, I1 = {tex}\int_{1}^{2} \frac{1}{x} e^{2 x}{/tex} (By parts we have)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I1 = {tex}[\frac{1}{x}]_1^2 \cdot \int _1^2 e^{2 x} d x{/tex} - {tex}\int_1^2-\frac{1}{x^{2}} \frac{e^{2 x}}{2} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I1 = {tex}\left[\frac{1}{x} \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}\right]_1^2{/tex} + {tex}\int _1^2\frac{1}{2 x^{2}} e^{2 x} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I1 = {tex}\left[\frac{1}{2 x} e^{2 x}\right]_{1}^{2}{/tex} + I2 
As, I = I1 - I2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\left[\frac{1}{2 x} e^{2 x}\right]_{1}^{2}{/tex} - I2 + I2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\left[\frac{1}{2 x} e^{2 x}\right]_{1}^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{2} e^{4}-e^{2}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}e2 (e2 - 1)

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the value of {tex}\int_\limits1^4|x-5| d x{/tex}
explanation: Here I = {tex}\int_1^{4}|x-5| d x{/tex} Now, |x - 5| = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} x-5, x \geq 5 \\ 5-x, x<5 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int_{1}^{4}(5-x) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\left[5 x-\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{1}^{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = [20 - {tex} \frac{16}{2}{/tex} - {tex}5 + \frac{1^2}{2}]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 20 - {tex}\frac{16}{2}{/tex} - 5 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 20 - 8 - 5 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 7 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{15}{2}{/tex}
solution: Here I = {tex}\int_1^{4}|x-5| d x{/tex} Now, |x - 5| = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} x-5, x \geq 5 \\ 5-x, x<5 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int_{1}^{4}(5-x) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\left[5 x-\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{1}^{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = [20 - {tex} \frac{16}{2}{/tex} - {tex}5 + \frac{1^2}{2}]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 20 - {tex}\frac{16}{2}{/tex} - 5 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 20 - 8 - 5 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 7 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{15}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here I = {tex}\int_1^{4}|x-5| d x{/tex} 
Now, |x - 5| = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} x-5, x \geq 5 \\ 5-x, x<5 \end{array}\right.{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int_{1}^{4}(5-x) d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\left[5 x-\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{1}^{4}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = [20 -{tex} \frac{16}{2}{/tex} - {tex}5 + \frac{1^2}{2}]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 20 - {tex}\frac{16}{2}{/tex} - 5 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 20 - 8 - 5 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 7 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{15}{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: {tex}\int \frac{x+1}{(x+2)(x+3)} d x{/tex} {tex}\frac{x+1}{(x+2)(x+3)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{x+2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{B}{x+3}{/tex} x + 1 = A(x + 3) + B(x + 2) x + 1 = (A + B)x + (3A + 2B) A + B = 1 and 3A + 2B = 1 A = -1, B = 2 {tex}\frac{x+1}{(x+2)(x+3)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{x+2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{2}{x+3}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{x+1}{(x+2)(x+3)} d x{/tex} = {tex}-\int \frac{1}{x+2} dx{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{2}{x+3} dx{/tex} = -log |x + 2| + 2log |x + 3| + C
solution: {tex}\int \frac{x+1}{(x+2)(x+3)} d x{/tex} {tex}\frac{x+1}{(x+2)(x+3)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{x+2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{B}{x+3}{/tex} x + 1 = A(x + 3) + B(x + 2) x + 1 = (A + B)x + (3A + 2B) A + B = 1 and 3A + 2B = 1 A = -1, B = 2 {tex}\frac{x+1}{(x+2)(x+3)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{x+2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{2}{x+3}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{x+1}{(x+2)(x+3)} d x{/tex} = {tex}-\int \frac{1}{x+2} dx{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{2}{x+3} dx{/tex} = -log |x + 2| + 2log |x + 3| + C
solution_html:
{tex}\int \frac{x+1}{(x+2)(x+3)} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{x+1}{(x+2)(x+3)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{x+2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{B}{x+3}{/tex} 
x + 1 = A(x + 3) + B(x + 2)
x + 1 = (A + B)x + (3A + 2B)
A + B = 1 and 3A + 2B = 1
A = -1, B = 2
{tex}\frac{x+1}{(x+2)(x+3)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{x+2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{2}{x+3}{/tex} 
{tex}\int \frac{x+1}{(x+2)(x+3)} d x{/tex} = {tex}-\int \frac{1}{x+2} dx{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{2}{x+3} dx{/tex} 
= -log |x + 2| + 2log |x + 3| + C

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the value of: {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-2 x}{1+x-x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex}
question_text: Find the value of: {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-2 x}{1+x-x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex}
explanation: {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-2 x}{1+x-x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}I=\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-x-x)}{1+x(1-x)}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1}(1-x) d x{/tex} - {tex}\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1} x d x{/tex} = [(1 - x) tan -1 (1 - x) - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\left.\ln \left(1+(1-x)^{2}\right)\right]_{0}^{1}{/tex} - [x tan -1 x - {tex}\left.\frac{1}{2} \ln \left(1+x^{2}\right)\right]_{0}^{1}{/tex} = [0 {tex}\times{/tex} tan -1 0 - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log (1) - tan -1 1 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log (1)] - [tan -1 (1) - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log 2 - tan -1 (0) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log (1)] = 0 - 0 - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log2 - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log 2 = - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + log 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = log 2 - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-2 x}{1+x-x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}I=\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-x-x)}{1+x(1-x)}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1}(1-x) d x{/tex} - {tex}\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1} x d x{/tex} = [(1 - x) tan -1 (1 - x) - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\left.\ln \left(1+(1-x)^{2}\right)\right]_{0}^{1}{/tex} - [x tan -1 x - {tex}\left.\frac{1}{2} \ln \left(1+x^{2}\right)\right]_{0}^{1}{/tex} = [0 {tex}\times{/tex} tan -1 0 - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log (1) - tan -1 1 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log (1)] - [tan -1 (1) - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log 2 - tan -1 (0) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log (1)] = 0 - 0 - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log2 - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log 2 = - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + log 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = log 2 - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\int \limits_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-2 x}{1+x-x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}I=\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-x-x)}{1+x(1-x)}\right) d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1}(1-x) d x{/tex} - {tex}\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1} x d x{/tex} 
= [(1 - x) tan-1 (1 - x) - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\left.\ln \left(1+(1-x)^{2}\right)\right]_{0}^{1}{/tex} - [x tan-1 x - {tex}\left.\frac{1}{2} \ln \left(1+x^{2}\right)\right]_{0}^{1}{/tex} 
= [0 {tex}\times{/tex} tan-1 0 - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log (1) - tan-1 1 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}log (1)] - [tan-1 (1) - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}log 2 - tan-1 (0) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}log (1)]
= 0 - 0 - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log2 - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log 2
= -{tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + log 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I = log 2 - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

explanation: I = {tex}\int \sec ^{3} x \ d x{/tex} by Integration by Pats with: u = secx and dv = sec 2 xdx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} du = secxtanxdx and v = tanx, = secxtanx − {tex}\int \sec x \tan ^{2} x d x{/tex} ​​​​​​by tan 2 x = sec 2 x − 1 = secxtanx − {tex}\int\left(\sec ^{3} x-\sec x\right) d x{/tex} Since {tex}\int \sec ^{3} x d x=I{/tex} = secx tanx − I + {tex}\int \sec x d x{/tex} by adding I and {tex}\int \sec x d x=\ln |\sec x+\tan x|+C_{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 I=\sec x \tan x+\ln |\sec x+\tan x|+C_{1}{/tex} by dividing by 2, {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{1}{2} \sec x \tan x+\frac{1}{2} \ln |\sec x+\tan x|+\frac{C_{1}}{2}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\int \sec ^{3} d x=\frac{1}{2} \sec x \tan x+\frac{1}{2} \ln |\sec x+\tan x|{/tex} + C
solution: I = {tex}\int \sec ^{3} x \ d x{/tex} by Integration by Pats with: u = secx and dv = sec 2 xdx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} du = secxtanxdx and v = tanx, = secxtanx − {tex}\int \sec x \tan ^{2} x d x{/tex} ​​​​​​by tan 2 x = sec 2 x − 1 = secxtanx − {tex}\int\left(\sec ^{3} x-\sec x\right) d x{/tex} Since {tex}\int \sec ^{3} x d x=I{/tex} = secx tanx − I + {tex}\int \sec x d x{/tex} by adding I and {tex}\int \sec x d x=\ln |\sec x+\tan x|+C_{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 I=\sec x \tan x+\ln |\sec x+\tan x|+C_{1}{/tex} by dividing by 2, {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{1}{2} \sec x \tan x+\frac{1}{2} \ln |\sec x+\tan x|+\frac{C_{1}}{2}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\int \sec ^{3} d x=\frac{1}{2} \sec x \tan x+\frac{1}{2} \ln |\sec x+\tan x|{/tex} + C
solution_html:
I =  {tex}\int \sec ^{3} x \ d x{/tex}
by Integration by Pats with:
u = secx and dv = sec2xdx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} du = secxtanxdx and v = tanx,
= secxtanx −  {tex}\int \sec x \tan ^{2} x d x{/tex}
​​​​​​by tan2x = sec2x − 1
= secxtanx −  {tex}\int\left(\sec ^{3} x-\sec x\right) d x{/tex}
Since {tex}\int \sec ^{3} x d x=I{/tex}
= secx tanx − I +  {tex}\int \sec x d x{/tex}
by adding I and {tex}\int \sec x d x=\ln |\sec x+\tan x|+C_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 I=\sec x \tan x+\ln |\sec x+\tan x|+C_{1}{/tex}
by dividing by 2,
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{1}{2} \sec x \tan x+\frac{1}{2} \ln |\sec x+\tan x|+\frac{C_{1}}{2}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\int \sec ^{3} d x=\frac{1}{2} \sec x \tan x+\frac{1}{2} \ln |\sec x+\tan x|{/tex}  + C

solution: {tex}\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{x}+x} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{x}(1+\sqrt{x})} d x{/tex} Let, 1 + {tex}\sqrt x{/tex} = t 0 + {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}} d x{/tex} = dt {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}} d x{/tex} = 2dt {tex}=2 \int \frac{1}{t} d t{/tex} = 2log t + C = 2log (1 + {tex}\sqrt x{/tex} ) + C

Let, 1 + {tex}\sqrt x{/tex} = t
0 + {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}} d x{/tex} = dt
{tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}} d x{/tex} = 2dt
{tex}=2 \int \frac{1}{t} d t{/tex}
= 2log t + C
= 2log (1 +{tex}\sqrt x{/tex}) + C

explanation: Let I = {tex}\int{/tex} sec 3 x dx, then we have {tex} I=\int\underset I\sec\;x\cdot\underset{II}\sec^{2\;}x\;dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = sec x { {tex}\int{/tex} sec 2 x dx} - {tex}\int{/tex} {( {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (sec x)( {tex}\int{/tex} sec 2 x dx)} dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = sec x tan x - {tex}\int{/tex} sec x tan x tan x dx =sec x tan x - J sec x tan 2 x dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = sec x tan x - I sec x (sec 2 x - 1) dx = sec x tan x - {tex}\int{/tex} sec 3 x dx + {tex}\int{/tex} sec x dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = sec x tan x - I + log| sec x + tan x + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2I = sec x tan x + log| sec x + tan x + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} sec tan x + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |sec x + tan x| + C Which is the required soution.
solution: Let I = {tex}\int{/tex} sec 3 x dx, then we have {tex} I=\int\underset I\sec\;x\cdot\underset{II}\sec^{2\;}x\;dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = sec x { {tex}\int{/tex} sec 2 x dx} - {tex}\int{/tex} {( {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (sec x)( {tex}\int{/tex} sec 2 x dx)} dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = sec x tan x - {tex}\int{/tex} sec x tan x tan x dx =sec x tan x - J sec x tan 2 x dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = sec x tan x - I sec x (sec 2 x - 1) dx = sec x tan x - {tex}\int{/tex} sec 3 x dx + {tex}\int{/tex} sec x dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = sec x tan x - I + log| sec x + tan x + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2I = sec x tan x + log| sec x + tan x + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} sec tan x + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |sec x + tan x| + C Which is the required soution.
solution_html:
Let I =  {tex}\int{/tex}sec3 x dx, then we have
{tex} I=\int\underset I\sec\;x\cdot\underset{II}\sec^{2\;}x\;dx{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = sec x {{tex}\int{/tex} sec2 x dx} - {tex}\int{/tex}{({tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (sec x)({tex}\int{/tex} sec2 x dx)} dx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = sec x tan x - {tex}\int{/tex} sec x tan x tan x dx =sec x tan x - J sec x tan2 x dx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = sec x tan x - I sec x (sec2 x - 1) dx = sec x tan x - {tex}\int{/tex} sec3 x dx + {tex}\int{/tex} sec x dx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = sec x tan x - I + log| sec x + tan x + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2I = sec x tan x + log| sec x + tan x + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} sec tan x + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |sec x + tan x| + C
Which is the required soution.

subject: Mathematics
explanation: {tex}\int_{ - 1}^{3/2} {\left| {x\sin \left( {\pi x} \right)} \right|dx} {/tex} {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left| {x\sin \pi x} \right| = \left\{ \begin{gathered} x\sin \pi x\,\,for\, - 1 \leqslant x \leqslant 1 \hfill \\ - x\sin \pi x\,\,for\,\,1 \leqslant x \leqslant \frac{3}{2} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} {tex}\int_{ - 1}^{3/2} {\left| {x.\sin \left( {\pi x} \right)} \right|} dx = \int_{ - 1}^1 {\mathop x\limits \mathop {\sin }\limits_{} \pi xdx + \int_{ 1}^{3/2} { - x\sin \pi xdx} }{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\frac{{ - x\cos \pi x}}{\pi } +\frac{{\sin \pi x}}{{{\pi ^2}}}} \right]_{ - 1}^1 - \left[ {\frac{{ - x\cos \pi x}}{\pi } + \frac{{\sin \pi x}}{{{\pi ^2}}}} \right]_{ 1}^{ 3/2}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{2}{\pi } - \left[ { - \frac{1}{{{\pi ^2}}} - \frac{1}{\pi }} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{3}{\pi } +\frac{1}{{{\pi ^2}}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\int_{ - 1}^{3/2} {\left| {x\sin \left( {\pi x} \right)} \right|dx} {/tex} {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left| {x\sin \pi x} \right| = \left\{ \begin{gathered} x\sin \pi x\,\,for\, - 1 \leqslant x \leqslant 1 \hfill \\ - x\sin \pi x\,\,for\,\,1 \leqslant x \leqslant \frac{3}{2} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} {tex}\int_{ - 1}^{3/2} {\left| {x.\sin \left( {\pi x} \right)} \right|} dx = \int_{ - 1}^1 {\mathop x\limits \mathop {\sin }\limits_{} \pi xdx + \int_{ 1}^{3/2} { - x\sin \pi xdx} }{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\frac{{ - x\cos \pi x}}{\pi } +\frac{{\sin \pi x}}{{{\pi ^2}}}} \right]_{ - 1}^1 - \left[ {\frac{{ - x\cos \pi x}}{\pi } + \frac{{\sin \pi x}}{{{\pi ^2}}}} \right]_{ 1}^{ 3/2}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{2}{\pi } - \left[ { - \frac{1}{{{\pi ^2}}} - \frac{1}{\pi }} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{3}{\pi } +\frac{1}{{{\pi ^2}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\int_{ - 1}^{3/2} {\left| {x\sin \left( {\pi x} \right)} \right|dx} {/tex}
{tex}f\left( x \right) = \left| {x\sin \pi x} \right| = \left\{ \begin{gathered} x\sin \pi x\,\,for\, - 1 \leqslant x \leqslant 1 \hfill \\ - x\sin \pi x\,\,for\,\,1 \leqslant x \leqslant \frac{3}{2} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
{tex}\int_{ - 1}^{3/2} {\left| {x.\sin \left( {\pi x} \right)} \right|} dx = \int_{ - 1}^1 {\mathop x\limits \mathop {\sin }\limits_{} \pi xdx + \int_{ 1}^{3/2} { - x\sin \pi xdx} }{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ {\frac{{ - x\cos \pi x}}{\pi } +\frac{{\sin \pi x}}{{{\pi ^2}}}} \right]_{ - 1}^1 - \left[ {\frac{{ - x\cos \pi x}}{\pi } + \frac{{\sin \pi x}}{{{\pi ^2}}}} \right]_{ 1}^{ 3/2}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{2}{\pi } - \left[ { - \frac{1}{{{\pi ^2}}} - \frac{1}{\pi }} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{3}{\pi } +\frac{1}{{{\pi ^2}}}{/tex}

solution: {tex}I=\int_0^1 {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{2x - 1}}{{1 + x - {x^2}}}} \right)dx} {/tex} {tex}I = \int_0^1 {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{x + x - 1}}{{1 - x\left( {x - 1} \right)}}} \right)dx} {/tex} {tex}I = \int_0^1 {\left[ {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( x \right) + {{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( {x - 1} \right)} \right]dx}{/tex} ...(1) {tex}I = \int_0^1 {\left[ {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( {1 - x} \right) + {{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( {1 - x - 1} \right)} \right]dx} {/tex} {tex}[\because {P_4}{/tex} {tex}]{/tex} {tex}I = \int_0^1 {\left[ {{{-\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( {x - 1} \right) - {{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( x \right)} \right]dx} {/tex} ...(2) [ {tex}\because tan^{-1}(-\theta)=-tan^{-1}\theta{/tex} ] (1) + (2) 2I = 0 I = 0

explanation: If x {tex}\in{/tex} [-1, 0] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = -x + x + 1 - x + 5 = 6 - x If x {tex}\in{/tex} [0, 5] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = x + x + 1 - x + 5 = x + 6 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int\limits_{-1}^5{/tex} (|x| + |x+1| + |x-5|) dx = {tex}\int\limits_{-1}^0{/tex} (6 - x)dx + {tex}\int\limits_{0}^5{/tex} (x + 6)dx = {tex}\left[\frac{(6-\text x)^2}{-2}\right]_{-1}^0+\left[\frac{(\text x+6)^2}{2}\right]_0^5{/tex} = {tex}\frac{13}{2}+\frac{85}{2}{/tex} = 49
solution: If x {tex}\in{/tex} [-1, 0] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = -x + x + 1 - x + 5 = 6 - x If x {tex}\in{/tex} [0, 5] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = x + x + 1 - x + 5 = x + 6 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int\limits_{-1}^5{/tex} (|x| + |x+1| + |x-5|) dx = {tex}\int\limits_{-1}^0{/tex} (6 - x)dx + {tex}\int\limits_{0}^5{/tex} (x + 6)dx = {tex}\left[\frac{(6-\text x)^2}{-2}\right]_{-1}^0+\left[\frac{(\text x+6)^2}{2}\right]_0^5{/tex} = {tex}\frac{13}{2}+\frac{85}{2}{/tex} = 49

If x {tex}\in{/tex} [0, 5] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = x + x + 1 - x + 5 = x + 6
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int\limits_{-1}^5{/tex}(|x| + |x+1| + |x-5|) dx = {tex}\int\limits_{-1}^0{/tex}(6 - x)dx + {tex}\int\limits_{0}^5{/tex}(x + 6)dx

explanation: I = {tex}\int\limits_0^\pi \frac{\text x \sin \text x}{1+\cos ^2 \text x} \text {dx}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int\limits_0^\pi \frac{(\pi-\text x) \sin (\pi-\text x)}{1+\cos ^2(\pi-\text x)} \text {dx}{/tex} = {tex}\int\limits_0^\pi \frac{(\pi-\text x) \sin \text x}{1+\cos ^2 \text x} \text {dx}{/tex} ...(ii) Adding (i) and (ii) 2I = {tex}\int\limits_0^\pi \frac{\pi \sin \text x}{1+\cos ^2 \text x} \text {dx}{/tex} Putting cos x = t gives I = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2} \int\limits_{-1}^1 \frac{d t}{1+t^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_{-1}^1=\frac{\pi^2}{4}{/tex}
solution: I = {tex}\int\limits_0^\pi \frac{\text x \sin \text x}{1+\cos ^2 \text x} \text {dx}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int\limits_0^\pi \frac{(\pi-\text x) \sin (\pi-\text x)}{1+\cos ^2(\pi-\text x)} \text {dx}{/tex} = {tex}\int\limits_0^\pi \frac{(\pi-\text x) \sin \text x}{1+\cos ^2 \text x} \text {dx}{/tex} ...(ii) Adding (i) and (ii) 2I = {tex}\int\limits_0^\pi \frac{\pi \sin \text x}{1+\cos ^2 \text x} \text {dx}{/tex} Putting cos x = t gives I = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2} \int\limits_{-1}^1 \frac{d t}{1+t^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_{-1}^1=\frac{\pi^2}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
I = {tex}\int\limits_0^\pi \frac{\text x \sin \text x}{1+\cos ^2 \text x} \text {dx}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int\limits_0^\pi \frac{(\pi-\text x) \sin (\pi-\text x)}{1+\cos ^2(\pi-\text x)} \text {dx}{/tex} = {tex}\int\limits_0^\pi \frac{(\pi-\text x) \sin \text x}{1+\cos ^2 \text x} \text {dx}{/tex} ...(ii)
Adding (i) and (ii)
2I = {tex}\int\limits_0^\pi \frac{\pi \sin \text x}{1+\cos ^2 \text x} \text {dx}{/tex}
Putting cos x = t gives I = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2} \int\limits_{-1}^1 \frac{d t}{1+t^2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_{-1}^1=\frac{\pi^2}{4}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let {tex}I=\int \frac{x^3+1}{x^3-x} d x=\int \frac{(x+1)\left(x^2-x+1\right)}{x\left(x^2-1\right)} d x{/tex} {tex} =\int \frac{\left(x^2-x+1\right)}{x(x-1)} d x=\int\left[1+\frac{1}{x(x-1)}\right] d x{/tex} Consider, {tex}\frac{1}{x(x-1)}=\frac{A}{x}+\frac{B}{x-1}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x(x-1)}=\frac{A(x-1)+B x}{x(x-1)} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 1=A(x-1)+B x {/tex} On solving, we get {tex}A=-1, B=1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x(x-1)}=\frac{-1}{x}+\frac{1}{x-1} {/tex} {tex} \therefore I=\int\left(1+\frac{1}{x-1}-\frac{1}{x}\right) d x=x+\log |x-1|-\log |x|+c {/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I=\int \frac{x^3+1}{x^3-x} d x=\int \frac{(x+1)\left(x^2-x+1\right)}{x\left(x^2-1\right)} d x{/tex} {tex} =\int \frac{\left(x^2-x+1\right)}{x(x-1)} d x=\int\left[1+\frac{1}{x(x-1)}\right] d x{/tex} Consider, {tex}\frac{1}{x(x-1)}=\frac{A}{x}+\frac{B}{x-1}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x(x-1)}=\frac{A(x-1)+B x}{x(x-1)} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 1=A(x-1)+B x {/tex} On solving, we get {tex}A=-1, B=1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x(x-1)}=\frac{-1}{x}+\frac{1}{x-1} {/tex} {tex} \therefore I=\int\left(1+\frac{1}{x-1}-\frac{1}{x}\right) d x=x+\log |x-1|-\log |x|+c {/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I=\int \frac{x^3+1}{x^3-x} d x=\int \frac{(x+1)\left(x^2-x+1\right)}{x\left(x^2-1\right)} d x{/tex}
{tex} =\int \frac{\left(x^2-x+1\right)}{x(x-1)} d x=\int\left[1+\frac{1}{x(x-1)}\right] d x{/tex}
Consider, {tex}\frac{1}{x(x-1)}=\frac{A}{x}+\frac{B}{x-1}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x(x-1)}=\frac{A(x-1)+B x}{x(x-1)} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 1=A(x-1)+B x {/tex}
On solving, we get {tex}A=-1, B=1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x(x-1)}=\frac{-1}{x}+\frac{1}{x-1} {/tex}
{tex} \therefore I=\int\left(1+\frac{1}{x-1}-\frac{1}{x}\right) d x=x+\log |x-1|-\log |x|+c {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: To solve the integral {tex}\int \frac{\tan ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x} d x{/tex} , we can first rewrite the expression in terms of simpler trigonometric functions. We know that: {tex} \tan x=\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}{/tex} Therefore, {tex} \tan ^3 x=\frac{\sin ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x}{/tex} Now, substitute this into the original integral: {tex} \int \frac{\tan ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x} d x=\int \frac{\sin ^3 x}{\cos ^6 x} d x{/tex} We will now attempt to express this in a more solvable form using a substitution. Let: {tex} u=\cos x \Rightarrow d u=-\sin x d x{/tex} Thus, {tex}\sin x d x=-d u{/tex} , and the integral becomes: {tex} \int \frac{\left(1-u^2\right) u^3}{u^6}(-d u){/tex} Simplifying the expression inside the integral: {tex} =\int \frac{-\left(1-u^2\right)}{u^3} d u{/tex} Now separate the terms: {tex} =-\int\left(\frac{1}{u^3}-\frac{1}{u}\right) d u{/tex} Now we can integrate each term separately: {tex} =-\left(\int u^{-3} d u-\int u^{-1} d u\right){/tex} Using the standard integral formulas: {tex} \int u^{-3} d u=\frac{u^{-2}}{-2}=-\frac{1}{2 u^2}, \int u^{-1} d u=\ln |u|{/tex} Thus, the result becomes: {tex} =-\left(-\frac{1}{2 u^2}-\ln |u|\right){/tex} Substitute back {tex}u=\cos x{/tex} : {tex} =\frac{1}{2 \cos ^2 x}+\ln |\cos x|+C{/tex} Finally, we have the solution to the integral: {tex} \int \frac{\tan ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x} d x=\frac{1}{2 \cos ^2 x}+\ln |\cos x|+C{/tex}
solution: To solve the integral {tex}\int \frac{\tan ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x} d x{/tex} , we can first rewrite the expression in terms of simpler trigonometric functions. We know that: {tex} \tan x=\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}{/tex} Therefore, {tex} \tan ^3 x=\frac{\sin ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x}{/tex} Now, substitute this into the original integral: {tex} \int \frac{\tan ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x} d x=\int \frac{\sin ^3 x}{\cos ^6 x} d x{/tex} We will now attempt to express this in a more solvable form using a substitution. Let: {tex} u=\cos x \Rightarrow d u=-\sin x d x{/tex} Thus, {tex}\sin x d x=-d u{/tex} , and the integral becomes: {tex} \int \frac{\left(1-u^2\right) u^3}{u^6}(-d u){/tex} Simplifying the expression inside the integral: {tex} =\int \frac{-\left(1-u^2\right)}{u^3} d u{/tex} Now separate the terms: {tex} =-\int\left(\frac{1}{u^3}-\frac{1}{u}\right) d u{/tex} Now we can integrate each term separately: {tex} =-\left(\int u^{-3} d u-\int u^{-1} d u\right){/tex} Using the standard integral formulas: {tex} \int u^{-3} d u=\frac{u^{-2}}{-2}=-\frac{1}{2 u^2}, \int u^{-1} d u=\ln |u|{/tex} Thus, the result becomes: {tex} =-\left(-\frac{1}{2 u^2}-\ln |u|\right){/tex} Substitute back {tex}u=\cos x{/tex} : {tex} =\frac{1}{2 \cos ^2 x}+\ln |\cos x|+C{/tex} Finally, we have the solution to the integral: {tex} \int \frac{\tan ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x} d x=\frac{1}{2 \cos ^2 x}+\ln |\cos x|+C{/tex}
solution_html:
To solve the integral {tex}\int \frac{\tan ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x} d x{/tex}, we can first rewrite the expression in terms of simpler trigonometric functions.
We know that:
{tex} \tan x=\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}{/tex}
Therefore,
{tex} \tan ^3 x=\frac{\sin ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x}{/tex}
Now, substitute this into the original integral:
{tex} \int \frac{\tan ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x} d x=\int \frac{\sin ^3 x}{\cos ^6 x} d x{/tex}
We will now attempt to express this in a more solvable form using a substitution. Let:
{tex} u=\cos x \Rightarrow d u=-\sin x d x{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\sin x d x=-d u{/tex}, and the integral becomes:
{tex} \int \frac{\left(1-u^2\right) u^3}{u^6}(-d u){/tex}
Simplifying the expression inside the integral:
{tex} =\int \frac{-\left(1-u^2\right)}{u^3} d u{/tex}
Now separate the terms:
{tex} =-\int\left(\frac{1}{u^3}-\frac{1}{u}\right) d u{/tex}
Now we can integrate each term separately:
{tex} =-\left(\int u^{-3} d u-\int u^{-1} d u\right){/tex}
Using the standard integral formulas:
{tex} \int u^{-3} d u=\frac{u^{-2}}{-2}=-\frac{1}{2 u^2}, \int u^{-1} d u=\ln |u|{/tex}
Thus, the result becomes:
{tex} =-\left(-\frac{1}{2 u^2}-\ln |u|\right){/tex}
Substitute back {tex}u=\cos x{/tex} :
{tex} =\frac{1}{2 \cos ^2 x}+\ln |\cos x|+C{/tex}
Finally, we have the solution to the integral:
{tex} \int \frac{\tan ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x} d x=\frac{1}{2 \cos ^2 x}+\ln |\cos x|+C{/tex}

solution: Consider {tex}I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \cos ^2 x d x{/tex} ...(1) {tex} I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \cos ^2\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right) d x\left(\int_0^a f(x) d x=\int_0^a f(a-x) d x\right) {/tex} {tex} I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \sin ^2 x d x{/tex} ...(2) Adding 1 and 2, we get, {tex} 2 {I}=\int_0^{\pi / 2} {dx} {/tex} {tex} 2 {I}=\frac{\pi}{2}-0 {/tex} {tex} {I}=\frac{\pi}{4} {/tex}
solution_html:
Consider {tex}I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \cos ^2 x d x{/tex} ...(1)
{tex} I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \cos ^2\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right) d x\left(\int_0^a f(x) d x=\int_0^a f(a-x) d x\right) {/tex}
{tex} I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \sin ^2 x d x{/tex} ...(2)
Adding 1 and 2, we get,
{tex} 2 {I}=\int_0^{\pi / 2} {dx} {/tex}
{tex} 2 {I}=\frac{\pi}{2}-0 {/tex}
{tex} {I}=\frac{\pi}{4} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate: {tex}\int \frac{\cos x}{(1+\sin x)(2+\sin x)} d x{/tex}.
question_text: Evaluate: {tex}\int \frac{\cos x}{(1+\sin x)(2+\sin x)} d x{/tex} .
explanation: Let, {tex}I=\int \frac{\cos x}{(1+\sin x)(2+\sin x)} d x{/tex} Put, t = sin x dt = cos x dx {tex}I=\int \frac{d t}{(1+t)(2+t)}{/tex} Using partial fractions, {tex}\frac{1}{(1+t)(2+t)}=\frac{A}{1+t}+\frac{B}{2+t}{/tex} .... (1) A(2 + t) + B(1 + t) = 1 Now put t + 1 = 0 Therefore, t = -1 A(2 - 1) + B(0) = 1 A = 1 Now put t + 2 = 0 Therefore, t = -2 A(0) + B(-2 + 1) = 1 B = -1 Now From equation (1) we get {tex}\frac{1}{(1+t)(2+t)}=\frac{1}{1+t}+\frac{-1}{2+t}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{1}{(1+t)(2+t)} d t=\int \frac{1}{1+t} d t-\int \frac{1}{2+t} d t{/tex} {tex}=\log |1+t|-\log |t+2|+c{/tex} {tex}=\log \left|\frac{t+1}{t+2}\right|+c{/tex} So, {tex}I=\int \frac{\cos x}{(1+\sin x)(2+\sin x)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\log \left|\frac{\sin x+1}{\sin x+2}\right|+c{/tex}
solution: Let, {tex}I=\int \frac{\cos x}{(1+\sin x)(2+\sin x)} d x{/tex} Put, t = sin x dt = cos x dx {tex}I=\int \frac{d t}{(1+t)(2+t)}{/tex} Using partial fractions, {tex}\frac{1}{(1+t)(2+t)}=\frac{A}{1+t}+\frac{B}{2+t}{/tex} .... (1) A(2 + t) + B(1 + t) = 1 Now put t + 1 = 0 Therefore, t = -1 A(2 - 1) + B(0) = 1 A = 1 Now put t + 2 = 0 Therefore, t = -2 A(0) + B(-2 + 1) = 1 B = -1 Now From equation (1) we get {tex}\frac{1}{(1+t)(2+t)}=\frac{1}{1+t}+\frac{-1}{2+t}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{1}{(1+t)(2+t)} d t=\int \frac{1}{1+t} d t-\int \frac{1}{2+t} d t{/tex} {tex}=\log |1+t|-\log |t+2|+c{/tex} {tex}=\log \left|\frac{t+1}{t+2}\right|+c{/tex} So, {tex}I=\int \frac{\cos x}{(1+\sin x)(2+\sin x)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\log \left|\frac{\sin x+1}{\sin x+2}\right|+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let, {tex}I=\int \frac{\cos x}{(1+\sin x)(2+\sin x)} d x{/tex}
Put, t = sin x
dt = cos x dx
{tex}I=\int \frac{d t}{(1+t)(2+t)}{/tex}
Using partial fractions,
 {tex}\frac{1}{(1+t)(2+t)}=\frac{A}{1+t}+\frac{B}{2+t}{/tex} .... (1)
A(2 + t) + B(1 + t) = 1
Now put t + 1 = 0
Therefore, t = -1
A(2 - 1) + B(0) = 1
A = 1
Now put t + 2 = 0
Therefore, t = -2
A(0) + B(-2 + 1) = 1
B = -1
Now From equation (1) we get
{tex}\frac{1}{(1+t)(2+t)}=\frac{1}{1+t}+\frac{-1}{2+t}{/tex}
{tex}\int \frac{1}{(1+t)(2+t)} d t=\int \frac{1}{1+t} d t-\int \frac{1}{2+t} d t{/tex}
{tex}=\log |1+t|-\log |t+2|+c{/tex}
{tex}=\log \left|\frac{t+1}{t+2}\right|+c{/tex}
So,
{tex}I=\int \frac{\cos x}{(1+\sin x)(2+\sin x)} d x{/tex}{tex}=\log \left|\frac{\sin x+1}{\sin x+2}\right|+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let I = {tex}\int{/tex} sin x sin 2x sin 3x. Then, {tex}I=\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} (2 sin 2x sin x) sin 3x dx {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} cos x - cos 3x) sin 3x dx {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{1}{4} \int{/tex} (2 sin 3x cos x - 2 sin 3x cos 3x) dx {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{1}{4} \int{/tex} (sin 4x + sin 2x - sin 6x) dx = {tex}\frac{1}{4}\left\{-\frac{\cos 4 x}{4}-\frac{\cos 2 x}{2}+\frac{\cos 6 x}{6}\right\}+C{/tex}
solution: Let I = {tex}\int{/tex} sin x sin 2x sin 3x. Then, {tex}I=\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} (2 sin 2x sin x) sin 3x dx {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} cos x - cos 3x) sin 3x dx {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{1}{4} \int{/tex} (2 sin 3x cos x - 2 sin 3x cos 3x) dx {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{1}{4} \int{/tex} (sin 4x + sin 2x - sin 6x) dx = {tex}\frac{1}{4}\left\{-\frac{\cos 4 x}{4}-\frac{\cos 2 x}{2}+\frac{\cos 6 x}{6}\right\}+C{/tex}

{tex}I=\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} (2 sin 2x sin x) sin 3x dx
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} cos x - cos 3x) sin 3x dx
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{1}{4} \int{/tex} (2 sin 3x cos x - 2 sin 3x cos 3x) dx

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let I = {tex}\int \sqrt{3-2 x-x^{2}} \;d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \sqrt{-\left(x^{2}+2 x-3\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \sqrt{-\left(x^{2}+1+2 x-3-1\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \sqrt{-\left[(x+1)^{2}-4\right]} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \sqrt{4-(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \sqrt{2^{2}-(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}\left[\because\int\sqrt{a^2-x^2}dx=\frac x2\sqrt{a^2-x^2}+\frac{a^2}2\sin^{-1}\left(\frac xa\right)+c\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}(x+1) \sqrt{4-(x+1)^{2}} +\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+1}{2}\right)+c{/tex} {tex}\therefore \ \mathrm{I}=\int \sqrt{2^{2}-(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}(x+1) \sqrt{3-2 x-x^{2}}+2 \sin ^{-1} \frac{(x+1)}{2}+c{/tex}
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \sqrt{3-2 x-x^{2}} \;d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \sqrt{-\left(x^{2}+2 x-3\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \sqrt{-\left(x^{2}+1+2 x-3-1\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \sqrt{-\left[(x+1)^{2}-4\right]} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \sqrt{4-(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \sqrt{2^{2}-(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}\left[\because\int\sqrt{a^2-x^2}dx=\frac x2\sqrt{a^2-x^2}+\frac{a^2}2\sin^{-1}\left(\frac xa\right)+c\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}(x+1) \sqrt{4-(x+1)^{2}} +\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+1}{2}\right)+c{/tex} {tex}\therefore \ \mathrm{I}=\int \sqrt{2^{2}-(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}(x+1) \sqrt{3-2 x-x^{2}}+2 \sin ^{-1} \frac{(x+1)}{2}+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \sqrt{3-2 x-x^{2}} \;d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int \sqrt{-\left(x^{2}+2 x-3\right)} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int \sqrt{-\left(x^{2}+1+2 x-3-1\right)} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int \sqrt{-\left[(x+1)^{2}-4\right]} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int \sqrt{4-(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int \sqrt{2^{2}-(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}\left[\because\int\sqrt{a^2-x^2}dx=\frac x2\sqrt{a^2-x^2}+\frac{a^2}2\sin^{-1}\left(\frac xa\right)+c\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}(x+1) \sqrt{4-(x+1)^{2}} +\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+1}{2}\right)+c{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ \mathrm{I}=\int \sqrt{2^{2}-(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}(x+1) \sqrt{3-2 x-x^{2}}+2 \sin ^{-1} \frac{(x+1)}{2}+c{/tex}

explanation: {tex}\int \frac{\sin (x-a)}{\sin (x+a)} d x=\int \frac{\sin (x+a-2 a)}{\sin (x+a)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int\left[\frac{\sin (x+a) \cos 2 a}{\sin (x+a)}-\frac{\cos (x+a) \sin 2 a}{\sin (x+a)}\right] d x{/tex} [applying formula of sin(A - B)] {tex}=\cos 2 a \int d x-\sin 2 a \int \cot (x+a) d x{/tex} {tex}=x \cdot \cos 2 a-\sin 2 a \cdot \log |\sin (x+a)|+C{/tex}

explanation: {tex}\int \frac{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x}{\sin ^{2} x \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int(\sec x \cdot \tan x+ cosec \;x \cdot \cot x) d x{/tex} = sec x - cosec x + C
solution: {tex}\int \frac{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x}{\sin ^{2} x \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int(\sec x \cdot \tan x+ cosec \;x \cdot \cot x) d x{/tex} = sec x - cosec x + C

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x-3}{(x-1)^{3}} e^{x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{x-2-1}{(x-1)^{3}} e^{x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{x-1-2}{(x-1)^{3}} e^{x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int\left[\frac{x-1}{(x-1)^{3}}-\frac{2}{(x-1)^{3}}\right] e^{x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int\left[\frac{1}{(x-1)^{2}}-\frac{2}{(x-1)^{3}}\right] e^{x} d x{/tex} ...(i) Here, {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left[\frac{1}{(x-1)^{2}}\right]=\frac{d}{d x}(x-1)^{-2}=-2(x-1)^{-3}{/tex} We know that, {tex}\int\left[f(x)+f^{\prime}(x)\right] \mathrm{e}^{x} d x=e^{x} f(x)+C{/tex} ....(i) Then for equation (i), we have {tex}\int\left[\frac{1}{(x-1)^{2}}-\frac{2}{(x-1)^{3}}\right] e^{x} d x=\frac{e^{x}}{(x-1)^{2}}+C{/tex}
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x-3}{(x-1)^{3}} e^{x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{x-2-1}{(x-1)^{3}} e^{x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{x-1-2}{(x-1)^{3}} e^{x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int\left[\frac{x-1}{(x-1)^{3}}-\frac{2}{(x-1)^{3}}\right] e^{x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int\left[\frac{1}{(x-1)^{2}}-\frac{2}{(x-1)^{3}}\right] e^{x} d x{/tex} ...(i) Here, {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left[\frac{1}{(x-1)^{2}}\right]=\frac{d}{d x}(x-1)^{-2}=-2(x-1)^{-3}{/tex} We know that, {tex}\int\left[f(x)+f^{\prime}(x)\right] \mathrm{e}^{x} d x=e^{x} f(x)+C{/tex} ....(i) Then for equation (i), we have {tex}\int\left[\frac{1}{(x-1)^{2}}-\frac{2}{(x-1)^{3}}\right] e^{x} d x=\frac{e^{x}}{(x-1)^{2}}+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x-3}{(x-1)^{3}} e^{x} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int \frac{x-2-1}{(x-1)^{3}} e^{x} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int \frac{x-1-2}{(x-1)^{3}} e^{x} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int\left[\frac{x-1}{(x-1)^{3}}-\frac{2}{(x-1)^{3}}\right] e^{x} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int\left[\frac{1}{(x-1)^{2}}-\frac{2}{(x-1)^{3}}\right] e^{x} d x{/tex} ...(i)
Here, {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left[\frac{1}{(x-1)^{2}}\right]=\frac{d}{d x}(x-1)^{-2}=-2(x-1)^{-3}{/tex}
We know that, {tex}\int\left[f(x)+f^{\prime}(x)\right] \mathrm{e}^{x} d x=e^{x} f(x)+C{/tex} ....(i)
Then for equation (i), we have
{tex}\int\left[\frac{1}{(x-1)^{2}}-\frac{2}{(x-1)^{3}}\right] e^{x} d x=\frac{e^{x}}{(x-1)^{2}}+C{/tex}

question_html: Find: {tex}\int \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{1+\sin 2 x}} d x{/tex}, 0 < x < {tex}\frac {\pi} {2}{/tex}
question_text: Find: {tex}\int \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{1+\sin 2 x}} d x{/tex} , 0 < x < {tex}\frac {\pi} {2}{/tex}
explanation: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{1+\sin 2 x}} d x, 0<x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}I=\int \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{\sin ^{2} x+\cos ^{2} x+2 \sin x \cdot \cos x}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{(\sin x+\cos x)^{2}}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sin x+\cos x} d x{/tex} Let sinx + cosx = t, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (cos x - sin x) dx = dt {tex}I=\int \frac{-1}{t} d t{/tex} = -In t + C = {tex}\ln \left(\frac{1}{t}\right)+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\ln \left(\frac{1}{\sin x+\cos x}\right)+C{/tex}
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{1+\sin 2 x}} d x, 0<x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}I=\int \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{\sin ^{2} x+\cos ^{2} x+2 \sin x \cdot \cos x}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{(\sin x+\cos x)^{2}}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sin x+\cos x} d x{/tex} Let sinx + cosx = t, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (cos x - sin x) dx = dt {tex}I=\int \frac{-1}{t} d t{/tex} = -In t + C = {tex}\ln \left(\frac{1}{t}\right)+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\ln \left(\frac{1}{\sin x+\cos x}\right)+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{1+\sin 2 x}} d x, 0<x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}I=\int \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{\sin ^{2} x+\cos ^{2} x+2 \sin x \cdot \cos x}} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{(\sin x+\cos x)^{2}}} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sin x+\cos x} d x{/tex}
Let sinx + cosx = t,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (cos x - sin x) dx = dt
{tex}I=\int \frac{-1}{t} d t{/tex}
= -In t + C
= {tex}\ln \left(\frac{1}{t}\right)+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\ln \left(\frac{1}{\sin x+\cos x}\right)+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find: {tex}\int \frac{\sin 2 x}{\left(\sin ^{2} x+1\right)\left(\sin ^{2} x+3\right)} d x{/tex}
question_text: Find: {tex}\int \frac{\sin 2 x}{\left(\sin ^{2} x+1\right)\left(\sin ^{2} x+3\right)} d x{/tex}
explanation: {tex}I=\int \frac{\sin 2 x}{\left(\sin ^{2} x+1\right)\left(\sin ^{2} x+3\right)} d x{/tex} Put sin 2 x = t On differentiation we get 2sinx cosx = dt {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin 2x dx = dt {tex}\therefore I=\int \frac{d t}{(t+1)(t+3)}{/tex} {tex}=\int\left(\frac{1 / 2}{t+1}+\frac{-1 / 2}{t+3}\right) d t{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \log |\mathrm{t}+1|-\frac{1}{2} \log |\mathrm{t}+3|+\mathrm{c}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\sin ^{2} x+1\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\sin ^{2}x+3\right)+c{/tex}
solution: {tex}I=\int \frac{\sin 2 x}{\left(\sin ^{2} x+1\right)\left(\sin ^{2} x+3\right)} d x{/tex} Put sin 2 x = t On differentiation we get 2sinx cosx = dt {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin 2x dx = dt {tex}\therefore I=\int \frac{d t}{(t+1)(t+3)}{/tex} {tex}=\int\left(\frac{1 / 2}{t+1}+\frac{-1 / 2}{t+3}\right) d t{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \log |\mathrm{t}+1|-\frac{1}{2} \log |\mathrm{t}+3|+\mathrm{c}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\sin ^{2} x+1\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\sin ^{2}x+3\right)+c{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}I=\int \frac{\sin 2 x}{\left(\sin ^{2} x+1\right)\left(\sin ^{2} x+3\right)} d x{/tex}
Put sin2x = t 
On differentiation we get
2sinx cosx = dt {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin 2x dx = dt
{tex}\therefore I=\int \frac{d t}{(t+1)(t+3)}{/tex}{tex}=\int\left(\frac{1 / 2}{t+1}+\frac{-1 / 2}{t+3}\right) d t{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \log |\mathrm{t}+1|-\frac{1}{2} \log |\mathrm{t}+3|+\mathrm{c}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\sin ^{2} x+1\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\sin ^{2}x+3\right)+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let {tex}I = \int {\frac{{{{\sec }^2}x}}{{\sqrt {{{\tan }^2}x + 4} }}dx} {/tex} ...(i) Putting tan x = t {tex} \Rightarrow {\sec ^2}x = \frac{{dt}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\sec ^2}xdx = dt{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} From eq. (i), {tex}I = \int {\frac{{dt}}{{\sqrt {{t^2} + 4} }}} {/tex} {tex}= \int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt {{t^2} + {{\left( 2 \right)}^2}} }}dt} {/tex} {tex} = \log \left| {t + \sqrt {{t^2} + {{\left( 2 \right)}^2}} } \right| + c{/tex} {tex}= \log \left| {\tan x + \sqrt {{{\tan }^2}x + 4} } \right| + c{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I = \int {\frac{{{{\sec }^2}x}}{{\sqrt {{{\tan }^2}x + 4} }}dx} {/tex} ...(i) Putting tan x = t {tex} \Rightarrow {\sec ^2}x = \frac{{dt}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\sec ^2}xdx = dt{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} From eq. (i), {tex}I = \int {\frac{{dt}}{{\sqrt {{t^2} + 4} }}} {/tex} {tex}= \int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt {{t^2} + {{\left( 2 \right)}^2}} }}dt} {/tex} {tex} = \log \left| {t + \sqrt {{t^2} + {{\left( 2 \right)}^2}} } \right| + c{/tex} {tex}= \log \left| {\tan x + \sqrt {{{\tan }^2}x + 4} } \right| + c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I = \int {\frac{{{{\sec }^2}x}}{{\sqrt {{{\tan }^2}x + 4} }}dx} {/tex} ...(i)
Putting tan x = t
{tex} \Rightarrow {\sec ^2}x = \frac{{dt}}{{dx}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {\sec ^2}xdx = dt{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} From eq. (i),
{tex}I = \int {\frac{{dt}}{{\sqrt {{t^2} + 4} }}} {/tex}
{tex}= \int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt {{t^2} + {{\left( 2 \right)}^2}} }}dt} {/tex}
{tex} = \log \left| {t + \sqrt {{t^2} + {{\left( 2 \right)}^2}} } \right| + c{/tex}
{tex}= \log \left| {\tan x + \sqrt {{{\tan }^2}x + 4} } \right| + c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: {tex}\int(\log x)^{2} \cdot \operatorname{1dx}=(\log x)^{2} \cdot x-\int 2 \cdot \log x \cdot \frac{1}{x} \cdot x d x{/tex} {tex}=(\log x)^{2} \cdot x-2\int \log x d x{/tex} {tex}=x \cdot(\log x)^{2}-\left\{\log x \cdot 2 x-\int \frac{1}{x} \cdot 2 x d x\right\}{/tex} {tex}=x(\log x)^{2}-2 x \log x+2 x+C{/tex}
solution: {tex}\int(\log x)^{2} \cdot \operatorname{1dx}=(\log x)^{2} \cdot x-\int 2 \cdot \log x \cdot \frac{1}{x} \cdot x d x{/tex} {tex}=(\log x)^{2} \cdot x-2\int \log x d x{/tex} {tex}=x \cdot(\log x)^{2}-\left\{\log x \cdot 2 x-\int \frac{1}{x} \cdot 2 x d x\right\}{/tex} {tex}=x(\log x)^{2}-2 x \log x+2 x+C{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\int(\log x)^{2} \cdot \operatorname{1dx}=(\log x)^{2} \cdot x-\int 2 \cdot \log x \cdot \frac{1}{x} \cdot x d x{/tex}
{tex}=(\log x)^{2} \cdot x-2\int \log x d x{/tex}
{tex}=x \cdot(\log x)^{2}-\left\{\log x \cdot 2 x-\int \frac{1}{x} \cdot 2 x d x\right\}{/tex}
{tex}=x(\log x)^{2}-2 x \log x+2 x+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let I = {tex}\int\underset Ix\;\underset{II}\tan^{-1}\;x\;dx{/tex} . Then, we have I = (tan -1 x) {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}-\int \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} \times \frac{x^{2}}{2} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{x^{2}+1-1}{x^{2}+1} d x=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{2} \int 1-\frac{1}{x^{2}+1} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{2}\left(x-\tan ^{-1} x\right)+C{/tex}
solution: Let I = {tex}\int\underset Ix\;\underset{II}\tan^{-1}\;x\;dx{/tex} . Then, we have I = (tan -1 x) {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}-\int \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} \times \frac{x^{2}}{2} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{x^{2}+1-1}{x^{2}+1} d x=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{2} \int 1-\frac{1}{x^{2}+1} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{2}\left(x-\tan ^{-1} x\right)+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int\underset Ix\;\underset{II}\tan^{-1}\;x\;dx{/tex}. Then, we have
I = (tan-1 x) {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}-\int \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} \times \frac{x^{2}}{2} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{x^{2}+1-1}{x^{2}+1} d x=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{2} \int 1-\frac{1}{x^{2}+1} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{2}\left(x-\tan ^{-1} x\right)+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let {tex}I=\int \sin ^{-1}(2 x) \cdot 1 d x{/tex} Let sin -1 2x = t 2x = sin t 2dx = cos t dt {tex}\therefore~I=2 \int t \cos t d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=2\left[t \sin t-\int 1 \cdot \sin t d t\right]{/tex} ...[Using by parts] {tex}\Rightarrow ~ I=2[t \sin t+\cos t]+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}I = 2[2x sin^{-1}2x + cos(sin^{-1}2x)] + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow ~ \mathrm{I}=2\left[2 x \sin ^{-1} 2 x+\sqrt{1-4 x^{2}}\right]+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I=\int \sin ^{-1}(2 x) \cdot 1 d x{/tex} Let sin -1 2x = t 2x = sin t 2dx = cos t dt {tex}\therefore~I=2 \int t \cos t d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=2\left[t \sin t-\int 1 \cdot \sin t d t\right]{/tex} ...[Using by parts] {tex}\Rightarrow ~ I=2[t \sin t+\cos t]+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}I = 2[2x sin^{-1}2x + cos(sin^{-1}2x)] + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow ~ \mathrm{I}=2\left[2 x \sin ^{-1} 2 x+\sqrt{1-4 x^{2}}\right]+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I=\int \sin ^{-1}(2 x) \cdot 1 d x{/tex}
Let sin-12x = t
2x = sin t
2dx = cos t dt
{tex}\therefore~I=2 \int t \cos t d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=2\left[t \sin t-\int 1 \cdot \sin t d t\right]{/tex}    ...[Using by parts]
{tex}\Rightarrow ~ I=2[t \sin t+\cos t]+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}I = 2[2x sin^{-1}2x + cos(sin^{-1}2x)] + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow ~ \mathrm{I}=2\left[2 x \sin ^{-1} 2 x+\sqrt{1-4 x^{2}}\right]+\mathrm{C}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let {tex}I=\int \frac{\tan ^{2} x \sec ^{2} x}{1-\tan ^{6} x} d x{/tex} {tex}I=\int \frac{\tan ^{2} x \sec ^{2} x}{1-\left(\tan ^{3} x\right)^{2}} d x{/tex} Put tan 3 x = t 3tan 2 x sec 2 xdx=dt {tex}\tan ^{2} x \cdot \sec ^{2} d x=\frac{d t}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{d t}{1-t^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{2 \times 1} \log \left|\frac{1+t}{1-t}\right|+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{6} \log \left|\frac{1+t}{1-t}\right|+C{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{6} \log \left|\frac{1+\tan ^{3} x}{1-\tan ^{3} x}\right|+C{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I=\int \frac{\tan ^{2} x \sec ^{2} x}{1-\tan ^{6} x} d x{/tex} {tex}I=\int \frac{\tan ^{2} x \sec ^{2} x}{1-\left(\tan ^{3} x\right)^{2}} d x{/tex} Put tan 3 x = t 3tan 2 x sec 2 xdx=dt {tex}\tan ^{2} x \cdot \sec ^{2} d x=\frac{d t}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{d t}{1-t^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{2 \times 1} \log \left|\frac{1+t}{1-t}\right|+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{6} \log \left|\frac{1+t}{1-t}\right|+C{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{6} \log \left|\frac{1+\tan ^{3} x}{1-\tan ^{3} x}\right|+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I=\int \frac{\tan ^{2} x \sec ^{2} x}{1-\tan ^{6} x} d x{/tex}
{tex}I=\int \frac{\tan ^{2} x \sec ^{2} x}{1-\left(\tan ^{3} x\right)^{2}} d x{/tex}
Put tan3x = t
3tan2x sec2xdx=dt
{tex}\tan ^{2} x \cdot \sec ^{2} d x=\frac{d t}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{d t}{1-t^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{2 \times 1} \log \left|\frac{1+t}{1-t}\right|+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{6} \log \left|\frac{1+t}{1-t}\right|+C{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{6} \log \left|\frac{1+\tan ^{3} x}{1-\tan ^{3} x}\right|+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let {tex}I=\int \frac{3 x+5}{x^{2}+3 x-18} d x{/tex} Here, (3x + 5) = A(2x + 3) + B Comparing coefficients of x 3 = 2A {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{3}{2}=A{/tex} Comparing contant terms, 5 = 3A + B {tex}5=\frac{9}{2}+B{/tex} {tex}B=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I=\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{2 x+3}{x^{2}+3 x-18} d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+3 x-18}{/tex} Let {tex}I_{1}=\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{2 x+3}{x^{2}+3 x-18} d x{/tex} Put x 2 + 3x - 18 = t (2x + 3) dx = dt {tex}I_{1}=\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{d t}{t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}_{1}=\frac{3}{2} \log |t|+\mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}_{1}=\frac{3}{2} \log \left|x^{2}+3 x-18\right|+\mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex} Now, {tex}I_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+3 x-18}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+2 x \cdot \frac{3}{2}+\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}-18}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}_{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{d x}{\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{2 \times 9} \log \left|\frac{x+\frac{3}{2}-\frac{9}{2}}{x+\frac{3}{2}+\frac{9}{2}}\right|+C_{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}_{2}=\frac{1}{18} \log \left|\frac{2 x-6}{2 x+12}\right|+C_{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = I 1 + I 2 {tex}I=\frac{3}{2} \log \left|x^{2}+3 x-18\right|+\frac{1}{18} \log \left|\frac{x-3}{x+6}\right|+C{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I=\int \frac{3 x+5}{x^{2}+3 x-18} d x{/tex} Here, (3x + 5) = A(2x + 3) + B Comparing coefficients of x 3 = 2A {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{3}{2}=A{/tex} Comparing contant terms, 5 = 3A + B {tex}5=\frac{9}{2}+B{/tex} {tex}B=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I=\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{2 x+3}{x^{2}+3 x-18} d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+3 x-18}{/tex} Let {tex}I_{1}=\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{2 x+3}{x^{2}+3 x-18} d x{/tex} Put x 2 + 3x - 18 = t (2x + 3) dx = dt {tex}I_{1}=\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{d t}{t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}_{1}=\frac{3}{2} \log |t|+\mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}_{1}=\frac{3}{2} \log \left|x^{2}+3 x-18\right|+\mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex} Now, {tex}I_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+3 x-18}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+2 x \cdot \frac{3}{2}+\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}-18}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}_{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{d x}{\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{2 \times 9} \log \left|\frac{x+\frac{3}{2}-\frac{9}{2}}{x+\frac{3}{2}+\frac{9}{2}}\right|+C_{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}_{2}=\frac{1}{18} \log \left|\frac{2 x-6}{2 x+12}\right|+C_{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = I 1 + I 2 {tex}I=\frac{3}{2} \log \left|x^{2}+3 x-18\right|+\frac{1}{18} \log \left|\frac{x-3}{x+6}\right|+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I=\int \frac{3 x+5}{x^{2}+3 x-18} d x{/tex}
Here, (3x + 5) = A(2x + 3) + B
Comparing coefficients of x
3 = 2A
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{3}{2}=A{/tex}
Comparing contant terms,
5 = 3A + B
{tex}5=\frac{9}{2}+B{/tex}
{tex}B=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I=\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{2 x+3}{x^{2}+3 x-18} d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+3 x-18}{/tex}
Let {tex}I_{1}=\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{2 x+3}{x^{2}+3 x-18} d x{/tex}
Put x2 + 3x - 18 = t
(2x + 3) dx = dt
{tex}I_{1}=\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{d t}{t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}_{1}=\frac{3}{2} \log |t|+\mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}_{1}=\frac{3}{2} \log \left|x^{2}+3 x-18\right|+\mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex}
Now, {tex}I_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+3 x-18}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+2 x \cdot \frac{3}{2}+\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}-18}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}_{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{d x}{\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)^{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{2 \times 9} \log \left|\frac{x+\frac{3}{2}-\frac{9}{2}}{x+\frac{3}{2}+\frac{9}{2}}\right|+C_{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}_{2}=\frac{1}{18} \log \left|\frac{2 x-6}{2 x+12}\right|+C_{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I = I1 + I2
{tex}I=\frac{3}{2} \log \left|x^{2}+3 x-18\right|+\frac{1}{18} \log \left|\frac{x-3}{x+6}\right|+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Prove that {tex}\int\limits_{0}^{\mathfrak{a}}{/tex} f(x) dx = {tex}\int\limits_{0}^{\mathfrak{a}}{/tex} f(a - x) dx. hence evaluate {tex}\int\limits_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex}.
question_text: Prove that {tex}\int\limits_{0}^{\mathfrak{a}}{/tex} f(x) dx = {tex}\int\limits_{0}^{\mathfrak{a}}{/tex} f(a - x) dx. hence evaluate {tex}\int\limits_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} .
explanation: To prove: {tex}\int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x{/tex} Proof: Let x = a - t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = -dt Also, x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = a and x = a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = 0 {tex}\therefore \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=-\int_{a}^{0} f(a-t) d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-t) d t{/tex} ... {tex}\left[\int_{b}^{a} f(x) d x=-\int_{b}^{a} f(x) d x\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x{/tex} ... {tex}\left[\because \int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(t) d t\right]{/tex} Hence proved. Now, Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \sin x d x}{1+\cos ^{2} x}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \sin (\pi-x)}{1+\cos ^{2}(\pi-x)} d x{/tex} [Applying above propertey] {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \sin d x}{1+\cos ^{2} x}{/tex} ...(ii) Adding equation (i) and (ii), we get {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} Put cos x = t -sin x dx = dt when x = 0, t = 1 and x = {tex}\pi{/tex} , t = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=-\pi \int_{1}^{-1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=-\pi\left[\tan ^{-1}(t)\right]_{1}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=-\pi\left[\tan ^{-1}(-1)-\tan ^{-1}(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=-\pi\left[-\tan ^{-1}(1)-\tan ^{-1}(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \tan ^{-1}(-x)=\tan ^{-1} x\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=-\pi\left[-2 \tan ^{-1}(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=-\pi\left(-\frac{2 \pi}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=\frac{2 \pi^{2}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}=\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex}
solution: To prove: {tex}\int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x{/tex} Proof: Let x = a - t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = -dt Also, x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = a and x = a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = 0 {tex}\therefore \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=-\int_{a}^{0} f(a-t) d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-t) d t{/tex} ... {tex}\left[\int_{b}^{a} f(x) d x=-\int_{b}^{a} f(x) d x\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x{/tex} ... {tex}\left[\because \int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(t) d t\right]{/tex} Hence proved. Now, Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \sin x d x}{1+\cos ^{2} x}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \sin (\pi-x)}{1+\cos ^{2}(\pi-x)} d x{/tex} [Applying above propertey] {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \sin d x}{1+\cos ^{2} x}{/tex} ...(ii) Adding equation (i) and (ii), we get {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} Put cos x = t -sin x dx = dt when x = 0, t = 1 and x = {tex}\pi{/tex} , t = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=-\pi \int_{1}^{-1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=-\pi\left[\tan ^{-1}(t)\right]_{1}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=-\pi\left[\tan ^{-1}(-1)-\tan ^{-1}(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=-\pi\left[-\tan ^{-1}(1)-\tan ^{-1}(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \tan ^{-1}(-x)=\tan ^{-1} x\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=-\pi\left[-2 \tan ^{-1}(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=-\pi\left(-\frac{2 \pi}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=\frac{2 \pi^{2}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}=\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
To prove: {tex}\int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x{/tex}
Proof: Let x = a - t
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = -dt
Also, x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = a
and x = a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = 0
{tex}\therefore \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=-\int_{a}^{0} f(a-t) d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-t) d t{/tex}     ...{tex}\left[\int_{b}^{a} f(x) d x=-\int_{b}^{a} f(x) d x\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x{/tex}    ...{tex}\left[\because \int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(t) d t\right]{/tex}
Hence proved.
Now, Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \sin x d x}{1+\cos ^{2} x}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \sin (\pi-x)}{1+\cos ^{2}(\pi-x)} d x{/tex} [Applying above propertey]
{tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \sin d x}{1+\cos ^{2} x}{/tex} ...(ii)
Adding equation (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}2 \mathrm{I}=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
Put cos x = t
-sin x dx = dt
when x = 0, t = 1 and x = {tex}\pi{/tex}, t = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=-\pi \int_{1}^{-1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=-\pi\left[\tan ^{-1}(t)\right]_{1}^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=-\pi\left[\tan ^{-1}(-1)-\tan ^{-1}(1)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=-\pi\left[-\tan ^{-1}(1)-\tan ^{-1}(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \tan ^{-1}(-x)=\tan ^{-1} x\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=-\pi\left[-2 \tan ^{-1}(1)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=-\pi\left(-\frac{2 \pi}{4}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{I}=\frac{2 \pi^{2}}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}=\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Prove that {tex}\int\limits_a^b f(x) d x=\int\limits_a^b f(a+b-x) d x{/tex} and hence evaluate {tex}\int\limits_{\frac {\pi}6}^{\frac{\pi}3} \frac{d x}{1+\sqrt{\tan x}}{/tex}.
question_text: Prove that {tex}\int\limits_a^b f(x) d x=\int\limits_a^b f(a+b-x) d x{/tex} and hence evaluate {tex}\int\limits_{\frac {\pi}6}^{\frac{\pi}3} \frac{d x}{1+\sqrt{\tan x}}{/tex} .
explanation: Here, RHS {tex}=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex} , {tex}=-\int_{b}^{a} f(t) d t{/tex} , where a + b - x = t, dx = - dt {tex}=\int_{a}^{b} f(t) d t{/tex} {tex}=\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x{/tex} {tex}=L H S{/tex} Now, Let I {tex}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{1+\sqrt{\tan \mathrm{x}}}{/tex} {tex}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}} d x{/tex} .........(i) Also, I {tex}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos (\pi / 2-x)}}{\sqrt{\cos (\pi / 2-x)}+\sqrt{\sin (\pi / 2-x)}} d x{/tex} ...[ by using above formula] {tex}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}} d x{/tex} ..............................(ii) Adding (i) and (ii), we get 2I {tex}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} 1 \cdot \mathrm{dx}{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{x} ]_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3}=\pi / 6{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\frac{\pi}{12}{/tex}
solution: Here, RHS {tex}=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex} , {tex}=-\int_{b}^{a} f(t) d t{/tex} , where a + b - x = t, dx = - dt {tex}=\int_{a}^{b} f(t) d t{/tex} {tex}=\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x{/tex} {tex}=L H S{/tex} Now, Let I {tex}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{1+\sqrt{\tan \mathrm{x}}}{/tex} {tex}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}} d x{/tex} .........(i) Also, I {tex}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos (\pi / 2-x)}}{\sqrt{\cos (\pi / 2-x)}+\sqrt{\sin (\pi / 2-x)}} d x{/tex} ...[ by using above formula] {tex}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}} d x{/tex} ..............................(ii) Adding (i) and (ii), we get 2I {tex}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} 1 \cdot \mathrm{dx}{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{x} ]_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3}=\pi / 6{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\frac{\pi}{12}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here,
RHS {tex}=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex}, 
{tex}=-\int_{b}^{a} f(t) d t{/tex}, where a + b - x = t, dx = - dt
{tex}=\int_{a}^{b} f(t) d t{/tex}
{tex}=\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x{/tex}
{tex}=L H S{/tex}
Now, Let I {tex}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{1+\sqrt{\tan \mathrm{x}}}{/tex}
{tex}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}} d x{/tex} .........(i)
Also,  I{tex}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos (\pi / 2-x)}}{\sqrt{\cos (\pi / 2-x)}+\sqrt{\sin (\pi / 2-x)}} d x{/tex}    ...[ by using above formula]
{tex}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}} d x{/tex} ..............................(ii)
Adding (i) and (ii), we get
2I {tex}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} 1 \cdot \mathrm{dx}{/tex}
{tex}=\mathrm{x} ]_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3}=\pi / 6{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\frac{\pi}{12}{/tex}

explanation: The given integral is: I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x(1-\tan x)^{2}} d x=\int \frac{sec^2x}{(1-tanx)^2}dx{/tex} Let {tex}1-tanx=z~ \Rightarrow~ -sec^2xdx=dz{/tex} {tex}\therefore ~ I = -\int\frac 1{z^2}dz\\=-\frac {z^{-2+1}}{-2+1}+c \\ =\frac1z+c\\=\frac1{1-tanx}+c{/tex}
solution: The given integral is: I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x(1-\tan x)^{2}} d x=\int \frac{sec^2x}{(1-tanx)^2}dx{/tex} Let {tex}1-tanx=z~ \Rightarrow~ -sec^2xdx=dz{/tex} {tex}\therefore ~ I = -\int\frac 1{z^2}dz\\=-\frac {z^{-2+1}}{-2+1}+c \\ =\frac1z+c\\=\frac1{1-tanx}+c{/tex}
solution_html:
The given integral is:
I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x(1-\tan x)^{2}} d x=\int \frac{sec^2x}{(1-tanx)^2}dx{/tex}
Let {tex}1-tanx=z~ \Rightarrow~ -sec^2xdx=dz{/tex}
{tex}\therefore ~ I = -\int\frac 1{z^2}dz\\=-\frac {z^{-2+1}}{-2+1}+c \\ =\frac1z+c\\=\frac1{1-tanx}+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int e^{2 x} \sin x d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int e^{2 x} \sin x d x{/tex}
explanation: Let the given integral be, I = {tex}\int e^{2 x} \sin x d x{/tex} ......(i) Integrating by parts, we get I = sin x {tex}\int e^{2 x} d x{/tex} - {tex}\int\left\{\left(\frac{d}{d x} \sin x\right) \int e^{2 x} d x\right\} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\sin x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}-\int \cos x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \sin x}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \int e^{2 x} \cos x d x{/tex} Again using integration by parts, we get I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \cdot \sin x}{2}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\cos x \int e^{2 x} d x\right.{/tex} - {tex}\int\left\{\left(\frac{d}{d x} \cos x\right) \int e^{2 x} d x\right\} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \sin x}{2}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\cos x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}-\int(-\sin x) \frac{e^{2 x}}{2} d x\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \cdot \sin x}{2}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \int e^{2 x} \sin x d x\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \sin x}{2}-\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{4}-\frac{1}{4} I{/tex} [From (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I + {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \cdot \sin x}{2}-\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{5}{4} I{/tex} = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \sin x}{2}-\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{4}{5}\left[\frac{e^{2 x} \sin x}{2}-\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{4}\right]{/tex} + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x}}{5}[2 \sin x-\cos x]{/tex} + C
solution: Let the given integral be, I = {tex}\int e^{2 x} \sin x d x{/tex} ......(i) Integrating by parts, we get I = sin x {tex}\int e^{2 x} d x{/tex} - {tex}\int\left\{\left(\frac{d}{d x} \sin x\right) \int e^{2 x} d x\right\} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\sin x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}-\int \cos x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \sin x}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \int e^{2 x} \cos x d x{/tex} Again using integration by parts, we get I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \cdot \sin x}{2}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\cos x \int e^{2 x} d x\right.{/tex} - {tex}\int\left\{\left(\frac{d}{d x} \cos x\right) \int e^{2 x} d x\right\} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \sin x}{2}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\cos x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}-\int(-\sin x) \frac{e^{2 x}}{2} d x\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \cdot \sin x}{2}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \int e^{2 x} \sin x d x\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \sin x}{2}-\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{4}-\frac{1}{4} I{/tex} [From (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I + {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \cdot \sin x}{2}-\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{5}{4} I{/tex} = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \sin x}{2}-\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{4}{5}\left[\frac{e^{2 x} \sin x}{2}-\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{4}\right]{/tex} + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x}}{5}[2 \sin x-\cos x]{/tex} + C
solution_html:
Let the given integral  be, I = {tex}\int e^{2 x} \sin x d x{/tex}  ......(i)
Integrating by parts, we get
I = sin x {tex}\int e^{2 x} d x{/tex} - {tex}\int\left\{\left(\frac{d}{d x} \sin x\right) \int e^{2 x} d x\right\} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\sin x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}-\int \cos x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \sin x}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \int e^{2 x} \cos x d x{/tex} 
Again using integration by parts, we get
I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \cdot \sin x}{2}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\cos x \int e^{2 x} d x\right.{/tex} - {tex}\int\left\{\left(\frac{d}{d x} \cos x\right) \int e^{2 x} d x\right\} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \sin x}{2}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\cos x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}-\int(-\sin x) \frac{e^{2 x}}{2} d x\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \cdot \sin x}{2}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \int e^{2 x} \sin x d x\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \sin x}{2}-\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{4}-\frac{1}{4} I{/tex}  [From (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I + {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \cdot \sin x}{2}-\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{5}{4} I{/tex} = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x} \sin x}{2}-\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{4}{5}\left[\frac{e^{2 x} \sin x}{2}-\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{4}\right]{/tex} + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{e^{2 x}}{5}[2 \sin x-\cos x]{/tex} + C

subject: Mathematics
explanation: We can write, cos(x + a) = cos(x + b + a - b), we get I = {tex}\int \frac{\cos (x+b+a-b)}{\sin (x+b)} d x{/tex} Using cos(A+B)= cosA cosB - sinA sinB, we get I = {tex}\int\frac{[\cos (x+b) \cos (a-b)-\sin(x+b)\sin (a-b)]}{sin(x+b)} d x{/tex} = {tex} cos(a-b)\int \frac {cos(x+b)}{sin(x+b)}dx+sin(a-b)\int \frac {sin(x+b)}{sin(x+b)}dx{/tex} = {tex}cos(a-b).log(sin(x+b))+x.sin(a-b)+c{/tex}
solution: We can write, cos(x + a) = cos(x + b + a - b), we get I = {tex}\int \frac{\cos (x+b+a-b)}{\sin (x+b)} d x{/tex} Using cos(A+B)= cosA cosB - sinA sinB, we get I = {tex}\int\frac{[\cos (x+b) \cos (a-b)-\sin(x+b)\sin (a-b)]}{sin(x+b)} d x{/tex} = {tex} cos(a-b)\int \frac {cos(x+b)}{sin(x+b)}dx+sin(a-b)\int \frac {sin(x+b)}{sin(x+b)}dx{/tex} = {tex}cos(a-b).log(sin(x+b))+x.sin(a-b)+c{/tex}
solution_html:
We can write, cos(x + a) = cos(x + b + a - b), we get
I = {tex}\int \frac{\cos (x+b+a-b)}{\sin (x+b)} d x{/tex}
Using cos(A+B)= cosA cosB - sinA sinB, we get
I = {tex}\int\frac{[\cos (x+b) \cos (a-b)-\sin(x+b)\sin (a-b)]}{sin(x+b)} d x{/tex}
= {tex} cos(a-b)\int \frac {cos(x+b)}{sin(x+b)}dx+sin(a-b)\int \frac {sin(x+b)}{sin(x+b)}dx{/tex}
= {tex}cos(a-b).log(sin(x+b))+x.sin(a-b)+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Prove that {tex}\int\limits_0^a f(x) d x=\int\limits_0^a f(a-x) d x{/tex}, and hence evaluate {tex}\int\limits_0^1 x^2(1-x)^n d x{/tex}.
question_text: Prove that {tex}\int\limits_0^a f(x) d x=\int\limits_0^a f(a-x) d x{/tex} , and hence evaluate {tex}\int\limits_0^1 x^2(1-x)^n d x{/tex} .
explanation: Let a - x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -dx = dt RHS = {tex}-\int \limits_{a}^{0} f(t) d t=\int \limits_{0}^{a} f(t) d t=\int \limits_{0}^{a} f(x) d x{/tex} As change of variable is allowed in definite integrals. Now, I = {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{1} x^{2}(1-x)^{n} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{1}(1-x)^{2} x^{n} d x{/tex} [ USING ABOVE] = {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{1}\left[x^{n}+x^{n+2}-2 x^{n+1}\right] d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+\frac{x^{n+3}}{n+3}-\left.\frac{2 x^{n+2}}{n+2}\right|_{0} ^{1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{n+1}+\frac{1}{n+3}-\frac{2}{n+2}{/tex}
solution: Let a - x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -dx = dt RHS = {tex}-\int \limits_{a}^{0} f(t) d t=\int \limits_{0}^{a} f(t) d t=\int \limits_{0}^{a} f(x) d x{/tex} As change of variable is allowed in definite integrals. Now, I = {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{1} x^{2}(1-x)^{n} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{1}(1-x)^{2} x^{n} d x{/tex} [ USING ABOVE] = {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{1}\left[x^{n}+x^{n+2}-2 x^{n+1}\right] d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+\frac{x^{n+3}}{n+3}-\left.\frac{2 x^{n+2}}{n+2}\right|_{0} ^{1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{n+1}+\frac{1}{n+3}-\frac{2}{n+2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let a - x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -dx = dt
RHS = {tex}-\int \limits_{a}^{0} f(t) d t=\int \limits_{0}^{a} f(t) d t=\int \limits_{0}^{a} f(x) d x{/tex}
As change of variable is allowed in definite integrals.
Now, I = {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{1} x^{2}(1-x)^{n} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{1}(1-x)^{2} x^{n} d x{/tex}     [ USING ABOVE]
= {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{1}\left[x^{n}+x^{n+2}-2 x^{n+1}\right] d x{/tex}
={tex}\frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+\frac{x^{n+3}}{n+3}-\left.\frac{2 x^{n+2}}{n+2}\right|_{0} ^{1}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{n+1}+\frac{1}{n+3}-\frac{2}{n+2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate the integral:{tex}\int \frac{\sin ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate the integral: {tex}\int \frac{\sin ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x{/tex}
explanation: Take sin -1 x = t So we get {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} \mathrm{d} x=\mathrm{dt}{/tex} By integrating w.r.t. t {tex}\int t^{1} d t=\frac{t^{2}}{2}+c{/tex} By substituting the value of t {tex}=\frac{\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^{2}}{2}+c{/tex}
solution: Take sin -1 x = t So we get {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} \mathrm{d} x=\mathrm{dt}{/tex} By integrating w.r.t. t {tex}\int t^{1} d t=\frac{t^{2}}{2}+c{/tex} By substituting the value of t {tex}=\frac{\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^{2}}{2}+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Take sin -1 x = t
So we get
{tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} \mathrm{d} x=\mathrm{dt}{/tex}
By integrating w.r.t. t
{tex}\int t^{1} d t=\frac{t^{2}}{2}+c{/tex}
By substituting the value of t
{tex}=\frac{\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^{2}}{2}+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find: {tex}\int{/tex} ex(cos x - sin x)cosec2 xdx
question_text: Find: {tex}\int{/tex} e x (cos x - sin x)cosec 2 xdx
explanation: {tex}\int e^{x}(\cos x-\sin x) \operatorname{cosec}^{2} x d x{/tex} = {tex}-\int e^{x}[\operatorname{cosec} x-\operatorname{cosec} x \cot x] d x{/tex} = {tex}-e^{x} \ cosec x{/tex} + C {using {tex}\int e^x\lbrack\;f(x){/tex} + f ' (x)]dx = e x f(x) +C}
solution: {tex}\int e^{x}(\cos x-\sin x) \operatorname{cosec}^{2} x d x{/tex} = {tex}-\int e^{x}[\operatorname{cosec} x-\operatorname{cosec} x \cot x] d x{/tex} = {tex}-e^{x} \ cosec x{/tex} + C {using {tex}\int e^x\lbrack\;f(x){/tex} + f ' (x)]dx = e x f(x) +C}
solution_html:
{tex}\int e^{x}(\cos x-\sin x) \operatorname{cosec}^{2} x d x{/tex}
= {tex}-\int e^{x}[\operatorname{cosec} x-\operatorname{cosec} x \cot x] d x{/tex}
= {tex}-e^{x} \ cosec x{/tex}  + C {using {tex}\int e^x\lbrack\;f(x){/tex} + f'(x)]dx = exf(x) +C}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: {tex}\int \frac{x-1}{(x-2)(x-3)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{-x+3 +2x -4}{(x-2)(x-3)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{-1(x-3) +2(x -2)}{(x-2)(x-3)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int\left(\frac{-1}{x-2}+\frac{2}{x-3}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}-\log |x-2|{/tex} + {tex}2 \log |x-3|{/tex} + C
solution: {tex}\int \frac{x-1}{(x-2)(x-3)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{-x+3 +2x -4}{(x-2)(x-3)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{-1(x-3) +2(x -2)}{(x-2)(x-3)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int\left(\frac{-1}{x-2}+\frac{2}{x-3}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}-\log |x-2|{/tex} + {tex}2 \log |x-3|{/tex} + C
solution_html:
{tex}\int \frac{x-1}{(x-2)(x-3)} d x{/tex}

= {tex}\int \frac{-x+3 +2x -4}{(x-2)(x-3)} d x{/tex}

={tex}\int \frac{-1(x-3) +2(x -2)}{(x-2)(x-3)} d x{/tex} 
={tex}\int\left(\frac{-1}{x-2}+\frac{2}{x-3}\right) d x{/tex}
= {tex}-\log |x-2|{/tex} + {tex}2 \log |x-3|{/tex} + C
