solution: Let, I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} Also let {tex}\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{(\mathrm{x}-1)\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{x}-1}+\frac{\mathrm{B}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = A(x 2 + 1) + B(x - 1) For, x = 1, A = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} For, x = 0, B = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x-1}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\log |x-1|+\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} x+c{/tex} Hence, {tex}\int \frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{(\mathrm{x}-1)\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+1\right)} \mathrm{dx}=\frac{1}{2} \log |\mathrm{x}-1|{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} x+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let, I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex}
Also let {tex}\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{(\mathrm{x}-1)\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{x}-1}+\frac{\mathrm{B}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = A(x2 + 1) + B(x - 1)
For, x = 1, A = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
For, x = 0, B = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x-1}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\log |x-1|+\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} x+c{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\int \frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{(\mathrm{x}-1)\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+1\right)} \mathrm{dx}=\frac{1}{2} \log |\mathrm{x}-1|{/tex}{tex}+\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} x+c{/tex}

question_html: Evaluate the integral: {tex}\int \sqrt{2 x-x^{2}} d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate the integral: {tex}\int \sqrt{2 x-x^{2}} d x{/tex}
explanation: Let I = {tex}\int \sqrt{2 x-x^{2}} d x{/tex} , then we have I = {tex}\int \sqrt{x(2-x)} d x{/tex} Put, x = 1 + sin u or dx = cos u du {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \sqrt{(1+\sin u)(1-\sin u)} \cos u d u{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \cos ^{2} u d u{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int(\cos 2 u+1) d u{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2} \sin 2 u+u\right){/tex} + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (sin u cos u + u) + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (sin u {tex}\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} u}{/tex} + u) + C {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (x - 1) {tex}\sqrt{2 x-x^{2}}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} sin -1 (x - 1) + C [ {tex}\because{/tex} u = sin -1 (x - 1)]
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \sqrt{2 x-x^{2}} d x{/tex} , then we have I = {tex}\int \sqrt{x(2-x)} d x{/tex} Put, x = 1 + sin u or dx = cos u du {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \sqrt{(1+\sin u)(1-\sin u)} \cos u d u{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \cos ^{2} u d u{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int(\cos 2 u+1) d u{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2} \sin 2 u+u\right){/tex} + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (sin u cos u + u) + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (sin u {tex}\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} u}{/tex} + u) + C {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (x - 1) {tex}\sqrt{2 x-x^{2}}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} sin -1 (x - 1) + C [ {tex}\because{/tex} u = sin -1 (x - 1)]
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \sqrt{2 x-x^{2}} d x{/tex} , then we have
I = {tex}\int \sqrt{x(2-x)} d x{/tex} 
Put, x = 1 + sin u 
or dx = cos u du
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \sqrt{(1+\sin u)(1-\sin u)} \cos u d u{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \cos ^{2} u d u{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int(\cos 2 u+1) d u{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2} \sin 2 u+u\right){/tex} + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(sin u cos u + u) + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (sin u {tex}\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} u}{/tex} + u) + C
{tex}\therefore{/tex}   I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (x - 1){tex}\sqrt{2 x-x^{2}}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} sin-1 (x - 1) + C [{tex}\because{/tex} u = sin-1 (x - 1)]

explanation: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x}{\sqrt{32-x^{2}}} d x{/tex} Put 32 - x 2 = t 2 {tex}\implies {/tex} -2xdx = 2t dt {tex}\implies {/tex} xdx= -tdt I = {tex}\int\frac{-tdt}t{/tex} = {tex}-\int 1 . dt{/tex} = -t + C = {tex}-\sqrt{32-x^{2}}{/tex} +C
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x}{\sqrt{32-x^{2}}} d x{/tex} Put 32 - x 2 = t 2 {tex}\implies {/tex} -2xdx = 2t dt {tex}\implies {/tex} xdx= -tdt I = {tex}\int\frac{-tdt}t{/tex} = {tex}-\int 1 . dt{/tex} = -t + C = {tex}-\sqrt{32-x^{2}}{/tex} +C
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x}{\sqrt{32-x^{2}}} d x{/tex}  

Put 32 - x2 = t2

{tex}\implies {/tex}-2xdx = 2t dt{tex}\implies {/tex}xdx= -tdt

I = {tex}\int\frac{-tdt}t{/tex}

= {tex}-\int 1 . dt{/tex} 
= -t + C

= {tex}-\sqrt{32-x^{2}}{/tex}+C

question_html: Evaluate {tex} \int \frac { x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ( x + 2 ) } d x.{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate {tex} \int \frac { x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ( x + 2 ) } d x.{/tex}
explanation: Let, {tex} I = \int \frac { x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ( x + 2 ) } d x{/tex} By using partial fractions , {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ( x + 2 ) } = \frac { A } { x + 2 } + \frac { B x + C } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Rightarrow x^2 + x + 1 = A(x^2 +1)+ (Bx+ C)(x + 2){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^2 + x + 1 = x^2 (A + B) + x(2B + C) + (A + 2C){/tex} Comparing the coefficients of {tex}x^2, x{/tex} and constant terms on both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow 1=A+B{/tex} .....(ii) {tex}\Rightarrow 1=2B+C{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\Rightarrow 1=A+2C{/tex} ...(iv) On solving Equations (ii) and (iii) we get , {tex}1=2A-C{/tex} ...(v) On solving Equations (iv) and (v) we get , {tex}C = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \text { and } A = \frac { 3 } { 5 }{/tex} From Eq. (ii), we get B = {tex}1-\frac { 3 } { 5 } = \frac { 2 } { 5 }{/tex} Thus, from Eq. (i), we have {tex}\frac { x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ( x + 2 ) } = \frac { 3 } { 5 } \cdot \frac { 1 } { ( x + 2 ) } + \frac { 1 } { 5 } \frac { ( 2 x + 1 ) } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) }{/tex} Integrating both sides w.r.t to x ,we get : {tex}\Rightarrow I = \int\frac { x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ( x + 2 ) }dx= \frac { 3 } { 5 } \int \frac { d x } { x + 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 5 } \int \frac { ( 2 x + 1 ) } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 } { 5 } \int \frac { d x } { x + 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 5 } \int \frac { 2 x } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 } d x + \frac { 1 } { 5 } \int \frac { d x } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 } { 5 } \log | x + 2 | + \frac { 1 } { 5 } \log \left| x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right| + \frac { 1 } { 5 } \tan ^ { - 1 } x + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r l } { \because \int \frac { d x } { x ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } } } & { = \frac { 1 } { a } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { a } \right) + C \\ { and } \ \int \frac { f ^ { \prime } ( x ) } { f ( x ) } d x { = \log | f ( x ) | + C } } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore I={ { 3 } \log | x + 2 | + \log \left| x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right| + tan ^ { - 1 } x \over 5 } + C{/tex}
solution: Let, {tex} I = \int \frac { x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ( x + 2 ) } d x{/tex} By using partial fractions , {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ( x + 2 ) } = \frac { A } { x + 2 } + \frac { B x + C } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Rightarrow x^2 + x + 1 = A(x^2 +1)+ (Bx+ C)(x + 2){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^2 + x + 1 = x^2 (A + B) + x(2B + C) + (A + 2C){/tex} Comparing the coefficients of {tex}x^2, x{/tex} and constant terms on both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow 1=A+B{/tex} .....(ii) {tex}\Rightarrow 1=2B+C{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\Rightarrow 1=A+2C{/tex} ...(iv) On solving Equations (ii) and (iii) we get , {tex}1=2A-C{/tex} ...(v) On solving Equations (iv) and (v) we get , {tex}C = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \text { and } A = \frac { 3 } { 5 }{/tex} From Eq. (ii), we get B = {tex}1-\frac { 3 } { 5 } = \frac { 2 } { 5 }{/tex} Thus, from Eq. (i), we have {tex}\frac { x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ( x + 2 ) } = \frac { 3 } { 5 } \cdot \frac { 1 } { ( x + 2 ) } + \frac { 1 } { 5 } \frac { ( 2 x + 1 ) } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) }{/tex} Integrating both sides w.r.t to x ,we get : {tex}\Rightarrow I = \int\frac { x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ( x + 2 ) }dx= \frac { 3 } { 5 } \int \frac { d x } { x + 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 5 } \int \frac { ( 2 x + 1 ) } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 } { 5 } \int \frac { d x } { x + 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 5 } \int \frac { 2 x } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 } d x + \frac { 1 } { 5 } \int \frac { d x } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 } { 5 } \log | x + 2 | + \frac { 1 } { 5 } \log \left| x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right| + \frac { 1 } { 5 } \tan ^ { - 1 } x + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r l } { \because \int \frac { d x } { x ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } } } & { = \frac { 1 } { a } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { a } \right) + C \\ { and } \ \int \frac { f ^ { \prime } ( x ) } { f ( x ) } d x { = \log | f ( x ) | + C } } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore I={ { 3 } \log | x + 2 | + \log \left| x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right| + tan ^ { - 1 } x \over 5 } + C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let, {tex} I = \int \frac { x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ( x + 2 ) } d x{/tex}
By using partial fractions ,  
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ( x + 2 ) } = \frac { A } { x + 2 } + \frac { B x + C } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex}...(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow x^2 + x + 1 = A(x^2 +1)+ (Bx+ C)(x + 2){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x^2 + x + 1 = x^2 (A + B) + x(2B + C) + (A + 2C){/tex} 
Comparing the coefficients of {tex}x^2, x{/tex} and constant terms on both sides,
{tex}\Rightarrow 1=A+B{/tex}.....(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow 1=2B+C{/tex}...(iii)
{tex}\Rightarrow  1=A+2C{/tex}...(iv) 
On solving Equations (ii) and (iii)  we get ,
{tex}1=2A-C{/tex}...(v)
On solving Equations (iv) and (v)  we get ,
{tex}C = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \text { and } A = \frac { 3 } { 5 }{/tex}
From Eq. (ii), we get B = {tex}1-\frac { 3 } { 5 } = \frac { 2 } { 5 }{/tex}
Thus, from Eq. (i), we have
{tex}\frac { x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ( x + 2 ) } = \frac { 3 } { 5 } \cdot \frac { 1 } { ( x + 2 ) } + \frac { 1 } { 5 } \frac { ( 2 x + 1 ) } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) }{/tex}
Integrating both sides w.r.t to x ,we get :
{tex}\Rightarrow I = \int\frac { x ^ { 2 } + x + 1 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ( x + 2 ) }dx= \frac { 3 } { 5 } \int \frac { d x } { x + 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 5 } \int \frac { ( 2 x + 1 ) } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 3 } { 5 } \int \frac { d x } { x + 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 5 } \int \frac { 2 x } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 } d x + \frac { 1 } { 5 } \int \frac { d x } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 3 } { 5 } \log | x + 2 | + \frac { 1 } { 5 } \log \left| x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right| + \frac { 1 } { 5 } \tan ^ { - 1 } x + C{/tex}{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r l } { \because \int \frac { d x } { x ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } } } & { = \frac { 1 } { a } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { a } \right) + C \\ { and } \ \int \frac { f ^ { \prime } ( x ) } { f ( x ) } d x { = \log | f ( x ) | + C } } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore I={ { 3 } \log | x + 2 | + \log \left| x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right| + tan ^ { - 1 } x \over 5 } + C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int \frac { x \sin ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } d x.{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int \frac { x \sin ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } d x.{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}I = \int \frac { x \sin ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex} {tex}Put, \ \ sin^{-1} x = t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } d x = d t{/tex} {tex} I = \int {\mathop t\limits_I } \mathop {\sin }\limits_{II} tdt{/tex} Using integration by parts, taking t as the first function and sin t as the second function, {tex}\Rightarrow I = t \int \sin t d t - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d t } ( t ) \cdot \int \sin t d t \right] d t{/tex} {tex}= - t \cos t - \int ( 1 \times - \cos t ) d t{/tex} {tex}= - t \cos t + \int \cos t d t{/tex} {tex}= -t\ cos t + sin t + C{/tex} {tex}= - t \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } t } + \sin t + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \cos ^ { 2 } t = 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } t \Rightarrow \cos t = \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } t } \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = - \sin ^ { - 1 } x \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } + x + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \text { put } t = \sin ^ { - 1 } x \Rightarrow x = \sin t \right]{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I = \int \frac { x \sin ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex} {tex}Put, \ \ sin^{-1} x = t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } d x = d t{/tex} {tex} I = \int {\mathop t\limits_I } \mathop {\sin }\limits_{II} tdt{/tex} Using integration by parts, taking t as the first function and sin t as the second function, {tex}\Rightarrow I = t \int \sin t d t - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d t } ( t ) \cdot \int \sin t d t \right] d t{/tex} {tex}= - t \cos t - \int ( 1 \times - \cos t ) d t{/tex} {tex}= - t \cos t + \int \cos t d t{/tex} {tex}= -t\ cos t + sin t + C{/tex} {tex}= - t \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } t } + \sin t + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \cos ^ { 2 } t = 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } t \Rightarrow \cos t = \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } t } \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = - \sin ^ { - 1 } x \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } + x + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \text { put } t = \sin ^ { - 1 } x \Rightarrow x = \sin t \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I = \int \frac { x \sin ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex}
{tex}Put, \ \ sin^{-1} x = t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } d x = d t{/tex}
{tex} I = \int {\mathop t\limits_I } \mathop {\sin }\limits_{II} tdt{/tex}
Using integration by parts, taking t as the first function and sin t as the second function,
{tex}\Rightarrow I = t \int \sin t d t - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d t } ( t ) \cdot \int \sin t d t \right] d t{/tex}
{tex}= - t \cos t - \int ( 1 \times - \cos t ) d t{/tex}
{tex}= - t \cos t + \int \cos t d t{/tex}
{tex}= -t\ cos t + sin t + C{/tex}
{tex}= - t \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } t } + \sin t + C{/tex}
{tex}\left[ \because \cos ^ { 2 } t = 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } t \Rightarrow \cos t = \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } t } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I = - \sin ^ { - 1 } x \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } + x + C{/tex}
{tex}\left[ \text { put } t = \sin ^ { - 1 } x \Rightarrow x = \sin t \right]{/tex}

According to the question , {tex}I =\int _ { - 1 } ^ { 2 } \left| x ^ { 3 } - x \right| d x{/tex}
We can observe that,
{tex}\left| x ^ { 3 } - x \right| = \left\{ \begin{array} { c } { \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) , \text { when } - 1 < x < 0 } \\ { - \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) , \text { when } 0 \leq x < 1 } \\ { \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) , \text { when } 1 \leq x < 2 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}
By Splitting the intervals , we get  
{tex}I =\int _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 } \left| x ^ { 3 } - x \right| d x + \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \left| x ^ { 3 } - x \right| d x +\int _ { 1} ^ { 2 } \left| x ^ { 3 } - x \right| d x{/tex}
 {tex}\therefore I = \int _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 } \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) d x + \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } - \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) d x + \int _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 } \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) d x - \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) d x + \int _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { 4 } - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 } - \left[ \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { 4 } - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } + \left[ \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { 4 } - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ 0 - \left( \frac { 1 } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) \right] - \left[ \left( \frac { 1 } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) - 0 \right]{/tex}{tex}+ \left[ \left( \frac { 16 } { 4 } - \frac { 4 } { 2 } \right) - \left( \frac { 1 } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 4 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 4 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } + 4 - 2 - \frac { 1 } { 4 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { 3 } { 4 } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } + 2{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { - 3 + 6 + 8 } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 11 } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore I = \frac { 11 } { 4 }{/tex} sq units.

question_html: Evaluate: {tex}\int \frac{(3 \sin x-2) \cos x}{5-\cos ^{2} x-4 \sin x} d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate: {tex}\int \frac{(3 \sin x-2) \cos x}{5-\cos ^{2} x-4 \sin x} d x{/tex}
explanation: Let the given integral be I = {tex}\int \frac{(3 \sin x-2) \cos x}{5-\cos ^{2} x-4 \sin x} d x{/tex} {tex}\therefore \ I=\int \frac{(3 \sin x-2) \cos x}{5-\left(1-\sin ^{2} x\right)-4 \sin x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1=\int \frac{(3 \sin x-2) \cos x}{5-1+\sin ^{2} x-4 \sin x} d x{/tex} Substitute sin x = t => cos x dx = dt Thus, {tex}I=\int \frac{(3 t-2)}{4+t^{2}-4 t} d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\int \frac{(3 t-2)}{t^{2}-4 t+4} d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{(3 t-2)}{(t-2)^{2}} d t{/tex} Now let us separate the integrand into the simplest form using partial fractions. {tex}\frac{(3 t-2)}{(t-2)^{2}}=\frac{A}{(t-2)}+\frac{B}{(t-2)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A(t-2)+B}{(t-2)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A t-2 A+B}{(t-2)^{2}}{/tex} = 3t - 2 = At - 2A + B Comparing the coefficients, we have, A = 3 and -2A + B = -2 Substituting the value of A = 3 in the above equation, we have, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2 {tex} \times{/tex} 3 + B = -2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -6 + B = -2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = 6 -2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = 4 Therefore we have, {tex}I=\int \frac{(3 t-2)}{(t-2)^{2}} d t{/tex} becomes {tex}I=\int \frac{3}{(t-2)} d t+\int \frac{4}{(t-2)^{2}} d t{/tex} = 3 log |t - 2| - 4 {tex}(\frac{1}{t-2})+C{/tex} = 3 log |2 - t| + 4 {tex}(\frac{1}{2-t})+C{/tex} Now substituting t = sin x, we have, I = 3 log |2 - sin x| +4 {tex}(\frac{1}{2-\sin x})+C{/tex}
solution: Let the given integral be I = {tex}\int \frac{(3 \sin x-2) \cos x}{5-\cos ^{2} x-4 \sin x} d x{/tex} {tex}\therefore \ I=\int \frac{(3 \sin x-2) \cos x}{5-\left(1-\sin ^{2} x\right)-4 \sin x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1=\int \frac{(3 \sin x-2) \cos x}{5-1+\sin ^{2} x-4 \sin x} d x{/tex} Substitute sin x = t => cos x dx = dt Thus, {tex}I=\int \frac{(3 t-2)}{4+t^{2}-4 t} d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\int \frac{(3 t-2)}{t^{2}-4 t+4} d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{(3 t-2)}{(t-2)^{2}} d t{/tex} Now let us separate the integrand into the simplest form using partial fractions. {tex}\frac{(3 t-2)}{(t-2)^{2}}=\frac{A}{(t-2)}+\frac{B}{(t-2)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A(t-2)+B}{(t-2)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A t-2 A+B}{(t-2)^{2}}{/tex} = 3t - 2 = At - 2A + B Comparing the coefficients, we have, A = 3 and -2A + B = -2 Substituting the value of A = 3 in the above equation, we have, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2 {tex} \times{/tex} 3 + B = -2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -6 + B = -2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = 6 -2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = 4 Therefore we have, {tex}I=\int \frac{(3 t-2)}{(t-2)^{2}} d t{/tex} becomes {tex}I=\int \frac{3}{(t-2)} d t+\int \frac{4}{(t-2)^{2}} d t{/tex} = 3 log |t - 2| - 4 {tex}(\frac{1}{t-2})+C{/tex} = 3 log |2 - t| + 4 {tex}(\frac{1}{2-t})+C{/tex} Now substituting t = sin x, we have, I = 3 log |2 - sin x| +4 {tex}(\frac{1}{2-\sin x})+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the given integral be

I = {tex}\int \frac{(3 \sin x-2) \cos x}{5-\cos ^{2} x-4 \sin x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ I=\int \frac{(3 \sin x-2) \cos x}{5-\left(1-\sin ^{2} x\right)-4 \sin x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 1=\int \frac{(3 \sin x-2) \cos x}{5-1+\sin ^{2} x-4 \sin x} d x{/tex}
Substitute sin x = t
=> cos x dx = dt
Thus,
{tex}I=\int \frac{(3 t-2)}{4+t^{2}-4 t} d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\int \frac{(3 t-2)}{t^{2}-4 t+4} d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{(3 t-2)}{(t-2)^{2}} d t{/tex}
Now let us separate the integrand into the simplest form using partial fractions.
{tex}\frac{(3 t-2)}{(t-2)^{2}}=\frac{A}{(t-2)}+\frac{B}{(t-2)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{A(t-2)+B}{(t-2)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{A t-2 A+B}{(t-2)^{2}}{/tex}
= 3t - 2 = At - 2A + B
Comparing the coefficients, we have,
A = 3 and -2A + B = -2
Substituting the value of A = 3 in the above equation, we have,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}-2 {tex} \times{/tex} 3 + B = -2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}-6 + B = -2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = 6 -2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = 4
Therefore we have, {tex}I=\int \frac{(3 t-2)}{(t-2)^{2}} d t{/tex} becomes
{tex}I=\int \frac{3}{(t-2)} d t+\int \frac{4}{(t-2)^{2}} d t{/tex}
= 3 log |t - 2| - 4 {tex}(\frac{1}{t-2})+C{/tex}
= 3 log |2 - t| + 4 {tex}(\frac{1}{2-t})+C{/tex}
Now substituting t = sin x, we have,
I = 3 log |2 - sin x| +4 {tex}(\frac{1}{2-\sin x})+C{/tex}

question_html: Find {tex} \int ( x + 3 ) \sqrt { \left( 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } \right) } d x.{/tex}
question_text: Find {tex} \int ( x + 3 ) \sqrt { \left( 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } \right) } d x.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, {tex} I = \int ( x + 3 ) \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} Above integral is the form of {tex} \int ( p x + q ) \sqrt { a x ^ { 2 } + b x + c } d x{/tex} Let {tex} ( x + 3 ) = A \frac { d } { d x } \left( 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } \right) + B{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x + 3 = A(-4 - 2x) + B{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x + 3 = (- 4A + B) - 2A x{/tex} Comparing the coefficients of x and constant terms, {tex}\Rightarrow -2 A = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad A = - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} and - 4A + B = 3 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 2 + B = 3 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} B = 1 Thus, {tex} ( x + 3 ) = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( - 4 - 2 x ) + 1{/tex} [from Eq. (i)] Given integral becomes {tex} I = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int ( - 4 - 2 x ) \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex} + \int \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}I = I_1 + I_2 {/tex} (say) Consider {tex} I _ { 1 } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int ( - 4 - 2 x ) \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} Put {tex}3-4x-x^2= t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(- 4 - 2x)dx = dt{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I _ { 1 } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \sqrt { t } d t = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 2 } { 3 } ( t ) ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } + C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left( 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } + C _ { 1 }{/tex} and {tex}I _ { 2 } = \int \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \sqrt { - \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 x - 3 \right) } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \sqrt { - \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \times 2 x + 4 - 4 - 3 \right) } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \sqrt { - \left\{ ( x + 2 ) ^ { 2 } - 7 \right\} } d x{/tex} {tex} = \int \sqrt { 7 - ( x + 2 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \sqrt { ( \sqrt { 7 } ) ^ { 2 } - ( x + 2 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ ( x + 2 ) \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } } + 7 \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x + 2 } { \sqrt { 7 } } \right) \right] + C _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r } { \because \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ x \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \right. } \\ { + a ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { a } \right) ] + c } \end{array} \right]{/tex} From Eq. (ii), we have {tex} I = - \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left( 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( x + 2 ) \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} + \frac { 7 } { 2 } \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x + 2 } { \sqrt { 7 } } \right) + C{/tex} where, C = C 1 + C 2 .
solution: According to the question, {tex} I = \int ( x + 3 ) \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} Above integral is the form of {tex} \int ( p x + q ) \sqrt { a x ^ { 2 } + b x + c } d x{/tex} Let {tex} ( x + 3 ) = A \frac { d } { d x } \left( 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } \right) + B{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x + 3 = A(-4 - 2x) + B{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x + 3 = (- 4A + B) - 2A x{/tex} Comparing the coefficients of x and constant terms, {tex}\Rightarrow -2 A = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad A = - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} and - 4A + B = 3 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 2 + B = 3 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} B = 1 Thus, {tex} ( x + 3 ) = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( - 4 - 2 x ) + 1{/tex} [from Eq. (i)] Given integral becomes {tex} I = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int ( - 4 - 2 x ) \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex} + \int \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}I = I_1 + I_2 {/tex} (say) Consider {tex} I _ { 1 } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int ( - 4 - 2 x ) \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} Put {tex}3-4x-x^2= t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(- 4 - 2x)dx = dt{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I _ { 1 } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \sqrt { t } d t = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 2 } { 3 } ( t ) ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } + C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left( 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } + C _ { 1 }{/tex} and {tex}I _ { 2 } = \int \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \sqrt { - \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 x - 3 \right) } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \sqrt { - \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \times 2 x + 4 - 4 - 3 \right) } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \sqrt { - \left\{ ( x + 2 ) ^ { 2 } - 7 \right\} } d x{/tex} {tex} = \int \sqrt { 7 - ( x + 2 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \sqrt { ( \sqrt { 7 } ) ^ { 2 } - ( x + 2 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ ( x + 2 ) \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } } + 7 \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x + 2 } { \sqrt { 7 } } \right) \right] + C _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r } { \because \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ x \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \right. } \\ { + a ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { a } \right) ] + c } \end{array} \right]{/tex} From Eq. (ii), we have {tex} I = - \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left( 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( x + 2 ) \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} + \frac { 7 } { 2 } \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x + 2 } { \sqrt { 7 } } \right) + C{/tex} where, C = C 1 + C 2 .
solution_html:
According to the question,  {tex} I = \int ( x + 3 ) \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
Above integral is the form of
{tex} \int ( p x + q ) \sqrt { a x ^ { 2 } + b x + c } d x{/tex}
Let {tex} ( x + 3 ) = A \frac { d } { d x } \left( 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } \right) + B{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x + 3 = A(-4 - 2x) + B{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x + 3 = (- 4A + B) - 2A x{/tex}
Comparing the coefficients of x and constant terms, 
{tex}\Rightarrow -2 A = 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad A = - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
and - 4A + B = 3 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex}2 + B = 3 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} B = 1
Thus, {tex} ( x + 3 ) = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( - 4 - 2 x ) + 1{/tex} [from Eq. (i)]
Given integral becomes
{tex} I = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int ( - 4 - 2 x ) \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}{tex} + \int \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}I = I_1 + I_2 {/tex}(say)
Consider
{tex} I _ { 1 } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int ( - 4 - 2 x ) \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
Put  {tex}3-4x-x^2= t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(- 4 - 2x)dx = dt{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I _ { 1 } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \sqrt { t } d t = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { 2 } { 3 } ( t ) ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } + C _ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left( 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } + C _ { 1 }{/tex}
and {tex}I _ { 2 } = \int \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int \sqrt { - \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 x - 3 \right) } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int \sqrt { - \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \times 2 x + 4 - 4 - 3 \right) } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int \sqrt { - \left\{ ( x + 2 ) ^ { 2 } - 7 \right\} } d x{/tex}
{tex} = \int \sqrt { 7 - ( x + 2 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int \sqrt { ( \sqrt { 7 } ) ^ { 2 } - ( x + 2 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ ( x + 2 ) \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } } + 7 \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x + 2 } { \sqrt { 7 } } \right) \right] + C _ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r } { \because \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ x \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } \right. } \\ { + a ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { a } \right) ] + c } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
From Eq. (ii), we have 
{tex} I = - \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left( 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( x + 2 ) \sqrt { 3 - 4 x - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}{tex} + \frac { 7 } { 2 } \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x + 2 } { \sqrt { 7 } } \right) + C{/tex}
where, C = C1 + C2.

explanation: Let, I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}}{x^{4}+x^{2}-2} d x{/tex} Using partial fractions, {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{x^{4}+x^{2}-2}={/tex} {tex}\frac{A}{x+1}+\frac{B}{x-1}+\frac{C}{x^{2}+2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = A(x - 1) (x 2 + 2) + B (x + 1) (x 2 + 2) + C (x 2 - 1) For, x = 1, A = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} For, x = -1, B = - {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} For, x = 0, C = {tex}-\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x+1}-\frac{1}{6} \int \frac{d x}{x-1}-\frac{2}{3} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{6} \log |x+1|-\frac{1}{6} \log |x-1|{/tex} {tex}-\frac{2}{3 \sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+\mathrm{c}{/tex}
solution: Let, I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}}{x^{4}+x^{2}-2} d x{/tex} Using partial fractions, {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{x^{4}+x^{2}-2}={/tex} {tex}\frac{A}{x+1}+\frac{B}{x-1}+\frac{C}{x^{2}+2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = A(x - 1) (x 2 + 2) + B (x + 1) (x 2 + 2) + C (x 2 - 1) For, x = 1, A = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} For, x = -1, B = - {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} For, x = 0, C = {tex}-\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x+1}-\frac{1}{6} \int \frac{d x}{x-1}-\frac{2}{3} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{6} \log |x+1|-\frac{1}{6} \log |x-1|{/tex} {tex}-\frac{2}{3 \sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+\mathrm{c}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let, I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}}{x^{4}+x^{2}-2} d x{/tex}
Using partial fractions,
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{x^{4}+x^{2}-2}={/tex} {tex}\frac{A}{x+1}+\frac{B}{x-1}+\frac{C}{x^{2}+2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = A(x - 1) (x2 + 2) + B (x + 1) (x2 + 2) + C (x2 - 1)
For, x = 1, A = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex}
For, x = -1, B = -{tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex}
For, x = 0, C = {tex}-\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x+1}-\frac{1}{6} \int \frac{d x}{x-1}-\frac{2}{3} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{6} \log |x+1|-\frac{1}{6} \log |x-1|{/tex}{tex}-\frac{2}{3 \sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+\mathrm{c}{/tex}

question_html: Find {tex} \int \frac { \sqrt { x } } { \sqrt { a ^ { 3 } - x ^ { 3 } } } d x.{/tex}
question_text: Find {tex} \int \frac { \sqrt { x } } { \sqrt { a ^ { 3 } - x ^ { 3 } } } d x.{/tex}
explanation: Given {tex}I= \int \frac { \sqrt { x } } { \sqrt { a ^ { 3 } - x ^ { 3 } } } d x = \int \frac { \sqrt { x } } { \sqrt { \left( a ^ { 3 / 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } - \left( x ^ { 3 / 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex} {tex}Let,\ \ x^{3/2} = a^{3/2} t{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { 3 } { 2 } x ^ { 1 / 2 } d x = a ^ { 3 / 2 } d t {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { x } d x = \frac { 2 } { 3 } a ^ { 3 / 2 } d t{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad I = \int \frac { \frac { 2 } { 3 } a ^ { 3 / 2 } } { \sqrt { \left( a ^ { 3 / 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } - \left( a ^ { 3 / 2 } t \right) ^ { 2 } } } d t{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } a ^ { 3 / 2 } \int \frac { d t } { a ^ { 3 / 2 } \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } \int \frac { d t } { \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { 2 } { 3 } \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { t } { 1 } \right) + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { a ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { a } \right) + c \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x ^ { 3 / 2 } } { a ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right) + C \quad \left[ \text { put } t = \frac { x ^ { 3 / 2 } } { a ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 2 } { 3 } \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \sqrt { \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { a ^ { 3 } } } \right) + C{/tex}
solution: Given {tex}I= \int \frac { \sqrt { x } } { \sqrt { a ^ { 3 } - x ^ { 3 } } } d x = \int \frac { \sqrt { x } } { \sqrt { \left( a ^ { 3 / 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } - \left( x ^ { 3 / 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex} {tex}Let,\ \ x^{3/2} = a^{3/2} t{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { 3 } { 2 } x ^ { 1 / 2 } d x = a ^ { 3 / 2 } d t {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { x } d x = \frac { 2 } { 3 } a ^ { 3 / 2 } d t{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad I = \int \frac { \frac { 2 } { 3 } a ^ { 3 / 2 } } { \sqrt { \left( a ^ { 3 / 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } - \left( a ^ { 3 / 2 } t \right) ^ { 2 } } } d t{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } a ^ { 3 / 2 } \int \frac { d t } { a ^ { 3 / 2 } \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } \int \frac { d t } { \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { 2 } { 3 } \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { t } { 1 } \right) + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { a ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { a } \right) + c \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x ^ { 3 / 2 } } { a ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right) + C \quad \left[ \text { put } t = \frac { x ^ { 3 / 2 } } { a ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 2 } { 3 } \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \sqrt { \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { a ^ { 3 } } } \right) + C{/tex}
solution_html:
Given  {tex}I= \int \frac { \sqrt { x } } { \sqrt { a ^ { 3 } - x ^ { 3 } } } d x = \int \frac { \sqrt { x } } { \sqrt { \left( a ^ { 3 / 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } - \left( x ^ { 3 / 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex}
{tex}Let,\ \ x^{3/2} = a^{3/2} t{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { 3 } { 2 } x ^ { 1 / 2 } d x = a ^ { 3 / 2 } d t {/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { x } d x = \frac { 2 } { 3 } a ^ { 3 / 2 } d t{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad I = \int \frac { \frac { 2 } { 3 } a ^ { 3 / 2 } } { \sqrt { \left( a ^ { 3 / 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } - \left( a ^ { 3 / 2 } t \right) ^ { 2 } } } d t{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } a ^ { 3 / 2 } \int \frac { d t } { a ^ { 3 / 2 } \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}

Let,  {tex}I=\int \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 \right) } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 3 \right) \left( x ^ { 2 } - 5 \right) } d x{/tex}
Consider, {tex}\frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 \right) } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 3 \right) \left( x ^ { 2 } - 5 \right) }{/tex}
Put {tex}x^2 = y{/tex}
Then, {tex}\frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 \right) } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 3 \right) \left( x ^ { 2 } - 5 \right) } = \frac { ( y + 1 ) ( y + 4 ) } { ( y + 3 ) ( y - 5 ) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { y ^ { 2 } + 5 y + 4 } { y ^ { 2 } - 2 y - 15 }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { \left( y ^ { 2 } - 2 y - 15 \right) + ( 7 y + 19 ) } { y ^ { 2 } - 2 y - 15 }{/tex}
{tex}= 1 + \frac { 7 y + 19 } { y ^ { 2 } - 2 y - 15 } {/tex}

{tex}= 1 + \frac { 7 y + 19 } { ( y + 3 ) ( y - 5 ) }{/tex}...(i)
Using partial fractions,
{tex}\frac { 7 y + 19 } { ( y + 3 ) ( y - 5 ) } = \frac { A } { y + 3 } + \frac { B } { y - 5 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7y + 19 = A(y - 5) + B(y + 3){/tex}
Putting y = 5, 
{tex}\Rightarrow 35 + 19 = 8B{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow B = \frac { 54 } { 8 } = \frac { 27 } { 4 }{/tex}
Putting y = -3 
{tex}\Rightarrow - 21 + 19 = - 8 A \Rightarrow A = \frac { ( - 2 ) } { ( - 8 ) } = \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\frac { 7 y + 19 } { ( y + 3 ) ( y - 3 ) } = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \cdot \frac { 1 } { ( y + 3 ) } + \frac { 27 } { 4 } \cdot \frac { 1 } { ( y - 5 ) }{/tex}...(ii)
From Equations (i) and (ii), 
{tex}I = 1 + \frac { 7 y + 19 } { ( y + 3 ) ( y - 5 ) }= 1 + \frac { 1 } { 4 } \cdot \frac { 1 } { \left( y + 3 \right) }{/tex}{tex}+ \frac { 27 } { 4 } \frac { 1 } { \left( y- 5 \right) }{/tex}

{tex}\therefore I= \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 \right) } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 3 \right) \left( x ^ { 2 } - 5 \right) }= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \cdot \frac { 1 } { ( y + 3 ) } + \frac { 27 } { 4 } \cdot \frac { 1 } { ( y - 5 ) } = 1 + \frac { 1 } { 4 } \cdot \frac { 1 } { \left( x^2 + 3 \right) }+ \frac { 27 } { 4 } \frac { 1 } { \left( x^2- 5 \right) } \Big[ \because x^2=y\Big]{/tex}

{tex}= x + \frac { 1 } { 4 \sqrt { 3 } } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) + \frac { 27 } { 4 } \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { 5 } } \log \left| \frac { x - \sqrt { 5 } } { x + \sqrt { 5 } } \right| + C{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { x ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { a } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { a } \right) + C \text { and } \int \frac { d x } { x ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } }\right.{/tex}{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 a } \log \left| \frac { x - a } { x + a } \right| + C \Big]{/tex}

Given, {tex}I = \int ( 2 x + 5 ) \sqrt { 10 - 4 x - 3 x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
Let,  {tex}2 x + 5 = A \frac { d } { d x } \left( 10 - 4 x - 3 x ^ { 2 } \right) + B,{/tex}
where A and B are constants.
{tex}\Rightarrow  2x + 5 = A(-4 - 6x) + B{/tex}  ...(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow 2x + 5 = -6Ax + (B - 4A){/tex} 
Comparing the coefficient of x and constant term we get , 
{tex}\Rightarrow -6A = 2{/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow A = \frac { - 1 } { 3 }{/tex} 
{tex}B - 4A = 5{/tex}
 {tex} = 5 + 4 \left( \frac { - 1 } { 3 } \right) = \frac { 11 } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore\quad A = \frac { - 1 } { 3 } \text { and } B = \frac { 11 } { 3 }{/tex}
Thus,  {tex}(2x + 5) =\frac { - 1 } { 3 } ( - 4 - 6 x ) + \frac { 11 } { 3 }{/tex}[From Eq. (i)]
Now, {tex}I = \frac { - 1 } { 3 } \int ( - 4 - 6 x ) \sqrt { 10 - 4 x - 3 x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}{tex}+ \frac { 11 } { 3 } \int \sqrt { 10 - 4 x - 3 x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}let \ \ I = \frac { - 1 } { 3 } I _ { 1 } + \frac { 11 } { 3 } I _ { 2 }{/tex}   ...(ii)
Consider {tex}I _ { 1 } = \int ( - 4 - 6 x ) \sqrt { 10 - 4 x - 3 x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}Put, \ \ 10 - 4x - 3x^2 = t{/tex}

 {tex}\Rightarrow  (-4 - 6x)dx = dt{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I _ { 1 } = \int \sqrt { t } d t = \frac { 2 } { 3 } t ^ { 3 / 2 } + C _ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 2 } { 3 } \left( 10 - 4 x - 3 x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } + C _ { 1 }{/tex}...(iii)
consider  {tex}I_2 =\int \sqrt { 10 - 4 x - 3 x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { 3 } \int \sqrt { - \left( x ^ { 2 } + \frac { 4 } { 3 } x - \frac { 10 } { 3 } \right) } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { 3 } \sqrt { - \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \cdot \frac { 2 } { 3 } x + \left( \frac { 2 } { 3 } \right) ^ { 2 } - \left( \frac { 2 } { 3 } \right) ^ { 2 } - \frac { 10 } { 3 } \right) } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { 3 } \int \sqrt { \frac { 34 } { 9 } - \left( x + \frac { 2 } { 3 } \right) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}

\int \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{{e^{2x}}}}{2}\sin \left( {\frac{\pi }{4} + x} \right) - \frac{1}{2}\int {\mathop {{e^{2x}}}\limits_{II} } \mathop {\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{4} + x} \right)}\limits_I dx{/tex} By using integration by parts for second integral , we get {tex} = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right){/tex} - {tex} \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } - \int - \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } d x \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) - \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 4 } \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right){/tex} {tex} - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \int e ^ { 2 x } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I _ { 1 } = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 4 } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \right\} - \frac { 1 } { 4 } I _ { 1 }{/tex} [From eq.(ii)] {tex} \Rightarrow I _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 4 } I _ { 1 } = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 4 } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \right\}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 5 } { 4 } I _ { 1 } = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 4 } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \right\}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad I _ { 1 } = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 5 } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \right\}{/tex} From (i) and (ii) ,we get {tex} \therefore \quad I = \left[ I _ { 1 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi }{/tex} {tex} = \left[ \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 5 } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \right\} \right] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \left[ e ^ { 2 \pi } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \pi \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \pi \right) \right\}\right.{/tex} {tex}- e ^ { 0 } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + 0 \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + 0 \right) \right\} ]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \left[ e ^ { 2 \pi } \left\{ - 2 \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right\} - e ^ { 0 } \left\{ 2 \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right\} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \left[ e ^ { 2 \pi } \left\{ - 2 \times \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right\} - 1 \left\{ 2 \times \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right\} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \left[ e ^ { 2 \pi } \left\{ - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right\} - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right] {/tex} {tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 5 \sqrt { 2 } } \left[ e ^ { 2 \pi } + 1 \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore I = - \frac { 1 } { 5 \sqrt { 2 } } \left[ e ^ { 2 \pi } + 1 \right]{/tex} sq units.

\int \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{{e^{2x}}}}{2}\sin \left( {\frac{\pi }{4} + x} \right) - \frac{1}{2}\int {\mathop {{e^{2x}}}\limits_{II} } \mathop {\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{4} + x} \right)}\limits_I dx{/tex} By using integration by parts for second integral , we get {tex} = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right){/tex} - {tex} \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } - \int - \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } d x \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) - \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 4 } \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right){/tex} {tex} - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \int e ^ { 2 x } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I _ { 1 } = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 4 } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \right\} - \frac { 1 } { 4 } I _ { 1 }{/tex} [From eq.(ii)] {tex} \Rightarrow I _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 4 } I _ { 1 } = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 4 } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \right\}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 5 } { 4 } I _ { 1 } = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 4 } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \right\}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad I _ { 1 } = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 5 } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \right\}{/tex} From (i) and (ii) ,we get {tex} \therefore \quad I = \left[ I _ { 1 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi }{/tex} {tex} = \left[ \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 5 } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \right\} \right] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \left[ e ^ { 2 \pi } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \pi \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \pi \right) \right\}\right.{/tex} {tex}- e ^ { 0 } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + 0 \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + 0 \right) \right\} ]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \left[ e ^ { 2 \pi } \left\{ - 2 \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right\} - e ^ { 0 } \left\{ 2 \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right\} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \left[ e ^ { 2 \pi } \left\{ - 2 \times \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right\} - 1 \left\{ 2 \times \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right\} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \left[ e ^ { 2 \pi } \left\{ - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right\} - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right] {/tex} {tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 5 \sqrt { 2 } } \left[ e ^ { 2 \pi } + 1 \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore I = - \frac { 1 } { 5 \sqrt { 2 } } \left[ e ^ { 2 \pi } + 1 \right]{/tex} sq units.
solution_html:
According to the question , {tex}I=\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } e ^ { 2 x } \cdot \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) d x{/tex}...(i)
Consider , {tex}I_1= \int {\mathop {{e^{2x}}}\limits_{II} } \operatorname{s} \mathop {in\left( {\frac{\pi }{4} + x} \right)}\limits_I dx{/tex}..........(ii)
By using integration bi parts, we get 
= {tex}\sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \int e ^ { 2 x } d x{/tex}{tex}- \int \left\{ \frac { d } { d x } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \int e ^ { 2 x } d x \right\} d x{/tex}
{tex} = \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } - \int \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{{e^{2x}}}}{2}\sin \left( {\frac{\pi }{4} + x} \right) - \frac{1}{2}\int {\mathop {{e^{2x}}}\limits_{II} } \mathop {\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{4} + x} \right)}\limits_I dx{/tex}
By using integration by parts for second integral , we get 
{tex} = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right){/tex}- {tex} \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } - \int - \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } d x \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) - \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 4 } \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right){/tex}{tex} - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \int e ^ { 2 x } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) d x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow I _ { 1 } = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 4 } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \right\} - \frac { 1 } { 4 } I _ { 1 }{/tex} [From eq.(ii)]
{tex} \Rightarrow I _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 4 } I _ { 1 } = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 4 } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \right\}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 5 } { 4 } I _ { 1 } = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 4 } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \right\}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad I _ { 1 } = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 5 } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \right\}{/tex}
From (i) and (ii) ,we get
{tex} \therefore \quad I = \left[ I _ { 1 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi }{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 5 } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + x \right) \right\} \right] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \left[ e ^ { 2 \pi } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \pi \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \pi \right) \right\}\right.{/tex}{tex}- e ^ { 0 } \left\{ 2 \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + 0 \right) - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + 0 \right) \right\} ]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \left[ e ^ { 2 \pi } \left\{ - 2 \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right\} - e ^ { 0 } \left\{ 2 \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \cos \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right\} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \left[ e ^ { 2 \pi } \left\{ - 2 \times \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right\} - 1 \left\{ 2 \times \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right\} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \left[ e ^ { 2 \pi } \left\{ - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right\} - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right] {/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 5 \sqrt { 2 } } \left[ e ^ { 2 \pi } + 1 \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore I = - \frac { 1 } { 5 \sqrt { 2 } } \left[ e ^ { 2 \pi } + 1 \right]{/tex} sq units.

solution_html:
According to the question ,  {tex}I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { x } { 1 + \sin \alpha \sin x } d x{/tex}.........(i)
{tex} = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) } { 1 + \sin \alpha \sin ( \pi - x ) } d x{/tex} {tex}[\therefore \int_0^a f(x)= \int_0^a f(a-x)]{/tex}
{tex} = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) } { 1 + \sin \alpha \sin x } d x{/tex} {tex}[\because sin(\pi-x)=sinx]{/tex}............(ii)  
On adding Equation (i) and (ii), we get
{tex} 2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { \pi } { 1 + \sin \alpha \sin x } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \pi \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { d x } { 1 + \sin \alpha \left( \frac { 2 \tan x / 2 } { 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } x / 2 } \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \pi \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { \left( 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } x / 2 \right) d x } { \left( 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } x / 2 \right) + 2 \sin \alpha \tan x / 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \pi \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { \left( \sec ^ { 2 } x / 2 \right) d x } { \tan ^ { 2 } x / 2 + 2 \sin \alpha \cdot \tan x / 2 + 1 }{/tex}
Put tan{tex} \frac x2{/tex}= t 
{tex} \Rightarrow \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } \frac { 1 } { 2 } d x = d t{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } d x = 2 d t{/tex}
Lower limit , when x = 0, then t = 0
Upper limit , when x = {tex} \pi{/tex}, then {tex} t \rightarrow \infty{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad 2 I = \pi \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { 2 d t } { t ^ { 2 } + 2 \sin \alpha \cdot t + 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \pi \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { d t } { t ^ { 2 } + 2 \sin \alpha \cdot t + \sin ^ { 2 } \alpha + \cos ^ { 2 } \alpha }{/tex}
{tex}= \pi \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { d t } { ( t + \sin \alpha ) ^ { 2 } + ( \cos \alpha ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \pi } { \cos \alpha } \left[ \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { t + \sin \alpha } { \cos \alpha } \right) \right] _ { 0 } ^ { \infty }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \pi } { \cos \alpha } \left[ \tan ^ { - 1 } ( \infty ) - \tan ^ { - 1 } ( \tan \alpha ) \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { \pi } { \cos \alpha } \left[ \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \alpha \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore I = \frac { \pi } { \cos \alpha } \left[ \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \alpha \right]{/tex} sq units.

Let {tex}I = \int {\frac{{\sqrt x }}{{\sqrt {{a^3} - {x^3}} }}dx} = \int {\frac{{\sqrt x }}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {{a^{3/2}}} \right)}^2} - {{\left( {{x^{3/2}}} \right)}^2}} }}} {/tex}

Put {tex}{x^{3/2}} = t \Rightarrow \frac{3}{2}{x^{1/2}}dx = dt{/tex}

{tex}\therefore I = \frac{2}{3}\int {\frac{{dt}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {{a^{3/2}}} \right)}^2} - {t^2}} }} = \frac{2}{3}{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\frac{t}{{{a^{3/2}}}}} + C{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{2}{3}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\frac{{{x^{3/2}}}}{{{a^{3/2}}}} + C = \frac{2}{3}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\sqrt {\frac{{{x^3}}}{{{a^3}}}} + C{/tex}

question_html: Evaluate the integral: {tex}\int(3 x+1) \sqrt{4-3 x-2 x^{2}} d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate the integral: {tex}\int(3 x+1) \sqrt{4-3 x-2 x^{2}} d x{/tex}
explanation: Let the given integral be, I = {tex}\int(3 x+1) \sqrt{4-3 x-2 x^{2}} d x{/tex} Let (3x + 1) = {tex}A \frac{d}{d x}\left(4-3 x-2 x^{2}\right){/tex} + B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3x + 1) = A(-3 - 4x) + B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3x + 1) = -4Ax + (B - 3A) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 = -4A and (B - 3A) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = - {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} and B = - {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = - {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} {tex}\int(-3-4 x) \sqrt{4-3 x-2 x^{2}} d x{/tex} - {tex}\frac{5}{4} \int \sqrt{4-3 x-2 x^{2}} d x{/tex} Let I = - {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} I 1 - {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex} I 2 .....(i) Now, I 1 = {tex}\int(-3-4 x) \sqrt{4-3 x-2 x^{2}} d x{/tex} Let (4 - 3x - 2x 2 ) = t or (-3 - 4x)dx = dt {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 = {tex}\int \sqrt{t} d t{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{3} t^{\frac{3}{2}}+c_{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 = {tex}\frac{2}{3}\left(4-3 x-2 x^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} + c 1 Now, I 2 = {tex}\int \sqrt{4-3 x-2 x^{2}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \sqrt{2\left(2-\frac{3}{2} x-x^{2}\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2} \int \sqrt{\left(\frac{17}{4}-\frac{9}{4}-\frac{3}{2} x-x^{2}\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2} \int \sqrt{\left[\left(\frac{\sqrt{17}}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{9}{4}+\frac{3}{2} x+x^{2}\right)\right]} d x{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2} \int \sqrt{\left[\left(\frac{\sqrt{17}}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}\right]} d x{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2} \sin \left(\frac{x+\frac{3}{2}}{\frac{\sqrt{17}}{2}}\right){/tex} + c 2 = {tex}\sqrt{2} \sin \left(\frac{2 x+3}{\sqrt{17}}\right){/tex} + c 2 Using (i), we get I = {tex}-\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{2}{3}\left(4-3 x-2 x^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{5}{4} \times \sqrt{2} \sin \left(\frac{2 x+3}{\sqrt{17}}\right){/tex} + C {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}\left(4-3 x-2 x^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{5 \sqrt{2}}{4} \sin \left(\frac{2 x+3}{\sqrt{17}}\right){/tex} + C
solution: Let the given integral be, I = {tex}\int(3 x+1) \sqrt{4-3 x-2 x^{2}} d x{/tex} Let (3x + 1) = {tex}A \frac{d}{d x}\left(4-3 x-2 x^{2}\right){/tex} + B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3x + 1) = A(-3 - 4x) + B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3x + 1) = -4Ax + (B - 3A) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 = -4A and (B - 3A) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = - {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} and B = - {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = - {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} {tex}\int(-3-4 x) \sqrt{4-3 x-2 x^{2}} d x{/tex} - {tex}\frac{5}{4} \int \sqrt{4-3 x-2 x^{2}} d x{/tex} Let I = - {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} I 1 - {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex} I 2 .....(i) Now, I 1 = {tex}\int(-3-4 x) \sqrt{4-3 x-2 x^{2}} d x{/tex} Let (4 - 3x - 2x 2 ) = t or (-3 - 4x)dx = dt {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 = {tex}\int \sqrt{t} d t{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{3} t^{\frac{3}{2}}+c_{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 = {tex}\frac{2}{3}\left(4-3 x-2 x^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} + c 1 Now, I 2 = {tex}\int \sqrt{4-3 x-2 x^{2}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \sqrt{2\left(2-\frac{3}{2} x-x^{2}\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2} \int \sqrt{\left(\frac{17}{4}-\frac{9}{4}-\frac{3}{2} x-x^{2}\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2} \int \sqrt{\left[\left(\frac{\sqrt{17}}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{9}{4}+\frac{3}{2} x+x^{2}\right)\right]} d x{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2} \int \sqrt{\left[\left(\frac{\sqrt{17}}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}\right]} d x{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2} \sin \left(\frac{x+\frac{3}{2}}{\frac{\sqrt{17}}{2}}\right){/tex} + c 2 = {tex}\sqrt{2} \sin \left(\frac{2 x+3}{\sqrt{17}}\right){/tex} + c 2 Using (i), we get I = {tex}-\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{2}{3}\left(4-3 x-2 x^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{5}{4} \times \sqrt{2} \sin \left(\frac{2 x+3}{\sqrt{17}}\right){/tex} + C {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}\left(4-3 x-2 x^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{5 \sqrt{2}}{4} \sin \left(\frac{2 x+3}{\sqrt{17}}\right){/tex} + C
solution_html:
Let the given integral be,

I = {tex}\int(3 x+1) \sqrt{4-3 x-2 x^{2}} d x{/tex} 
Let (3x + 1) = {tex}A \frac{d}{d x}\left(4-3 x-2 x^{2}\right){/tex} + B
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3x + 1) = A(-3 - 4x) + B
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3x + 1) = -4Ax + (B - 3A)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 = -4A and (B - 3A) = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = -{tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} and B = -{tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = -{tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} {tex}\int(-3-4 x) \sqrt{4-3 x-2 x^{2}} d x{/tex} - {tex}\frac{5}{4} \int \sqrt{4-3 x-2 x^{2}} d x{/tex} 
Let I = - {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}I1 - {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex}I2 .....(i)
Now,
I1 = {tex}\int(-3-4 x) \sqrt{4-3 x-2 x^{2}} d x{/tex} 
Let (4 - 3x - 2x2) = t or (-3 - 4x)dx = dt 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I1 = {tex}\int \sqrt{t} d t{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{2}{3} t^{\frac{3}{2}}+c_{1}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I1 =  {tex}\frac{2}{3}\left(4-3 x-2 x^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} + c1 
Now, I2 = {tex}\int \sqrt{4-3 x-2 x^{2}} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int \sqrt{2\left(2-\frac{3}{2} x-x^{2}\right)} d x{/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt{2} \int \sqrt{\left(\frac{17}{4}-\frac{9}{4}-\frac{3}{2} x-x^{2}\right)} d x{/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt{2} \int \sqrt{\left[\left(\frac{\sqrt{17}}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{9}{4}+\frac{3}{2} x+x^{2}\right)\right]} d x{/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt{2} \int \sqrt{\left[\left(\frac{\sqrt{17}}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}\right]} d x{/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt{2} \sin \left(\frac{x+\frac{3}{2}}{\frac{\sqrt{17}}{2}}\right){/tex} + c2 
= {tex}\sqrt{2} \sin \left(\frac{2 x+3}{\sqrt{17}}\right){/tex} + c2 
Using (i), we get
I = {tex}-\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{2}{3}\left(4-3 x-2 x^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{5}{4} \times \sqrt{2} \sin \left(\frac{2 x+3}{\sqrt{17}}\right){/tex} + C
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}\left(4-3 x-2 x^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{5 \sqrt{2}}{4} \sin \left(\frac{2 x+3}{\sqrt{17}}\right){/tex} + C

explanation: I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{x(1+\log x)} d x{/tex} Put 1+log x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} dx = dt I = {tex}\int{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{t}dt{/tex} = log t +C = log (1+ log x) + C
solution: I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{x(1+\log x)} d x{/tex} Put 1+log x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} dx = dt I = {tex}\int{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{t}dt{/tex} = log t +C = log (1+ log x) + C
solution_html:

explanation: {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x+4 \sin ^{2} x} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sec ^{2} x}{1+4 \tan ^{2} x} d x{/tex} Let tan x = t, sec 2 x dx = dt {tex}I=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{1}{1+4 t^{2}} d t{/tex} {tex}=\left.\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} 2 t\right|_{0} ^{1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} 2{/tex}
solution: {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x+4 \sin ^{2} x} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sec ^{2} x}{1+4 \tan ^{2} x} d x{/tex} Let tan x = t, sec 2 x dx = dt {tex}I=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{1}{1+4 t^{2}} d t{/tex} {tex}=\left.\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} 2 t\right|_{0} ^{1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} 2{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x+4 \sin ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sec ^{2} x}{1+4 \tan ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
Let tan x = t, sec2x dx = dt

{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} 2{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let I = {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{3}{2}}|x \cos \pi x| \mathrm{d} x{/tex} , then we have {tex}I=\int \limits_{0}^{1/2}|x \cos \pi x| d x{/tex} {tex}I=\int \limits_{0}^{1/2} x \cos \pi x d x{/tex} {tex}+\int \limits_{1/2}^{3 / 2} x(-\cos \pi x) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = I 1 + I 2 ........ (i) Now, {tex}I_{1}=\int \limits_{0}^{1/2} x \cos \pi x d x{/tex} {tex}=\left[x \int \sin x \, d x\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}_{0}{/tex} {tex}-\int \limits_{0}^{\frac{1}{2}}(\cos \pi x d x) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{1}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} x & \frac{\sin \pi x}{\pi} \end{array}\right]_{0}^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{\pi^{2}}[\cos \pi x)]_{0} ^{1/2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{1}=\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\frac{1}{2} \sin \frac {\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{\pi^{2}}\left[\cos \frac{\pi}{2}-\cos x\right]{/tex} {tex}I_{1}=\frac{1}{2 \pi}-\frac{1}{\pi^{2}}{/tex} Also we have {tex}I_{2}=-\int \limits_{1 / 2}^{3/2} x \cos \pi x{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{\pi}[x \sin \pi x]_{1/2}^{3 / 2}-\frac{1}{\pi^{2}}[\cos \pi x]_{1/2}^{3/2}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{\pi}\left[ \frac {3}{2} \sin \frac{3 \pi}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \sin \frac {\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{\pi^{2}}\left[cos \frac{3\pi}{2}- \frac {\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{x}\left[-\frac{3}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\right]-\frac{1}{\pi^{2}} [0]= \frac {2}{\pi}{/tex} {tex}\therefore I=\frac{1}{2 \pi}- \frac {1}{\pi^2}+\frac {2}{\pi}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5}{2 \pi}-\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex}
solution: Let I = {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{3}{2}}|x \cos \pi x| \mathrm{d} x{/tex} , then we have {tex}I=\int \limits_{0}^{1/2}|x \cos \pi x| d x{/tex} {tex}I=\int \limits_{0}^{1/2} x \cos \pi x d x{/tex} {tex}+\int \limits_{1/2}^{3 / 2} x(-\cos \pi x) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = I 1 + I 2 ........ (i) Now, {tex}I_{1}=\int \limits_{0}^{1/2} x \cos \pi x d x{/tex} {tex}=\left[x \int \sin x \, d x\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}_{0}{/tex} {tex}-\int \limits_{0}^{\frac{1}{2}}(\cos \pi x d x) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{1}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} x & \frac{\sin \pi x}{\pi} \end{array}\right]_{0}^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{\pi^{2}}[\cos \pi x)]_{0} ^{1/2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{1}=\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\frac{1}{2} \sin \frac {\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{\pi^{2}}\left[\cos \frac{\pi}{2}-\cos x\right]{/tex} {tex}I_{1}=\frac{1}{2 \pi}-\frac{1}{\pi^{2}}{/tex} Also we have {tex}I_{2}=-\int \limits_{1 / 2}^{3/2} x \cos \pi x{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{\pi}[x \sin \pi x]_{1/2}^{3 / 2}-\frac{1}{\pi^{2}}[\cos \pi x]_{1/2}^{3/2}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{\pi}\left[ \frac {3}{2} \sin \frac{3 \pi}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \sin \frac {\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{\pi^{2}}\left[cos \frac{3\pi}{2}- \frac {\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{x}\left[-\frac{3}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\right]-\frac{1}{\pi^{2}} [0]= \frac {2}{\pi}{/tex} {tex}\therefore I=\frac{1}{2 \pi}- \frac {1}{\pi^2}+\frac {2}{\pi}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5}{2 \pi}-\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{3}{2}}|x \cos \pi x| \mathrm{d} x{/tex}, then we have
 {tex}I=\int \limits_{0}^{1/2}|x \cos \pi x| d x{/tex}
{tex}I=\int \limits_{0}^{1/2} x \cos \pi x d x{/tex} {tex}+\int \limits_{1/2}^{3 / 2} x(-\cos \pi x) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = I1 + I2 ........ (i)
Now, {tex}I_{1}=\int \limits_{0}^{1/2} x \cos \pi x d x{/tex}{tex}=\left[x \int \sin x \, d x\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}_{0}{/tex}{tex}-\int \limits_{0}^{\frac{1}{2}}(\cos \pi x d x) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{1}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} x & \frac{\sin \pi x}{\pi} \end{array}\right]_{0}^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}{tex}+\frac{1}{\pi^{2}}[\cos \pi x)]_{0} ^{1/2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{1}=\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\frac{1}{2} \sin \frac {\pi}{2}\right]{/tex}{tex}+\frac{1}{\pi^{2}}\left[\cos \frac{\pi}{2}-\cos x\right]{/tex}
{tex}I_{1}=\frac{1}{2 \pi}-\frac{1}{\pi^{2}}{/tex}
Also we have
{tex}I_{2}=-\int \limits_{1 / 2}^{3/2} x \cos \pi x{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{\pi}[x \sin \pi x]_{1/2}^{3 / 2}-\frac{1}{\pi^{2}}[\cos \pi x]_{1/2}^{3/2}{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{1}{\pi}\left[ \frac {3}{2} \sin \frac{3 \pi}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \sin \frac {\pi}{2}\right]{/tex}{tex}-\frac{1}{\pi^{2}}\left[cos \frac{3\pi}{2}- \frac {\pi}{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{1}{x}\left[-\frac{3}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\right]-\frac{1}{\pi^{2}} [0]= \frac {2}{\pi}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore I=\frac{1}{2 \pi}- \frac {1}{\pi^2}+\frac {2}{\pi}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5}{2 \pi}-\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex}

explanation: {tex}\int x \sin ^{-1} x d x{/tex} {tex}=\sin ^{-1} x \frac{x^2}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{x^2}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{x^2 \cdot \sin ^{-1} x}{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2}\left\{\int \sqrt{1-x^2} d x-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} d x\right\}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{x^2 \cdot \sin ^{-1} x}{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2}\left\{\frac{x \sqrt{1-x^2}}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1} x-\sin ^{-1} x\right\}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{x^2 \cdot \sin ^{-1} x}{2}+\frac{x \sqrt{1-x^2}}{4}{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{4} \cdot \sin ^{-1} x+C{/tex}
solution: {tex}\int x \sin ^{-1} x d x{/tex} {tex}=\sin ^{-1} x \frac{x^2}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{x^2}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{x^2 \cdot \sin ^{-1} x}{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2}\left\{\int \sqrt{1-x^2} d x-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} d x\right\}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{x^2 \cdot \sin ^{-1} x}{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2}\left\{\frac{x \sqrt{1-x^2}}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1} x-\sin ^{-1} x\right\}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{x^2 \cdot \sin ^{-1} x}{2}+\frac{x \sqrt{1-x^2}}{4}{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{4} \cdot \sin ^{-1} x+C{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\int x \sin ^{-1} x d x{/tex} {tex}=\sin ^{-1} x \frac{x^2}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{x^2}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{x^2 \cdot \sin ^{-1} x}{2}{/tex}{tex}+\frac{1}{2}\left\{\int \sqrt{1-x^2} d x-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} d x\right\}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{x^2 \cdot \sin ^{-1} x}{2}{/tex}{tex}+\frac{1}{2}\left\{\frac{x \sqrt{1-x^2}}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1} x-\sin ^{-1} x\right\}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{x^2 \cdot \sin ^{-1} x}{2}+\frac{x \sqrt{1-x^2}}{4}{/tex}{tex}-\frac{1}{4} \cdot \sin ^{-1} x+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int \frac { \sin ( x - a ) } { \sin ( x + a ) } d x.{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int \frac { \sin ( x - a ) } { \sin ( x + a ) } d x.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, {tex}I = \int \frac { \sin ( x - a ) } { \sin ( x + a ) } d x{/tex} Put {tex}x + a = t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = dt {tex}\therefore I = \int \frac { \sin ( t - a - a ) } { \sin t } d t = \int \frac { \sin ( t - 2 a ) } { \sin t } d t{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { \sin t \cos 2 a - \cos t \sin 2 a } { \sin t } d t{/tex} [ {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}sin (A-B) = sin Acos B - cos Asin B{/tex} ] {tex}= \int \cos 2 a d t - \int \sin 2 a \cdot \cot t d t{/tex} = {tex}cos2a [t]-sin2a [log|sint|]+ C_1{/tex} = {tex}(x + a)cos2a-sin2a log |sin(x + a)|+ C_1{/tex} [put t = x + a) = {tex}xcos2a-sin2a log|sin(x + a) |+ C1{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}I = \int \frac { \sin ( x - a ) } { \sin ( x + a ) } d x{/tex} Put {tex}x + a = t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = dt {tex}\therefore I = \int \frac { \sin ( t - a - a ) } { \sin t } d t = \int \frac { \sin ( t - 2 a ) } { \sin t } d t{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { \sin t \cos 2 a - \cos t \sin 2 a } { \sin t } d t{/tex} [ {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}sin (A-B) = sin Acos B - cos Asin B{/tex} ] {tex}= \int \cos 2 a d t - \int \sin 2 a \cdot \cot t d t{/tex} = {tex}cos2a [t]-sin2a [log|sint|]+ C_1{/tex} = {tex}(x + a)cos2a-sin2a log |sin(x + a)|+ C_1{/tex} [put t = x + a) = {tex}xcos2a-sin2a log|sin(x + a) |+ C1{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,  {tex}I = \int \frac { \sin ( x - a ) } { \sin ( x + a ) } d x{/tex}
Put {tex}x + a = t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = dt
{tex}\therefore I = \int \frac { \sin ( t - a - a ) } { \sin t } d t = \int \frac { \sin ( t - 2 a ) } { \sin t } d t{/tex}
 {tex}= \int \frac { \sin t \cos 2 a - \cos t \sin 2 a } { \sin t } d t{/tex}
[{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}sin (A-B) = sin Acos B - cos Asin B{/tex}]
{tex}= \int \cos 2 a d t - \int \sin 2 a \cdot \cot t d t{/tex}
= {tex}cos2a [t]-sin2a [log|sint|]+ C_1{/tex}
= {tex}(x + a)cos2a-sin2a log |sin(x + a)|+ C_1{/tex}
[put t = x + a)
= {tex}xcos2a-sin2a log|sin(x + a) |+ C1{/tex}

question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { x \tan x } { \sec x \cdot \text{cosec} x } d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { x \tan x } { \sec x \cdot \text{cosec} x } d x{/tex}
explanation: According to the question , {tex}I =\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { x \tan x } { \sec x \cdot \text{cosec} x } d x{/tex} ........(i) we know that , {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( x ) d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( a - x ) d x{/tex} we get {tex}I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) \tan ( \pi - x ) } { \sec ( \pi - x ) \text{cosec} ( \pi - x ) } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) ( - \tan x ) } { - \sec x \text{cosec} x } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) \tan x } { \sec x \text{cosec} x } d x{/tex} ......(ii) On adding Equations (i) and (ii) we get, {tex}2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { \pi \tan x } { \sec x \text{cosec} x } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \frac { \pi } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { \sin ^ { 2 } x ( \cos x ) } { ( \cos x ) } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \sin ^ { 2 } x d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \left( \frac { 1 - \cos 2 x } { 2 } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 } \left[ x - \frac { \sin 2 x } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \frac { \pi } { 4 } \left[ \pi - \frac { \sin 2 \pi } { 2 } - 0 + \frac { \sin ( 0 ) } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \frac { \pi } { 4 } [ \pi - 0 ] {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}
solution: According to the question , {tex}I =\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { x \tan x } { \sec x \cdot \text{cosec} x } d x{/tex} ........(i) we know that , {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( x ) d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( a - x ) d x{/tex} we get {tex}I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) \tan ( \pi - x ) } { \sec ( \pi - x ) \text{cosec} ( \pi - x ) } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) ( - \tan x ) } { - \sec x \text{cosec} x } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) \tan x } { \sec x \text{cosec} x } d x{/tex} ......(ii) On adding Equations (i) and (ii) we get, {tex}2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { \pi \tan x } { \sec x \text{cosec} x } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \frac { \pi } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { \sin ^ { 2 } x ( \cos x ) } { ( \cos x ) } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \sin ^ { 2 } x d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \left( \frac { 1 - \cos 2 x } { 2 } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 } \left[ x - \frac { \sin 2 x } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \frac { \pi } { 4 } \left[ \pi - \frac { \sin 2 \pi } { 2 } - 0 + \frac { \sin ( 0 ) } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \frac { \pi } { 4 } [ \pi - 0 ] {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question , {tex}I =\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { x \tan x } { \sec x \cdot \text{cosec} x } d x{/tex} ........(i)
we know that ,  {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( x ) d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( a - x ) d x{/tex}
we get
{tex}I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) \tan ( \pi - x ) } { \sec ( \pi - x ) \text{cosec} ( \pi - x ) } d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) ( - \tan x ) } { - \sec x \text{cosec} x } d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) \tan x } { \sec x \text{cosec} x } d x{/tex}......(ii)
On adding Equations (i) and (ii) we get,
{tex}2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { \pi \tan x } { \sec x \text{cosec} x } d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = \frac { \pi } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { \sin ^ { 2 } x ( \cos x ) } { ( \cos x ) } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \pi } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \sin ^ { 2 } x d x{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \pi } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \left( \frac { 1 - \cos 2 x } { 2 } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 } \left[ x - \frac { \sin 2 x } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = \frac { \pi } { 4 } \left[ \pi - \frac { \sin 2 \pi } { 2 } - 0 + \frac { \sin ( 0 ) } { 2 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = \frac { \pi } { 4 } [ \pi - 0 ] {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: According to the question, {tex}I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log ( 1 + \tan x ) d x{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log \left[ 1 + \tan \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } - x \right) \right] d x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( x ) d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( a - x ) d x \right]{/tex} {tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log \left( 1 + \frac { \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \tan x } { 1 + \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } \tan x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \tan ( x - y ) = \frac { \tan x - \tan y } { 1 + \tan x \tan y } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log \left( 1 + \frac { 1 - \tan x } { 1 + \tan x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log \left( \frac { 2 } { 1 + \tan x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } ( \log 2 ) d x - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log ( 1 + \tan x ) d x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log \frac { m } { n } = \log m - \log n \right]{/tex} {tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } ( \log 2 ) d x - I{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log 2 d x = \log 2 [ x ] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } = \log 2 \left[ \frac { \pi } { 4 } - 0 \right]{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad I = \frac { \pi } { 8 } \log 2{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log ( 1 + \tan x ) d x{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log \left[ 1 + \tan \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } - x \right) \right] d x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( x ) d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( a - x ) d x \right]{/tex} {tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log \left( 1 + \frac { \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \tan x } { 1 + \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } \tan x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \tan ( x - y ) = \frac { \tan x - \tan y } { 1 + \tan x \tan y } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log \left( 1 + \frac { 1 - \tan x } { 1 + \tan x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log \left( \frac { 2 } { 1 + \tan x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } ( \log 2 ) d x - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log ( 1 + \tan x ) d x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log \frac { m } { n } = \log m - \log n \right]{/tex} {tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } ( \log 2 ) d x - I{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log 2 d x = \log 2 [ x ] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } = \log 2 \left[ \frac { \pi } { 4 } - 0 \right]{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad I = \frac { \pi } { 8 } \log 2{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log ( 1 + \tan x ) d x{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log \left[ 1 + \tan \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } - x \right) \right] d x{/tex}
{tex}\left[ \because \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( x ) d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( a - x ) d x \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log \left( 1 + \frac { \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \tan x } { 1 + \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } \tan x } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\left[ \because \tan ( x - y ) = \frac { \tan x - \tan y } { 1 + \tan x \tan y } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log \left( 1 + \frac { 1 - \tan x } { 1 + \tan x } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log \left( \frac { 2 } { 1 + \tan x } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } ( \log 2 ) d x - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log ( 1 + \tan x ) d x{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \log \frac { m } { n } = \log m - \log n \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } ( \log 2 ) d x - I{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \log 2 d x = \log 2 [ x ] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } = \log 2 \left[ \frac { \pi } { 4 } - 0 \right]{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad I = \frac { \pi } { 8 } \log 2{/tex}

explanation: According to the question , {tex} I = \int \left[ \log ( \log x ) + \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } \right] d x{/tex} By Using integration by parts for first integral, we get {tex} = \int {\log } (\mathop {\mathop{\rm l}\nolimits} \limits_Iogx) \cdot \mathop 1\limits_{II} dx + \int {\frac{1}{{{{(\log x)}^2}}}} dx{/tex} {tex}I = log (log x) \int 1 d x - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d x } \log ( \log x )\Big]\Big[ \int 1 d x \right] d x + \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= x log (log x) - \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) } d x + \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x {/tex} {tex}= x log (log x)-\int (\mathop {\mathop{\rm\frac { 1}{logx}}\nolimits} \limits_I ) \cdot \mathop 1\limits_{II} dx + \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x {/tex} By Using integration by parts for second integral, we get {tex}= x log (log x) -\frac{x}{logx}+ \int [\frac {d}{dx}(\frac {1}{logx})]. (\int 1dx)dx+ \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x {/tex} {tex}= x log (log x) -\frac{x}{logx}- \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x \frac {1}{x} . x+ \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x {/tex} {tex}= x log (log x) -\frac{x}{logx}- \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x + \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x {/tex} {tex} = x \log ( \log x )-\frac{x}{logx}+ C{/tex} {tex}\therefore I = x \log ( \log x ) -\frac{x}{logx}+ C{/tex}
solution: According to the question , {tex} I = \int \left[ \log ( \log x ) + \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } \right] d x{/tex} By Using integration by parts for first integral, we get {tex} = \int {\log } (\mathop {\mathop{\rm l}\nolimits} \limits_Iogx) \cdot \mathop 1\limits_{II} dx + \int {\frac{1}{{{{(\log x)}^2}}}} dx{/tex} {tex}I = log (log x) \int 1 d x - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d x } \log ( \log x )\Big]\Big[ \int 1 d x \right] d x + \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= x log (log x) - \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) } d x + \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x {/tex} {tex}= x log (log x)-\int (\mathop {\mathop{\rm\frac { 1}{logx}}\nolimits} \limits_I ) \cdot \mathop 1\limits_{II} dx + \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x {/tex} By Using integration by parts for second integral, we get {tex}= x log (log x) -\frac{x}{logx}+ \int [\frac {d}{dx}(\frac {1}{logx})]. (\int 1dx)dx+ \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x {/tex} {tex}= x log (log x) -\frac{x}{logx}- \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x \frac {1}{x} . x+ \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x {/tex} {tex}= x log (log x) -\frac{x}{logx}- \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x + \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x {/tex} {tex} = x \log ( \log x )-\frac{x}{logx}+ C{/tex} {tex}\therefore I = x \log ( \log x ) -\frac{x}{logx}+ C{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question ,  {tex} I = \int \left[ \log ( \log x ) + \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } \right] d x{/tex}
By Using integration by parts for first integral, we get
{tex} = \int {\log } (\mathop {\mathop{\rm l}\nolimits} \limits_Iogx) \cdot \mathop 1\limits_{II} dx + \int {\frac{1}{{{{(\log x)}^2}}}} dx{/tex}
{tex}I = log (log x) \int 1 d x - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d x } \log ( \log x )\Big]\Big[ \int 1 d x \right] d x + \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}

{tex}= x log (log x) - \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) } d x + \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x {/tex}

{tex}= x log (log x)-\int (\mathop {\mathop{\rm\frac { 1}{logx}}\nolimits} \limits_I ) \cdot \mathop 1\limits_{II} dx + \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x {/tex}

By Using integration by parts for second integral, we get

{tex}= x log (log x) -\frac{x}{logx}+ \int [\frac {d}{dx}(\frac {1}{logx})]. (\int 1dx)dx+ \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x {/tex}
{tex}= x log (log x) -\frac{x}{logx}- \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x \frac {1}{x} . x+ \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x {/tex}

{tex}= x log (log x) -\frac{x}{logx}- \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x + \int \frac { 1 } { ( \log x ) ^ { 2 } } d x {/tex}
{tex} = x \log ( \log x )-\frac{x}{logx}+ C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore I = x \log ( \log x ) -\frac{x}{logx}+ C{/tex}

2 + 2 } d t{/tex} {tex} = 2 \int \frac { \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { t ^ { 2 } } \right) } { \left( t - \frac { 1 } { t } \right) ^ { 2 } + 2 } d t{/tex} Again, put {tex} t-\frac { 1 } { t } = y \Rightarrow \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { t ^ { 2 } } \right) d t = d y{/tex} {tex} \therefore I = 2 \int \frac { d y } { y ^ { 2 } + ( \sqrt { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } {/tex} {tex} I = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { \left( t - \frac { 1 } { t } \right) } { \sqrt { 2 } } + C \quad \left[ \text { put } y = t - \frac { 1 } { t } \right]{/tex} {tex} = \sqrt { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { t ^ { 2 } -
