solution_html:
Let  {tex}I = \int [ \sqrt { \cot x } + \sqrt { \tan x } ] d x{/tex}

{tex}=\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{tanx}} + \sqrt{tanx} \ [\because cotx = \frac {1}{tanx}]{/tex}
{tex} = \int \sqrt { \tan x } \Bigg[1 +\frac {1}{\Big(\sqrt { \tan x }\Big)^2}\Bigg] d x{/tex}
put {tex} tan x = t^2 \implies sec^2 x dx = 2t dt{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad d x = \frac { 2 t } { sec^2x}dt{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \quad d x = \frac { 2 t } { 1 +tan^2x }dt{/tex}{tex}[ \because 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } x = \sec ^ { 2 } x]{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \quad d x = \frac { 2 t } { 1 + (t ^ { 2 })^2 }{/tex}{tex}[ tan x = t^2 ]{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \quad d x = \frac { 2 t } { 1 + t ^ { 4 } }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad I = \int t \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { t ^ { 2 } } \right) \frac { 2 t } { \left( 1 + t ^ { 4 } \right) } d t [\because tanx =t^2 \implies \sqrt {tanx} = t]{/tex}
{tex} = 2 \int \frac { t ^ { 2 } + 1 } { t ^ { 4 } + 1 } d t{/tex}
On dividing numerator and denominator by {tex}t^2{/tex} , we get
{tex} I = 2 \int \frac { \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { t ^ { 2 } } \right) } { \left( t ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { t ^ { 2 } } \right) } d t {/tex}

{tex}= 2 \int \frac { 1 + \frac { 1 } { t ^ { 2 } } } { t ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { t ^ { 2 } } - 2 + 2 } d t{/tex}
{tex} = 2 \int \frac { \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { t ^ { 2 } } \right) } { \left( t - \frac { 1 } { t } \right) ^ { 2 } + 2 } d t{/tex}
Again, put  {tex} t-\frac { 1 } { t } = y \Rightarrow \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { t ^ { 2 } } \right) d t = d y{/tex}
{tex} \therefore I = 2 \int \frac { d y } { y ^ { 2 } + ( \sqrt { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } {/tex}

{tex} I = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { y } { \sqrt { 2 } } + C{/tex}{tex} \left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { 1 } { a } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { a } \right) + c \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { \left( t - \frac { 1 } { t } \right) } { \sqrt { 2 } } + C \quad \left[ \text { put } y = t - \frac { 1 } { t } \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { t ^ { 2 } - 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } t } \right) + C{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \tan x - 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \tan x } } \right) + C{/tex}   {tex}[Put \ t^2 = tan x]{/tex}

{tex}I = \sqrt { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \tan x - 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \tan x } } \right) + C{/tex}

{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and C = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x-1)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{x-1}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} Using values of A ,B ,C we have, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x-1)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times \frac{1}{x-1}-\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{x-1}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x-1)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2(x-1)}-\frac{x}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} Therefore integrating we have, I = {tex}\int \frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x-1)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int\left[\frac{1}{2(x-1)}-\frac{x}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}+\frac{1}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}\right] d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{2(x-1)}-\int \frac{x d x}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} {tex}+\int \frac{d x}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{(x-1)}-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{x d x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{(x-1)}-\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{2 x d x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log |x-1|-\frac{1}{4} \log \left(x^{2}+1\right){/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} x+C{/tex} .

1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = Ax 2 + A + Bx 2 - Bx + Cx - C Comparing the coefficients, we have, A+B = 0, - B + C = 1 and A - C = 0 Solving the equation, we get, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} , B = - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and C = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x-1)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{x-1}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} Using values of A ,B ,C we have, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x-1)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times \frac{1}{x-1}-\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{x-1}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x-1)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2(x-1)}-\frac{x}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} Therefore integrating we have, I = {tex}\int \frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x-1)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int\left[\frac{1}{2(x-1)}-\frac{x}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}+\frac{1}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}\right] d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{2(x-1)}-\int \frac{x d x}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} {tex}+\int \frac{d x}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{(x-1)}-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{x d x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{(x-1)}-\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{2 x d x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log |x-1|-\frac{1}{4} \log \left(x^{2}+1\right){/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} x+C{/tex} .
solution_html:
Consider the integral
I = {tex}\int \frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x-1)} d x{/tex}
Now let us separate the fraction {tex}\frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x-1)}{/tex}
though partial fraction
{tex}\frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x-1)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{x-1}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x-1)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A\left(x^{2}+1\right)+(B x+C)(x-1)}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x-1)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = A(x2 + 1) + (Bx + C) (x - 1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = Ax2 + A + Bx2 - Bx + Cx - C
Comparing the coefficients, we have,
A+B = 0, - B + C = 1 and A - C = 0
Solving the equation, we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, B = - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and C = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x-1)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{x-1}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+1}{/tex}

 Using values of A ,B ,C we have,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x-1)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times \frac{1}{x-1}-\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{x-1}{x^{2}+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x-1)}{/tex}  = {tex}\frac{1}{2(x-1)}-\frac{x}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex}{tex}+\frac{1}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex}
Therefore integrating we have,
I = {tex}\int \frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x-1)} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int\left[\frac{1}{2(x-1)}-\frac{x}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}+\frac{1}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}\right] d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int \frac{d x}{2(x-1)}-\int \frac{x d x}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} {tex}+\int \frac{d x}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{(x-1)}-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{x d x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{(x-1)}-\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{2 x d x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log |x-1|-\frac{1}{4} \log \left(x^{2}+1\right){/tex}{tex}+\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} x+C{/tex}.

explanation: According to the question, {tex}I = \int {\mathop {{e^{2x}}}\limits_{II} } \sin (\mathop {3x}\limits_I + 1)dx{/tex} ...(i) {tex}= \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) \int e ^ { 2 x } d x - \int \left\{ \frac { d } { d x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) \int e ^ { 2 x } d x \right] d x{/tex} [Using integration by parts] {tex}= \frac { \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) \cdot e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } - \int 3 \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) \cdot \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } d x{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{{e^{2x}}\sin (3x + 1)}}{2} - \frac{3}{2}\int {\mathop {{e^{2x}}}\limits_{II} } \mathop {\cos }\limits_I (3x + 1)dx{/tex} {tex}= \frac { e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left[ \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) \int e ^ { 2 x } d x\right.{/tex} {tex}- \int \left\{ \frac { d } { d x } \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) \int e ^ { 2 x } d x \right\} d x{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex}= \frac { e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left[ \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) \cdot \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 }\right.{/tex} {tex}- \int - 3 \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } d x ] + C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 4 } e ^ { 2 x } \cos ( 3 x + 1 ){/tex} {tex}- \frac { 9 } { 4 } \int e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) d x + C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 4 } e ^ { 2 x } \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) - \frac { 9 } { 4 } I + C _ { 1 }{/tex} [from Equation (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 13 } { 4 } I = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 2 } - \frac { 3 e ^ { 2 x } \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 4 } + C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = \frac { 2 e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 13 } - \frac { 3 e ^ { 2 x } \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 13 } + C{/tex} where, {tex}C = \frac { 4 C _ { 1 } } { 13 }.{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}I = \int {\mathop {{e^{2x}}}\limits_{II} } \sin (\mathop {3x}\limits_I + 1)dx{/tex} ...(i) {tex}= \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) \int e ^ { 2 x } d x - \int \left\{ \frac { d } { d x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) \int e ^ { 2 x } d x \right] d x{/tex} [Using integration by parts] {tex}= \frac { \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) \cdot e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } - \int 3 \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) \cdot \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } d x{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{{e^{2x}}\sin (3x + 1)}}{2} - \frac{3}{2}\int {\mathop {{e^{2x}}}\limits_{II} } \mathop {\cos }\limits_I (3x + 1)dx{/tex} {tex}= \frac { e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left[ \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) \int e ^ { 2 x } d x\right.{/tex} {tex}- \int \left\{ \frac { d } { d x } \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) \int e ^ { 2 x } d x \right\} d x{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex}= \frac { e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left[ \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) \cdot \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 }\right.{/tex} {tex}- \int - 3 \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } d x ] + C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 4 } e ^ { 2 x } \cos ( 3 x + 1 ){/tex} {tex}- \frac { 9 } { 4 } \int e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) d x + C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 4 } e ^ { 2 x } \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) - \frac { 9 } { 4 } I + C _ { 1 }{/tex} [from Equation (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 13 } { 4 } I = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 2 } - \frac { 3 e ^ { 2 x } \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 4 } + C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = \frac { 2 e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 13 } - \frac { 3 e ^ { 2 x } \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 13 } + C{/tex} where, {tex}C = \frac { 4 C _ { 1 } } { 13 }.{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}I = \int {\mathop {{e^{2x}}}\limits_{II} } \sin (\mathop {3x}\limits_I + 1)dx{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}= \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) \int e ^ { 2 x } d x - \int \left\{ \frac { d } { d x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) \int e ^ { 2 x } d x \right] d x{/tex}
[Using integration by parts]
{tex}= \frac { \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) \cdot e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } - \int 3 \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) \cdot \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } d x{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{{e^{2x}}\sin (3x + 1)}}{2} - \frac{3}{2}\int {\mathop {{e^{2x}}}\limits_{II} } \mathop {\cos }\limits_I (3x + 1)dx{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left[ \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) \int e ^ { 2 x } d x\right.{/tex}
{tex}- \int \left\{ \frac { d } { d x } \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) \int e ^ { 2 x } d x \right\} d x{/tex}
[using integration by parts]
{tex}= \frac { e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \left[ \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) \cdot \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 }\right.{/tex}
{tex}- \int - 3 \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } d x ] + C _ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 4 } e ^ { 2 x } \cos ( 3 x + 1 ){/tex}
{tex}- \frac { 9 } { 4 } \int e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) d x + C _ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 4 } e ^ { 2 x } \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) - \frac { 9 } { 4 } I + C _ { 1 }{/tex}
[from Equation (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 13 } { 4 } I = \frac { e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 2 } - \frac { 3 e ^ { 2 x } \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 4 } + C _ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I = \frac { 2 e ^ { 2 x } \sin ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 13 } - \frac { 3 e ^ { 2 x } \cos ( 3 x + 1 ) } { 13 } + C{/tex}
where, {tex}C = \frac { 4 C _ { 1 } } { 13 }.{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate {tex} \int _ { - \pi / 2 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x.{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate {tex} \int _ { - \pi / 2 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, {tex} I=\int _ { - \pi / 2 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x{/tex} {tex} = \int _ { - \pi / 2 } ^ { 0 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x{/tex} In first integral, put x = -t {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} dx = -dt In Upper limit when x = 0, {tex}then\ t = 0{/tex} In Lower limit when x = {tex} - \frac { \pi } { 2 } , \text { then } t = \frac { \pi } { 2 }.{/tex} {tex} \therefore I = \int _ { \pi / 2 } ^ { 0 } \frac { \cos t } { 1 + e ^ { - t } } ( - d t ) + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x{/tex} {tex} = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos t } { 1 + \frac { 1 } { e ^ { t } } } d t + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because - \int _ { a } ^ { 0 } f ( x ) d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( x ) d x \right]{/tex} {tex} = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { e ^ { t } \cos t } { e ^ { t } + 1 } d t + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x{/tex} {tex} = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { e ^ { x } \cos x } { e ^ { x } + 1 } d x + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \int _ { a } ^ { b } f ( x ) d x = \int _ { a } ^ { b } f ( t ) d t \right]{/tex} {tex} = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \left( 1 + e ^ { x } \right) \cos x } { \left( e ^ { x } + 1 \right) } d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \cos x d x{/tex} {tex} = [ \sin x ] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } = \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 0 = 1{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex} I=\int _ { - \pi / 2 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x{/tex} {tex} = \int _ { - \pi / 2 } ^ { 0 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x{/tex} In first integral, put x = -t {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} dx = -dt In Upper limit when x = 0, {tex}then\ t = 0{/tex} In Lower limit when x = {tex} - \frac { \pi } { 2 } , \text { then } t = \frac { \pi } { 2 }.{/tex} {tex} \therefore I = \int _ { \pi / 2 } ^ { 0 } \frac { \cos t } { 1 + e ^ { - t } } ( - d t ) + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x{/tex} {tex} = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos t } { 1 + \frac { 1 } { e ^ { t } } } d t + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because - \int _ { a } ^ { 0 } f ( x ) d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( x ) d x \right]{/tex} {tex} = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { e ^ { t } \cos t } { e ^ { t } + 1 } d t + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x{/tex} {tex} = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { e ^ { x } \cos x } { e ^ { x } + 1 } d x + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \int _ { a } ^ { b } f ( x ) d x = \int _ { a } ^ { b } f ( t ) d t \right]{/tex} {tex} = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \left( 1 + e ^ { x } \right) \cos x } { \left( e ^ { x } + 1 \right) } d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \cos x d x{/tex} {tex} = [ \sin x ] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } = \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 0 = 1{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex} I=\int _ { - \pi / 2 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x{/tex}
{tex} = \int _ { - \pi / 2 } ^ { 0 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x{/tex}
In first integral, put x = -t {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} dx = -dt
In Upper limit when x = 0, {tex}then\ t = 0{/tex}
In Lower limit when x = {tex} - \frac { \pi } { 2 } , \text { then } t = \frac { \pi } { 2 }.{/tex}
{tex} \therefore I = \int _ { \pi / 2 } ^ { 0 } \frac { \cos t } { 1 + e ^ { - t } } ( - d t ) + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x{/tex}
{tex} = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos t } { 1 + \frac { 1 } { e ^ { t } } } d t + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x{/tex}
{tex} \left[ \because - \int _ { a } ^ { 0 } f ( x ) d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( x ) d x \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { e ^ { t } \cos t } { e ^ { t } + 1 } d t + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x{/tex}
{tex} = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { e ^ { x } \cos x } { e ^ { x } + 1 } d x + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x } { 1 + e ^ { x } } d x{/tex}
{tex} \left[ \because \int _ { a } ^ { b } f ( x ) d x = \int _ { a } ^ { b } f ( t ) d t \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \left( 1 + e ^ { x } \right) \cos x } { \left( e ^ { x } + 1 \right) } d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \cos x d x{/tex}
{tex} = [ \sin x ] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } = \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 0 = 1{/tex}

question_html: Find {tex}\int \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) e ^ { x } } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x.{/tex}
question_text: Find {tex}\int \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) e ^ { x } } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, {tex}I = \int e ^ { x } \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 + 2 x - 2 x \right) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } - 2 x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x [\therefore (a+b)^2 = a^2 +b^2+2ab]{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( 1 - \frac { 2 x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } d x - 2 \int e ^ { x } \cdot \frac { x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { x } - 2 \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x + 1 - 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { x } - 2 \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x+1 } { ( x + 1 )^2 } + \frac { ( - 1 ) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { x } - 2 \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) } + \frac { ( - 1 ) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex} Consider {tex}f ( x ) = \frac { 1 } { x + 1 },{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}f ^ { \prime } ( x ) = \frac { ( - 1 ) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Thus, the above integral is of the form {tex}\because \int e ^ { x } f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C {/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = e ^ { x } - 2 e ^ { x }\Big[ \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) } \Big]+ C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x + 1 - 2 } { x + 1 } \right) + C {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x - 1 } { x + 1 } \right) + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \int e ^ { x } \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x = e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x - 1 } { x + 1 } \right) + C{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}I = \int e ^ { x } \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 + 2 x - 2 x \right) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } - 2 x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x [\therefore (a+b)^2 = a^2 +b^2+2ab]{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( 1 - \frac { 2 x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } d x - 2 \int e ^ { x } \cdot \frac { x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { x } - 2 \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x + 1 - 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { x } - 2 \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x+1 } { ( x + 1 )^2 } + \frac { ( - 1 ) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { x } - 2 \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) } + \frac { ( - 1 ) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex} Consider {tex}f ( x ) = \frac { 1 } { x + 1 },{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}f ^ { \prime } ( x ) = \frac { ( - 1 ) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Thus, the above integral is of the form {tex}\because \int e ^ { x } f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C {/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = e ^ { x } - 2 e ^ { x }\Big[ \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) } \Big]+ C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x + 1 - 2 } { x + 1 } \right) + C {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x - 1 } { x + 1 } \right) + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \int e ^ { x } \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x = e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x - 1 } { x + 1 } \right) + C{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}I = \int e ^ { x } \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int e ^ { x } \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 + 2 x - 2 x \right) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } - 2 x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x [\therefore (a+b)^2 = a^2 +b^2+2ab]{/tex}
{tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( 1 - \frac { 2 x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int e ^ { x } d x - 2 \int e ^ { x } \cdot \frac { x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= e ^ { x } - 2 \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x + 1 - 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex}

{tex}= e ^ { x } - 2 \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x+1 } { ( x + 1 )^2 } + \frac { ( - 1 ) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}= e ^ { x } - 2 \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) } + \frac { ( - 1 ) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex}
Consider {tex}f ( x ) = \frac { 1 } { x + 1 },{/tex}{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}f ^ { \prime } ( x ) = \frac { ( - 1 ) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
Thus, the above integral is of the form
{tex}\because \int e ^ { x } f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C {/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I = e ^ { x } - 2 e ^ { x }\Big[ \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) } \Big]+ C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x + 1 - 2 } { x + 1 } \right) + C {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x - 1 } { x + 1 } \right) + C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \int e ^ { x } \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x = e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x - 1 } { x + 1 } \right) + C{/tex}

1 / 2 } + t ^ { 3 / 2 } \right) d t{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ 2 t ^ { 1 / 2 } + \frac { 2 } { 5 } t ^ { 5 / 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } {/tex} {tex}={2\over2} \left[ t ^ { 1 / 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 5 } t ^ { 5 / 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}= \left[ t ^ { 1 / 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 5 } t ^ { 5 / 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 }{/tex} {tex} = ( 1 ) ^ { 1 / 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 5 } ( 1 ) ^ { 5 / 2 } - 0 = 1 + \frac { 1 } { 5 } = \frac { 6 } { 5 }{/tex}

4 } x \sin ^ { 1 / 2 } x } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { \sec ^ { 4 } x } { \cos ^ { - 1 / 2 } x \sin ^ { 1 / 2 } x } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } x . sec^2x } { \sin ^ { 1 / 2 } x \over \cos ^ { 1 / 2 }x} d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } x \left( 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } x \right) } { \tan ^ { 1 / 2 } x } d x{/tex} [ {tex}sec^2x - solution_html:
According to the question, {tex} I= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { d x } { \cos ^ { 3 } x \sqrt { 2 \sin 2 x } }{/tex}
{tex} = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { d x } { \cos ^ { 3 } x \sqrt { 2.2 \sin x \cos x } }{/tex}[{tex} \because{/tex} {tex}sin 2x = 2 sin\ x\ cos\ x{/tex}]

{tex} = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { d x } { \cos ^ { 3 } x \sqrt { 4 \sin x \cos x } }{/tex}

{tex} = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { d x } { (\cos ^ { 3 } x) (2 )\sqrt { \sin x \cos x } }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { d x } { \cos ^ { 3 } x \cos ^ { 1 / 2 } x \sin ^ { 1 / 2 } x }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { d x } { \cos ^ { 7 / 2 } x \sin ^ { 1 / 2 } x }{/tex}
 Dividing numerator and denominator by {tex}cos^4x{/tex}

{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { {1\over cos^4x }d x }{ { \cos ^ { 7 / 2 } x \sin ^ { 1 / 2 } x }\over cos^4x}{ [\because{1\over cosx }= sec x]}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { \sec ^ { 4 } x } { \cos ^ { \frac { 7 } { 2 } - 4 } x \sin ^ { 1 / 2 } x } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { \sec ^ { 4 } x } { \cos ^ { - 1 / 2 } x \sin ^ { 1 / 2 } x } d x{/tex}

{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } x . sec^2x } { \sin ^ { 1 / 2 } x \over \cos ^ { 1 / 2 }x} d x{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } x \left( 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } x \right) } { \tan ^ { 1 / 2 } x } d x{/tex}[ {tex}sec^2x - tan^2x = 1\implies sec^2 = 1+tan^2x \ and \ {{\ sinx\over cosx} = tanx}{/tex}]
Put {tex}tanx = t{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}sec^2x dx = dt{/tex}
Lower Limit, when x = 0, then t = tan 0 = 0
Upper Limit, when x = {tex} \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}, then t = tan{tex} \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} = 1
{tex}\therefore \quad I = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \left( \frac { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } { t ^ { 1 / 2 } } \right) d t{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \quad I = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { 1 } { t ^ { 1 / 2 } } d t +\frac { 1 } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { t ^ { 1 / 2 } } d t{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \left( t ^ { - 1 / 2 } + t ^ { 3 / 2 } \right) d t{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ 2 t ^ { 1 / 2 } + \frac { 2 } { 5 } t ^ { 5 / 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } {/tex}

{tex}={2\over2} \left[ t ^ { 1 / 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 5 } t ^ { 5 / 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 }{/tex}

{tex}= \left[ t ^ { 1 / 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 5 } t ^ { 5 / 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex} = ( 1 ) ^ { 1 / 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 5 } ( 1 ) ^ { 5 / 2 } - 0 = 1 + \frac { 1 } { 5 } = \frac { 6 } { 5 }{/tex}

\frac { 3 } { 2 } \times 2 \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \sin ^ { - 1}{ ( x ) } + C _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { f ^ { \prime } ( x ) } { \sqrt { f ( x ) } } d x = 2 \sqrt { f ( x ) } + C \right]{/tex} {tex}= - 3 \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \sin ^ { - 1 } ( x ) + C _ { 2 }{/tex} ...(iii) From Equations (i), (ii) and (iii), we get {tex}I = ( - 1 ) \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \frac { x } { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sin ^ { - x ^ { 2 } } + C{/tex} [ {tex}where \ \ C = -C_1 - C_2{/tex} ]
solution_html:
Let {tex}I={/tex} {tex}\int \frac { x ^ { 2 } - 3 x + 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}

{tex} = ( - 1 ) \int \frac { - x ^ { 2 } + 3 x - 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex}

{tex} = ( - 1 ) \int \frac { - x ^ { 2 } + 3 x - 1+1-1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= ( - 1 ) \int \frac { 1 - x ^ { 2 } + 3 x - 2 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= ( - 1 ) \int \left[ \frac { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } + \frac { 3 x - 2 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } \right] d x{/tex}
{tex}= ( - 1 ) \int \left[ \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } + \int \frac { 3 x - 2 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } \right] d x{/tex}
{tex}= ( - 1 ) \left[ \int \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } d x + \int \frac { 3 x - 2 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } d x \right]{/tex}
{tex}= (-1) (I_1 + I_2){/tex} .......(i)
consider, {tex}I _ { 1 } = \int \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ x \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } + \sin ^ { - 1 } ( x ) \right] + C _ { 1 }{/tex}...(ii){tex}\left[ \because \int \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ x \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } + a ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { a } \right) \right] + C \right]{/tex}
consider {tex}I _ { 2 } = \int \frac { 3 x - 2 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { 3 x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } d x - 2 \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \int \frac { - 2 x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } d x - 2 \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \times 2 \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \sin ^ { - 1}{ ( x ) } + C _ { 2 }{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { f ^ { \prime } ( x ) } { \sqrt { f ( x ) } } d x = 2 \sqrt { f ( x ) } + C \right]{/tex}
{tex}= - 3 \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \sin ^ { - 1 } ( x ) + C _ { 2 }{/tex}...(iii)
From Equations (i), (ii) and (iii), we get
{tex}I = ( - 1 ) \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \frac { x } { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sin ^ { - 1 } ( x ) }+ C _ { 1 } { - 2 \sin ^ { - 1 } ( x ) - 3 \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } +C _ { 2 } } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}I= \frac { 3 } { 2 } \sin ^ { - 1 } ( x ) - \frac { x } { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } + 3 \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } + C{/tex}  [ {tex}where \ \ C = -C_1 - C_2{/tex}]

explanation: Let {tex}I = \int {\frac{{\log |x|}}{{{{(x + 1)}^2}}}} dx = \mathop {\int {\log } }\limits |x| \cdot \mathop {\frac{1}{{{{(x + 1)}^2}}}}\limits_{} dx{/tex} On applying integration by parts, we get, {tex}I = \log | x | \cdot \int \frac { d x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } - \int \frac { d } { d x } ( \log | x | ) \cdot \left( \int \frac { d x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex} {tex} \left[ {\int {\mathop u\limits_I .} \mathop v\limits_{II} dx = u\int v dx - \int {\left( {\frac{d}{{dx}}(u)\int v dx} \right)} dx} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \log | x | \frac { ( - 1 ) } { x + 1 } + \int \frac { 1 } { x ( x + 1 ) } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - \log | x | } { x + 1 } + I _ { 1 }{/tex} (say)..........(i) Consider, {tex}I _ { 1 } = \int \frac { d x } { x ( x + 1 ) }{/tex} Now, by using partial fraction method, Let {tex}\frac { 1 } { x ( x + 1 ) } = \frac { A } { x } + \frac { B } { x + 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A(x + 1) + Bx Therefore,on putting x = 0, we get A = 1 and again putting x = -1, we get B= -1 {tex}\therefore \quad I _ { 1 } = \int \left( \frac { 1 } { x } - \frac { 1 } { x + 1 } \right) d x = \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x - \int \frac { d x } { x + 1 }{/tex} = log |x| - log |x + 1| + C ...(ii) Therefore, from Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}I = \frac { - \log | x | } { x + 1 } + \log | x | - \log | x + 1 | + C{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - \log | x | } { x + 1 } + \log \left| \frac { x } { x + 1 } \right| + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \frac { m } { n } \right]{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I = \int {\frac{{\log |x|}}{{{{(x + 1)}^2}}}} dx = \mathop {\int {\log } }\limits |x| \cdot \mathop {\frac{1}{{{{(x + 1)}^2}}}}\limits_{} dx{/tex} On applying integration by parts, we get, {tex}I = \log | x | \cdot \int \frac { d x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } - \int \frac { d } { d x } ( \log | x | ) \cdot \left( \int \frac { d x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex} {tex} \left[ {\int {\mathop u\limits_I .} \mathop v\limits_{II} dx = u\int v dx - \int {\left( {\frac{d}{{dx}}(u)\int v dx} \right)} dx} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \log | x | \frac { ( - 1 ) } { x + 1 } + \int \frac { 1 } { x ( x + 1 ) } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - \log | x | } { x + 1 } + I _ { 1 }{/tex} (say)..........(i) Consider, {tex}I _ { 1 } = \int \frac { d x } { x ( x + 1 ) }{/tex} Now, by using partial fraction method, Let {tex}\frac { 1 } { x ( x + 1 ) } = \frac { A } { x } + \frac { B } { x + 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A(x + 1) + Bx Therefore,on putting x = 0, we get A = 1 and again putting x = -1, we get B= -1 {tex}\therefore \quad I _ { 1 } = \int \left( \frac { 1 } { x } - \frac { 1 } { x + 1 } \right) d x = \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x - \int \frac { d x } { x + 1 }{/tex} = log |x| - log |x + 1| + C ...(ii) Therefore, from Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}I = \frac { - \log | x | } { x + 1 } + \log | x | - \log | x + 1 | + C{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - \log | x | } { x + 1 } + \log \left| \frac { x } { x + 1 } \right| + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \frac { m } { n } \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I = \int {\frac{{\log |x|}}{{{{(x + 1)}^2}}}} dx = \mathop {\int {\log } }\limits |x| \cdot \mathop {\frac{1}{{{{(x + 1)}^2}}}}\limits_{} dx{/tex}
On applying integration by parts, we get,
{tex}I = \log | x | \cdot \int \frac { d x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } - \int \frac { d } { d x } ( \log | x | ) \cdot \left( \int \frac { d x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex} \left[ {\int {\mathop u\limits_I .} \mathop v\limits_{II} dx = u\int v dx - \int {\left( {\frac{d}{{dx}}(u)\int v dx} \right)} dx} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \log | x | \frac { ( - 1 ) } { x + 1 } + \int \frac { 1 } { x ( x + 1 ) } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { - \log | x | } { x + 1 } + I _ { 1 }{/tex} (say)..........(i)
Consider, {tex}I _ { 1 } = \int \frac { d x } { x ( x + 1 ) }{/tex}
Now, by using partial fraction method,
Let {tex}\frac { 1 } { x ( x + 1 ) } = \frac { A } { x } + \frac { B } { x + 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A(x + 1) + Bx
Therefore,on putting x = 0, we get A = 1
and again putting x = -1, we get B= -1
{tex}\therefore \quad I _ { 1 } = \int \left( \frac { 1 } { x } - \frac { 1 } { x + 1 } \right) d x = \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x - \int \frac { d x } { x + 1 }{/tex}
= log |x| - log |x + 1| + C ...(ii)
Therefore, from Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}I = \frac { - \log | x | } { x + 1 } + \log | x | - \log | x + 1 | + C{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { - \log | x | } { x + 1 } + \log \left| \frac { x } { x + 1 } \right| + C{/tex}
{tex}\left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \frac { m } { n } \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the integral: {tex}\int \frac{x+2}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5} d x{/tex}
question_text: Find the integral: {tex}\int \frac{x+2}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5} d x{/tex}
explanation: Let, I = {tex}∫\frac{(x+2)}{(2x^2+6x+5)}dx{/tex} we express {tex}x+2=\mathrm{A} \frac{d}{d x}\left(2 x^{2}+6 x+5\right)+\mathrm{B}{/tex} = A(4x + 6) + B Equating the coefficients of x and the constant terms from both sides, we get 4A = 1 and 6A + B = 2 or {tex}A=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} and {tex}B=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{x+2}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5}=\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{4 x+6}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5} d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{I}_{1}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex} .....(i) In I 1 , put 2x 2 + 6x + 5 = t, so that (4x + 6) dx = dt Therefore, {tex}I_{1}=\int \frac{d t}{t}=\log |t|+C_{1}{/tex} = log |2x 2 + 6x + 5| + C .....(ii) and {tex}I_{2}=\int \frac{d x}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+3 x+\frac{5}{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} Put {tex}x+\frac{3}{2}=t{/tex} , so that dx = dt, we get {tex}I_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{t^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{1}{2 \times \frac{1}{2}} \tan ^{-1} 2 t+C_{2}{/tex} {tex}\tan ^{-1} 2\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)+\mathrm{C}_{2}=\tan ^{-1}(2 x+3)+\mathrm{C}_{2}{/tex} ......(iii) Using (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get {tex}\int \frac{x+2}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5} d x=\frac{1}{4} \log \left|2 x^{2}+6 x+5\right|+\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1}(2 x+3)+\mathrm{C}{/tex} where, {tex}C=\frac{C_{1}}{4}+\frac{C_{2}}{2}{/tex}
solution: Let, I = {tex}∫\frac{(x+2)}{(2x^2+6x+5)}dx{/tex} we express {tex}x+2=\mathrm{A} \frac{d}{d x}\left(2 x^{2}+6 x+5\right)+\mathrm{B}{/tex} = A(4x + 6) + B Equating the coefficients of x and the constant terms from both sides, we get 4A = 1 and 6A + B = 2 or {tex}A=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} and {tex}B=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{x+2}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5}=\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{4 x+6}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5} d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{I}_{1}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex} .....(i) In I 1 , put 2x 2 + 6x + 5 = t, so that (4x + 6) dx = dt Therefore, {tex}I_{1}=\int \frac{d t}{t}=\log |t|+C_{1}{/tex} = log |2x 2 + 6x + 5| + C .....(ii) and {tex}I_{2}=\int \frac{d x}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+3 x+\frac{5}{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} Put {tex}x+\frac{3}{2}=t{/tex} , so that dx = dt, we get {tex}I_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{t^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{1}{2 \times \frac{1}{2}} \tan ^{-1} 2 t+C_{2}{/tex} {tex}\tan ^{-1} 2\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)+\mathrm{C}_{2}=\tan ^{-1}(2 x+3)+\mathrm{C}_{2}{/tex} ......(iii) Using (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get {tex}\int \frac{x+2}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5} d x=\frac{1}{4} \log \left|2 x^{2}+6 x+5\right|+\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1}(2 x+3)+\mathrm{C}{/tex} where, {tex}C=\frac{C_{1}}{4}+\frac{C_{2}}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let, I = {tex}∫\frac{(x+2)}{(2x^2+6x+5)}dx{/tex}
we express
{tex}x+2=\mathrm{A} \frac{d}{d x}\left(2 x^{2}+6 x+5\right)+\mathrm{B}{/tex} = A(4x + 6) + B
Equating the coefficients of x and the constant terms from both sides, we get
4A = 1 and 6A + B = 2 or {tex}A=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} and {tex}B=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{x+2}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5}=\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{4 x+6}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5} d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{I}_{1}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex}  .....(i)
In I1, put 2x2 + 6x + 5 = t, so that (4x + 6) dx = dt
Therefore, {tex}I_{1}=\int \frac{d t}{t}=\log |t|+C_{1}{/tex} 
= log |2x2 + 6x +  5| + C .....(ii)
and {tex}I_{2}=\int \frac{d x}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+3 x+\frac{5}{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} 
Put {tex}x+\frac{3}{2}=t{/tex}, so that dx = dt, we get
{tex}I_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{t^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{1}{2 \times \frac{1}{2}} \tan ^{-1} 2 t+C_{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\tan ^{-1} 2\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)+\mathrm{C}_{2}=\tan ^{-1}(2 x+3)+\mathrm{C}_{2}{/tex}  ......(iii)
Using (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get
{tex}\int \frac{x+2}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5} d x=\frac{1}{4} \log \left|2 x^{2}+6 x+5\right|+\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1}(2 x+3)+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
where, {tex}C=\frac{C_{1}}{4}+\frac{C_{2}}{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let the given integral be, I = {tex}\int \frac{\log x}{(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex} Now solving by parts. Let the first function be (log x) and second function be {tex}\frac{1}{(x+1)^{2}}{/tex} First we find the integral of the second function, i.e., {tex}\int \frac{1}{(x+1)^{2}}{/tex} Put t = (x + 1). Then dt = dx Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{1}{(x+1)^{2}} d x=\int{/tex} t -2 dt {tex}=-\frac{1}{t}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{1+x}{/tex} Hence, using integration by parts, we get {tex}\int \frac{\log x}{(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex} = (log x) {tex}\int \frac{1}{(x+1)^{2}} d x-\int\left[\left(\frac{d(\log x)}{d x}\right) \int \frac{1}{(x+1)^{2}} d x\right] d x{/tex} = (log x) {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{1+x}\right)-\int\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\left(-\frac{1}{1+x}\right){/tex} dx {tex}=-\frac{\log x}{1+x}+\int\left(\frac{1}{x^{2}+x}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{\log x}{1+x}+\int \frac{1}{x^{2}+x+\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{4}} d x{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{\log x}{1+x}+\int \frac{1}{\left(x+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{\log x}{1+x}+\frac{1}{2 \times \frac{1}{2}} \log \left|\frac{x+\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2}}{x+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}}\right|+c{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{\log x}{1+x}+\log \left|\frac{x}{x+1}\right|+c{/tex} Hence, {tex}\int \frac{\log x}{(x+1)^{2}} d x=-\frac{\log x}{1+x}+\log \left|\frac{x}{x+1}\right|+c{/tex}
solution: Let the given integral be, I = {tex}\int \frac{\log x}{(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex} Now solving by parts. Let the first function be (log x) and second function be {tex}\frac{1}{(x+1)^{2}}{/tex} First we find the integral of the second function, i.e., {tex}\int \frac{1}{(x+1)^{2}}{/tex} Put t = (x + 1). Then dt = dx Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{1}{(x+1)^{2}} d x=\int{/tex} t -2 dt {tex}=-\frac{1}{t}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{1+x}{/tex} Hence, using integration by parts, we get {tex}\int \frac{\log x}{(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex} = (log x) {tex}\int \frac{1}{(x+1)^{2}} d x-\int\left[\left(\frac{d(\log x)}{d x}\right) \int \frac{1}{(x+1)^{2}} d x\right] d x{/tex} = (log x) {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{1+x}\right)-\int\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\left(-\frac{1}{1+x}\right){/tex} dx {tex}=-\frac{\log x}{1+x}+\int\left(\frac{1}{x^{2}+x}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{\log x}{1+x}+\int \frac{1}{x^{2}+x+\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{4}} d x{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{\log x}{1+x}+\int \frac{1}{\left(x+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{\log x}{1+x}+\frac{1}{2 \times \frac{1}{2}} \log \left|\frac{x+\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2}}{x+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}}\right|+c{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{\log x}{1+x}+\log \left|\frac{x}{x+1}\right|+c{/tex} Hence, {tex}\int \frac{\log x}{(x+1)^{2}} d x=-\frac{\log x}{1+x}+\log \left|\frac{x}{x+1}\right|+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the given integral be,

 I = {tex}\int \frac{\log x}{(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex}

Now solving by parts.
Let the first function be (log x) and second function be {tex}\frac{1}{(x+1)^{2}}{/tex}
First we find the integral of the second function, i.e., {tex}\int \frac{1}{(x+1)^{2}}{/tex}
Put t = (x + 1). Then dt = dx
Therefore,
{tex}\int \frac{1}{(x+1)^{2}} d x=\int{/tex}t-2 dt
{tex}=-\frac{1}{t}{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{1}{1+x}{/tex}
Hence, using integration by parts, we get
{tex}\int \frac{\log x}{(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex} = (log x) {tex}\int \frac{1}{(x+1)^{2}} d x-\int\left[\left(\frac{d(\log x)}{d x}\right) \int \frac{1}{(x+1)^{2}} d x\right] d x{/tex}
= (log x) {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{1+x}\right)-\int\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\left(-\frac{1}{1+x}\right){/tex} dx
{tex}=-\frac{\log x}{1+x}+\int\left(\frac{1}{x^{2}+x}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{\log x}{1+x}+\int \frac{1}{x^{2}+x+\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{4}} d x{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{\log x}{1+x}+\int \frac{1}{\left(x+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}} d x{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{\log x}{1+x}+\frac{1}{2 \times \frac{1}{2}} \log \left|\frac{x+\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2}}{x+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}}\right|+c{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{\log x}{1+x}+\log \left|\frac{x}{x+1}\right|+c{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\int \frac{\log x}{(x+1)^{2}} d x=-\frac{\log x}{1+x}+\log \left|\frac{x}{x+1}\right|+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let, I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\sin x+\sin 2 x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\sin x+2 \sin x \cos x} d x{/tex} Multiplying and dividing by sin x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{\sin ^{2} x+2 \sin ^{2} x \cdot \cos x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{1-\cos ^{2} x+2\left(1-\cos ^{2} x\right) \cos x} d x{/tex} Let cos x = t, - sinx dx = dt {tex}\because{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{d t}{\left(t^{2}-1\right)+2\left(t^{2}-1\right) t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{d t}{\left(t^{2}-1\right)(1+2 t)}{/tex} Now solving by using partial fractions, Let, {tex}\frac{1}{\left(t^{2}-1\right)(1+2 t)}={/tex} {tex}\frac{A}{t-1}+\frac{B}{1+t}+\frac{C}{1+2 t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A(1 + t) (1 + 2t) + B (t - 1) (1 + 2t) + C(t 2 - 1) For t = 1, A = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} For t = -1,B = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} For t = - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} , C = {tex}-\frac{4}{3}{/tex} So, I = {tex}=\frac{1}{6} \int \frac{d t}{t-1}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{t+1}{/tex} {tex}-\frac{4}{3} \int \frac{d t}{1+2 t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{6} \log |t-1|+\frac{1}{2} \log |1+t|{/tex} {tex}-\frac{2}{3} \log |1+2 \mathrm{t}|+\mathrm{c}{/tex} So, I = {tex}\frac{1}{6} \log |\cos x-1|+\frac{1}{2} \log |1+\cos x|{/tex} {tex}-\frac{2}{3} \log |1+2 \cos x|+c{/tex} .
solution: Let, I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\sin x+\sin 2 x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\sin x+2 \sin x \cos x} d x{/tex} Multiplying and dividing by sin x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{\sin ^{2} x+2 \sin ^{2} x \cdot \cos x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{1-\cos ^{2} x+2\left(1-\cos ^{2} x\right) \cos x} d x{/tex} Let cos x = t, - sinx dx = dt {tex}\because{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{d t}{\left(t^{2}-1\right)+2\left(t^{2}-1\right) t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{d t}{\left(t^{2}-1\right)(1+2 t)}{/tex} Now solving by using partial fractions, Let, {tex}\frac{1}{\left(t^{2}-1\right)(1+2 t)}={/tex} {tex}\frac{A}{t-1}+\frac{B}{1+t}+\frac{C}{1+2 t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A(1 + t) (1 + 2t) + B (t - 1) (1 + 2t) + C(t 2 - 1) For t = 1, A = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} For t = -1,B = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} For t = - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} , C = {tex}-\frac{4}{3}{/tex} So, I = {tex}=\frac{1}{6} \int \frac{d t}{t-1}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{t+1}{/tex} {tex}-\frac{4}{3} \int \frac{d t}{1+2 t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{6} \log |t-1|+\frac{1}{2} \log |1+t|{/tex} {tex}-\frac{2}{3} \log |1+2 \mathrm{t}|+\mathrm{c}{/tex} So, I = {tex}\frac{1}{6} \log |\cos x-1|+\frac{1}{2} \log |1+\cos x|{/tex} {tex}-\frac{2}{3} \log |1+2 \cos x|+c{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let, I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\sin x+\sin 2 x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\sin x+2 \sin x \cos x} d x{/tex}
Multiplying and dividing by sin x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{\sin ^{2} x+2 \sin ^{2} x \cdot \cos x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{1-\cos ^{2} x+2\left(1-\cos ^{2} x\right) \cos x} d x{/tex}
Let cos x = t, - sinx dx = dt
{tex}\because{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{d t}{\left(t^{2}-1\right)+2\left(t^{2}-1\right) t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{d t}{\left(t^{2}-1\right)(1+2 t)}{/tex}
Now solving by using partial fractions,

Let, {tex}\frac{1}{\left(t^{2}-1\right)(1+2 t)}={/tex} {tex}\frac{A}{t-1}+\frac{B}{1+t}+\frac{C}{1+2 t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A(1 + t)  (1 + 2t) + B (t - 1) (1 + 2t) + C(t2 - 1)
For t = 1, A = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex}
For t = -1,B = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
For t = -{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, C = {tex}-\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
So, I = {tex}=\frac{1}{6} \int \frac{d t}{t-1}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{t+1}{/tex}{tex}-\frac{4}{3} \int \frac{d t}{1+2 t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{6} \log |t-1|+\frac{1}{2} \log |1+t|{/tex}{tex}-\frac{2}{3} \log |1+2 \mathrm{t}|+\mathrm{c}{/tex}
So, I = {tex}\frac{1}{6} \log |\cos x-1|+\frac{1}{2} \log |1+\cos x|{/tex}{tex}-\frac{2}{3} \log |1+2 \cos x|+c{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}-3 x+1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x{/tex} , Also let x = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} . Then, dx = d (sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) = cos {tex}\theta{/tex} d {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I=\int \frac{\sin ^{2} \theta-3 \sin \theta+1}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} d {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} (sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} - 3 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} + 1) d {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int\left(\frac{1-\cos 2 \theta}{2}-3 \sin \theta+1\right) d \theta=\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} (3 - 6 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} ) d {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{1}{2}\left(3 \theta+6 \cos \theta-\frac{1}{2} \sin 2 \theta\right)+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{1}{2}\{3 \theta+6 \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2}} \theta-\sin \theta \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}\}+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{1}{2}\left\{3 \sin ^{-1} x+6 \sqrt{1-x^{2}}-x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}\right\}+C{/tex}
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}-3 x+1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x{/tex} , Also let x = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} . Then, dx = d (sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) = cos {tex}\theta{/tex} d {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I=\int \frac{\sin ^{2} \theta-3 \sin \theta+1}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} d {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} (sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} - 3 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} + 1) d {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int\left(\frac{1-\cos 2 \theta}{2}-3 \sin \theta+1\right) d \theta=\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} (3 - 6 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} ) d {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{1}{2}\left(3 \theta+6 \cos \theta-\frac{1}{2} \sin 2 \theta\right)+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{1}{2}\{3 \theta+6 \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2}} \theta-\sin \theta \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}\}+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{1}{2}\left\{3 \sin ^{-1} x+6 \sqrt{1-x^{2}}-x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}\right\}+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}-3 x+1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x{/tex},
 Also let x = sin{tex}\theta{/tex}. Then, dx = d (sin {tex}\theta{/tex}) = cos{tex}\theta{/tex} d{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I=\int \frac{\sin ^{2} \theta-3 \sin \theta+1}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}}{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex} d{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\int{/tex} (sin2 {tex}\theta{/tex} - 3 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} + 1) d{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int\left(\frac{1-\cos 2 \theta}{2}-3 \sin \theta+1\right) d \theta=\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} (3 - 6 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - cos 2{tex}\theta{/tex}) d{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{1}{2}\left(3 \theta+6 \cos \theta-\frac{1}{2} \sin 2 \theta\right)+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{1}{2}\{3 \theta+6 \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2}} \theta-\sin \theta \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}\}+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{1}{2}\left\{3 \sin ^{-1} x+6 \sqrt{1-x^{2}}-x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}\right\}+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: {tex}\text { Let } \sin ^{-1} x=\theta \text { or, } x=\sin \theta . \text { Then, } d x=d(\sin \theta)=\cos \theta d \theta{/tex} {tex}\text { Now, } \quad x=0 \Rightarrow \sin \theta=0 \Rightarrow \theta=0 \text { and } x=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I=\int_{0}^{1 / \sqrt{2}} \frac{\sin ^{-1} x}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)^{3/2}} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\theta}{\cos ^{3} \theta} \cos \theta d \theta={/tex} {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \theta \sec ^{2} \theta d \theta{/tex} Now using integratio by parts. {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\left[\begin{array}{ll} \theta & \tan \theta \end{array}\right]_{0}^{\pi / 4}+[\log \cos \theta]_{0}^{\pi / 4}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}+\left\{\log \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)-\log 1\right\}=\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2} \log2{/tex}
solution: {tex}\text { Let } \sin ^{-1} x=\theta \text { or, } x=\sin \theta . \text { Then, } d x=d(\sin \theta)=\cos \theta d \theta{/tex} {tex}\text { Now, } \quad x=0 \Rightarrow \sin \theta=0 \Rightarrow \theta=0 \text { and } x=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I=\int_{0}^{1 / \sqrt{2}} \frac{\sin ^{-1} x}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)^{3/2}} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\theta}{\cos ^{3} \theta} \cos \theta d \theta={/tex} {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \theta \sec ^{2} \theta d \theta{/tex} Now using integratio by parts. {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\left[\begin{array}{ll} \theta & \tan \theta \end{array}\right]_{0}^{\pi / 4}+[\log \cos \theta]_{0}^{\pi / 4}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}+\left\{\log \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)-\log 1\right\}=\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2} \log2{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\text { Let } \sin ^{-1} x=\theta \text { or, } x=\sin \theta . \text { Then, } d x=d(\sin \theta)=\cos \theta d \theta{/tex}
{tex}\text { Now, } \quad x=0 \Rightarrow \sin \theta=0 \Rightarrow \theta=0 \text { and } x=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I=\int_{0}^{1 / \sqrt{2}} \frac{\sin ^{-1} x}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)^{3/2}} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\theta}{\cos ^{3} \theta} \cos \theta d \theta={/tex} {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \theta \sec ^{2} \theta d \theta{/tex}

Now using integratio by parts.
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\left[\begin{array}{ll} \theta & \tan \theta \end{array}\right]_{0}^{\pi / 4}+[\log \cos \theta]_{0}^{\pi / 4}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}+\left\{\log \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)-\log 1\right\}=\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2} \log2{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Here we have, {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{1}{1+\sqrt{\tan x}} d x=\int_{0}^{\frac{x}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}} d x{/tex} Let, {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}} d x{/tex} ......(i) {tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}}{\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}+\sqrt{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}} d x{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x\right]{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}}{/tex} .......(ii) Adding (i) & (ii), we get {tex}2 I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}} d x+\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}}{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}}{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}} d x{/tex} {tex}2 I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}2 I=[x]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution: Here we have, {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{1}{1+\sqrt{\tan x}} d x=\int_{0}^{\frac{x}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}} d x{/tex} Let, {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}} d x{/tex} ......(i) {tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}}{\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}+\sqrt{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}} d x{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x\right]{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}}{/tex} .......(ii) Adding (i) & (ii), we get {tex}2 I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}} d x+\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}}{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}}{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}} d x{/tex} {tex}2 I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}2 I=[x]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here we have,
{tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{1}{1+\sqrt{\tan x}} d x=\int_{0}^{\frac{x}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}} d x{/tex}
Let,
{tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}} d x{/tex} ......(i)
{tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}}{\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}+\sqrt{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}} d x{/tex}{tex}\left[\because \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}}{/tex} .......(ii)
Adding (i) & (ii), we get
{tex}2 I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}} d x+\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}}{/tex}
{tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}}{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}} d x{/tex}
{tex}2 I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} d x{/tex}
{tex}2 I=[x]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let, I = {tex}\int \frac{x}{(x-1)^{2}(x+2)} d x{/tex} Using the method of partial fractions, we have, {tex}\frac{x}{(x-1)^{2}(x+2)}=\frac{A}{(x-1)}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{B}{(x-1)^{2}}+\frac{C}{(x+2)}{/tex} x = A (x - 1) ( x + 2) + B (x + 2) + C(x - 1) 2 Substituting x = 1, we get B = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} Equating the coefficients of x 2 and constant term, we get A + C = 0 -2A + 2B + C = 0 On solving, we obtain A = {tex}\frac{2}{9}{/tex} and C = {tex}\frac{-2}{9}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{(x-1)^{2}(x+2)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{9(x-1)}+\frac{1}{3(x-1)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}-\frac{2}{9(x+2)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{x}{(x-1)^{2}(x+2)} d x=\frac{2}{9}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{1}{(x-1)} d x+\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{1}{(x-1)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}d x-\frac{2}{9} \int \frac{1}{(x+2)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{9} \log |x-1|+\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{-1}{x-1}\right){/tex} {tex}-\frac{2}{9} \log |x+2|+C{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{9} \log \left|\frac{x-1}{x+2}\right|{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{3(x-1)}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
solution: Let, I = {tex}\int \frac{x}{(x-1)^{2}(x+2)} d x{/tex} Using the method of partial fractions, we have, {tex}\frac{x}{(x-1)^{2}(x+2)}=\frac{A}{(x-1)}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{B}{(x-1)^{2}}+\frac{C}{(x+2)}{/tex} x = A (x - 1) ( x + 2) + B (x + 2) + C(x - 1) 2 Substituting x = 1, we get B = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} Equating the coefficients of x 2 and constant term, we get A + C = 0 -2A + 2B + C = 0 On solving, we obtain A = {tex}\frac{2}{9}{/tex} and C = {tex}\frac{-2}{9}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{(x-1)^{2}(x+2)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{9(x-1)}+\frac{1}{3(x-1)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}-\frac{2}{9(x+2)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{x}{(x-1)^{2}(x+2)} d x=\frac{2}{9}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{1}{(x-1)} d x+\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{1}{(x-1)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}d x-\frac{2}{9} \int \frac{1}{(x+2)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{9} \log |x-1|+\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{-1}{x-1}\right){/tex} {tex}-\frac{2}{9} \log |x+2|+C{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{9} \log \left|\frac{x-1}{x+2}\right|{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{3(x-1)}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let,
I = {tex}\int \frac{x}{(x-1)^{2}(x+2)} d x{/tex}
Using the method of partial fractions, we have,
 {tex}\frac{x}{(x-1)^{2}(x+2)}=\frac{A}{(x-1)}{/tex}{tex}+\frac{B}{(x-1)^{2}}+\frac{C}{(x+2)}{/tex}
x = A (x - 1) ( x + 2) + B (x + 2) + C(x - 1)2
Substituting x = 1, we get
B = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
Equating the coefficients of x2 and constant term, we get
A + C = 0
-2A + 2B + C = 0
On solving, we obtain
A = {tex}\frac{2}{9}{/tex} and C = {tex}\frac{-2}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{(x-1)^{2}(x+2)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{9(x-1)}+\frac{1}{3(x-1)^{2}}{/tex}{tex}-\frac{2}{9(x+2)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{x}{(x-1)^{2}(x+2)} d x=\frac{2}{9}{/tex}{tex}\int \frac{1}{(x-1)} d x+\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{1}{(x-1)^{2}}{/tex}{tex}d x-\frac{2}{9} \int \frac{1}{(x+2)} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2}{9} \log |x-1|+\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{-1}{x-1}\right){/tex} {tex}-\frac{2}{9} \log |x+2|+C{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2}{9} \log \left|\frac{x-1}{x+2}\right|{/tex}{tex}-\frac{1}{3(x-1)}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate: {tex}\int \frac{\sin x-x \cos x}{x(x+\sin x)} d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate: {tex}\int \frac{\sin x-x \cos x}{x(x+\sin x)} d x{/tex}
explanation: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x-x \cos x}{x(x+\sin x)} d x{/tex} . Then, {tex}I=\int \frac{(x+\sin x)-x-x \cos x}{x(x+\sin x)} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int \frac{(x+\sin x)-x(1+\cos x)}{x(x+\sin x)} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int\left\{\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1+\cos x}{x+\sin x}\right\} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\quad{/tex} I = log |x| - log (x - sin x) + C {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\log \left|\frac{x}{x+\sin x}\right|+c{/tex}
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x-x \cos x}{x(x+\sin x)} d x{/tex} . Then, {tex}I=\int \frac{(x+\sin x)-x-x \cos x}{x(x+\sin x)} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int \frac{(x+\sin x)-x(1+\cos x)}{x(x+\sin x)} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int\left\{\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1+\cos x}{x+\sin x}\right\} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\quad{/tex} I = log |x| - log (x - sin x) + C {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\log \left|\frac{x}{x+\sin x}\right|+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x-x \cos x}{x(x+\sin x)} d x{/tex}. Then,
{tex}I=\int \frac{(x+\sin x)-x-x \cos x}{x(x+\sin x)} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int \frac{(x+\sin x)-x(1+\cos x)}{x(x+\sin x)} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int\left\{\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1+\cos x}{x+\sin x}\right\} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\quad{/tex}I = log |x| - log (x - sin x) + C
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\log \left|\frac{x}{x+\sin x}\right|+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let the given integral be, l= {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{1+3 \sin ^{2} x} d x=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{1+3\left(1-\cos ^{2} x\right)} d x{/tex} I = {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x=\frac{-1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{-3 \cos ^{2} x}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{-1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{4-3 \cos ^{2} x-4}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{-1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{4-3 \cos ^{2} x}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x+\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{4}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{-1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} 1 d x+\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{4 \sec ^{2} x}{4 \sec ^{2} x-3} d x{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{-1}{3}[x]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}+\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{4 \sec ^{2} x}{4\left(1+\tan ^{2} x\right)-3} d x{/tex} {tex}I=-\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{2}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{2 \sec ^{2} x}{1+4 \tan ^{2} x} d x{/tex} ...(i) Consider, {tex} \int_{0}^\frac{\pi}{2}\frac{2 \sec ^{2} x}{1+4 \tan ^{2} x} d x{/tex} Let 2 tanx = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2sec 2 xdx = dt When x = 0, t = 0 and when x = {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex} , t = {tex}\infty{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{2 \sec ^{2} x}{1+4 \tan ^{2} x} d x=\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_{0}^{\infty}{/tex} {tex}=\left[\tan ^{-1}(\infty)-\tan ^{-1}(0)\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} Therefore, from (i), we obtain {tex}I=-\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{\pi}{2}\right]=\frac{\pi}{3}-\frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
solution: Let the given integral be, l= {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{1+3 \sin ^{2} x} d x=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{1+3\left(1-\cos ^{2} x\right)} d x{/tex} I = {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x=\frac{-1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{-3 \cos ^{2} x}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{-1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{4-3 \cos ^{2} x-4}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{-1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{4-3 \cos ^{2} x}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x+\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{4}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{-1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} 1 d x+\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{4 \sec ^{2} x}{4 \sec ^{2} x-3} d x{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{-1}{3}[x]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}+\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{4 \sec ^{2} x}{4\left(1+\tan ^{2} x\right)-3} d x{/tex} {tex}I=-\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{2}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{2 \sec ^{2} x}{1+4 \tan ^{2} x} d x{/tex} ...(i) Consider, {tex} \int_{0}^\frac{\pi}{2}\frac{2 \sec ^{2} x}{1+4 \tan ^{2} x} d x{/tex} Let 2 tanx = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2sec 2 xdx = dt When x = 0, t = 0 and when x = {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex} , t = {tex}\infty{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{2 \sec ^{2} x}{1+4 \tan ^{2} x} d x=\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_{0}^{\infty}{/tex} {tex}=\left[\tan ^{-1}(\infty)-\tan ^{-1}(0)\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} Therefore, from (i), we obtain {tex}I=-\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{\pi}{2}\right]=\frac{\pi}{3}-\frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the  given integral be, l= {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{1+3 \sin ^{2} x} d x=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{1+3\left(1-\cos ^{2} x\right)} d x{/tex}
I = {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x=\frac{-1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{-3 \cos ^{2} x}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{-1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{4-3 \cos ^{2} x-4}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{-1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{4-3 \cos ^{2} x}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x+\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{4}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{-1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} 1 d x+\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{4 \sec ^{2} x}{4 \sec ^{2} x-3} d x{/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{-1}{3}[x]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}+\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{4 \sec ^{2} x}{4\left(1+\tan ^{2} x\right)-3} d x{/tex}
{tex}I=-\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{2}{3} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{2 \sec ^{2} x}{1+4 \tan ^{2} x} d x{/tex}...(i)
Consider, {tex} \int_{0}^\frac{\pi}{2}\frac{2 \sec ^{2} x}{1+4 \tan ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
Let 2 tanx = t
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2sec2 xdx = dt
When x = 0, t =  0 and when x = {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex}, t = {tex}\infty{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{2 \sec ^{2} x}{1+4 \tan ^{2} x} d x=\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_{0}^{\infty}{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\tan ^{-1}(\infty)-\tan ^{-1}(0)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
Therefore, from (i), we obtain
{tex}I=-\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{\pi}{2}\right]=\frac{\pi}{3}-\frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate:  {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\sin x+\cos x}{3+\sin 2 x} d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate: {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\sin x+\cos x}{3+\sin 2 x} d x{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}I={/tex} {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\sin x+\cos x}{3+\sin 2 x} d x{/tex} , then we have {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}\left(\frac{\sin x+\cos x}{3+1-(\cos x-\sin x)^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}\left(\frac{\sin x+\cos x}{4-(\cos x-\sin x)^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}I=\left.\frac{1}{4}\left[\log | \frac{2+\sin x-\cos x}{2-\sin x+\cos x}\right]\right|_{0} ^{\pi / 4}{/tex} {tex}I=-\frac{1}{4} \log \left(\frac{1}{3}\right){/tex} {tex}I=\frac{1}{4} \log _{e} 3{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I={/tex} {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\sin x+\cos x}{3+\sin 2 x} d x{/tex} , then we have {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}\left(\frac{\sin x+\cos x}{3+1-(\cos x-\sin x)^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}\left(\frac{\sin x+\cos x}{4-(\cos x-\sin x)^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}I=\left.\frac{1}{4}\left[\log | \frac{2+\sin x-\cos x}{2-\sin x+\cos x}\right]\right|_{0} ^{\pi / 4}{/tex} {tex}I=-\frac{1}{4} \log \left(\frac{1}{3}\right){/tex} {tex}I=\frac{1}{4} \log _{e} 3{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I={/tex} {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{\sin x+\cos x}{3+\sin 2 x} d x{/tex}, then we have
{tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}\left(\frac{\sin x+\cos x}{3+1-(\cos x-\sin x)^{2}}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}\left(\frac{\sin x+\cos x}{4-(\cos x-\sin x)^{2}}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}I=\left.\frac{1}{4}\left[\log | \frac{2+\sin x-\cos x}{2-\sin x+\cos x}\right]\right|_{0} ^{\pi / 4}{/tex}
{tex}I=-\frac{1}{4} \log \left(\frac{1}{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{1}{4} \log _{e} 3{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: We have {tex}\frac{x^{4}}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=(x+1)+\frac{1}{x^{3}-x^{2}+x-1}{/tex} Using Partitial Fraction, = {tex}(x+1)+\frac{1}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} ......(i) Now express {tex}\frac{1}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{(x-1)}+\frac{\mathrm{B} x+\mathrm{C}}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} .....(ii) So 1 = A (x 2 + 1) + (Bx + C) (x - 1) = (A + B) x 2 + (C - B) x + A - C Equating coefficients on both sides, we get A + B = 0, C - B = 0 and A - C = 1, which gives {tex}A=\frac{1}{2}, B=C=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Substituting values of A, B and C in (ii), we get {tex}\frac{1}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{1}{2(x-1)}-\frac{1}{2} \frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}-\frac{1}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} .....(iii) Again, substituting (iii) in (i), we have {tex}\frac{x^{4}}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)}{/tex} = {tex}(x+1)+\frac{1}{2(x-1)}-\frac{1}{2} \frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}-\frac{1}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} Therefore {tex}\int \frac{x^{4}}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x+\frac{1}{2} \log |x-1|-\frac{1}{4} \log \left(x^{2}+1\right)-\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} x+C{/tex}
solution: We have {tex}\frac{x^{4}}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=(x+1)+\frac{1}{x^{3}-x^{2}+x-1}{/tex} Using Partitial Fraction, = {tex}(x+1)+\frac{1}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} ......(i) Now express {tex}\frac{1}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{(x-1)}+\frac{\mathrm{B} x+\mathrm{C}}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} .....(ii) So 1 = A (x 2 + 1) + (Bx + C) (x - 1) = (A + B) x 2 + (C - B) x + A - C Equating coefficients on both sides, we get A + B = 0, C - B = 0 and A - C = 1, which gives {tex}A=\frac{1}{2}, B=C=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Substituting values of A, B and C in (ii), we get {tex}\frac{1}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{1}{2(x-1)}-\frac{1}{2} \frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}-\frac{1}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} .....(iii) Again, substituting (iii) in (i), we have {tex}\frac{x^{4}}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)}{/tex} = {tex}(x+1)+\frac{1}{2(x-1)}-\frac{1}{2} \frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}-\frac{1}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} Therefore {tex}\int \frac{x^{4}}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x+\frac{1}{2} \log |x-1|-\frac{1}{4} \log \left(x^{2}+1\right)-\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} x+C{/tex}
solution_html:
We have {tex}\frac{x^{4}}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=(x+1)+\frac{1}{x^{3}-x^{2}+x-1}{/tex} 
Using Partitial Fraction, 
= {tex}(x+1)+\frac{1}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} ......(i)
Now express {tex}\frac{1}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{(x-1)}+\frac{\mathrm{B} x+\mathrm{C}}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} .....(ii)
So 1 = A (x2 + 1) + (Bx + C) (x - 1)
= (A + B) x2 + (C - B) x + A - C
Equating coefficients on both sides, we get A + B = 0, C - B = 0 and A - C = 1, which gives 
{tex}A=\frac{1}{2}, B=C=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
Substituting values of A, B and C in (ii), we get
{tex}\frac{1}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{1}{2(x-1)}-\frac{1}{2} \frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}-\frac{1}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} .....(iii)
Again, substituting (iii) in (i), we have
{tex}\frac{x^{4}}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)}{/tex} = {tex}(x+1)+\frac{1}{2(x-1)}-\frac{1}{2} \frac{x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}-\frac{1}{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{/tex} 
Therefore
{tex}\int \frac{x^{4}}{(x-1)\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x+\frac{1}{2} \log |x-1|-\frac{1}{4} \log \left(x^{2}+1\right)-\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} x+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate: {tex}\int \frac{1-\cos x}{\cos x(1+\cos x)} d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate: {tex}\int \frac{1-\cos x}{\cos x(1+\cos x)} d x{/tex}
explanation: Let, I = {tex}\int \frac{1-\cos x}{\cos x(1+\cos x)} d x{/tex} Put, cos x = y. Then, we get {tex}\frac{1-\cos x}{\cos x(1+\cos x)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1-y}{y(1+y)}{/tex} Using method of Partial fractions, we have, {tex}\frac{1-y}{y(1+y)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{y}+\frac{B}{1+y}{/tex} ......(i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - y = A(1 + y) + By ......(ii) Putting y = 0 in (ii), we get A = 1. Putting y = - 1 in (ii), we get B = - 2. Substituting the values of A and B in (i), we get {tex}\frac{1-y}{y(1+y)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{y}-\frac{2}{1+y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1-\cos x}{\cos x(1+\cos x)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\cos x}-\frac{2}{1+\cos x}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} y = cos x] {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1-\cos x}{\cos x(1+\cos x)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\cos x} d x{/tex} - {tex}\int \frac{2}{1+\cos x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \sec x d x{/tex} - {tex}\int \frac{2}{2 \cos ^{2} x / 2} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I{/tex} = {tex}\int \sec x-\int \sec ^{2} x / 2 d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = log |sec x + tan x| - 2 tan {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex} + C
solution: Let, I = {tex}\int \frac{1-\cos x}{\cos x(1+\cos x)} d x{/tex} Put, cos x = y. Then, we get {tex}\frac{1-\cos x}{\cos x(1+\cos x)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1-y}{y(1+y)}{/tex} Using method of Partial fractions, we have, {tex}\frac{1-y}{y(1+y)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{y}+\frac{B}{1+y}{/tex} ......(i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - y = A(1 + y) + By ......(ii) Putting y = 0 in (ii), we get A = 1. Putting y = - 1 in (ii), we get B = - 2. Substituting the values of A and B in (i), we get {tex}\frac{1-y}{y(1+y)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{y}-\frac{2}{1+y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1-\cos x}{\cos x(1+\cos x)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\cos x}-\frac{2}{1+\cos x}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} y = cos x] {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1-\cos x}{\cos x(1+\cos x)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\cos x} d x{/tex} - {tex}\int \frac{2}{1+\cos x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \sec x d x{/tex} - {tex}\int \frac{2}{2 \cos ^{2} x / 2} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I{/tex} = {tex}\int \sec x-\int \sec ^{2} x / 2 d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = log |sec x + tan x| - 2 tan {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex} + C
solution_html:
Let,
I = {tex}\int \frac{1-\cos x}{\cos x(1+\cos x)} d x{/tex} 
Put, cos x = y. Then, we get
{tex}\frac{1-\cos x}{\cos x(1+\cos x)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1-y}{y(1+y)}{/tex} 
Using method of Partial fractions, we have,
 {tex}\frac{1-y}{y(1+y)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{y}+\frac{B}{1+y}{/tex} ......(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - y = A(1 + y) + By ......(ii)
Putting y = 0 in (ii), we get A = 1. Putting y = - 1 in (ii), we get B = - 2.
Substituting the values of A and B in (i), we get
{tex}\frac{1-y}{y(1+y)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{y}-\frac{2}{1+y}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1-\cos x}{\cos x(1+\cos x)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\cos x}-\frac{2}{1+\cos x}{/tex}  [{tex}\because{/tex} y = cos x]
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  I = {tex}\int \frac{1-\cos x}{\cos x(1+\cos x)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\cos x} d x{/tex} - {tex}\int \frac{2}{1+\cos x} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \sec x d x{/tex} - {tex}\int \frac{2}{2 \cos ^{2} x / 2} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow I{/tex} = {tex}\int \sec x-\int \sec ^{2} x / 2 d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = log |sec x + tan x| - 2 tan {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex} + C

explanation: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{3}-1}{x^{3}+x} d x{/tex} , then we have I = {tex}\int 1-\frac{(x+1)}{x^{3}+x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int d x-\int \frac{x+1}{x^{3}+x} d x{/tex} Let {tex}\frac{x+1}{x\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{A}{x}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 1 = A (x 2 + 1) + (Bx + C) (x) = (A + B)x 2 + (B + C)x + A Equating similar terms, we get A + B = 0, C = 1, A = 1 Solving, we get, A = 1, B = -1, C = 1 Thus, we have I = {tex}-\int \frac{d x}{x}-\int \frac{-x+1}{x^{2}+1} d x+\int d x{/tex} = {tex}-\log |x|+\int \frac{x d x}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} - {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+1}+\int d x{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} I = x -log {tex}|x|+\frac{1}{2} \log \left|x^{2}+1\right|-\tan ^{-1} x+c{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} I = x - {tex}\log |x|+\frac{1}{2} \log \left|x^{2}+1\right|{/tex} {tex}-\tan ^{-1} x+c{/tex}
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{3}-1}{x^{3}+x} d x{/tex} , then we have I = {tex}\int 1-\frac{(x+1)}{x^{3}+x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int d x-\int \frac{x+1}{x^{3}+x} d x{/tex} Let {tex}\frac{x+1}{x\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{A}{x}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 1 = A (x 2 + 1) + (Bx + C) (x) = (A + B)x 2 + (B + C)x + A Equating similar terms, we get A + B = 0, C = 1, A = 1 Solving, we get, A = 1, B = -1, C = 1 Thus, we have I = {tex}-\int \frac{d x}{x}-\int \frac{-x+1}{x^{2}+1} d x+\int d x{/tex} = {tex}-\log |x|+\int \frac{x d x}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} - {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+1}+\int d x{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} I = x -log {tex}|x|+\frac{1}{2} \log \left|x^{2}+1\right|-\tan ^{-1} x+c{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} I = x - {tex}\log |x|+\frac{1}{2} \log \left|x^{2}+1\right|{/tex} {tex}-\tan ^{-1} x+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{3}-1}{x^{3}+x} d x{/tex}, then we have
I = {tex}\int 1-\frac{(x+1)}{x^{3}+x} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int d x-\int \frac{x+1}{x^{3}+x} d x{/tex}
Let {tex}\frac{x+1}{x\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{A}{x}{/tex}{tex}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 1 = A (x2 + 1) + (Bx + C) (x)
= (A + B)x2 + (B + C)x + A
Equating similar terms, we get
A + B = 0, C = 1, A = 1
Solving, we get, A = 1, B = -1, C = 1
Thus, we have
I = {tex}-\int \frac{d x}{x}-\int \frac{-x+1}{x^{2}+1} d x+\int d x{/tex}
= {tex}-\log |x|+\int \frac{x d x}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} - {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+1}+\int d x{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex}   I = x -log {tex}|x|+\frac{1}{2} \log \left|x^{2}+1\right|-\tan ^{-1} x+c{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex}   I = x - {tex}\log |x|+\frac{1}{2} \log \left|x^{2}+1\right|{/tex}{tex}-\tan ^{-1} x+c{/tex}

explanation: Let the Given integral be, I {tex}={/tex} {tex}\int \frac{x+3}{\sqrt{5-4 x-2 x^{2}}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{-\frac{1}{4}(-4-4 x)+2}{\sqrt{5-4 x-2 x^{2}}} d x\\ =-\frac14\int \frac {-4-4x}{\sqrt{5-4x-2x^2}}dx+2 \int \frac 1{\sqrt{5-4x-2x^2}}dx{/tex} For the 1st part let 5-4x-2x 2 = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (-4 - 4x ) dx = dt Therefore from above, we get = {tex}-\frac{1}{4} \int t^{-\frac12}dt{/tex} + {tex}\sqrt{2} \int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{(\sqrt{\frac{7}{2}})^{2}-(x+1)^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{5-4 x+2 x^{2}}{/tex} + {tex}\sqrt{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+1}{\sqrt{7 / 2}}\right){/tex} + C
solution: Let the Given integral be, I {tex}={/tex} {tex}\int \frac{x+3}{\sqrt{5-4 x-2 x^{2}}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{-\frac{1}{4}(-4-4 x)+2}{\sqrt{5-4 x-2 x^{2}}} d x\\ =-\frac14\int \frac {-4-4x}{\sqrt{5-4x-2x^2}}dx+2 \int \frac 1{\sqrt{5-4x-2x^2}}dx{/tex} For the 1st part let 5-4x-2x 2 = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (-4 - 4x ) dx = dt Therefore from above, we get = {tex}-\frac{1}{4} \int t^{-\frac12}dt{/tex} + {tex}\sqrt{2} \int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{(\sqrt{\frac{7}{2}})^{2}-(x+1)^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{5-4 x+2 x^{2}}{/tex} + {tex}\sqrt{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+1}{\sqrt{7 / 2}}\right){/tex} + C
