solution_html:
Let {tex}I = \int \frac { 1 } { \cos ^ { 4 } x + \sin ^ { 4 } x } d x{/tex}
Dividing numerator and denominator by cos4 x , we get
{tex}I= \int \frac { \frac{1}{cos^4x} } { \frac{\cos ^ { 4 } x + \sin ^ { 4 } x }{cos^4x} } d x{/tex}
{tex} = \int \frac { \sec ^ { 4 } x } { 1 + \tan ^ { 4 } x } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { \left( \sec ^ { 2 } x \right) \left( \sec ^ { 2 } x \right) } { 1 + \left( \tan ^ { 2 } x \right) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } x \left( 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } x \right) } { 1 + \left( \tan ^ { 2 } x \right) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}{tex} \ [\because sec^2x=1+tan^2x]{/tex}
Put tan x = t
{tex} \Rightarrow \sec ^ { 2 } x d x = d t{/tex}
{tex} \therefore ~ I = \int \frac { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } { 1 + t ^ { 4 } } d t{/tex}
Dividing numerator and denominator by t2 in RHS , we get
{tex} I = \int \frac { 1 + \frac { 1 } { t ^ { 2 } } } { t ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { t ^ { 2 } } + 2 - 2 } d t{/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { 1 + \frac { 1 } { t ^ { 2 } } } { \left( t - \frac { 1 } { t } \right) ^ { 2 } + 2 }dt{/tex}
Put , {tex} t - \frac { 1 } { t } = u{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { t ^ { 2 } } \right) d t = d u{/tex}
Then ,
{tex} I = \int \frac { d u } { u ^ { 2 } + ( \sqrt { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad I = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { u } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right) + C{/tex}{tex} \left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { 1 } { a } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { a } \right) + C \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad I = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { t - \frac { 1 } { t } } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right) + C{/tex}{tex} \left[\because u = t - \frac { 1 } { t } \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad I = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { t ^ { 2 } - 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } t } \right) + C{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad I = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \tan ^ { 2 } x - 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } \tan x } \right) + C{/tex}{tex}[\because t = tan x]{/tex}

According to the question , {tex} I = \int \frac { \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } - \cos ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } } { \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } + \cos ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } } d x{/tex}
We know that ,  {tex}sin^{-1} \sqrt { x } + \cos ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } = \pi / 2{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \cos ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x }{/tex}
{tex}I = \int \frac { \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } - \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } \right) } { \pi \over 2 } d x{/tex}
{tex} = \int \frac { 2 \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } - \frac { \pi } { 2 } } { \frac { \pi } { 2 } } d x {/tex}

{tex}= \frac { 2 } { \pi } \int \left( 2 \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } - \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 4 } { \pi } \int \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } d x - \int 1 d x {/tex}

{tex}= \frac { 4 } { \pi } \int \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } d x - x +C{/tex}
{tex} I = \frac { 4 } { \pi } I _ { 1 } - x +C{/tex}...(i)
Consider , {tex} I _ { 1 } = \int \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } d x{/tex}
put {tex} \sqrt { x } = t{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x = t^2 \Rightarrow dx = 2t dt{/tex} 
{tex} \therefore \quad I _ { 1 } = \int \sin ^ { - 1 } (t ) \times [2 t] d t {/tex}

{tex}= 2 \int \sin ^ { - 1 } (t) \times t \ d t{/tex}

By using integration by parts we get,
{tex} = 2 \left[ \sin ^ { - 1 } t \int t d t - \int \left\{ \frac { d } { d t } \left( \sin ^ { - 1 } t \right) \int t d t \right\} d t \right]{/tex}
{tex} = 2 \left[ \sin ^ { - 1 } (t) \times \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - \int \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } } \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } d t \right]{/tex}
{tex} = t ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { - 1 } t + \int \frac { \left( 1 - t ^ { 2 } \right) - 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } } d t{/tex}

{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ ( 2 x - 1 ) \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } + \sqrt { x } \sqrt { 1 - x } \right] + C _ { 1 }{/tex}{tex} [ \text { put } t = \sqrt { x } ]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ ( 2 x - 1 ) \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } + \sqrt { x - x ^ { 2 } } \right] + C _ { 1 }{/tex}
On putting the value of {tex}I_1{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get
{tex} I = \frac { 2 } { \pi } \left[ ( 2 x - 1 ) \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } + \sqrt { x - x ^ { 2 } } \right] - x + K \ [{/tex}where {tex}K= \frac { 4 } { \pi } C _ { 1 }+C \ ]{/tex}

We have to find the value of {tex}\int \frac { \sin ^ { 6 } x } { \cos ^ { 8 } x } d x.{/tex}

{tex}\therefore I = \int t ^ { 6 } d t = \frac { t ^ { 7 } } { 7 } + C = \frac { \tan ^ { 7 } x } { 7 } + C [ \text { put } t = \tan x ]{/tex}

solution: Given, {tex} I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } x ^ { 2 } \sin x d x{/tex} {tex}I = \int_{0} ^ {\pi/2} {{{\mathop x\limits_I }^2}} \sin \mathop x\limits_{II} dx {/tex} By using integration by parts we get , {tex}I= [- {x^2}\cos x ]_ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 }+ 2\int_0^{\pi/2} {\mathop x\limits_I } \cos \mathop x\limits_{II} dx{/tex} {tex} =[ - x ^ { 2 } \cos x ] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 }+ 2 \left[[ x ( \sin x )]_ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } - \int_0^{\pi/2} 1 \cdot ( \sin x ) d x \right] {/tex} {tex} =[ - x ^ { 2 } \cos x ] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 }+ 2 \left[ [x ( \sin x )] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } +[cosx ]_ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \right] {/tex} {tex} \therefore I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } x ^ { 2 } \sin x d x{/tex} {tex} = \left[ - x ^ { 2 } \cos x + 2 ( x \sin x + \cos x ) \right] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 }{/tex} {tex} = \left[ - \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) + 2 \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } + \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right)\right.{/tex} {tex} - 2 ( 0 + \cos 0 ) ]{/tex} {tex} = - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 } \times 0 + 2 \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + 0 \right) - 2 ( 0 + 1 ){/tex} {tex} \therefore I= \pi - 2{/tex}
solution_html:
Given,  {tex} I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } x ^ { 2 } \sin x d x{/tex}
{tex}I = \int_{0} ^ {\pi/2} {{{\mathop x\limits_I }^2}} \sin \mathop x\limits_{II} dx {/tex}

By using integration by parts we get ,

{tex}I= [- {x^2}\cos x ]_ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 }+ 2\int_0^{\pi/2} {\mathop x\limits_I } \cos \mathop x\limits_{II} dx{/tex}
{tex} =[ - x ^ { 2 } \cos x ] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 }+ 2 \left[[ x ( \sin x )]_ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } - \int_0^{\pi/2} 1 \cdot ( \sin x ) d x \right] {/tex}

{tex} =[ - x ^ { 2 } \cos x ] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 }+ 2 \left[ [x ( \sin x )] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } +[cosx ]_ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \right] {/tex}
{tex} \therefore I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } x ^ { 2 } \sin x d x{/tex}{tex} = \left[ - x ^ { 2 } \cos x + 2 ( x \sin x + \cos x ) \right] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 }{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ - \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) + 2 \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } + \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right)\right.{/tex}{tex} - 2 ( 0 + \cos 0 ) ]{/tex}
{tex} = - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 } \times 0 + 2 \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + 0 \right) - 2 ( 0 + 1 ){/tex}
{tex} \therefore I= \pi - 2{/tex}

question_html: Integrate the function {tex}\int {\frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x - {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x }}{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x + {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x }}dx} {/tex} , x {tex}\in{/tex} [0,1]
question_text: Integrate the function {tex}\int {\frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x - {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x }}{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x + {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x }}dx} {/tex} , x {tex}\in{/tex} [0,1]
explanation: {tex}I^{'}=\int {\frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x - {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x }}{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x + {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x }}dx} {/tex} {tex}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\sqrt x + {\cos ^{ - 1}}\sqrt x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} {tex}{\cos ^{ - 1}}\sqrt x = \frac{\pi }{2} - {\sin ^{ - 1}}\sqrt x {/tex} {tex}I{'} = \int {\frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x - \left( {\frac{\pi }{2} - {{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x } \right)}}{{\pi /2}}} dx{/tex} {tex} = \int {\frac{{2{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x - \frac{\pi }{2}}}{{\pi /2}}} dx{/tex} {tex} = \frac{4}{\pi }\int {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x } dx - \int {1dx} {/tex} {tex} = \frac{4}{\pi }I - x + c{/tex} ...(i) {tex}I = \int {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x dx} {/tex} Put {tex}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\sqrt x = t{/tex} {tex}\sin t = \sqrt x {/tex} {tex}{\sin ^2}t = x{/tex} {tex}2\sin t\cos t = dx{/tex} {tex} I= \int {t.2\sin t\cos t\,\,dt} {/tex} {tex} = \int {\mathop t\limits_I .\mathop {\sin }\limits_{II} 2t\,\,dt}{/tex} {tex} = - t\frac{{\cos 2t}}{2} - \int {1.\left( { - \frac{{\cos 2t}}{2}} \right)} dt{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{ - t\cos 2t}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\frac{{\sin 2t}}{2} + c{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{ - t(1 - 2{{\sin }^2}t)}}{2} + \frac{1}{4}2sint \ cost + c{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{ - t(1 - 2{{\sin }^2}t)}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\sin t\sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}t} + c{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{ - {{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x (1 - 2x)}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}.\sqrt x \sqrt {1 - x} + c{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x (1 - 2x)}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}.\sqrt x \sqrt {1 - x} + c{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x (2x - 1)}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}.\sqrt {x - {x^2}} + c{/tex} From (i), {tex}I{'}=\int {\frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x - {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x }}{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x + {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x }}dx} = \frac{4}{\pi }\left( {\frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x (2x - 1)}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\sqrt {x - {x^2}} } \right) - x + c{/tex}
solution: {tex}I^{'}=\int {\frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x - {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x }}{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x + {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x }}dx} {/tex} {tex}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\sqrt x + {\cos ^{ - 1}}\sqrt x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} {tex}{\cos ^{ - 1}}\sqrt x = \frac{\pi }{2} - {\sin ^{ - 1}}\sqrt x {/tex} {tex}I{'} = \int {\frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x - \left( {\frac{\pi }{2} - {{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x } \right)}}{{\pi /2}}} dx{/tex} {tex} = \int {\frac{{2{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x - \frac{\pi }{2}}}{{\pi /2}}} dx{/tex} {tex} = \frac{4}{\pi }\int {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x } dx - \int {1dx} {/tex} {tex} = \frac{4}{\pi }I - x + c{/tex} ...(i) {tex}I = \int {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x dx} {/tex} Put {tex}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\sqrt x = t{/tex} {tex}\sin t = \sqrt x {/tex} {tex}{\sin ^2}t = x{/tex} {tex}2\sin t\cos t = dx{/tex} {tex} I= \int {t.2\sin t\cos t\,\,dt} {/tex} {tex} = \int {\mathop t\limits_I .\mathop {\sin }\limits_{II} 2t\,\,dt}{/tex} {tex} = - t\frac{{\cos 2t}}{2} - \int {1.\left( { - \frac{{\cos 2t}}{2}} \right)} dt{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{ - t\cos 2t}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\frac{{\sin 2t}}{2} + c{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{ - t(1 - 2{{\sin }^2}t)}}{2} + \frac{1}{4}2sint \ cost + c{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{ - t(1 - 2{{\sin }^2}t)}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\sin t\sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}t} + c{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{ - {{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x (1 - 2x)}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}.\sqrt x \sqrt {1 - x} + c{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x (1 - 2x)}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}.\sqrt x \sqrt {1 - x} + c{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x (2x - 1)}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}.\sqrt {x - {x^2}} + c{/tex} From (i), {tex}I{'}=\int {\frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x - {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x }}{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x + {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x }}dx} = \frac{4}{\pi }\left( {\frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x (2x - 1)}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\sqrt {x - {x^2}} } \right) - x + c{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}I^{'}=\int {\frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x - {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x }}{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x + {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x }}dx} {/tex}

{tex}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\sqrt x + {\cos ^{ - 1}}\sqrt x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}

{tex}{\cos ^{ - 1}}\sqrt x = \frac{\pi }{2} - {\sin ^{ - 1}}\sqrt x {/tex}

{tex}I{'} = \int {\frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x - \left( {\frac{\pi }{2} - {{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x } \right)}}{{\pi /2}}} dx{/tex}

{tex} = \int {\frac{{2{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x - \frac{\pi }{2}}}{{\pi /2}}} dx{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{4}{\pi }\int {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x } dx - \int {1dx} {/tex}

{tex} = \frac{4}{\pi }I - x + c{/tex} ...(i)

{tex}I = \int {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x dx} {/tex}

Put {tex}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\sqrt x = t{/tex}

{tex}\sin t = \sqrt x {/tex}

{tex}{\sin ^2}t = x{/tex}

{tex}2\sin t\cos t = dx{/tex}

{tex} I= \int {t.2\sin t\cos t\,\,dt} {/tex}

{tex} = \int {\mathop t\limits_I .\mathop {\sin }\limits_{II} 2t\,\,dt}{/tex}

{tex} = - t\frac{{\cos 2t}}{2} - \int {1.\left( { - \frac{{\cos 2t}}{2}} \right)} dt{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{{ - t\cos 2t}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\frac{{\sin 2t}}{2} + c{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{{ - t(1 - 2{{\sin }^2}t)}}{2} + \frac{1}{4}2sint \ cost + c{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{{ - t(1 - 2{{\sin }^2}t)}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\sin t\sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}t} + c{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{{ - {{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x (1 - 2x)}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}.\sqrt x \sqrt {1 - x} + c{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x (1 - 2x)}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}.\sqrt x \sqrt {1 - x} + c{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x (2x - 1)}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}.\sqrt {x - {x^2}} + c{/tex}

From (i), 

{tex}I{'}=\int {\frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x - {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x }}{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x + {{\cos }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x }}dx} = \frac{4}{\pi }\left( {\frac{{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\sqrt x (2x - 1)}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\sqrt {x - {x^2}} } \right) - x + c{/tex}

explanation: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x \cos ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x{/tex} Put, t = cos -1 x {tex}\Rightarrow d t=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x{/tex} Also, cos t = x Thus, we have I = - {tex}\int{/tex} t cot dt Now let us solve this by the 'by parts' method. I = - [t sint - {tex}\int{/tex} sin t dt] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = -[t sin t - cos t] + C Substituting the value t = cos -1 x, we get I = -[cos -1 x sin t + x] + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = -[cos -1 x {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}}{/tex} + x] + c
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x \cos ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x{/tex} Put, t = cos -1 x {tex}\Rightarrow d t=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x{/tex} Also, cos t = x Thus, we have I = - {tex}\int{/tex} t cot dt Now let us solve this by the 'by parts' method. I = - [t sint - {tex}\int{/tex} sin t dt] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = -[t sin t - cos t] + C Substituting the value t = cos -1 x, we get I = -[cos -1 x sin t + x] + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = -[cos -1 x {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}}{/tex} + x] + c
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x \cos ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x{/tex}
Put, t = cos-1 x
{tex}\Rightarrow d t=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x{/tex}
Also, cos t = x
Thus, we have
I = - {tex}\int{/tex} t cot dt
Now let us solve this by the 'by parts' method.
I = - [t sint -{tex}\int{/tex} sin t dt]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = -[t sin t - cos t] + C
Substituting the value t = cos-1 x, we get
I = -[cos-1 x sin t + x] + c

subject: Mathematics
explanation: {tex}=\int(\sqrt{\cot x}+\sqrt{\tan x}) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int(\sqrt{\cot x}+\frac {1}{\sqrt{\cot x}})dx{/tex} [Using tan x = {tex}\frac {1}{cot x}{/tex} ] {tex}=\int\left(\frac{\cot x+1}{\sqrt{\cot x}}\right) d x{/tex} I = {tex}\int \sqrt{\tan x}(\cot x+1) d x{/tex} Put tan x = t 2 Let tan x = t 2 sec 2 x dx = 2t dt 1 + tan 2 x = 2t {tex}\frac {dt}{dx}{/tex} 1 + t 4 = 2t {tex}\frac {dt}{dx}{/tex} dx = {tex}\frac{2 t}{\left(1+t^4\right)} d t{/tex} Now, I {tex}=\int \sqrt{t^2}\left(\frac{1}{t^2}+1\right)\left(\frac{2 t}{1+t^4}\right) d t{/tex} {tex}=\int t\left(\frac{1}{t^2}+1\right)\left(\frac{2 t}{1+t^4}\right) d t{/tex} {tex}=\int 2 t^2\left(\frac{1+t^2}{t^2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{1+t^4}\right) d t{/tex} {tex}=2 \int\left(\frac{1+t^2}{1+t^4}\right) d t{/tex} {tex}=2 \int \frac{\frac{1+t^2}{t^2}}{\frac{1+t^4}{t^2}} d t{/tex} {tex}=2 \int \frac{1+\frac{1}{t^2}}{t^2+\frac{1}{t^2}+2-2} d t{/tex} {tex}I=2 \int \frac{1+\frac{1}{t^2}}{\left(t-\frac{1}{t}\right)^2+(\sqrt{2})^2} d t{/tex} Let {tex}t-\frac{1}{t}=y{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow\left(1+\frac{1}{t^2}\right) d t=d y{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I=2 \int \frac{d y}{y^2+(\sqrt{2})^2}{/tex} {tex}=2\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]+C{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{t-\frac{1}{t}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+C{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{t^2-1}{\sqrt{2} t}\right)+C{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\tan x-1}{\sqrt{2} \sqrt{\tan x}}\right)+C{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\tan x-1}{\sqrt{2 \tan x}}\right)+C{/tex}
solution: {tex}=\int(\sqrt{\cot x}+\sqrt{\tan x}) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int(\sqrt{\cot x}+\frac {1}{\sqrt{\cot x}})dx{/tex} [Using tan x = {tex}\frac {1}{cot x}{/tex} ] {tex}=\int\left(\frac{\cot x+1}{\sqrt{\cot x}}\right) d x{/tex} I = {tex}\int \sqrt{\tan x}(\cot x+1) d x{/tex} Put tan x = t 2 Let tan x = t 2 sec 2 x dx = 2t dt 1 + tan 2 x = 2t {tex}\frac {dt}{dx}{/tex} 1 + t 4 = 2t {tex}\frac {dt}{dx}{/tex} dx = {tex}\frac{2 t}{\left(1+t^4\right)} d t{/tex} Now, I {tex}=\int \sqrt{t^2}\left(\frac{1}{t^2}+1\right)\left(\frac{2 t}{1+t^4}\right) d t{/tex} {tex}=\int t\left(\frac{1}{t^2}+1\right)\left(\frac{2 t}{1+t^4}\right) d t{/tex} {tex}=\int 2 t^2\left(\frac{1+t^2}{t^2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{1+t^4}\right) d t{/tex} {tex}=2 \int\left(\frac{1+t^2}{1+t^4}\right) d t{/tex} {tex}=2 \int \frac{\frac{1+t^2}{t^2}}{\frac{1+t^4}{t^2}} d t{/tex} {tex}=2 \int \frac{1+\frac{1}{t^2}}{t^2+\frac{1}{t^2}+2-2} d t{/tex} {tex}I=2 \int \frac{1+\frac{1}{t^2}}{\left(t-\frac{1}{t}\right)^2+(\sqrt{2})^2} d t{/tex} Let {tex}t-\frac{1}{t}=y{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow\left(1+\frac{1}{t^2}\right) d t=d y{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I=2 \int \frac{d y}{y^2+(\sqrt{2})^2}{/tex} {tex}=2\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]+C{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{t-\frac{1}{t}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+C{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{t^2-1}{\sqrt{2} t}\right)+C{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\tan x-1}{\sqrt{2} \sqrt{\tan x}}\right)+C{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\tan x-1}{\sqrt{2 \tan x}}\right)+C{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}=\int(\sqrt{\cot x}+\sqrt{\tan x}) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int(\sqrt{\cot x}+\frac {1}{\sqrt{\cot x}})dx{/tex} [Using tan x = {tex}\frac {1}{cot x}{/tex}] 
{tex}=\int\left(\frac{\cot x+1}{\sqrt{\cot x}}\right) d x{/tex}
I = {tex}\int \sqrt{\tan x}(\cot x+1) d x{/tex} Put tan x = t2
Let tan x = t2
sec2x dx = 2t dt
1 + tan2x = 2t{tex}\frac {dt}{dx}{/tex}
1 + t4 = 2t{tex}\frac {dt}{dx}{/tex}
dx = {tex}\frac{2 t}{\left(1+t^4\right)} d t{/tex}
Now, I {tex}=\int \sqrt{t^2}\left(\frac{1}{t^2}+1\right)\left(\frac{2 t}{1+t^4}\right) d t{/tex}
{tex}=\int t\left(\frac{1}{t^2}+1\right)\left(\frac{2 t}{1+t^4}\right) d t{/tex}
{tex}=\int 2 t^2\left(\frac{1+t^2}{t^2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{1+t^4}\right) d t{/tex}
{tex}=2 \int\left(\frac{1+t^2}{1+t^4}\right) d t{/tex}
{tex}=2 \int \frac{\frac{1+t^2}{t^2}}{\frac{1+t^4}{t^2}} d t{/tex}
{tex}=2 \int \frac{1+\frac{1}{t^2}}{t^2+\frac{1}{t^2}+2-2} d t{/tex}
{tex}I=2 \int \frac{1+\frac{1}{t^2}}{\left(t-\frac{1}{t}\right)^2+(\sqrt{2})^2} d t{/tex}
Let {tex}t-\frac{1}{t}=y{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow\left(1+\frac{1}{t^2}\right) d t=d y{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I=2 \int \frac{d y}{y^2+(\sqrt{2})^2}{/tex}
{tex}=2\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]+C{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{t-\frac{1}{t}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+C{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{2} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{t^2-1}{\sqrt{2} t}\right)+C{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{2} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\tan x-1}{\sqrt{2} \sqrt{\tan x}}\right)+C{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{2} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\tan x-1}{\sqrt{2 \tan x}}\right)+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let I = {tex} \int \frac{(3 x+5)}{\left(x^{3}-x^{2}-x+1\right)} d x{/tex} where (x 3 - x 2 - x + 1) = x 2 (x - 1) - (x - 1) = (x - 1)(x 2 - 1) = (x - 1) 2 (x + 1) Now let {tex}\frac{3 x+5}{\left(x^{3}-x^{2}-x+1\right)}=\frac{3 x+5}{(x-1)^{2}(x+1)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A}{(x-1)}+\frac{B}{(x-1)^{2}}+\frac{C}{(x+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3x + 5) = A(x - 1) (x + 1) + B(x + 1) + C(x - 1) 2 Putting x = 1 on both sides of (i), we get B = 4 Putting x = -1 on both sides of (i), we get C {tex}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Comparing the coefficient of x 2 on both sides of (i), we get A + C = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = -C {tex}=\frac{-1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{(3 x+5)}{\left(x^{3}-x^{2}-x+1\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{2(x-1)}+\frac{4}{(x-1)^{2}}+\frac{1}{2(x+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\int \frac{(3 x+5)}{\left(x^{3}-x^{2}-x+1\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{(x-1)}+4 \int \frac{d x}{(x-1)^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{(x+1)}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \log |x-1|-\frac{4}{(x-1)}+\frac{1}{2} \log |x+1|+C{/tex}
solution: Let I = {tex} \int \frac{(3 x+5)}{\left(x^{3}-x^{2}-x+1\right)} d x{/tex} where (x 3 - x 2 - x + 1) = x 2 (x - 1) - (x - 1) = (x - 1)(x 2 - 1) = (x - 1) 2 (x + 1) Now let {tex}\frac{3 x+5}{\left(x^{3}-x^{2}-x+1\right)}=\frac{3 x+5}{(x-1)^{2}(x+1)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A}{(x-1)}+\frac{B}{(x-1)^{2}}+\frac{C}{(x+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3x + 5) = A(x - 1) (x + 1) + B(x + 1) + C(x - 1) 2 Putting x = 1 on both sides of (i), we get B = 4 Putting x = -1 on both sides of (i), we get C {tex}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Comparing the coefficient of x 2 on both sides of (i), we get A + C = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = -C {tex}=\frac{-1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{(3 x+5)}{\left(x^{3}-x^{2}-x+1\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{2(x-1)}+\frac{4}{(x-1)^{2}}+\frac{1}{2(x+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\int \frac{(3 x+5)}{\left(x^{3}-x^{2}-x+1\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{(x-1)}+4 \int \frac{d x}{(x-1)^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{(x+1)}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \log |x-1|-\frac{4}{(x-1)}+\frac{1}{2} \log |x+1|+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I  =  {tex} \int \frac{(3 x+5)}{\left(x^{3}-x^{2}-x+1\right)} d x{/tex}
where (x3 - x2 - x + 1) = x2(x - 1) - (x - 1) = (x - 1)(x2 - 1) = (x - 1)2(x + 1)
Now let {tex}\frac{3 x+5}{\left(x^{3}-x^{2}-x+1\right)}=\frac{3 x+5}{(x-1)^{2}(x+1)}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{A}{(x-1)}+\frac{B}{(x-1)^{2}}+\frac{C}{(x+1)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3x + 5) = A(x - 1) (x + 1) + B(x + 1) + C(x - 1)2
Putting x = 1 on both sides of (i), we get B = 4
Putting x = -1 on both sides of (i), we get C {tex}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Comparing the coefficient of x2 on both sides of (i), we get
A + C = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = -C {tex}=\frac{-1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{(3 x+5)}{\left(x^{3}-x^{2}-x+1\right)}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{-1}{2(x-1)}+\frac{4}{(x-1)^{2}}+\frac{1}{2(x+1)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\int \frac{(3 x+5)}{\left(x^{3}-x^{2}-x+1\right)} d x{/tex}{tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{(x-1)}+4 \int \frac{d x}{(x-1)^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{(x+1)}{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \log |x-1|-\frac{4}{(x-1)}+\frac{1}{2} \log |x+1|+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log (1+\tan x) d x{/tex} ............. (i) Then, {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log \left[1+\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right)\right] d x{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x\right]{/tex} or {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log \left[1+\frac{1-\tan x}{1+\tan x}\right] d x{/tex} or {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log \left(\frac{2}{1+\tan x}\right) d x{/tex} or {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log \left(\frac{2}{1+\tan x}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\text { or } \quad I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}[\log 2-\log (1+\tan x)] d x{/tex} or {tex}I=(\log 2) \cdot \int_{0}^{\pi / 4} d x-\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log (1+\tan x) d x{/tex} .......... (ii) Adding (I) and (ii), we get {tex}2 I=(\log 2) \int_{0}^{\pi / 4} d x=(\log 2) \cdot[x]_{0}^{\pi / 4}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}(\log 2){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I=\frac{\pi}{8}(\log 2){/tex} , i.e., {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log (1+\tan x) d x=\frac{\pi}{8}(\log 2){/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log (1+\tan x) d x{/tex} ............. (i) Then, {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log \left[1+\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right)\right] d x{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x\right]{/tex} or {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log \left[1+\frac{1-\tan x}{1+\tan x}\right] d x{/tex} or {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log \left(\frac{2}{1+\tan x}\right) d x{/tex} or {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log \left(\frac{2}{1+\tan x}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\text { or } \quad I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}[\log 2-\log (1+\tan x)] d x{/tex} or {tex}I=(\log 2) \cdot \int_{0}^{\pi / 4} d x-\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log (1+\tan x) d x{/tex} .......... (ii) Adding (I) and (ii), we get {tex}2 I=(\log 2) \int_{0}^{\pi / 4} d x=(\log 2) \cdot[x]_{0}^{\pi / 4}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}(\log 2){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I=\frac{\pi}{8}(\log 2){/tex} , i.e., {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log (1+\tan x) d x=\frac{\pi}{8}(\log 2){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log (1+\tan x) d x{/tex} ............. (i)
Then, {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log \left[1+\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right)\right] d x{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x\right]{/tex}
or {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log \left[1+\frac{1-\tan x}{1+\tan x}\right] d x{/tex}
or {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log \left(\frac{2}{1+\tan x}\right) d x{/tex}
or {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log \left(\frac{2}{1+\tan x}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\text { or } \quad I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}[\log 2-\log (1+\tan x)] d x{/tex}
or {tex}I=(\log 2) \cdot \int_{0}^{\pi / 4} d x-\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log (1+\tan x) d x{/tex} .......... (ii)
Adding (I) and (ii), we get
{tex}2 I=(\log 2) \int_{0}^{\pi / 4} d x=(\log 2) \cdot[x]_{0}^{\pi / 4}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}(\log 2){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I=\frac{\pi}{8}(\log 2){/tex}, i.e., {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \log (1+\tan x) d x=\frac{\pi}{8}(\log 2){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { x \sin x } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex}.
question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { x \sin x } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question , {tex}I =\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { x \sin x } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex} .......(i) {tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) \sin ( \pi - x ) } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } ( \pi - x ) } d x{/tex} {tex}[\because \int _0^a f(x)= \int_0^a f(a-x)]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) \sin x } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex} .....(ii) On Adding eqs.(i) and (ii),we get {tex}2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { \pi \sin x } { \left( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x \right) } d x{/tex} Put , {tex}cos x = t{/tex} {tex}-sin x dx = dt{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow sinx dx = -dt{/tex} Lower limit , when x = 0, then t = 1 Upper limit ,when {tex}x= \pi{/tex} , then t = -1 {tex}= - \pi \int _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \frac { d t } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 I = \pi \int _ { - 1 } ^ { 1 } \frac { d t } { \left( 1 + t ^ { 2 } \right) } {/tex} {tex}= \pi \left[ \tan ^ { - 1 } t \right] _ { - 1 } ^ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}= \pi \left[ \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \left( - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) \right] {/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} \therefore I = \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}
solution: According to the question , {tex}I =\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { x \sin x } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex} .......(i) {tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) \sin ( \pi - x ) } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } ( \pi - x ) } d x{/tex} {tex}[\because \int _0^a f(x)= \int_0^a f(a-x)]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) \sin x } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex} .....(ii) On Adding eqs.(i) and (ii),we get {tex}2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { \pi \sin x } { \left( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x \right) } d x{/tex} Put , {tex}cos x = t{/tex} {tex}-sin x dx = dt{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow sinx dx = -dt{/tex} Lower limit , when x = 0, then t = 1 Upper limit ,when {tex}x= \pi{/tex} , then t = -1 {tex}= - \pi \int _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \frac { d t } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 I = \pi \int _ { - 1 } ^ { 1 } \frac { d t } { \left( 1 + t ^ { 2 } \right) } {/tex} {tex}= \pi \left[ \tan ^ { - 1 } t \right] _ { - 1 } ^ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}= \pi \left[ \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \left( - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) \right] {/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} \therefore I = \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question , {tex}I =\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { x \sin x } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex} .......(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) \sin ( \pi - x ) } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } ( \pi - x ) } d x{/tex} {tex}[\because \int _0^a f(x)= \int_0^a f(a-x)]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) \sin x } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex}.....(ii)
On Adding eqs.(i) and (ii),we get
{tex}2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { \pi \sin x } { \left( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x \right) } d x{/tex}
Put , {tex}cos x = t{/tex}
{tex}-sin x dx = dt{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow sinx dx = -dt{/tex}
Lower limit , when x = 0, then t = 1
Upper limit ,when  {tex}x= \pi{/tex}, then t = -1
{tex}= - \pi \int _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \frac { d t } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 I = \pi \int _ { - 1 } ^ { 1 } \frac { d t } { \left( 1 + t ^ { 2 } \right) } {/tex}
{tex}= \pi \left[ \tan ^ { - 1 } t \right] _ { - 1 } ^ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}= \pi \left[ \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \left( - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) \right] {/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore I = \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: According to the question, {tex}I= \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { d x } { 1 + e ^ { \sin x } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { d x } { 1 + e ^ { \sin ( 2 \pi - x ) } } \left[ \because \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( x ) d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( a - x ) d x \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { d x } { 1 + e ^ { - \sin x } } = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { e ^ { \sin x } } { e ^ { \sin x } + 1 } d x{/tex} Adding Equations (i) and (ii), {tex}\Rightarrow I + I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { d x } { 1 + e ^ { \sin x } } + \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { e ^ { \sin x } } { 1 + e ^ { \sin x } } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { \left( 1 + e ^ { \sin x } \right) } { \left( 1 + e ^ { \sin x } \right) } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } 1 d x = [ x ] _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } = 2 \pi - 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = \pi{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}I= \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { d x } { 1 + e ^ { \sin x } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { d x } { 1 + e ^ { \sin ( 2 \pi - x ) } } \left[ \because \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( x ) d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( a - x ) d x \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { d x } { 1 + e ^ { - \sin x } } = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { e ^ { \sin x } } { e ^ { \sin x } + 1 } d x{/tex} Adding Equations (i) and (ii), {tex}\Rightarrow I + I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { d x } { 1 + e ^ { \sin x } } + \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { e ^ { \sin x } } { 1 + e ^ { \sin x } } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { \left( 1 + e ^ { \sin x } \right) } { \left( 1 + e ^ { \sin x } \right) } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } 1 d x = [ x ] _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } = 2 \pi - 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = \pi{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,  {tex}I= \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { d x } { 1 + e ^ { \sin x } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { d x } { 1 + e ^ { \sin ( 2 \pi - x ) } } \left[ \because \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( x ) d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f ( a - x ) d x \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { d x } { 1 + e ^ { - \sin x } } = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { e ^ { \sin x } } { e ^ { \sin x } + 1 } d x{/tex}
Adding Equations (i) and (ii), 
{tex}\Rightarrow I + I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { d x } { 1 + e ^ { \sin x } } + \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { e ^ { \sin x } } { 1 + e ^ { \sin x } } d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { \left( 1 + e ^ { \sin x } \right) } { \left( 1 + e ^ { \sin x } \right) } d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } 1 d x = [ x ] _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } = 2 \pi - 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I = \pi{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: According to the question , {tex} I = \int \frac { d x } { x \left( x ^ { 5 } + 3 \right) } {/tex} Multiplying the numerator and denominator by x 4 , we get {tex}= \int \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { x ^ { 5 } \left( x ^ { 5 } + 3 \right) } d x{/tex} Put {tex} t = x ^ { 5 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow d t = 5 x ^ { 4 } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { d t } { 5 t ( t + 3 ) }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad I = \frac {1}{5}\int \frac { d t } { t ( t + 3 ) }{/tex} ...(i) By using partial fractions , let {tex}\frac { 1 } { t ( t + 3 ) }= \frac {A}{t} + \frac {B}{t+3}{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}1 = A(t+3) + B(t){/tex} {tex}1 = t(A+B)+ 3A{/tex} By comparing the coefficient of t and constant terms , we get {tex}3A=1 \Rightarrow A = \frac {1}{3}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}A+B = 0 {/tex} {tex}\frac {1}{3} + B =0{/tex} {tex}B = -\frac {1}{3}{/tex} from (i) and (ii) , we get {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \int \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left[ \frac { 1 } { t } - \frac { 1 } { t + 3 } \right] d t{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 15 } [ \log | t | - \log | t + 3 | ] + C{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 15 } \log \left| \frac { t } { t + 3 } \right| + C{/tex} {tex}[\because log \ m - log \ n = log \ \frac {m}{n}]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 15 } \log \left| \frac { x ^ { 5 } } { x ^ { 5 } + 3 } \right| + C{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} t = x 5 ]
solution: According to the question , {tex} I = \int \frac { d x } { x \left( x ^ { 5 } + 3 \right) } {/tex} Multiplying the numerator and denominator by x 4 , we get {tex}= \int \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { x ^ { 5 } \left( x ^ { 5 } + 3 \right) } d x{/tex} Put {tex} t = x ^ { 5 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow d t = 5 x ^ { 4 } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { d t } { 5 t ( t + 3 ) }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad I = \frac {1}{5}\int \frac { d t } { t ( t + 3 ) }{/tex} ...(i) By using partial fractions , let {tex}\frac { 1 } { t ( t + 3 ) }= \frac {A}{t} + \frac {B}{t+3}{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}1 = A(t+3) + B(t){/tex} {tex}1 = t(A+B)+ 3A{/tex} By comparing the coefficient of t and constant terms , we get {tex}3A=1 \Rightarrow A = \frac {1}{3}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}A+B = 0 {/tex} {tex}\frac {1}{3} + B =0{/tex} {tex}B = -\frac {1}{3}{/tex} from (i) and (ii) , we get {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \int \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left[ \frac { 1 } { t } - \frac { 1 } { t + 3 } \right] d t{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 15 } [ \log | t | - \log | t + 3 | ] + C{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 15 } \log \left| \frac { t } { t + 3 } \right| + C{/tex} {tex}[\because log \ m - log \ n = log \ \frac {m}{n}]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 15 } \log \left| \frac { x ^ { 5 } } { x ^ { 5 } + 3 } \right| + C{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} t = x 5 ]
solution_html:
According to the question , {tex} I = \int \frac { d x } { x \left( x ^ { 5 } + 3 \right) } {/tex}
Multiplying the numerator and denominator by x4 , we get
{tex}= \int \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { x ^ { 5 } \left( x ^ { 5 } + 3 \right) } d x{/tex}
Put {tex} t = x ^ { 5 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow d t = 5 x ^ { 4 } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { d t } { 5 t ( t + 3 ) }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad I = \frac {1}{5}\int \frac { d t } { t ( t + 3 ) }{/tex} ...(i)
By using partial fractions , 
let {tex}\frac { 1 } { t ( t + 3 ) }= \frac {A}{t} + \frac {B}{t+3}{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}1 = A(t+3) + B(t){/tex}
{tex}1 = t(A+B)+ 3A{/tex}
By comparing the coefficient of t and constant terms , we get 
{tex}3A=1 \Rightarrow A = \frac {1}{3}{/tex}
{tex}{/tex}{tex}A+B = 0 {/tex}
{tex}\frac {1}{3} + B =0{/tex}
{tex}B = -\frac {1}{3}{/tex}
from (i) and (ii) , we get 
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \int \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left[ \frac { 1 } { t } - \frac { 1 } { t + 3 } \right] d t{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 15 } [ \log | t | - \log | t + 3 | ] + C{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 15 } \log \left| \frac { t } { t + 3 } \right| + C{/tex} {tex}[\because log \ m - log \ n = log \ \frac {m}{n}]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 15 } \log \left| \frac { x ^ { 5 } } { x ^ { 5 } + 3 } \right| + C{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex}t = x5]

explanation: According to the question , {tex} I ={/tex} {tex} \int _ { 2 } ^ { 5 } [ | x - 2 | + | x - 3 | + | x - 5 | ]{/tex} dx. Consider , {tex} 2 \leq x < 5 , | x - 2 | = ( x - 2 ){/tex} {tex} 2 \leq x < 3 , | x - 3 | = - ( x - 3 ){/tex} {tex} 3 \leq x < 5 . | x - 3 | = ( x - 3 ){/tex} {tex} 2 \leq x < 5 , | x - 5 | = ( 5 - x ){/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad I = \int _ { 2 } ^ { 5 } [ | x - 2 | + | x - 3 | + | x - 5 | ] d x{/tex} {tex} = \int _ { 2 } ^ { 5 } ( x - 2 ) d x + \int _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } ( 3 - x ) d x + \int _ { 3 } ^ { 5 } ( x - 3 ) d x + \int _ { 2 } ^ { 5 } ( 5 - x ) d x{/tex} {tex} = \left[ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - 2 x \right] _ { 2 } ^ { 5 } + \left[ 3 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } + \left[ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - 3 x \right] _ { 3 } ^ { 5 } + \left[ 5 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 2 } ^ { 5 }{/tex} {tex} = \left[ \left( \frac { 25 } { 2 } - 10 \right) - ( 2 - 4 ) \right] + \left[ \left( 9 - \frac { 9 } { 2 } \right) - ( 6 - 2 ) \right] + \left[ \left( \frac { 25 } { 2 } - 15 \right) - \left( \frac { 9 } { 2 } - 9 \right) \right] + \left[ \left( 25 - \frac { 25 } { 2 } \right) - ( 10 - 2 ) \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ \frac { 25 } { 2 } - 8 \right] + \left[ \frac { 9 } { 2 } - 4 \right] + [ 8 - 6 ] + \left[ \frac { 25 } { 2 } - 8 \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 9 } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } + 2 + \frac { 9 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 19 } { 2 } + 2 {/tex} {tex}= \frac { 23 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore I = \frac { 23 } { 2 }{/tex} sq units.
solution_html:
According to the question , {tex} I ={/tex} {tex} \int _ { 2 } ^ { 5 } [ | x - 2 | + | x - 3 | + | x - 5 | ]{/tex}dx.
Consider ,
{tex} 2 \leq x < 5 , | x - 2 | = ( x - 2 ){/tex}
{tex} 2 \leq x < 3 , | x - 3 | = - ( x - 3 ){/tex}
{tex} 3 \leq x < 5 . | x - 3 | = ( x - 3 ){/tex}
{tex} 2 \leq x < 5 , | x - 5 | = ( 5 - x ){/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad I = \int _ { 2 } ^ { 5 } [ | x - 2 | + | x - 3 | + | x - 5 | ] d x{/tex}
{tex} = \int _ { 2 } ^ { 5 } ( x - 2 ) d x + \int _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } ( 3 - x ) d x + \int _ { 3 } ^ { 5 } ( x - 3 ) d x + \int _ { 2 } ^ { 5 } ( 5 - x ) d x{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - 2 x \right] _ { 2 } ^ { 5 } + \left[ 3 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } + \left[ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - 3 x \right] _ { 3 } ^ { 5 } + \left[ 5 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 2 } ^ { 5 }{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ \left( \frac { 25 } { 2 } - 10 \right) - ( 2 - 4 ) \right] + \left[ \left( 9 - \frac { 9 } { 2 } \right) - ( 6 - 2 ) \right] + \left[ \left( \frac { 25 } { 2 } - 15 \right) - \left( \frac { 9 } { 2 } - 9 \right) \right] + \left[ \left( 25 - \frac { 25 } { 2 } \right) - ( 10 - 2 ) \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ \frac { 25 } { 2 } - 8 \right] + \left[ \frac { 9 } { 2 } - 4 \right] + [ 8 - 6 ] + \left[ \frac { 25 } { 2 } - 8 \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 9 } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } + 2 + \frac { 9 } { 2 }{/tex}

{tex} = \frac { 19 } { 2 } + 2 {/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 23 } { 2 }{/tex}

{tex}\therefore I = \frac { 23 } { 2 }{/tex} sq units.

solution: According to the question , {tex}I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } [ | x | + | x - 2 | + | x - 4 | ] d x{/tex} For, 0< x <4, |x| = x {tex}0 < x \leq 2 , | x - 2 | = - ( x - 2 ){/tex} {tex}2 \leq x < 4 , | x - 2 | = ( x - 2 ){/tex} {tex}0 < x < 4, |x - 4| = -(x -4){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } x d x + \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ( 2 - x ) d x + \int _ { 2 } ^ { 4 } ( x - 2) d x + \int _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } ( 4 - x ) d x{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } + \left[ 2 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + \left[ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - 2 x \right] _ { 2 } ^ { 4 } + \left[ 4 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 4 }{/tex} {tex}= ( 8 ) + [ ( 4 - 2 ) - 0 ] + [ ( 8 - 8 ) - ( 2 - 4 ) ] + \left[ 16 - \frac { 16 } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= 8 + 2 + 2 + (16 - 8){/tex} = 20 {tex}\therefore I = 20{/tex} sq units.

According to the question , {tex}I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } [ | x | + | x - 2 | + | x - 4 | ] d x{/tex}
For,
0< x <4, |x| = x 
{tex}0 < x \leq 2 , | x - 2 | = - ( x - 2 ){/tex}
{tex}2 \leq x < 4 , | x - 2 | = ( x - 2 ){/tex}
{tex}0 < x < 4, |x - 4| = -(x -4){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } x d x + \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ( 2 - x ) d x + \int _ { 2 } ^ { 4 } ( x - 2) d x + \int _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } ( 4 - x ) d x{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } + \left[ 2 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + \left[ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - 2 x \right] _ { 2 } ^ { 4 } + \left[ 4 x - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}= ( 8 ) + [ ( 4 - 2 ) - 0 ] + [ ( 8 - 8 ) - ( 2 - 4 ) ] + \left[ 16 - \frac { 16 } { 2 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= 8 + 2 + 2 + (16 - 8){/tex}
= 20
{tex}\therefore I = 20{/tex} sq units.

question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int e ^ { 2 x } \left( \frac { 1 - \sin 2 x } { 1 - \cos 2 x } \right) d x.{/tex} ​​​​​​​
explanation: According to the question, {tex}I = \int e ^ { 2 x } \left( \frac { 1 - \sin 2 x } { 1 - \cos 2 x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { 2 x } \left( \frac { 1 - 2 \sin x \cos x } { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { \because 1 - \cos 2 x = 2 \sin ^ { 2 } x } \\ { \text { and } \sin 2 x = 2 \sin x \cos x } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int e ^ { 2 x } \left( \ cosec ^ { 2 } x - 2 \cot x \right) d x{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{2}\int {\mathop {{e^{2x}}}\limits_I } {\mathop {\cos ec}\limits_{II} }^2xdx - \int {{e^{2x}}} \cot xdx{/tex} Using integration by parts for first integral : {tex}= \frac{1}{2}\left[ {{e^{2x}}\int {\cos e{c^2}} xdx - \int {\left\{ {\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {{e^{2x}}} \right)\int {\cos e{c^2}} xdx} \right\}} dx} \right]{/tex} {tex}- \int e ^ { 2 x } \cot x d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ - e ^ { 2 x } \cot x + \int 2 e ^ { 2 x } \cot x d x \right] + C - \int e ^ { 2 x } \cot x d x{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } \cot x + \int e ^ { 2 x } \cot x d x - \int e ^ { 2 x } \cot x d x + C{/tex} {tex}I= - \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } \cot x + C{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}I = \int e ^ { 2 x } \left( \frac { 1 - \sin 2 x } { 1 - \cos 2 x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { 2 x } \left( \frac { 1 - 2 \sin x \cos x } { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { \because 1 - \cos 2 x = 2 \sin ^ { 2 } x } \\ { \text { and } \sin 2 x = 2 \sin x \cos x } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int e ^ { 2 x } \left( \ cosec ^ { 2 } x - 2 \cot x \right) d x{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{2}\int {\mathop {{e^{2x}}}\limits_I } {\mathop {\cos ec}\limits_{II} }^2xdx - \int {{e^{2x}}} \cot xdx{/tex} Using integration by parts for first integral : {tex}= \frac{1}{2}\left[ {{e^{2x}}\int {\cos e{c^2}} xdx - \int {\left\{ {\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {{e^{2x}}} \right)\int {\cos e{c^2}} xdx} \right\}} dx} \right]{/tex} {tex}- \int e ^ { 2 x } \cot x d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ - e ^ { 2 x } \cot x + \int 2 e ^ { 2 x } \cot x d x \right] + C - \int e ^ { 2 x } \cot x d x{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } \cot x + \int e ^ { 2 x } \cot x d x - \int e ^ { 2 x } \cot x d x + C{/tex} {tex}I= - \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } \cot x + C{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,  {tex}I = \int e ^ { 2 x } \left( \frac { 1 - \sin 2 x } { 1 - \cos 2 x } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int e ^ { 2 x } \left( \frac { 1 - 2 \sin x \cos x } { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } x } \right) d x{/tex}{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { \because 1 - \cos 2 x = 2 \sin ^ { 2 } x } \\ { \text { and } \sin 2 x = 2 \sin x \cos x } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int e ^ { 2 x } \left( \ cosec ^ { 2 } x - 2 \cot x \right) d x{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{2}\int {\mathop {{e^{2x}}}\limits_I } {\mathop {\cos ec}\limits_{II} }^2xdx - \int {{e^{2x}}} \cot xdx{/tex}

Using integration by parts for first integral :
{tex}= \frac{1}{2}\left[ {{e^{2x}}\int {\cos e{c^2}} xdx - \int {\left\{ {\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {{e^{2x}}} \right)\int {\cos e{c^2}} xdx} \right\}} dx} \right]{/tex}{tex}- \int e ^ { 2 x } \cot x d x{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ - e ^ { 2 x } \cot x + \int 2 e ^ { 2 x } \cot x d x \right] + C - \int e ^ { 2 x } \cot x d x{/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } \cot x + \int e ^ { 2 x } \cot x d x - \int e ^ { 2 x } \cot x d x + C{/tex}
{tex}I= - \frac { e ^ { 2 x } } { 2 } \cot x + C{/tex}

question_html: Evaluate{tex}\int \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + 1 } { x ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 \right) } d x.{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + 1 } { x ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 \right) } d x.{/tex}
explanation: Let, {tex}I = \int \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + 1 } { x ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 \right) } d x{/tex} put {tex}x^2 = t{/tex} and then {tex}\frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + 1 } { x ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 \right) } = \frac { 2 t + 1 } { t ( t + 4 ) }{/tex} Using partial fractions, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 2 t + 1 } { t ( t + 4 ) } = \frac { A } { t } + \frac { B } { t + 4 } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 t + 1 = A ( t + 4 ) + B t{/tex} Comparing the coefficients of t and constant terms, {tex}\Rightarrow 2 = A + B\ and {/tex} {tex}1 = 4 A \Rightarrow A = \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad B = 2 - A = 2 - \frac { 1 } { 4 } = \frac { 7 } { 4 }{/tex} Thus, {tex}\frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + 1 } { x ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 \right) } = \frac { 2 t + 1 } { t ( t + 4 ) } = \frac { \frac{1} {4}} { t } + \frac { \frac{7} {4}} { t + 4 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 t } + \frac { 7 } { 4 \left( t + 4 \right) }{/tex} {tex}=\frac { 1 } { 4 x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 7 } { 4 \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 \right) }{/tex} {tex}[\because x^2 = t]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \int \frac { d x } { x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 7 } { 4 } \int \frac { d x } { x ^ { 2 } + 4 }{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 4 x } + \frac { 7 } { 4 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { 2 } \right) + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { a ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { a } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { a } \right) + C \right]{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 4 x } + \frac { 7 } { 8 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { 2 } \right) + C{/tex}
solution: Let, {tex}I = \int \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + 1 } { x ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 \right) } d x{/tex} put {tex}x^2 = t{/tex} and then {tex}\frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + 1 } { x ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 \right) } = \frac { 2 t + 1 } { t ( t + 4 ) }{/tex} Using partial fractions, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 2 t + 1 } { t ( t + 4 ) } = \frac { A } { t } + \frac { B } { t + 4 } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 t + 1 = A ( t + 4 ) + B t{/tex} Comparing the coefficients of t and constant terms, {tex}\Rightarrow 2 = A + B\ and {/tex} {tex}1 = 4 A \Rightarrow A = \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad B = 2 - A = 2 - \frac { 1 } { 4 } = \frac { 7 } { 4 }{/tex} Thus, {tex}\frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + 1 } { x ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 \right) } = \frac { 2 t + 1 } { t ( t + 4 ) } = \frac { \frac{1} {4}} { t } + \frac { \frac{7} {4}} { t + 4 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 t } + \frac { 7 } { 4 \left( t + 4 \right) }{/tex} {tex}=\frac { 1 } { 4 x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 7 } { 4 \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 \right) }{/tex} {tex}[\because x^2 = t]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \int \frac { d x } { x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 7 } { 4 } \int \frac { d x } { x ^ { 2 } + 4 }{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 4 x } + \frac { 7 } { 4 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { 2 } \right) + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { a ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { a } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { a } \right) + C \right]{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 4 x } + \frac { 7 } { 8 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { 2 } \right) + C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let,  {tex}I = \int \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + 1 } { x ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 \right) } d x{/tex}
put {tex}x^2 = t{/tex} and then {tex}\frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + 1 } { x ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 \right) } = \frac { 2 t + 1 } { t ( t + 4 ) }{/tex}
Using partial fractions,
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 2 t + 1 } { t ( t + 4 ) } = \frac { A } { t } + \frac { B } { t + 4 } {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 t + 1 = A ( t + 4 ) + B t{/tex}
Comparing the coefficients of t and constant terms,
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 = A + B\ and {/tex}{tex}1 = 4 A \Rightarrow A = \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad B = 2 - A = 2 - \frac { 1 } { 4 } = \frac { 7 } { 4 }{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + 1 } { x ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 \right) } = \frac { 2 t + 1 } { t ( t + 4 ) } = \frac { \frac{1} {4}} { t } + \frac { \frac{7} {4}} { t + 4 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 t } + \frac { 7 } { 4 \left( t + 4 \right) }{/tex}
{tex}=\frac { 1 } { 4 x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 7 } { 4 \left( x ^ { 2 } + 4 \right) }{/tex}{tex}[\because x^2 = t]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \int \frac { d x } { x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 7 } { 4 } \int \frac { d x } { x ^ { 2 } + 4 }{/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 4 x } + \frac { 7 } { 4 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { 2 } \right) + C{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { a ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { a } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { a } \right) + C \right]{/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 4 x } + \frac { 7 } { 8 } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { 2 } \right) + C{/tex}

explanation: Given, {tex} I = \int \frac { \cos 2 x - \cos 2 \alpha } { \cos x - \cos \alpha } d x{/tex} {tex} = \int \frac { \left( 2 \cos ^ { 2 } x - 1 \right) - \left( 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \alpha - 1 \right) } { \cos x - \cos \alpha } d x{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \cos 2 \theta = 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta - 1 \right]{/tex} {tex} = \int \frac { 2 \left( \cos ^ { 2 } x - \cos ^ { 2 } \alpha \right) } { ( \cos x - \cos \alpha ) } d x{/tex} {tex} = \int \frac { 2 ( \cos x - \cos \alpha ) ( \cos x + \cos \alpha ) } { ( \cos x - \cos \alpha ) } d x{/tex} {tex}[\because {/tex} {tex} a^2 - b^2 = (a + b) (a - b)]{/tex} {tex} = \int 2 ( \cos x + \cos \alpha ) d x{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} here {tex}cos\alpha{/tex} is a constant ] {tex} \therefore I = 2(sinx +cos\alpha \times x)+C{/tex} {tex} \therefore I = 2(sinx + x \cos \alpha)+C{/tex}
solution: Given, {tex} I = \int \frac { \cos 2 x - \cos 2 \alpha } { \cos x - \cos \alpha } d x{/tex} {tex} = \int \frac { \left( 2 \cos ^ { 2 } x - 1 \right) - \left( 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \alpha - 1 \right) } { \cos x - \cos \alpha } d x{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \cos 2 \theta = 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta - 1 \right]{/tex} {tex} = \int \frac { 2 \left( \cos ^ { 2 } x - \cos ^ { 2 } \alpha \right) } { ( \cos x - \cos \alpha ) } d x{/tex} {tex} = \int \frac { 2 ( \cos x - \cos \alpha ) ( \cos x + \cos \alpha ) } { ( \cos x - \cos \alpha ) } d x{/tex} {tex}[\because {/tex} {tex} a^2 - b^2 = (a + b) (a - b)]{/tex} {tex} = \int 2 ( \cos x + \cos \alpha ) d x{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} here {tex}cos\alpha{/tex} is a constant ] {tex} \therefore I = 2(sinx +cos\alpha \times x)+C{/tex} {tex} \therefore I = 2(sinx + x \cos \alpha)+C{/tex}

Given,  {tex} I = \int \frac { \cos 2 x - \cos 2 \alpha } { \cos x - \cos \alpha } d x{/tex}
{tex} = \int \frac { \left( 2 \cos ^ { 2 } x - 1 \right) - \left( 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \alpha - 1 \right) } { \cos x - \cos \alpha } d x{/tex}{tex} \left[ \because \cos 2 \theta = 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta - 1 \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \int \frac { 2 \left( \cos ^ { 2 } x - \cos ^ { 2 } \alpha \right) } { ( \cos x - \cos \alpha ) } d x{/tex}
{tex} = \int \frac { 2 ( \cos x - \cos \alpha ) ( \cos x + \cos \alpha ) } { ( \cos x - \cos \alpha ) } d x{/tex}{tex}[\because {/tex} {tex} a^2 - b^2 = (a + b) (a - b)]{/tex}
{tex} = \int 2 ( \cos x + \cos \alpha ) d x{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex}here {tex}cos\alpha{/tex} is a constant ]
{tex} \therefore I = 2(sinx +cos\alpha \times x)+C{/tex}

{tex} \therefore I = 2(sinx + x \cos \alpha)+C{/tex}

question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int \frac { \sqrt { 1 - \sin x } } { 1 + \cos x } e ^ { \frac { - x } { 2 } } d x.{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int \frac { \sqrt { 1 - \sin x } } { 1 + \cos x } e ^ { \frac { - x } { 2 } } d x.{/tex}
explanation: Given, {tex}I=\int \frac { \sqrt { 1 - \sin x } } { 1 + \cos x } \cdot e ^ { \frac { - x } { 2 } } d x{/tex} Let {tex}\frac { - x } { 2 } = t \Rightarrow d x = - 2 d t{/tex} {tex} I = \int \frac { \sqrt { 1 - \sin ( - 2 t ) } } { 1 + \cos ( - 2 t ) } e ^ { t } ( - 2 d t ){/tex} {tex}[\because x = -2t]{/tex} {tex}= - 2 \int e ^ { t } \frac { \sqrt { 1 + \sin 2 t } } { 1 + \cos 2 t } d t{/tex} {tex}[ \because \sin ( - \theta ) = - \sin \theta \text { and } \cos ( - \theta ) = \cos \theta ]{/tex} {tex}= - 2 \int e ^ { t } \frac { \sqrt { cos^2x+sin^2x+2sinx cosx } } { 1 + \cos 2 t } d t [\because cos^2x+sin^2x=1 , sin2x=2sinx cosx ]{/tex} {tex}= - 2 \int e ^ { t } \left( \frac { \sqrt { ( \cos t + \sin t ) ^ { 2 } } } { 2 \cos ^ { 2 } t } \right) d t [\because (a+b)^2 =a^2+b^2+2ab]{/tex} {tex}= - 2 \int e ^ { t } \left( \frac { \cos t + \sin t } { 2 \cos ^ { 2 } t } \right) d t{/tex} {tex}= - \int e ^ { t } ( \sec t + \tan t \sec t ) d t{/tex} {tex}[\because{1\over cosx} = secx , {sinx\over cosx }= tanx]{/tex} we know that , {tex}\int e ^ { t } \left[ f ( t ) + f ^ { \prime } ( t ) \right] d t = e^tf(t)+C{/tex} Now, consider {tex}f(t) = sec\ t{/tex} then {tex}f'(t) = sec\ t\ tan\ t{/tex} {tex}\therefore I= e ^ { t} \sec t + C{/tex} {tex}= - e ^ { - x / 2 } \sec \frac { x } { 2 } + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because t = \frac { - x } { 2 } \text { and } \sec ( - \theta ) = \sec \theta \right]{/tex} {tex}I = - e ^ { - x / 2 } \sec \frac { x } { 2 } + C{/tex}
solution: Given, {tex}I=\int \frac { \sqrt { 1 - \sin x } } { 1 + \cos x } \cdot e ^ { \frac { - x } { 2 } } d x{/tex} Let {tex}\frac { - x } { 2 } = t \Rightarrow d x = - 2 d t{/tex} {tex} I = \int \frac { \sqrt { 1 - \sin ( - 2 t ) } } { 1 + \cos ( - 2 t ) } e ^ { t } ( - 2 d t ){/tex} {tex}[\because x = -2t]{/tex} {tex}= - 2 \int e ^ { t } \frac { \sqrt { 1 + \sin 2 t } } { 1 + \cos 2 t } d t{/tex} {tex}[ \because \sin ( - \theta ) = - \sin \theta \text { and } \cos ( - \theta ) = \cos \theta ]{/tex} {tex}= - 2 \int e ^ { t } \frac { \sqrt { cos^2x+sin^2x+2sinx cosx } } { 1 + \cos 2 t } d t [\because cos^2x+sin^2x=1 , sin2x=2sinx cosx ]{/tex} {tex}= - 2 \int e ^ { t } \left( \frac { \sqrt { ( \cos t + \sin t ) ^ { 2 } } } { 2 \cos ^ { 2 } t } \right) d t [\because (a+b)^2 =a^2+b^2+2ab]{/tex} {tex}= - 2 \int e ^ { t } \left( \frac { \cos t + \sin t } { 2 \cos ^ { 2 } t } \right) d t{/tex} {tex}= - \int e ^ { t } ( \sec t + \tan t \sec t ) d t{/tex} {tex}[\because{1\over cosx} = secx , {sinx\over cosx }= tanx]{/tex} we know that , {tex}\int e ^ { t } \left[ f ( t ) + f ^ { \prime } ( t ) \right] d t = e^tf(t)+C{/tex} Now, consider {tex}f(t) = sec\ t{/tex} then {tex}f'(t) = sec\ t\ tan\ t{/tex} {tex}\therefore I= e ^ { t} \sec t + C{/tex} {tex}= - e ^ { - x / 2 } \sec \frac { x } { 2 } + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because t = \frac { - x } { 2 } \text { and } \sec ( - \theta ) = \sec \theta \right]{/tex} {tex}I = - e ^ { - x / 2 } \sec \frac { x } { 2 } + C{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, {tex}I=\int \frac { \sqrt { 1 - \sin x } } { 1 + \cos x } \cdot e ^ { \frac { - x } { 2 } } d x{/tex}
Let {tex}\frac { - x } { 2 } = t \Rightarrow d x = - 2 d t{/tex}
{tex} I = \int \frac { \sqrt { 1 - \sin ( - 2 t ) } } { 1 + \cos ( - 2 t ) } e ^ { t } ( - 2 d t ){/tex} {tex}[\because x = -2t]{/tex}
{tex}= - 2 \int e ^ { t } \frac { \sqrt { 1 + \sin 2 t } } { 1 + \cos 2 t } d t{/tex}{tex}[ \because \sin ( - \theta ) = - \sin \theta \text { and } \cos ( - \theta ) = \cos \theta ]{/tex}

{tex}= - 2 \int e ^ { t } \frac { \sqrt { cos^2x+sin^2x+2sinx cosx } } { 1 + \cos 2 t } d t [\because cos^2x+sin^2x=1 , sin2x=2sinx cosx ]{/tex}
{tex}= - 2 \int e ^ { t } \left( \frac { \sqrt { ( \cos t + \sin t ) ^ { 2 } } } { 2 \cos ^ { 2 } t } \right) d t [\because (a+b)^2 =a^2+b^2+2ab]{/tex}
{tex}= - 2 \int e ^ { t } \left( \frac { \cos t + \sin t } { 2 \cos ^ { 2 } t } \right) d t{/tex}
{tex}= - \int e ^ { t } ( \sec t + \tan t \sec t ) d t{/tex}{tex}[\because{1\over cosx} = secx , {sinx\over cosx }= tanx]{/tex}

we know that , {tex}\int e ^ { t } \left[ f ( t ) + f ^ { \prime } ( t ) \right] d t = e^tf(t)+C{/tex}

Now, consider {tex}f(t) = sec\ t{/tex}

then {tex}f'(t) = sec\ t\ tan\ t{/tex}
{tex}\therefore I= e ^ { t} \sec t + C{/tex}
{tex}= - e ^ { - x / 2 } \sec \frac { x } { 2 } + C{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because t = \frac { - x } { 2 } \text { and } \sec ( - \theta ) = \sec \theta \right]{/tex}

{tex}I = - e ^ { - x / 2 } \sec \frac { x } { 2 } + C{/tex}

question_html: Evaluate {tex} \int \frac { 3 x + 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } ( x + 3 ) } d x.{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate {tex} \int \frac { 3 x + 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } ( x + 3 ) } d x.{/tex}
explanation: Given, {tex}I =\int \frac { 3 x + 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } ( x + 3 ) } d x{/tex} By using partial fractions , let {tex}\frac { 3 x + 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } ( x + 3 ) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { A } { ( x + 1 ) } + \frac { B } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } + \frac { C } { ( x + 3 ) }{/tex} .....(i) {tex}\Rightarrow 3x + 1 = A(x + 1) (x + 3) + B(x + 3) + C(x + 1)^2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3x + 1 = A(x^2 + 4x + 3) + B(x + 3) + C(x^2 + 1 + 2x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3x + 1 = (A + C)x^2 + (4A + B + 2C)x + 3A + 3B + C{/tex} Comparing coefficient of {tex}x^2 , x{/tex} and constant terms from both sides we get , {tex}\Rightarrow A + C = 0{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\Rightarrow 4A + B + 2C = 3{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\Rightarrow 3A + 3B + C = 1{/tex} ...(iv) On solving equations (ii) , (iii) and (iv), we get A = 2, B = -1 and C = -2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation (i) becomes {tex}\frac { 3 x + 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } ( x + 3 ) } = \frac { 2 } { ( x + 1 ) } - \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 2 } { ( x + 3 ) }{/tex} Integrating both sides w.r.t. x we get , {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac { 3 x + 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } ( x + 3 ) } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { 2 } { ( x + 1 ) } d x - \int \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x - \int \frac { 2 } { ( x + 3 ) } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = 2 \log | x + 1 | - \frac { ( x + 1 ) ^ { - 2 + 1 } } { ( - 2 + 1 ) } - 2 \log | x + 3 | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = 2 \log | x + 1 | - 2 \log | x + 3 |+ { ( x + 1 ) ^ { - 1 } } { } + C{/tex} {tex}= 2 \log \left| \frac { x + 1 } { x + 3 } \right| + \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) } + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \frac { m } { n } \right]{/tex}
solution: Given, {tex}I =\int \frac { 3 x + 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } ( x + 3 ) } d x{/tex} By using partial fractions , let {tex}\frac { 3 x + 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } ( x + 3 ) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { A } { ( x + 1 ) } + \frac { B } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } + \frac { C } { ( x + 3 ) }{/tex} .....(i) {tex}\Rightarrow 3x + 1 = A(x + 1) (x + 3) + B(x + 3) + C(x + 1)^2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3x + 1 = A(x^2 + 4x + 3) + B(x + 3) + C(x^2 + 1 + 2x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3x + 1 = (A + C)x^2 + (4A + B + 2C)x + 3A + 3B + C{/tex} Comparing coefficient of {tex}x^2 , x{/tex} and constant terms from both sides we get , {tex}\Rightarrow A + C = 0{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\Rightarrow 4A + B + 2C = 3{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\Rightarrow 3A + 3B + C = 1{/tex} ...(iv) On solving equations (ii) , (iii) and (iv), we get A = 2, B = -1 and C = -2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation (i) becomes {tex}\frac { 3 x + 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } ( x + 3 ) } = \frac { 2 } { ( x + 1 ) } - \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 2 } { ( x + 3 ) }{/tex} Integrating both sides w.r.t. x we get , {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac { 3 x + 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } ( x + 3 ) } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { 2 } { ( x + 1 ) } d x - \int \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x - \int \frac { 2 } { ( x + 3 ) } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = 2 \log | x + 1 | - \frac { ( x + 1 ) ^ { - 2 + 1 } } { ( - 2 + 1 ) } - 2 \log | x + 3 | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = 2 \log | x + 1 | - 2 \log | x + 3 |+ { ( x + 1 ) ^ { - 1 } } { } + C{/tex} {tex}= 2 \log \left| \frac { x + 1 } { x + 3 } \right| + \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) } + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \frac { m } { n } \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, {tex}I =\int \frac { 3 x + 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } ( x + 3 ) } d x{/tex}

By using partial fractions ,
 let {tex}\frac { 3 x + 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } ( x + 3 ) }{/tex}{tex}= \frac { A } { ( x + 1 ) } + \frac { B } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } + \frac { C } { ( x + 3 ) }{/tex} .....(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow 3x + 1 = A(x + 1) (x + 3) + B(x + 3) + C(x + 1)^2{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 3x + 1 = A(x^2 + 4x + 3) + B(x + 3) + C(x^2 + 1 + 2x){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 3x + 1 = (A + C)x^2 + (4A + B + 2C)x + 3A + 3B + C{/tex} 
Comparing coefficient of {tex}x^2 , x{/tex} and constant terms from both sides we get ,
{tex}\Rightarrow A + C = 0{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow 4A + B + 2C = 3{/tex} ...(iii)
{tex}\Rightarrow 3A + 3B + C = 1{/tex} ...(iv)
On solving equations (ii) , (iii) and (iv), we get A = 2, B = -1 and C = -2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation (i) becomes
{tex}\frac { 3 x + 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } ( x + 3 ) } = \frac { 2 } { ( x + 1 ) } - \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 2 } { ( x + 3 ) }{/tex}
Integrating both sides w.r.t. x we get ,
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac { 3 x + 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } ( x + 3 ) } d x{/tex}{tex}= \int \frac { 2 } { ( x + 1 ) } d x - \int \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x - \int \frac { 2 } { ( x + 3 ) } d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = 2 \log | x + 1 | - \frac { ( x + 1 ) ^ { - 2 + 1 } } { ( - 2 + 1 ) } - 2 \log | x + 3 | + C{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = 2 \log | x + 1 | - 2 \log | x + 3 |+ { ( x + 1 ) ^ { - 1 } } { } + C{/tex}
{tex}= 2 \log \left| \frac { x + 1 } { x + 3 } \right| + \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) } + C{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \frac { m } { n } \right]{/tex}

explanation: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x+2}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}}{/tex} x + 2 = {tex}A \frac{d}{d x}{/tex} [x 2 + 2x + 3] + B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 2 = 2Ax + 2A + B Comparing the coefficients, we have, 2A = 1 and 2A + B = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Substituting the value of A in 2 A + B = 2, we have, {tex}2 \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} + B = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 + B = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = 2 - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = 1 Thus we have, x + 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}[2 x+2]+1{/tex} Hence, using values of A , and B, we have {tex}I=\int \frac{x+2}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}} d x{/tex} {tex}\left.=\int \frac{\left[\frac{1}{2}[2 x+2]+1\right]}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}}\right] d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{\left[\frac{1}{2}[2 x+2]\right]}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}} d x+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{[2 x+2]}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}} d x+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}}{/tex} Substituting t = x 2 + 2x + 3 and dt = 2 x + 2 in the first integrand, we have, I = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{t}}+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \sqrt{t}+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+1+2}}+C{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{t}+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{(x+1)^{2}+(\sqrt{2})^{2}}}+c{/tex} I = {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}{/tex} + log [|x + 1| + {tex}\sqrt{(x+1)^{2}+(\sqrt{2})^{2}}]+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}{/tex} + log [|x + 1| {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}{/tex} ] + c
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x+2}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}}{/tex} x + 2 = {tex}A \frac{d}{d x}{/tex} [x 2 + 2x + 3] + B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 2 = 2Ax + 2A + B Comparing the coefficients, we have, 2A = 1 and 2A + B = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Substituting the value of A in 2 A + B = 2, we have, {tex}2 \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} + B = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 + B = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = 2 - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = 1 Thus we have, x + 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}[2 x+2]+1{/tex} Hence, using values of A , and B, we have {tex}I=\int \frac{x+2}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}} d x{/tex} {tex}\left.=\int \frac{\left[\frac{1}{2}[2 x+2]+1\right]}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}}\right] d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{\left[\frac{1}{2}[2 x+2]\right]}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}} d x+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{[2 x+2]}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}} d x+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}}{/tex} Substituting t = x 2 + 2x + 3 and dt = 2 x + 2 in the first integrand, we have, I = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{t}}+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \sqrt{t}+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+1+2}}+C{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{t}+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{(x+1)^{2}+(\sqrt{2})^{2}}}+c{/tex} I = {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}{/tex} + log [|x + 1| + {tex}\sqrt{(x+1)^{2}+(\sqrt{2})^{2}}]+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}{/tex} + log [|x + 1| {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}{/tex} ] + c
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x+2}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}}{/tex}
x + 2 = {tex}A \frac{d}{d x}{/tex} [x2 + 2x + 3] + B
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 2 = 2Ax + 2A + B
Comparing the coefficients, we have, 2A = 1 and 2A + B = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Substituting the value of A in 2 A + B = 2, we have, {tex}2 \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} + B = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 + B = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = 2 - 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = 1
Thus we have, x + 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}[2 x+2]+1{/tex}
Hence, using values of A ,  and B, we have
{tex}I=\int \frac{x+2}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}} d x{/tex}
{tex}\left.=\int \frac{\left[\frac{1}{2}[2 x+2]+1\right]}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}}\right] d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int \frac{\left[\frac{1}{2}[2 x+2]\right]}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}} d x+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{[2 x+2]}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}} d x+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}}{/tex}
Substituting t = x2 + 2x + 3 and dt = 2 x + 2
in the first integrand, we have, I = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{t}}+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \sqrt{t}+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+1+2}}+C{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{t}+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{(x+1)^{2}+(\sqrt{2})^{2}}}+c{/tex}
I = {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}{/tex} + log [|x + 1| + {tex}\sqrt{(x+1)^{2}+(\sqrt{2})^{2}}]+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}{/tex} + log [|x + 1| {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+3}{/tex}] + c

explanation: Consider the integral I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)} d x{/tex} Let y = x 2 Thus, {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y+1}{(y+4)(y+25)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y+1}{(y+4)(y+25)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{y+4}+\frac{B}{y+25}{/tex} by using partial fraction {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y+1}{(y+4)(y+25)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A(y+25)+B(y+4)}{(y+4)(y+25)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y + 1 = Ay + 25A + By + 4B Comparing the coefficients,we have, A + B = 1 and 25A + 4B = 1 Solving the above equation, We have, A = {tex}\frac{-1}{7}{/tex} and B = {tex}\frac{8}{7}{/tex} Thus using values of A, and B, we get, I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\frac{-1}{7}}{x^{2}+4} d x{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{\frac{8}{7}}{x^{2}+25} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{7} \int \frac{1}{x^{2}+4} d x{/tex} {tex}+\frac{8}{7} \int \frac{1}{x^{2}+25} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{7} \times \frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{8}{7} \times \frac{1}{5} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{5}+C{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{14} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}+\frac{8}{35}{/tex} {tex}\tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{5}+c{/tex}
solution: Consider the integral I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)} d x{/tex} Let y = x 2 Thus, {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y+1}{(y+4)(y+25)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y+1}{(y+4)(y+25)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{y+4}+\frac{B}{y+25}{/tex} by using partial fraction {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y+1}{(y+4)(y+25)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A(y+25)+B(y+4)}{(y+4)(y+25)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y + 1 = Ay + 25A + By + 4B Comparing the coefficients,we have, A + B = 1 and 25A + 4B = 1 Solving the above equation, We have, A = {tex}\frac{-1}{7}{/tex} and B = {tex}\frac{8}{7}{/tex} Thus using values of A, and B, we get, I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\frac{-1}{7}}{x^{2}+4} d x{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{\frac{8}{7}}{x^{2}+25} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{7} \int \frac{1}{x^{2}+4} d x{/tex} {tex}+\frac{8}{7} \int \frac{1}{x^{2}+25} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{7} \times \frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{8}{7} \times \frac{1}{5} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{5}+C{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{14} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}+\frac{8}{35}{/tex} {tex}\tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{5}+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Consider the integral
I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)} d x{/tex}
Let y = x2
Thus,
{tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y+1}{(y+4)(y+25)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y+1}{(y+4)(y+25)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{y+4}+\frac{B}{y+25}{/tex}

by using partial fraction
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y+1}{(y+4)(y+25)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A(y+25)+B(y+4)}{(y+4)(y+25)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y + 1 = Ay + 25A + By + 4B
Comparing the coefficients,we have,
A + B = 1 and 25A + 4B = 1
Solving the above equation, We have,
A = {tex}\frac{-1}{7}{/tex} and B = {tex}\frac{8}{7}{/tex}
Thus using values of A, and B, we get, I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int \frac{\frac{-1}{7}}{x^{2}+4} d x{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{\frac{8}{7}}{x^{2}+25} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-1}{7} \int \frac{1}{x^{2}+4} d x{/tex} {tex}+\frac{8}{7} \int \frac{1}{x^{2}+25} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-1}{7} \times \frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{8}{7} \times \frac{1}{5} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{5}+C{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-1}{14} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}+\frac{8}{35}{/tex} {tex}\tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{5}+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let {tex}I=\int \frac{3 x+5}{x^{3}-x^{2}-x+1} d x{/tex} Also let {tex}\frac{3 x+5}{(x-1)^{2}(x+1)}={/tex} {tex}\frac{A}{x-1}+\frac{B}{(x-1)^{2}}+\frac{C}{x+1}{/tex} 3x + 5 = A(x - 1)(x + 1) + B(x + 1) + C(x - 1) 2 For x = 1, B = 4 For x = -1, C = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} For x = 0, A = - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} , Therefore we have {tex}I=\int \frac{3 x+5}{(x-1)^{2}(x+1)} d x={/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x-1}+4{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{d x}{(x-1)^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \ln |(x-1)|-\frac{4}{(x-1)}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2} \ln |(x+1)|+c{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \ln \left|\frac{x+1}{x-1}\right|-\frac{4}{(x-1)}+c{/tex} .
solution: Let {tex}I=\int \frac{3 x+5}{x^{3}-x^{2}-x+1} d x{/tex} Also let {tex}\frac{3 x+5}{(x-1)^{2}(x+1)}={/tex} {tex}\frac{A}{x-1}+\frac{B}{(x-1)^{2}}+\frac{C}{x+1}{/tex} 3x + 5 = A(x - 1)(x + 1) + B(x + 1) + C(x - 1) 2 For x = 1, B = 4 For x = -1, C = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} For x = 0, A = - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} , Therefore we have {tex}I=\int \frac{3 x+5}{(x-1)^{2}(x+1)} d x={/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x-1}+4{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{d x}{(x-1)^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \ln |(x-1)|-\frac{4}{(x-1)}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2} \ln |(x+1)|+c{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \ln \left|\frac{x+1}{x-1}\right|-\frac{4}{(x-1)}+c{/tex} .

Let {tex}I=\int \frac{3 x+5}{x^{3}-x^{2}-x+1} d x{/tex}
Also let {tex}\frac{3 x+5}{(x-1)^{2}(x+1)}={/tex}{tex}\frac{A}{x-1}+\frac{B}{(x-1)^{2}}+\frac{C}{x+1}{/tex}
3x + 5 = A(x - 1)(x + 1) + B(x + 1) + C(x - 1)2
For x = 1, B = 4
For x = -1, C = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
For x = 0, A = -{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex},
Therefore we have
{tex}I=\int \frac{3 x+5}{(x-1)^{2}(x+1)} d x={/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x-1}+4{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{d x}{(x-1)^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex}

= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \ln \left|\frac{x+1}{x-1}\right|-\frac{4}{(x-1)}+c{/tex}.

explanation: Solving this by using partial fractions Let {tex}\frac{8}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{x+2}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+4}{/tex} ......(i) Then, 8 = A (x 2 + 4) + (Bx + C) (x + 2) ......(ii) Putting x + 2 = 0 i.e. x = - 2 in (ii), we get 8 = 8A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = 1 Putting x = 0 and 1 respectively in (ii), we get 8 = 4A + 2C and 8 = 5A + 3B + 3C Solving these equations, we obtain A = 1, C = 2 and B = - 1 Substituting the values of A, B and C in (i), we obtain {tex}\frac{8}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x+2}+\frac{-x+2}{x^{2}+4}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{8}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+4\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{x+2} d x{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{-x+2}{x^{2}+4} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{x+2} d x{/tex} - {tex}\int \frac{x}{x^{2}+4} d x{/tex} + {tex}2 \int \frac{1}{x^{2}+4} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = log Ix + 2| - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{t} d t{/tex} + {tex}2 \times \frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}{/tex} + C = log |x + 2| - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log (x 2 + 4) + tan -1 {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex} + C
solution: Solving this by using partial fractions Let {tex}\frac{8}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{x+2}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+4}{/tex} ......(i) Then, 8 = A (x 2 + 4) + (Bx + C) (x + 2) ......(ii) Putting x + 2 = 0 i.e. x = - 2 in (ii), we get 8 = 8A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = 1 Putting x = 0 and 1 respectively in (ii), we get 8 = 4A + 2C and 8 = 5A + 3B + 3C Solving these equations, we obtain A = 1, C = 2 and B = - 1 Substituting the values of A, B and C in (i), we obtain {tex}\frac{8}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x+2}+\frac{-x+2}{x^{2}+4}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{8}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+4\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{x+2} d x{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{-x+2}{x^{2}+4} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{x+2} d x{/tex} - {tex}\int \frac{x}{x^{2}+4} d x{/tex} + {tex}2 \int \frac{1}{x^{2}+4} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = log Ix + 2| - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{t} d t{/tex} + {tex}2 \times \frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}{/tex} + C = log |x + 2| - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log (x 2 + 4) + tan -1 {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex} + C
solution_html:
Solving this by using partial fractions

Let {tex}\frac{8}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{x+2}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+4}{/tex} ......(i)
Then, 8 = A (x2 + 4) + (Bx + C) (x + 2) ......(ii)
Putting x + 2 = 0 i.e. x = - 2 in (ii), we get
8 = 8A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = 1
Putting x = 0 and 1 respectively in (ii), we get
8 = 4A + 2C and 8 = 5A + 3B + 3C
Solving these equations, we obtain
A = 1, C = 2 and B = - 1
Substituting the values of A, B and C in (i), we obtain
{tex}\frac{8}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x+2}+\frac{-x+2}{x^{2}+4}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{8}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+4\right)} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{x+2} d x{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{-x+2}{x^{2}+4} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{x+2} d x{/tex} - {tex}\int \frac{x}{x^{2}+4} d x{/tex} + {tex}2 \int \frac{1}{x^{2}+4} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = log Ix + 2| - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{t} d t{/tex} + {tex}2 \times \frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}{/tex} + C
= log |x + 2| - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}log (x2 + 4) + tan-1 {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex} + C

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{18}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+4\right)} d x{/tex} Also let {tex}\frac{18}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{x+2}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 18 = A (x 2 + 4) + (Bx + C) (x + 2) 18 = (A + B)x 2 + (2B + C) x + (4A + 2C) Equating similar terms, we get A + B = 0, 2B + C = 0, 4A + 2C = 18 Solving, we get, A = {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex} , B = - {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex} , C = {tex}\frac{9}{2}{/tex} Thus, I = {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x+2}+\left(-\frac{9}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\int \frac{x}{x^{2}+4} d x+\frac{9}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+4}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex} {tex}\log |x+2|-\frac{9}{8} \log | x^{2}+{/tex} {tex}4 |+\frac{9}{4} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+c{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+a^{2}}=\frac{1}{a} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{9}\right]{/tex} .
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{18}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+4\right)} d x{/tex} Also let {tex}\frac{18}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{x+2}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 18 = A (x 2 + 4) + (Bx + C) (x + 2) 18 = (A + B)x 2 + (2B + C) x + (4A + 2C) Equating similar terms, we get A + B = 0, 2B + C = 0, 4A + 2C = 18 Solving, we get, A = {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex} , B = - {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex} , C = {tex}\frac{9}{2}{/tex} Thus, I = {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x+2}+\left(-\frac{9}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\int \frac{x}{x^{2}+4} d x+\frac{9}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+4}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex} {tex}\log |x+2|-\frac{9}{8} \log | x^{2}+{/tex} {tex}4 |+\frac{9}{4} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+c{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+a^{2}}=\frac{1}{a} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{9}\right]{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \frac{18}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+4\right)} d x{/tex}
Also let {tex}\frac{18}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{x+2}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 18 = A (x2 + 4) + (Bx + C) (x + 2)
18 = (A + B)x2 + (2B + C) x + (4A + 2C)
Equating similar terms, we get
A + B = 0, 2B + C = 0, 4A + 2C = 18
Solving, we get, A = {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex}, B = -{tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex}, C = {tex}\frac{9}{2}{/tex}
Thus,
I = {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex}{tex}\int \frac{d x}{x+2}+\left(-\frac{9}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\int \frac{x}{x^{2}+4} d x+\frac{9}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+4}{/tex}
I = {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex} {tex}\log |x+2|-\frac{9}{8} \log | x^{2}+{/tex}{tex}4 |+\frac{9}{4} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+c{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+a^{2}}=\frac{1}{a} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{9}\right]{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
explanation: The given functions can be written as {tex}|x|=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} x, & x \geq 0 \\ -x, & x<0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}|x-2|=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} x-2, & x \geq 2 \\ 2-x, & x<2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}|x-4|=\left\{\begin{array}{l} x-4, x \geq 4 \\ 4-x, x<4 \end{array}\right.{/tex} Splitting the limits of the integral, we get {tex}\int_{0}^{4}(|x|+|x-2|+|x-4|) d x{/tex} {tex}\left.=\int_{0}^{2}(|x|+|x-2|+|x-4|) d x+\int_{2}^{4}|| x|+| x-2|+| x-4 |\right\} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{2}(x+2-x+4-x) d x+\int_{2}^{4}(x+x-2+4-x) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{2}(6-x) d x+\int_{2}^{4}(2+x) d x{/tex} {tex}=\left[6 x-\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{2}+\left[2 x+\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{2}^{4}{/tex} = [12 - 2] + [16 - 6] = 10 + 10 = 20
solution: The given functions can be written as {tex}|x|=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} x, & x \geq 0 \\ -x, & x<0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}|x-2|=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} x-2, & x \geq 2 \\ 2-x, & x<2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}|x-4|=\left\{\begin{array}{l} x-4, x \geq 4 \\ 4-x, x<4 \end{array}\right.{/tex} Splitting the limits of the integral, we get {tex}\int_{0}^{4}(|x|+|x-2|+|x-4|) d x{/tex} {tex}\left.=\int_{0}^{2}(|x|+|x-2|+|x-4|) d x+\int_{2}^{4}|| x|+| x-2|+| x-4 |\right\} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{2}(x+2-x+4-x) d x+\int_{2}^{4}(x+x-2+4-x) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{2}(6-x) d x+\int_{2}^{4}(2+x) d x{/tex} {tex}=\left[6 x-\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{2}+\left[2 x+\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{2}^{4}{/tex} = [12 - 2] + [16 - 6] = 10 + 10 = 20
solution_html:
The given functions can be written as

 {tex}|x|=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} x, & x \geq 0 \\ -x, & x<0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}|x-2|=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} x-2, & x \geq 2 \\ 2-x, & x<2 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}|x-4|=\left\{\begin{array}{l} x-4, x \geq 4 \\ 4-x, x<4 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
Splitting the limits of the integral, we get
{tex}\int_{0}^{4}(|x|+|x-2|+|x-4|) d x{/tex}
{tex}\left.=\int_{0}^{2}(|x|+|x-2|+|x-4|) d x+\int_{2}^{4}|| x|+| x-2|+| x-4 |\right\} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int_{0}^{2}(x+2-x+4-x) d x+\int_{2}^{4}(x+x-2+4-x) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int_{0}^{2}(6-x) d x+\int_{2}^{4}(2+x) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\left[6 x-\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{2}+\left[2 x+\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{2}^{4}{/tex}
= [12 - 2] + [16 - 6]
= 10 + 10
= 20
