explanation: Let {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)} d x{/tex} Put, t = x 2 ,and then by partial fractions we have, {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)}=\frac{t+1}{(t+4)(t+25)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A}{t+4}+\frac{B}{t+25}{/tex} .... (1) (A + B)t + (25A + 4B) = t + 1 A + B = 1 ….(1) 25A + 4B = 1 ....(2) Solving equation (1)and(2), we get, {tex}A=\frac{-1}{7}{/tex} and {tex}B=\frac{8}{7}{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{t+1}{(t+4)(t+25)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{7} \times \frac{1}{t+4}+\frac{8}{7} \times \frac{1}{t+25}{/tex} {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{7} \times \frac{1}{x^{2}+4}+\frac{8}{7} \times \frac{1}{x^{2}+25}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{7} \times \frac{1}{x^{2}+4}+\frac{8}{7} \times \frac{1}{x^{2}+25}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{7} \times \frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+\frac{8}{7} \times \frac{1}{5} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{5}\right)+c{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{14} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+\frac{8}{35} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{5}\right)+c{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)} d x{/tex} Put, t = x 2 ,and then by partial fractions we have, {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)}=\frac{t+1}{(t+4)(t+25)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A}{t+4}+\frac{B}{t+25}{/tex} .... (1) (A + B)t + (25A + 4B) = t + 1 A + B = 1 ….(1) 25A + 4B = 1 ....(2) Solving equation (1)and(2), we get, {tex}A=\frac{-1}{7}{/tex} and {tex}B=\frac{8}{7}{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{t+1}{(t+4)(t+25)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{7} \times \frac{1}{t+4}+\frac{8}{7} \times \frac{1}{t+25}{/tex} {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{7} \times \frac{1}{x^{2}+4}+\frac{8}{7} \times \frac{1}{x^{2}+25}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{7} \times \frac{1}{x^{2}+4}+\frac{8}{7} \times \frac{1}{x^{2}+25}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{7} \times \frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+\frac{8}{7} \times \frac{1}{5} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{5}\right)+c{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{14} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+\frac{8}{35} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{5}\right)+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)} d x{/tex}
Put, t = x2  ,and then by partial fractions we have, 
{tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)}=\frac{t+1}{(t+4)(t+25)}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{A}{t+4}+\frac{B}{t+25}{/tex} .... (1)
(A + B)t + (25A + 4B) = t + 1
A + B = 1 ….(1)
25A + 4B = 1 ....(2)
Solving equation (1)and(2), we get,
{tex}A=\frac{-1}{7}{/tex} and {tex}B=\frac{8}{7}{/tex}
Now,
{tex}\frac{t+1}{(t+4)(t+25)}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{-1}{7} \times \frac{1}{t+4}+\frac{8}{7} \times \frac{1}{t+25}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{-1}{7} \times \frac{1}{x^{2}+4}+\frac{8}{7} \times \frac{1}{x^{2}+25}{/tex}
{tex}\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{\left(x^{2}+4\right)\left(x^{2}+25\right)} d x{/tex}{tex}=\frac{-1}{7} \times \frac{1}{x^{2}+4}+\frac{8}{7} \times \frac{1}{x^{2}+25}{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{1}{7} \times \frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+\frac{8}{7} \times \frac{1}{5} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{5}\right)+c{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{1}{14} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+\frac{8}{35} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{5}\right)+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate: {tex}\int_{1}^{4} f(x) d x{/tex}, where f(x) =|x - 1| + |x - 2| + |x - 3|
question_text: Evaluate: {tex}\int_{1}^{4} f(x) d x{/tex} , where f(x) =|x - 1| + |x - 2| + |x - 3|
solution_html:
Let y = {tex}\int_{1}^{4} f(x) d x{/tex}, then we have
{tex}y=\int_{1}^{2} f(x) d x+\int_{2}^{3} f(x) d x+\int_{3}^{4} f(x) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int_{1}^{2}[(x-1)-(x-2)-(x-3)] d x{/tex} {tex}+\int_{2}^{3}\{(x-1)+(x-2)-(x-3)\} d x{/tex}{tex}+\int_{3}^{4}\{(x-1)+(x-2)+(x-3)\} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int_{1}^{2}(-x+4) d x+\int_{2}^{3} x d x+\int_{3}^{4}(3 x-6) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\frac{-x^{2}}{2}+4 x\right]_{1}^{2}+\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{2}^{3}+\left[\frac{3 x^{2}}{2}-6 x\right]_{3}^{4}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{5}{2}+\frac{5}{2}+\frac{9}{2}\right)=\frac{19}{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let {tex}y=\int \limits_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \frac{1}{(1+\sqrt{\tan x})} d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}} d x{/tex} ....... (i) Use King theorem of definite integral {tex}\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\pi}{6}-x\right)}}{(\sqrt{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\pi}{6}-x\right)}+\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\pi}{6}-x\right)})} d x{/tex} ....... (ii) Adding eq.(i) and eq.(ii) {tex}2 y=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{(\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x})} d x{/tex} {tex}+\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{(\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x})} d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}}{(\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x})} d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} 1 d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} 1 d x{/tex} {tex}y=\frac{\pi}{12}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}y=\int \limits_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \frac{1}{(1+\sqrt{\tan x})} d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}} d x{/tex} ....... (i) Use King theorem of definite integral {tex}\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\pi}{6}-x\right)}}{(\sqrt{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\pi}{6}-x\right)}+\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\pi}{6}-x\right)})} d x{/tex} ....... (ii) Adding eq.(i) and eq.(ii) {tex}2 y=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{(\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x})} d x{/tex} {tex}+\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{(\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x})} d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}}{(\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x})} d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} 1 d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} 1 d x{/tex} {tex}y=\frac{\pi}{12}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y=\int \limits_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \frac{1}{(1+\sqrt{\tan x})} d x{/tex}
{tex}y=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}} d x{/tex} ....... (i)
Use King theorem of definite integral
{tex}\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex}
{tex}y=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}}{/tex}{tex}\frac{\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\pi}{6}-x\right)}}{(\sqrt{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\pi}{6}-x\right)}+\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\pi}{6}-x\right)})} d x{/tex} ....... (ii)
Adding eq.(i) and eq.(ii)
{tex}2 y=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{(\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x})} d x{/tex}{tex}+\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{(\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x})} d x{/tex}
{tex}2 y=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}}{(\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x})} d x{/tex}
{tex}2 y=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} 1 d x{/tex}
{tex}2 y=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} 1 d x{/tex}
{tex}y=\frac{\pi}{12}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x}{(1+\sin x)} d x{/tex} .........(i) Then, {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x)}{1+\sin (\pi-x)} d x=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x)}{(1+\sin x)} d x{/tex} ..........(ii) Adding (i) and (ii), we get {tex}2 I=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{d x}{(1+\sin x)}{/tex} {tex}=\pi \cdot \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{1}{(1+\sin x)} \times \frac{(1-\sin x)}{(1-\sin x)} d x{/tex} or {tex}2 I=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi}\left(\frac{1-\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=\pi \cdot\left[\int_{0}^{\pi} \sec ^{2} x d x-\int_{0}^{\pi} \sec x \tan x d x\right]{/tex} {tex}=\pi \cdot\left[[\tan x]_{0}^{\pi}-[\sec x]_{0}^{\pi}\right]=2 \pi{/tex} {tex}\therefore I=\pi, \text { i.e., } \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x}{(1+\sin x)} d x=\pi{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x}{(1+\sin x)} d x{/tex} .........(i) Then, {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x)}{1+\sin (\pi-x)} d x=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x)}{(1+\sin x)} d x{/tex} ..........(ii) Adding (i) and (ii), we get {tex}2 I=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{d x}{(1+\sin x)}{/tex} {tex}=\pi \cdot \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{1}{(1+\sin x)} \times \frac{(1-\sin x)}{(1-\sin x)} d x{/tex} or {tex}2 I=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi}\left(\frac{1-\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=\pi \cdot\left[\int_{0}^{\pi} \sec ^{2} x d x-\int_{0}^{\pi} \sec x \tan x d x\right]{/tex} {tex}=\pi \cdot\left[[\tan x]_{0}^{\pi}-[\sec x]_{0}^{\pi}\right]=2 \pi{/tex} {tex}\therefore I=\pi, \text { i.e., } \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x}{(1+\sin x)} d x=\pi{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x}{(1+\sin x)} d x{/tex} .........(i)
Then, {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x)}{1+\sin (\pi-x)} d x=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x)}{(1+\sin x)} d x{/tex} ..........(ii)
Adding (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}2 I=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{d x}{(1+\sin x)}{/tex} {tex}=\pi \cdot \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{1}{(1+\sin x)} \times \frac{(1-\sin x)}{(1-\sin x)} d x{/tex}
or {tex}2 I=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi}\left(\frac{1-\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=\pi \cdot\left[\int_{0}^{\pi} \sec ^{2} x d x-\int_{0}^{\pi} \sec x \tan x d x\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\pi \cdot\left[[\tan x]_{0}^{\pi}-[\sec x]_{0}^{\pi}\right]=2 \pi{/tex}
{tex}\therefore I=\pi, \text { i.e., } \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x}{(1+\sin x)} d x=\pi{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Given, {tex}\int e ^ { x } ( \tan x + 1 ) \sec x d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C.{/tex} Write f(x) satisfying above.
question_text: Given, {tex}\int e ^ { x } ( \tan x + 1 ) \sec x d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C.{/tex} Write f(x) satisfying above.
explanation: Given, {tex}\int{/tex} e x (tan x + 1) sec x dx = e x . f(x) + C {tex}\Rightarrow \int e ^ { x } ( \sec x + \sec x \tan x ) d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad e ^ { x } \cdot \sec x + C = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c } { \because \int e ^ { x } \left\{ f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) \right\} d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C } \\ { \text { and here } \frac { d } { d x } ( \sec x ) = \sec x \tan x } \end{array} \right]{/tex} On comparing both sides, we get, f(x) = sec x
solution: Given, {tex}\int{/tex} e x (tan x + 1) sec x dx = e x . f(x) + C {tex}\Rightarrow \int e ^ { x } ( \sec x + \sec x \tan x ) d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad e ^ { x } \cdot \sec x + C = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c } { \because \int e ^ { x } \left\{ f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) \right\} d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C } \\ { \text { and here } \frac { d } { d x } ( \sec x ) = \sec x \tan x } \end{array} \right]{/tex} On comparing both sides, we get, f(x) = sec x
solution_html:
Given, {tex}\int{/tex}ex (tan x + 1) sec x dx = ex. f(x) + C

{tex}\Rightarrow \int e ^ { x } ( \sec x + \sec x \tan x ) d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad e ^ { x } \cdot \sec x + C = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c } { \because \int e ^ { x } \left\{ f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) \right\} d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C } \\ { \text { and here } \frac { d } { d x } ( \sec x ) = \sec x \tan x } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
On comparing both sides, we get,
f(x) = sec x

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the integral: {tex} \int \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } x } { cosec ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex}
question_text: Find the integral: {tex} \int \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } x } { cosec ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex}
explanation: Let I = {tex}\int \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } x } { cosec ^ { 2 } x } d x = \int \frac { \left( \frac { 1 } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } \right) } { \left( \frac { 1 } { \sin ^ { 2 } x } \right) } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { \sin ^ { 2 } x } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex} {tex} = \int \tan ^ { 2 } x d x = \int \left( \sec ^ { 2 } x - 1 \right) d x{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \tan ^ { 2 } x = \sec ^ { 2 } x - 1 \right]{/tex} {tex} = \int \sec ^ { 2 } x d x - \int 1 d x = \tan x - x + c{/tex}
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } x } { cosec ^ { 2 } x } d x = \int \frac { \left( \frac { 1 } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } \right) } { \left( \frac { 1 } { \sin ^ { 2 } x } \right) } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { \sin ^ { 2 } x } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex} {tex} = \int \tan ^ { 2 } x d x = \int \left( \sec ^ { 2 } x - 1 \right) d x{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \tan ^ { 2 } x = \sec ^ { 2 } x - 1 \right]{/tex} {tex} = \int \sec ^ { 2 } x d x - \int 1 d x = \tan x - x + c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } x } { cosec ^ { 2 } x } d x = \int \frac { \left( \frac { 1 } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } \right) } { \left( \frac { 1 } { \sin ^ { 2 } x } \right) } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { \sin ^ { 2 } x } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex}
{tex} = \int \tan ^ { 2 } x d x = \int \left( \sec ^ { 2 } x - 1 \right) d x{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \tan ^ { 2 } x = \sec ^ { 2 } x - 1 \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \int \sec ^ { 2 } x d x - \int 1 d x = \tan x - x + c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{5 x+3}{\sqrt{x^{2}+4 x+10}} d x{/tex} take 5x + 3 = A {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (x2 + 4x + 10) + B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x + 3 = A(2x + 4) + B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x + 3 = 2Ax + (4A + B) Equating coefficients 2A = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and 4A + B = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = 3 - 4( {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = -7 i.e. 5x + 3 = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} (2x + 4) - 7 {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int {\frac{{\frac{5}{2}(2x + 4) - 7}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 4x + 10} }}dx} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} {tex}\int {\frac{{(2x + 4) - 7}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 4x + 10} }}dx} {/tex} - 7 {tex}\int {\frac{{dx}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 4x + 10} }}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} I 1 - 7I 2 ......(i) Now, I 1 = {tex}\int {\frac{{(2x + 4) - 7}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 4x + 10} }}dx} {/tex} Put x 2 + 4x + 10 = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2x + 4)dx = dt {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 = {tex}\int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt t }}dt}{/tex} = {tex}\int {{t^{ - \frac{1}{2}}}dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{t^{\frac{1}{2}}}}}{{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} + C 1 = 2 {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10}{/tex} + C 1 .....(ii) Now, I 2 = {tex}\int {\frac{{dx}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 4x + 10} }}} {/tex} = {tex}\int {\frac{{dx}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 4x + 10 + {2^2} - {2^2}} }}}{/tex} = {tex}\int {\frac{{dx}}{{\sqrt {{{(x + 2)}^2} + {{(\sqrt 6 )}^2}} }}}{/tex} = log|(x + 2) + {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10}{/tex} | + C 2 ........(iii) Using eq (ii) and (iii) in (i) we get I = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} (2 {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10}{/tex} + C 1 ) - 7 (log |(x + 2) + {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10}{/tex} | + C 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 5 {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10} + C_1^{'}{/tex} - 7 log |(x + 2) + {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10} + C_2^{'}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 5 {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10}{/tex} - 7 log | (x + 2) + {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10}{/tex} + C 2
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{5 x+3}{\sqrt{x^{2}+4 x+10}} d x{/tex} take 5x + 3 = A {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (x2 + 4x + 10) + B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x + 3 = A(2x + 4) + B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x + 3 = 2Ax + (4A + B) Equating coefficients 2A = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and 4A + B = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = 3 - 4( {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = -7 i.e. 5x + 3 = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} (2x + 4) - 7 {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int {\frac{{\frac{5}{2}(2x + 4) - 7}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 4x + 10} }}dx} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} {tex}\int {\frac{{(2x + 4) - 7}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 4x + 10} }}dx} {/tex} - 7 {tex}\int {\frac{{dx}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 4x + 10} }}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} I 1 - 7I 2 ......(i) Now, I 1 = {tex}\int {\frac{{(2x + 4) - 7}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 4x + 10} }}dx} {/tex} Put x 2 + 4x + 10 = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2x + 4)dx = dt {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 = {tex}\int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt t }}dt}{/tex} = {tex}\int {{t^{ - \frac{1}{2}}}dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{t^{\frac{1}{2}}}}}{{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} + C 1 = 2 {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10}{/tex} + C 1 .....(ii) Now, I 2 = {tex}\int {\frac{{dx}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 4x + 10} }}} {/tex} = {tex}\int {\frac{{dx}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 4x + 10 + {2^2} - {2^2}} }}}{/tex} = {tex}\int {\frac{{dx}}{{\sqrt {{{(x + 2)}^2} + {{(\sqrt 6 )}^2}} }}}{/tex} = log|(x + 2) + {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10}{/tex} | + C 2 ........(iii) Using eq (ii) and (iii) in (i) we get I = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} (2 {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10}{/tex} + C 1 ) - 7 (log |(x + 2) + {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10}{/tex} | + C 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 5 {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10} + C_1^{'}{/tex} - 7 log |(x + 2) + {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10} + C_2^{'}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 5 {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10}{/tex} - 7 log | (x + 2) + {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10}{/tex} + C 2
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \frac{5 x+3}{\sqrt{x^{2}+4 x+10}} d x{/tex}
take 5x + 3 = A {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (x2 + 4x + 10) + B
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x + 3 = A(2x + 4) + B
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x + 3 = 2Ax + (4A + B)
Equating coefficients 
2A = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} 
and 4A + B = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = 3 - 4({tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = -7
i.e. 5x + 3 = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}(2x + 4) - 7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int {\frac{{\frac{5}{2}(2x + 4) - 7}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 4x + 10} }}dx} {/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} {tex}\int {\frac{{(2x + 4) - 7}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 4x + 10} }}dx} {/tex} - 7{tex}\int {\frac{{dx}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 4x + 10} }}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}I1 - 7I2 ......(i)
Now, I1 = {tex}\int {\frac{{(2x + 4) - 7}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 4x + 10} }}dx} {/tex} 
Put x2 + 4x + 10 = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2x + 4)dx = dt
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I1 = {tex}\int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt t }}dt}{/tex} = {tex}\int {{t^{ - \frac{1}{2}}}dt}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{{{t^{\frac{1}{2}}}}}{{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} + C1 
= 2{tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10}{/tex} + C1 .....(ii)
Now, I2 =  {tex}\int {\frac{{dx}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 4x + 10} }}} {/tex} 
= {tex}\int {\frac{{dx}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 4x + 10 + {2^2} - {2^2}} }}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\int {\frac{{dx}}{{\sqrt {{{(x + 2)}^2} + {{(\sqrt 6 )}^2}} }}}{/tex} 
= log|(x + 2) + {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10}{/tex} | + C2  ........(iii) 
Using eq (ii) and (iii) in (i) we get
I = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}(2{tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10}{/tex} + C1) - 7 (log |(x + 2) + {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10}{/tex} | + C2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 5{tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10} + C_1^{'}{/tex} - 7 log |(x + 2) + {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10} + C_2^{'}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = 5{tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10}{/tex} - 7 log | (x + 2) + {tex}\sqrt{x^2 + 4x + 10}{/tex} + C2

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Given, {tex}I = \int \frac { 2 } { ( 1 - x ) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } d x{/tex} Using partial fraction, {tex}\frac { 2 } { ( 1 - x ) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { A } { 1 - x } + \frac { B x + C } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 2 } { ( 1 - x ) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { A \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) + ( B x + C ) ( 1 - x ) } { ( 1 - x ) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 = A (1 + x^2) + (Bx + C) (1 - x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 = A + Ax^2 + Bx + C - Bx^2 -Cx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 = (A - B)x^2 + (B- C)x + (A+ C){/tex} Comparing coefficients of {tex}x^2{/tex} , x and constant terms from both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow A - B=0 {/tex} ...(ii) {tex}B - C=0{/tex} ...(iii) and A + C=2 ...(iv) Solving Equations (ii), (iii) and (iv), A = 1, B = 1 and C = 1 Now, Eq. (i) becomes {tex}\frac { 2 } { ( 1 - x ) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { 1 } { 1 - x } + \frac { x + 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Integrating both sides w.r.t.x, {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac { 2 } { ( 1 - x ) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } d x = \int \frac { 1 } { 1 - x } d x + \int \frac { x + 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= - \log | 1 - x | + \int \frac { x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x + \int \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= - \log | 1 - x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { 2 x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x + \int \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= - \log | 1 - x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| + \tan ^ { - 1 } x + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { f ^ { \prime } ( x ) } { f ( x ) } d x = \log | f ( x ) | + C \right]{/tex}
solution: Given, {tex}I = \int \frac { 2 } { ( 1 - x ) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } d x{/tex} Using partial fraction, {tex}\frac { 2 } { ( 1 - x ) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { A } { 1 - x } + \frac { B x + C } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 2 } { ( 1 - x ) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { A \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) + ( B x + C ) ( 1 - x ) } { ( 1 - x ) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 = A (1 + x^2) + (Bx + C) (1 - x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 = A + Ax^2 + Bx + C - Bx^2 -Cx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 = (A - B)x^2 + (B- C)x + (A+ C){/tex} Comparing coefficients of {tex}x^2{/tex} , x and constant terms from both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow A - B=0 {/tex} ...(ii) {tex}B - C=0{/tex} ...(iii) and A + C=2 ...(iv) Solving Equations (ii), (iii) and (iv), A = 1, B = 1 and C = 1 Now, Eq. (i) becomes {tex}\frac { 2 } { ( 1 - x ) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { 1 } { 1 - x } + \frac { x + 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Integrating both sides w.r.t.x, {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac { 2 } { ( 1 - x ) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } d x = \int \frac { 1 } { 1 - x } d x + \int \frac { x + 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= - \log | 1 - x | + \int \frac { x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x + \int \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= - \log | 1 - x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { 2 x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x + \int \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= - \log | 1 - x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| + \tan ^ { - 1 } x + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { f ^ { \prime } ( x ) } { f ( x ) } d x = \log | f ( x ) | + C \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given,  {tex}I = \int \frac { 2 } { ( 1 - x ) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } d x{/tex}
Using partial fraction,
{tex}\frac { 2 } { ( 1 - x ) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { A } { 1 - x } + \frac { B x + C } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 2 } { ( 1 - x ) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { A \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) + ( B x + C ) ( 1 - x ) } { ( 1 - x ) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 = A (1 + x^2) + (Bx + C) (1 - x){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 = A + Ax^2 + Bx + C - Bx^2 -Cx{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 = (A - B)x^2 + (B- C)x + (A+ C){/tex}
Comparing coefficients of {tex}x^2{/tex}, x and constant terms from both sides, 
{tex}\Rightarrow A - B=0 {/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}B - C=0{/tex} ...(iii)
and A + C=2 ...(iv)
Solving Equations (ii), (iii) and (iv),
A = 1, B = 1 and C = 1
Now, Eq. (i) becomes
{tex}\frac { 2 } { ( 1 - x ) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { 1 } { 1 - x } + \frac { x + 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
Integrating both sides w.r.t.x, 
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac { 2 } { ( 1 - x ) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } d x = \int \frac { 1 } { 1 - x } d x + \int \frac { x + 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= - \log | 1 - x | + \int \frac { x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x + \int \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= - \log | 1 - x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { 2 x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x + \int \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= - \log | 1 - x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| + \tan ^ { - 1 } x + C{/tex}
{tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { f ^ { \prime } ( x ) } { f ( x ) } d x = \log | f ( x ) | + C \right]{/tex}

explanation: We have to evaluate {tex}\int ( 1 - x ) \sqrt { x } d x.{/tex} Let I = {tex}\int ( 1 - x ) \sqrt { x } d x = \int ( \sqrt { x } - x \sqrt { x } ) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \left( x ^ { 1 / 2 } - x ^ { 3 / 2 } \right) d x = \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 / 2 } - \frac { 2 } { 5 } x ^ { 5 / 2 } + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int x ^ { n } d x = \frac { x ^ { n + 1 } } { n + 1 } + C , n \neq - 1 \right]{/tex}
solution: We have to evaluate {tex}\int ( 1 - x ) \sqrt { x } d x.{/tex} Let I = {tex}\int ( 1 - x ) \sqrt { x } d x = \int ( \sqrt { x } - x \sqrt { x } ) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \left( x ^ { 1 / 2 } - x ^ { 3 / 2 } \right) d x = \frac { 2 } { 3 } x ^ { 3 / 2 } - \frac { 2 } { 5 } x ^ { 5 / 2 } + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int x ^ { n } d x = \frac { x ^ { n + 1 } } { n + 1 } + C , n \neq - 1 \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have to evaluate {tex}\int ( 1 - x ) \sqrt { x } d x.{/tex}

Let I = {tex}\int ( 1 - x ) \sqrt { x } d x = \int ( \sqrt { x } - x \sqrt { x } ) d x{/tex}

explanation: Let {tex} I =\int \frac { 2 } { 1 + \cos 2 x } d x{/tex} we know that {tex} 1+ \cos 2 \theta = 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { 2 } { 2 \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { 1} { \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \sec ^ { 2 } x d x{/tex} {tex} = \tan x + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore I = \tan x + C{/tex}
solution: Let {tex} I =\int \frac { 2 } { 1 + \cos 2 x } d x{/tex} we know that {tex} 1+ \cos 2 \theta = 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { 2 } { 2 \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { 1} { \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \sec ^ { 2 } x d x{/tex} {tex} = \tan x + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore I = \tan x + C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex} I =\int \frac { 2 } { 1 + \cos 2 x } d x{/tex}

we know that  {tex} 1+ \cos 2 \theta = 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta{/tex}

{tex}= \int \frac { 2 } { 2 \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex}

{tex}= \int \frac { 1} { \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex}

question_html: Write the value of {tex} \int \frac { x + \cos 6 x } { 3 x ^ { 2 } + \sin 6 x } d x.{/tex}
question_text: Write the value of {tex} \int \frac { x + \cos 6 x } { 3 x ^ { 2 } + \sin 6 x } d x.{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}I = \int \frac { x + \cos 6 x } { 3 x ^ { 2 } + \sin 6 x } d x{/tex} Put {tex}3x^2 + sin6x = t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (6 x + 6 \cos 6 x )dx= { d t }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 6( x + \cos 6 x ) d x = { d t }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow ( x + \cos 6 x ) d x = {{ d t }\over 6}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = \int \frac { d t } { 6 t } {/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 6 } \log | t | + C \left[ \because \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x = \log | x | + C \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 6 } \left[ \log \left| 3 x ^ { 2 } + \sin 6 x \right| \right] + C{/tex} [ where {tex}3x^2 + sin6x = t{/tex} ] {tex}\therefore I = \frac { 1 } { 6 } \left[ \log \left| 3 x ^ { 2 } + \sin 6 x \right| \right] + C{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I = \int \frac { x + \cos 6 x } { 3 x ^ { 2 } + \sin 6 x } d x{/tex} Put {tex}3x^2 + sin6x = t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (6 x + 6 \cos 6 x )dx= { d t }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 6( x + \cos 6 x ) d x = { d t }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow ( x + \cos 6 x ) d x = {{ d t }\over 6}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = \int \frac { d t } { 6 t } {/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 6 } \log | t | + C \left[ \because \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x = \log | x | + C \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 6 } \left[ \log \left| 3 x ^ { 2 } + \sin 6 x \right| \right] + C{/tex} [ where {tex}3x^2 + sin6x = t{/tex} ] {tex}\therefore I = \frac { 1 } { 6 } \left[ \log \left| 3 x ^ { 2 } + \sin 6 x \right| \right] + C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I = \int \frac { x + \cos 6 x } { 3 x ^ { 2 } + \sin 6 x } d x{/tex}
Put {tex}3x^2 + sin6x = t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow (6 x + 6 \cos 6 x )dx= { d t }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 6( x + \cos 6 x ) d x = { d t }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow ( x + \cos 6 x ) d x = {{ d t }\over 6}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I = \int \frac { d t } { 6 t } {/tex}

{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 6 } \log | t | + C \left[ \because \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x = \log | x | + C \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 6 } \left[ \log \left| 3 x ^ { 2 } + \sin 6 x \right| \right] + C{/tex} [ where {tex}3x^2 + sin6x = t{/tex}]

{tex}\therefore I = \frac { 1 } { 6 } \left[ \log \left| 3 x ^ { 2 } + \sin 6 x \right| \right] + C{/tex}

solution: According to the question , {tex}I =\int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 4 - x ^ { 2 } } d x {/tex} {tex}= \left[ \frac { x } { 2 } \sqrt { 4 - x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 4 } { 2 } \sin ^ { - 1 } \frac { x } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { x } { 2 } \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \sin ^ { - 1 } \frac { x } { a } + c \right]{/tex} {tex}= 0 + 2 \sin ^ { - 1 } 1-0-0{/tex} {tex} = 2 \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) {/tex} {tex}= {2 \times \frac { \pi } { 2 } = \pi}{/tex} {tex}[\because \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \sin \ x\right) = x ]{/tex}

question_text: Write the value of {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { e ^ { x } } { 1 + e ^ { 2 x } } d x.{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { e ^ { x } } { 1 + e ^ { 2 x } } d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { e ^ { x } } { 1 + \left( e ^ { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} Put {tex}e ^ { x } = t \Rightarrow e ^ { x } d x = d t{/tex} Also, when x = 0, then t = 1 and when x = 1, then t = e Now, {tex}I = \int _ { 1 } ^ { e } \frac { d t } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } = \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } t \right) _ { 1 } ^ { e }{/tex} {tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } e - \tan ^ { - 1 } 1 = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { e - 1 } { 1 + e } \right){/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { e ^ { x } } { 1 + e ^ { 2 x } } d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { e ^ { x } } { 1 + \left( e ^ { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} Put {tex}e ^ { x } = t \Rightarrow e ^ { x } d x = d t{/tex} Also, when x = 0, then t = 1 and when x = 1, then t = e Now, {tex}I = \int _ { 1 } ^ { e } \frac { d t } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } = \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } t \right) _ { 1 } ^ { e }{/tex} {tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } e - \tan ^ { - 1 } 1 = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { e - 1 } { 1 + e } \right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { e ^ { x } } { 1 + e ^ { 2 x } } d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { e ^ { x } } { 1 + \left( e ^ { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}

Put {tex}e ^ { x } = t \Rightarrow e ^ { x } d x = d t{/tex}
Also, when x = 0, then t = 1 and when x = 1, then t = e
Now, {tex}I = \int _ { 1 } ^ { e } \frac { d t } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } = \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } t \right) _ { 1 } ^ { e }{/tex}
{tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } e - \tan ^ { - 1 } 1 = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { e - 1 } { 1 + e } \right){/tex}

(i) by parts, we get {tex}I = \int x \mathop {{\mathop{\rm se}\nolimits} }\limits_Icx \cdot \frac{{x\cos x}}{{{{(x\sin x + {\mathop{\rm c}\nolimits} \mathop {os}\limits_{II} x)}^2}}}dx{/tex} {tex} = x \sec x \cdot \frac { ( - 1 ) } { x \sin x + \cos x }{/tex} {tex} - \int ( 1 \cdot \sec x + x \sec x \tan x ) \cdot \frac { - d x } { x \sin x + \cos x }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { - x \sec x } { x \sin x + \cos x } + \int \sec x \left( 1 + \frac { x \sin x } { \cos x } \right) \frac { d x } { x \sin x + \cos x }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { - x \sec x } { x \sin x + \cos x } + \int \sec ^ { 2 } x d x{/tex} {tex} = \frac { - x \sec x } { x \sin x + \cos x } + \tan x + C{/tex}

(i) by parts, we get {tex}I = \int x \mathop {{\mathop{\rm se}\nolimits} }\limits_Icx \cdot \frac{{x\cos x}}{{{{(x\sin x + {\mathop{\rm c}\nolimits} \mathop {os}\limits_{II} x)}^2}}}dx{/tex} {tex} = x \sec x \cdot \frac { ( - 1 ) } { x \sin x + \cos x }{/tex} {tex} - \int ( 1 \cdot \sec x + x \sec x \tan x ) \cdot \frac { - d x } { x \sin x + \cos x }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { - x \sec x } { x \sin x + \cos x } + \int \sec x \left( 1 + \frac { x \sin x } { \cos x } \right) \frac { d x } { x \sin x + \cos x }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { - x \sec x } { x \sin x + \cos x } + \int \sec ^ { 2 } x d x{/tex} {tex} = \frac { - x \sec x } { x \sin x + \cos x } + \tan x + C{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question , {tex} I = \int \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { ( x \sin x + \cos x ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex} I = \int \frac { x .x } { ( x \sin x + \cos x ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
On multiplying the numerator and denominator by cosx , we get  
{tex}= \frac {cosx}{cosx}\int \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { ( x \sin x + \cos x ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad I = x \sec x\int \frac { x \cos x } { ( x \sin x + \cos x ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} ...(i) {tex}[\because \frac {1}{cosx} = sec x]{/tex}
Put x sin x + cos x = t
{tex} \Rightarrow (xcosx+sinx-sinx)dx = dt{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow  xcosxdx = dt{/tex}
{tex} I = I_1 . x secx \ dx {/tex}
Consider , {tex}I _ { 1 } = \int \frac { x \cos x } { ( x \sin x + \cos x ) ^ { 2 } } d x {/tex}
put , t = x sinx + cos x
{tex}(xcosx + sinx -sinx ) dx = dt{/tex}
{tex}xcosx \ dx = dt{/tex}
{tex} = \int \frac { d t } { t ^ { 2 } } {/tex}
{tex}= \frac { - 1 } { t } {/tex}
{tex}= \frac { - 1 } { x \sin x + \cos x }{/tex}
Now, integrating Eq. (i) by parts, we get
{tex}I = \int x \mathop {{\mathop{\rm se}\nolimits} }\limits_Icx \cdot \frac{{x\cos x}}{{{{(x\sin x + {\mathop{\rm c}\nolimits} \mathop {os}\limits_{II} x)}^2}}}dx{/tex}
{tex} = x \sec x \cdot \frac { ( - 1 ) } { x \sin x + \cos x }{/tex}
{tex} - \int ( 1 \cdot \sec x + x \sec x \tan x ) \cdot \frac { - d x } { x \sin x + \cos x }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { - x \sec x } { x \sin x + \cos x } + \int \sec x \left( 1 + \frac { x \sin x } { \cos x } \right) \frac { d x } { x \sin x + \cos x }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { - x \sec x } { x \sin x + \cos x } + \int \sec ^ { 2 } x d x{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { - x \sec x } { x \sin x + \cos x } + \tan x + C{/tex}

question_html: Prove that {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } ( \sqrt { \tan x } + \sqrt { \cot x } ) d x=\sqrt2.\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
question_text: Prove that {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } ( \sqrt { \tan x } + \sqrt { \cot x } ) d x=\sqrt2.\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
explanation: According to question , {tex}I=\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } ( \sqrt { \tan x } + \sqrt { \cot x } ) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { \sqrt { \sin x } } { \sqrt { \cos x } } + \frac { \sqrt { \cos x } } { \sqrt { \sin x } } d x{/tex} {tex}[\because \frac {sinx}{cosx}=tanx \ \ and \ \ \frac{cosx}{sinx}=cotx]{/tex} {tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { ( \sin x + \cos x ) } { \sqrt { \sin x \cos x } } d x{/tex} Multiplying and dividing the numerator and denominator with {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} we get , {tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { ( \sin x + \cos x ) } { \sqrt { 2 \sin x \cos x } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { ( \sin x + \cos x ) } { \sqrt { 1 + 2 \sin x \cos x - 1 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { ( \sin x + \cos x ) } { \sqrt { 1 - ( 1 - 2 \sin x \cos x ) } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { ( \sin x + \cos x ) } { \sqrt { 1 - ( \sin x - \cos x ) ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex} Now, put {tex}sinx - cosx = t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (cos x + sin x)dx = dt{/tex} Lower limit , x = 0 then t = -1 Upper limit , x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} then t = 0 {tex}\therefore \quad I = \sqrt { 2 } \int _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 } \frac { d t } { \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} = \sqrt { 2 } \left[ \sin ^ { - 1 } t \right] _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 } {/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \left[ \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 0 ) - \sin ^ { - 1 } (- 1) \right]{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \left[ \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 0 ) + \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 1 ) \right]{/tex} {tex}[\because sin^{-1}(-x)= -sin^{-1}(x) ]{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \left[ \sin ^ { - 1 } [ sin(0) ] + \sin ^ { - 1 } [sin({\pi\over 2})]\right]{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \left[ 0+\frac { \pi } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}[\because sin^{-1}(sinx)=x ]{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \left[ \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } ( \sqrt { \tan x } + \sqrt { \cot x } ) d x=\sqrt2.\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} Hence Proved.
solution: According to question , {tex}I=\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } ( \sqrt { \tan x } + \sqrt { \cot x } ) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { \sqrt { \sin x } } { \sqrt { \cos x } } + \frac { \sqrt { \cos x } } { \sqrt { \sin x } } d x{/tex} {tex}[\because \frac {sinx}{cosx}=tanx \ \ and \ \ \frac{cosx}{sinx}=cotx]{/tex} {tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { ( \sin x + \cos x ) } { \sqrt { \sin x \cos x } } d x{/tex} Multiplying and dividing the numerator and denominator with {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} we get , {tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { ( \sin x + \cos x ) } { \sqrt { 2 \sin x \cos x } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { ( \sin x + \cos x ) } { \sqrt { 1 + 2 \sin x \cos x - 1 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { ( \sin x + \cos x ) } { \sqrt { 1 - ( 1 - 2 \sin x \cos x ) } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { ( \sin x + \cos x ) } { \sqrt { 1 - ( \sin x - \cos x ) ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex} Now, put {tex}sinx - cosx = t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (cos x + sin x)dx = dt{/tex} Lower limit , x = 0 then t = -1 Upper limit , x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} then t = 0 {tex}\therefore \quad I = \sqrt { 2 } \int _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 } \frac { d t } { \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} = \sqrt { 2 } \left[ \sin ^ { - 1 } t \right] _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 } {/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \left[ \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 0 ) - \sin ^ { - 1 } (- 1) \right]{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \left[ \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 0 ) + \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 1 ) \right]{/tex} {tex}[\because sin^{-1}(-x)= -sin^{-1}(x) ]{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \left[ \sin ^ { - 1 } [ sin(0) ] + \sin ^ { - 1 } [sin({\pi\over 2})]\right]{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \left[ 0+\frac { \pi } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}[\because sin^{-1}(sinx)=x ]{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \left[ \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } ( \sqrt { \tan x } + \sqrt { \cot x } ) d x=\sqrt2.\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} Hence Proved.
solution_html:
According to question , {tex}I=\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } ( \sqrt { \tan x } + \sqrt { \cot x } ) d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { \sqrt { \sin x } } { \sqrt { \cos x } } + \frac { \sqrt { \cos x } } { \sqrt { \sin x } } d x{/tex}{tex}[\because \frac {sinx}{cosx}=tanx \ \ and \ \ \frac{cosx}{sinx}=cotx]{/tex}
{tex}= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { ( \sin x + \cos x ) } { \sqrt { \sin x \cos x } } d x{/tex}

Multiplying and dividing the numerator and denominator with {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} we get , 
{tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { ( \sin x + \cos x ) } { \sqrt { 2 \sin x \cos x } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { ( \sin x + \cos x ) } { \sqrt { 1 + 2 \sin x \cos x - 1 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { ( \sin x + \cos x ) } { \sqrt { 1 - ( 1 - 2 \sin x \cos x ) } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } \frac { ( \sin x + \cos x ) } { \sqrt { 1 - ( \sin x - \cos x ) ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex}
Now, put {tex}sinx - cosx = t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow (cos x + sin x)dx = dt{/tex}
Lower limit , x = 0 then t = -1 
Upper limit , x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} then t = 0
{tex}\therefore \quad I = \sqrt { 2 } \int _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 } \frac { d t } { \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}

{tex} = \sqrt { 2 } \left[ \sin ^ { - 1 } t \right] _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 } {/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \left[ \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 0 ) - \sin ^ { - 1 } (- 1) \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \left[ \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 0 ) + \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 1 ) \right]{/tex}{tex}[\because sin^{-1}(-x)= -sin^{-1}(x) ]{/tex}

{tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \left[ \sin ^ { - 1 } [ sin(0) ] + \sin ^ { - 1 } [sin({\pi\over 2})]\right]{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \left[ 0+\frac { \pi } { 2 } \right]{/tex}{tex}[\because sin^{-1}(sinx)=x ]{/tex}

{tex}= \sqrt { 2 } \left[ \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right]{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } ( \sqrt { \tan x } + \sqrt { \cot x } ) d x=\sqrt2.\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
Hence Proved.

question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int \left( \frac {1+ \sin x } { 1 + \cos x } \right) e ^ { x } d x.{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int \left( \frac {1+ \sin x } { 1 + \cos x } \right) e ^ { x } d x.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, {tex}I =\int \left( \frac { 1 + \sin x } { 1 + \cos x } \right) e ^ { x } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { 1 + 2 \sin \frac { x } { 2 } \cos \frac { x } { 2 } } { 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } } \cdot e ^ { x } d x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { \because \sin x = 2 \sin \frac { x } { 2 } \cos \frac { x } { 2 } } \\ { \text { and } 1 + \cos x = 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { 1 } { 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } } \cdot e ^ { x } d x+ \int \frac { 2 \sin \frac { x } { 2 } \cos \frac { x } { 2 } } { 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } } \cdot e ^ { x } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } + \tan \frac { x } { 2 } \right) e ^ { x } d x{/tex} {tex}[ \because{1\over cosx }= secx , {{sinx\over cosx} = tanx} ]{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \tan \frac { x } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } \right) d x{/tex} As we know that, {tex}\int e ^ { x } \left[ f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) \right] d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C{/tex} Let, {tex}f ( x ) = \tan \frac { x } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad f ^ { \prime } ( x ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = e ^ { x } \tan \frac { x } { 2 } + C{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}I =\int \left( \frac { 1 + \sin x } { 1 + \cos x } \right) e ^ { x } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { 1 + 2 \sin \frac { x } { 2 } \cos \frac { x } { 2 } } { 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } } \cdot e ^ { x } d x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { \because \sin x = 2 \sin \frac { x } { 2 } \cos \frac { x } { 2 } } \\ { \text { and } 1 + \cos x = 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { 1 } { 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } } \cdot e ^ { x } d x+ \int \frac { 2 \sin \frac { x } { 2 } \cos \frac { x } { 2 } } { 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } } \cdot e ^ { x } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } + \tan \frac { x } { 2 } \right) e ^ { x } d x{/tex} {tex}[ \because{1\over cosx }= secx , {{sinx\over cosx} = tanx} ]{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \tan \frac { x } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } \right) d x{/tex} As we know that, {tex}\int e ^ { x } \left[ f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) \right] d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C{/tex} Let, {tex}f ( x ) = \tan \frac { x } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad f ^ { \prime } ( x ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = e ^ { x } \tan \frac { x } { 2 } + C{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,  {tex}I =\int \left( \frac { 1 + \sin x } { 1 + \cos x } \right) e ^ { x } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { 1 + 2 \sin \frac { x } { 2 } \cos \frac { x } { 2 } } { 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } } \cdot e ^ { x } d x{/tex}{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { \because \sin x = 2 \sin \frac { x } { 2 } \cos \frac { x } { 2 } } \\ { \text { and } 1 + \cos x = 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } } \end{array} \right]{/tex}

{tex}= \int \frac { 1 } { 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } } \cdot e ^ { x } d x+ \int \frac { 2 \sin \frac { x } { 2 } \cos \frac { x } { 2 } } { 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } } \cdot e ^ { x } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } + \tan \frac { x } { 2 } \right) e ^ { x } d x{/tex}{tex}[ \because{1\over cosx }= secx , {{sinx\over cosx} = tanx} ]{/tex}
{tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \tan \frac { x } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } \right) d x{/tex}
As we know that,{tex}\int e ^ { x } \left[ f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) \right] d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C{/tex}
Let, {tex}f ( x ) = \tan \frac { x } { 2 }{/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow \quad f ^ { \prime } ( x ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I = e ^ { x } \tan \frac { x } { 2 } + C{/tex}

explanation: According to the question , {tex}I =\int _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \cos 2 x \log ( \sin x ) d x{/tex} By Using integration by parts , we get {tex}I =\int_{\pi /4}^{\pi /2} {\mathop {\cos }\limits_{II} } 2x\mathop {\log (\sin x)}\limits_I dx{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \log ( \sin x ) \frac { \sin 2 x } { 2 } - \int \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \cos x \cdot \frac { \sin 2 x } { 2 } d x \right] _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \frac { \log ( \sin x ) \cdot \sin 2 x } { 2 } \right] _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 }{/tex} - {tex}\int _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x \cdot ( 2 \sin x \cos x ) } { 2 \sin x } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \log \left( \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) \cdot \sin \pi - \log \left( \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) \cdot \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}- \int _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } x d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ 0 - \log \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right] - \int _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { 1 + \cos 2 x } { 2 } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log 2 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ x + \frac { \sin 2 x } { 2 } \right] _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log 2 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + \frac { \sin \pi } { 2 } \right) - \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \frac { \sin \pi / 2 } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log 2 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log 2 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log 2 - \frac { \pi } { 8 } + \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore I = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log 2 - \frac { \pi } { 8 } + \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex} sq units.
solution: According to the question , {tex}I =\int _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \cos 2 x \log ( \sin x ) d x{/tex} By Using integration by parts , we get {tex}I =\int_{\pi /4}^{\pi /2} {\mathop {\cos }\limits_{II} } 2x\mathop {\log (\sin x)}\limits_I dx{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \log ( \sin x ) \frac { \sin 2 x } { 2 } - \int \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \cos x \cdot \frac { \sin 2 x } { 2 } d x \right] _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \frac { \log ( \sin x ) \cdot \sin 2 x } { 2 } \right] _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 }{/tex} - {tex}\int _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x \cdot ( 2 \sin x \cos x ) } { 2 \sin x } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \log \left( \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) \cdot \sin \pi - \log \left( \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) \cdot \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}- \int _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } x d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ 0 - \log \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right] - \int _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { 1 + \cos 2 x } { 2 } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log 2 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ x + \frac { \sin 2 x } { 2 } \right] _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log 2 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + \frac { \sin \pi } { 2 } \right) - \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \frac { \sin \pi / 2 } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log 2 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log 2 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log 2 - \frac { \pi } { 8 } + \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore I = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log 2 - \frac { \pi } { 8 } + \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex} sq units.
solution_html:
According to the question ,  {tex}I =\int _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \cos 2 x \log ( \sin x ) d x{/tex}
By Using integration by parts , we get 

{tex}I =\int_{\pi /4}^{\pi /2} {\mathop {\cos }\limits_{II} } 2x\mathop {\log (\sin x)}\limits_I dx{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \log ( \sin x ) \frac { \sin 2 x } { 2 } - \int \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \cos x \cdot \frac { \sin 2 x } { 2 } d x \right] _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \frac { \log ( \sin x ) \cdot \sin 2 x } { 2 } \right] _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 }{/tex} - {tex}\int _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { \cos x \cdot ( 2 \sin x \cos x ) } { 2 \sin x } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \log \left( \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) \cdot \sin \pi - \log \left( \sin \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) \cdot \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right]{/tex}{tex}- \int _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } x d x{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ 0 - \log \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right] - \int _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \frac { 1 + \cos 2 x } { 2 } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log 2 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ x + \frac { \sin 2 x } { 2 } \right] _ { \pi / 4 } ^ { \pi / 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log 2 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + \frac { \sin \pi } { 2 } \right) - \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } + \frac { \sin \pi / 2 } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log 2 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log 2 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log 2 - \frac { \pi } { 8 } + \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex}

{tex}\therefore I = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log 2 - \frac { \pi } { 8 } + \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex} sq units.

question_html:
Evaluate the integrals :
{tex}\text { If } \int_{0}^{a} 3 x^{2} d x{/tex} = 8, find the value of a.
question_text: Evaluate the integrals : {tex}\text { If } \int_{0}^{a} 3 x^{2} d x{/tex} = 8, find the value of a.
explanation: Given, {tex}\int_{0}^{a} 3 x^{2} d x{/tex} = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex} \left[x^{3}\right]_{0}^{a}{/tex} =8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[a^{3}-0^3\right]{/tex} =8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a 3 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a 3 = 2 3 {tex}\Rightarrow a=2{/tex}
solution: Given, {tex}\int_{0}^{a} 3 x^{2} d x{/tex} = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex} \left[x^{3}\right]_{0}^{a}{/tex} =8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[a^{3}-0^3\right]{/tex} =8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a 3 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a 3 = 2 3 {tex}\Rightarrow a=2{/tex}
solution_html:
Given,
{tex}\int_{0}^{a} 3 x^{2} d x{/tex} = 8
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex}{tex} \left[x^{3}\right]_{0}^{a}{/tex}=8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\left[a^{3}-0^3\right]{/tex}=8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a3 = 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a3 = 23
{tex}\Rightarrow a=2{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}\int(\frac{x-1}{x^{2}}){/tex} e x dx = f(x) e x + C, then write the value of f(x)
explanation: {tex}\text { Since } \int e^{x}\left(f(x)+f^{\prime}(x)\right) d x=e^{x} f(x)+C{/tex} We can write the expression as {tex}\int\left(\frac{x-1}{x^{2}}\right) e^{x} d x=f(x) e^{x}+C{/tex} {tex}\int e^{x}\left(\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right) d x=f(x) e^{x}+C{/tex} {tex}\int e^{z}\left(\frac{1}{x}+\left(-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)\right) d x=f(x) e^{x}+C{/tex} comparing with the equation {tex}\int e^{x}\left(\frac{1}{x}+\left(-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)\right) d x=f(x) e^{x}+C{/tex} we standard equation we have {tex}f(x)=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} therefore, {tex}\int e^{x}\left(\frac{1}{x}+\left(-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)\right) d x=\frac{1}{x} e^{x}+C{/tex} {tex}\text { Thus, } f(x)=\frac{1}{x}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\text { Since } \int e^{x}\left(f(x)+f^{\prime}(x)\right) d x=e^{x} f(x)+C{/tex} We can write the expression as {tex}\int\left(\frac{x-1}{x^{2}}\right) e^{x} d x=f(x) e^{x}+C{/tex} {tex}\int e^{x}\left(\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right) d x=f(x) e^{x}+C{/tex} {tex}\int e^{z}\left(\frac{1}{x}+\left(-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)\right) d x=f(x) e^{x}+C{/tex} comparing with the equation {tex}\int e^{x}\left(\frac{1}{x}+\left(-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)\right) d x=f(x) e^{x}+C{/tex} we standard equation we have {tex}f(x)=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} therefore, {tex}\int e^{x}\left(\frac{1}{x}+\left(-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)\right) d x=\frac{1}{x} e^{x}+C{/tex} {tex}\text { Thus, } f(x)=\frac{1}{x}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\text { Since } \int e^{x}\left(f(x)+f^{\prime}(x)\right) d x=e^{x} f(x)+C{/tex}
We can write the expression as
{tex}\int\left(\frac{x-1}{x^{2}}\right) e^{x} d x=f(x) e^{x}+C{/tex}
{tex}\int e^{x}\left(\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right) d x=f(x) e^{x}+C{/tex}
{tex}\int e^{z}\left(\frac{1}{x}+\left(-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)\right) d x=f(x) e^{x}+C{/tex}
comparing with the equation {tex}\int e^{x}\left(\frac{1}{x}+\left(-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)\right) d x=f(x) e^{x}+C{/tex} we standard equation we have
{tex}f(x)=\frac{1}{x}{/tex}
therefore, {tex}\int e^{x}\left(\frac{1}{x}+\left(-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)\right) d x=\frac{1}{x} e^{x}+C{/tex}
{tex}\text { Thus, } f(x)=\frac{1}{x}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: I = {tex}\int \frac{3}{(1-x)\left(1+x^{2}\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{(1-x)\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A}{1-x}+\frac{B x+c}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} 3 = A(1 - x 2 ) + (Bx + C) (1 - x) Equating similar terms A - B = 0 B - C = 0 A + C = 3 Solving A = {tex}\frac{3}{2}, \mathrm{B}=\frac{3}{2}, \mathrm{C}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} Thus, I = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{d x}{1-x}+\frac{3}{2} \int{/tex} {tex}\frac{x d x}{1+x^{2}}+\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{3}{2} \log |1-\mathrm{x}|+\frac{3}{2} \log | 1{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{x}^{2} |+\frac{3}{2} \tan ^{-1} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{c}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{3}{4}\left[\log \left|\frac{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}{(1-\mathrm{x})^{2}}\right|+2 \tan ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right]+\mathrm{C}{/tex} .
solution: I = {tex}\int \frac{3}{(1-x)\left(1+x^{2}\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{(1-x)\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A}{1-x}+\frac{B x+c}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} 3 = A(1 - x 2 ) + (Bx + C) (1 - x) Equating similar terms A - B = 0 B - C = 0 A + C = 3 Solving A = {tex}\frac{3}{2}, \mathrm{B}=\frac{3}{2}, \mathrm{C}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} Thus, I = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{d x}{1-x}+\frac{3}{2} \int{/tex} {tex}\frac{x d x}{1+x^{2}}+\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{3}{2} \log |1-\mathrm{x}|+\frac{3}{2} \log | 1{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{x}^{2} |+\frac{3}{2} \tan ^{-1} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{c}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{3}{4}\left[\log \left|\frac{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}{(1-\mathrm{x})^{2}}\right|+2 \tan ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right]+\mathrm{C}{/tex} .
solution_html:
I = {tex}\int \frac{3}{(1-x)\left(1+x^{2}\right)} d x{/tex}
{tex}\frac{3}{(1-x)\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{A}{1-x}+\frac{B x+c}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
3 = A(1 - x2) + (Bx + C) (1 - x)
Equating similar terms
A - B = 0
B - C = 0
A + C = 3
Solving
A = {tex}\frac{3}{2}, \mathrm{B}=\frac{3}{2}, \mathrm{C}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
Thus,
I = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{d x}{1-x}+\frac{3}{2} \int{/tex}{tex}\frac{x d x}{1+x^{2}}+\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}-\frac{3}{2} \log |1-\mathrm{x}|+\frac{3}{2} \log | 1{/tex}{tex}\mathrm{x}^{2} |+\frac{3}{2} \tan ^{-1} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{c}{/tex}
I = {tex}\frac{3}{4}\left[\log \left|\frac{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}{(1-\mathrm{x})^{2}}\right|+2 \tan ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right]+\mathrm{C}{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let I = {tex}\int {/tex} x 2 tan 1 x dx = tan 1 x {tex}\int {/tex} x 2 dx - {tex}\int\left(\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} \int x^{2} d x\right){/tex} (using integration by parts) = tan 1 x {tex}\left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right)-\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{x^{3}}{1+x^{2}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3} x^{3} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{3} \int\left(x-\frac{x}{1+x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3} x^{3} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{x^{2}}{2}+\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{x}{1+x^{2}} d x{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{3} x^{3} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{6} x^{2}+\frac{1}{6}{/tex} log |1 + x 2 | + C
solution: Let I = {tex}\int {/tex} x 2 tan 1 x dx = tan 1 x {tex}\int {/tex} x 2 dx - {tex}\int\left(\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} \int x^{2} d x\right){/tex} (using integration by parts) = tan 1 x {tex}\left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right)-\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{x^{3}}{1+x^{2}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3} x^{3} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{3} \int\left(x-\frac{x}{1+x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3} x^{3} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{x^{2}}{2}+\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{x}{1+x^{2}} d x{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{3} x^{3} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{6} x^{2}+\frac{1}{6}{/tex} log |1 + x 2 | + C
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int {/tex}x2 tan1 x dx   
= tan1 x {tex}\int {/tex} x2 dx - {tex}\int\left(\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} \int x^{2} d x\right){/tex}     (using integration by parts)
= tan1 x {tex}\left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right)-\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{x^{3}}{1+x^{2}} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{3} x^{3} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{3} \int\left(x-\frac{x}{1+x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{3} x^{3} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{x^{2}}{2}+\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{x}{1+x^{2}} d x{/tex}
I = {tex}\frac{1}{3} x^{3} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{6} x^{2}+\frac{1}{6}{/tex} log |1 + x2| + C

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let the given integral be, {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{(x+3)(x-1)^{2}} d x{/tex} Now using partial fractions by putting, {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{(x+3)(x-1)^{2}}=\frac{A}{(x+3)}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{B}{(x-1)}+\frac{C}{(x-1)^{2}}{/tex} .... (1) A(x - 1) 2 + B(x + 3)(x - 1) + C(x + 3) = x 2 + 1 Now put x - 1 = 0 Therefore, x = 1 A(0) + B(0) + C(4) = 2 {tex}c=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Now put x + 3 = 0 Therefore, x = -3 A(-3 - 1) 2 + B(0) + C(0) = 9 + 1 = 10 {tex}A=\frac{5}{8}{/tex} By equating the coefficient of x 2 , we get, A + B = 1 {tex}\frac{5}{8}+B=1{/tex} {tex}B=1-\frac{5}{8}=\frac{3}{8}{/tex} From equation (1), we get, {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{(x+3)(x-2)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5}{8} \times \frac{1}{(x+3)}+\frac{3}{8} \times \frac{1}{(x-2)}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{(x-2)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{(x+3)(x-2)^{2}} d x=\frac{5}{8} \int \frac{1}{(x+3)} d x{/tex} {tex}+\frac{3}{8} \int \frac{1}{(x-2)} d x+\int \frac{1}{(x-2)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5}{8} \log |x+3|+\frac{3}{8} \log |x-1|{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{2(x-1)}+c{/tex}
solution: Let the given integral be, {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{(x+3)(x-1)^{2}} d x{/tex} Now using partial fractions by putting, {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{(x+3)(x-1)^{2}}=\frac{A}{(x+3)}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{B}{(x-1)}+\frac{C}{(x-1)^{2}}{/tex} .... (1) A(x - 1) 2 + B(x + 3)(x - 1) + C(x + 3) = x 2 + 1 Now put x - 1 = 0 Therefore, x = 1 A(0) + B(0) + C(4) = 2 {tex}c=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Now put x + 3 = 0 Therefore, x = -3 A(-3 - 1) 2 + B(0) + C(0) = 9 + 1 = 10 {tex}A=\frac{5}{8}{/tex} By equating the coefficient of x 2 , we get, A + B = 1 {tex}\frac{5}{8}+B=1{/tex} {tex}B=1-\frac{5}{8}=\frac{3}{8}{/tex} From equation (1), we get, {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{(x+3)(x-2)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5}{8} \times \frac{1}{(x+3)}+\frac{3}{8} \times \frac{1}{(x-2)}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{(x-2)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{(x+3)(x-2)^{2}} d x=\frac{5}{8} \int \frac{1}{(x+3)} d x{/tex} {tex}+\frac{3}{8} \int \frac{1}{(x-2)} d x+\int \frac{1}{(x-2)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5}{8} \log |x+3|+\frac{3}{8} \log |x-1|{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{2(x-1)}+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the given integral be, {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{(x+3)(x-1)^{2}} d x{/tex}
Now using  partial fractions by putting, {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{(x+3)(x-1)^{2}}=\frac{A}{(x+3)}{/tex}{tex}+\frac{B}{(x-1)}+\frac{C}{(x-1)^{2}}{/tex} .... (1)
A(x - 1)2 + B(x + 3)(x - 1) + C(x + 3) = x2 + 1
Now put x - 1 = 0
Therefore, x = 1
A(0) + B(0) + C(4) = 2
{tex}c=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Now put x + 3 = 0
Therefore, x = -3
A(-3 - 1)2 + B(0) + C(0) = 9 + 1 = 10
{tex}A=\frac{5}{8}{/tex}
By equating the coefficient of x2, we get, A + B = 1
{tex}\frac{5}{8}+B=1{/tex}
{tex}B=1-\frac{5}{8}=\frac{3}{8}{/tex}
From equation (1), we get,
{tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{(x+3)(x-2)^{2}}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{5}{8} \times \frac{1}{(x+3)}+\frac{3}{8} \times \frac{1}{(x-2)}{/tex}{tex}+\frac{1}{(x-2)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{(x+3)(x-2)^{2}} d x=\frac{5}{8} \int \frac{1}{(x+3)} d x{/tex}{tex}+\frac{3}{8} \int \frac{1}{(x-2)} d x+\int \frac{1}{(x-2)^{2}} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{5}{8} \log |x+3|+\frac{3}{8} \log |x-1|{/tex}{tex}-\frac{1}{2(x-1)}+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate:{tex}\int{/tex} sin x sin 2x sin 3x dx.
question_text: Evaluate: {tex}\int{/tex} sin x sin 2x sin 3x dx.
explanation: Here {tex}I= \int{/tex} sin x sin 2x sin 3x dx Applying the formula: sin x {tex}\times{/tex} sin y = 1/2(cos(y-x) - cos(y+x)) = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} (cos 2x - cos 4x) sin 2x dx {tex}={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex} \int{/tex} sin 2x cos 2x dx - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} sin 2x cos 4x dx {tex}={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{4} \int{/tex} sin 4x dx - {tex}\frac{1}{4} \int{/tex} (sin 6x - sin 2x) dx {tex}= \frac{-\cos 4 x}{16}+\frac{\cos 6 x}{24}-\frac{\cos 2 x}{8}+c{/tex}
solution: Here {tex}I= \int{/tex} sin x sin 2x sin 3x dx Applying the formula: sin x {tex}\times{/tex} sin y = 1/2(cos(y-x) - cos(y+x)) = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} (cos 2x - cos 4x) sin 2x dx {tex}={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex} \int{/tex} sin 2x cos 2x dx - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} sin 2x cos 4x dx {tex}={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{4} \int{/tex} sin 4x dx - {tex}\frac{1}{4} \int{/tex} (sin 6x - sin 2x) dx {tex}= \frac{-\cos 4 x}{16}+\frac{\cos 6 x}{24}-\frac{\cos 2 x}{8}+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Here
{tex}I= \int{/tex} sin x sin 2x sin 3x dx
Applying the formula: sin x {tex}\times{/tex} sin y = 1/2(cos(y-x) - cos(y+x))
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex}(cos 2x - cos 4x) sin 2x dx
{tex}={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex} \int{/tex} sin 2x cos 2x dx - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} sin 2x cos 4x dx
{tex}={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{4} \int{/tex} sin 4x dx - {tex}\frac{1}{4} \int{/tex}(sin 6x - sin 2x) dx
{tex}= \frac{-\cos 4 x}{16}+\frac{\cos 6 x}{24}-\frac{\cos 2 x}{8}+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let the given integral be, I = {tex}\int_{0}^{2} \sqrt{2^{2}-x^{2}} d x{/tex} Putting {tex}x=2 \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{dx}=2 \cos \theta \mathrm{d} \theta{/tex} When, x = 0, {tex}\sin \theta=0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \theta=0{/tex} x = 2, {tex}\sin \theta=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sqrt{\left(4-4 \sin ^{2} \theta\right) \cdot 2 \cos \theta} d \theta{/tex} = 2 {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} 2 \cos ^{2} \theta d \theta{/tex} = 2 {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}(1+\cos 2 \theta) d \theta{/tex} { {tex}\because{/tex} 2cos 2 θ = 1 + cos 2θ} = 2 {tex}\left[\theta+\frac{\sin 2 \theta}{2}\right]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\left[\frac{\pi}{2}+0-0\right]{/tex} = 2 [ {tex}\pi{/tex} /2] = {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The required value is {tex}\pi{/tex}
solution: Let the given integral be, I = {tex}\int_{0}^{2} \sqrt{2^{2}-x^{2}} d x{/tex} Putting {tex}x=2 \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{dx}=2 \cos \theta \mathrm{d} \theta{/tex} When, x = 0, {tex}\sin \theta=0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \theta=0{/tex} x = 2, {tex}\sin \theta=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sqrt{\left(4-4 \sin ^{2} \theta\right) \cdot 2 \cos \theta} d \theta{/tex} = 2 {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} 2 \cos ^{2} \theta d \theta{/tex} = 2 {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}(1+\cos 2 \theta) d \theta{/tex} { {tex}\because{/tex} 2cos 2 θ = 1 + cos 2θ} = 2 {tex}\left[\theta+\frac{\sin 2 \theta}{2}\right]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\left[\frac{\pi}{2}+0-0\right]{/tex} = 2 [ {tex}\pi{/tex} /2] = {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The required value is {tex}\pi{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the given integral be,

I = {tex}\int_{0}^{2} \sqrt{2^{2}-x^{2}} d x{/tex}
Putting {tex}x=2 \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{dx}=2 \cos \theta \mathrm{d} \theta{/tex}
When, x = 0, {tex}\sin \theta=0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \theta=0{/tex}
x = 2, {tex}\sin \theta=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sqrt{\left(4-4 \sin ^{2} \theta\right) \cdot 2 \cos \theta} d \theta{/tex}
= 2 {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} 2 \cos ^{2} \theta d \theta{/tex}
= 2 {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}(1+\cos 2 \theta) d \theta{/tex} {{tex}\because{/tex} 2cos2θ = 1 + cos 2θ}
= 2 {tex}\left[\theta+\frac{\sin 2 \theta}{2}\right]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex}
= 2 {tex}\left[\frac{\pi}{2}+0-0\right]{/tex}
= 2 [{tex}\pi{/tex}/2]
= {tex}\pi{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The required value is {tex}\pi{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\cos ^2 x}{\cos ^2 x+4 \sin ^2 x} d x{/tex}
explanation: Given {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\cos ^{2} x+4 \sin ^{2} x} d x{/tex} Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\cos ^{2} x+4 \sin ^{2} x} d x{/tex} ....(i) {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos ^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\cos ^{2} \mathrm{x}+4\left(1-\cos ^{2} \mathrm{x}\right)} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\cos ^{2} x+4(1)-\left(4 \cos ^{2} x\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\frac{1}{3} \cdot 3 \cos ^{2} x}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{-3 \cos ^{2} x+4-4}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{4-3 \cos ^{2} x}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{4}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}(1) d x+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{4}{4-3\left(\frac{1}{\sec ^{2} x}\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \cdot[\mathrm{x}]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{4 \sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}}{4 \sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}-3} \mathrm{dx}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \cdot\left[\frac{\pi}{4}\right]+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{4 \sec ^{2} x}{4\left(1+\tan ^{2} x\right)-3} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=-\frac{\pi}{12}+\frac{2}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{2 \sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}}{1+4 \tan ^{2} \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=-\frac{\pi}{12}+\mathrm{I}_{1}{/tex} .....(ii) First solve for I 1 : {tex}I_{1}=\frac{2}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{2 \sec ^{2} x}{1+4 \tan ^{2} x} d x{/tex} Let 2 tan x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 sec 2 x dx dt When x = 0 then t = 0 and when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} then t = {tex}\infty{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{2 \sec ^{2} x}{1+4 \tan ^{2} x} d x=\frac{2}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{1} \frac{1}{1+t^{2}} d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{1}=\frac{2}{3}\left[\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{t}\right]_{0}^{1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{3}\left[\tan ^{-1} 1-\tan ^{-1} 0\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{1}=1 \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{1}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} Put this value in equation (ii) {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=-\frac{\pi}{12}+\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\frac{5\pi}{12}{/tex}
solution: Given {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\cos ^{2} x+4 \sin ^{2} x} d x{/tex} Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\cos ^{2} x+4 \sin ^{2} x} d x{/tex} ....(i) {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos ^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\cos ^{2} \mathrm{x}+4\left(1-\cos ^{2} \mathrm{x}\right)} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\cos ^{2} x+4(1)-\left(4 \cos ^{2} x\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\frac{1}{3} \cdot 3 \cos ^{2} x}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{-3 \cos ^{2} x+4-4}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{4-3 \cos ^{2} x}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{4}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}(1) d x+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{4}{4-3\left(\frac{1}{\sec ^{2} x}\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \cdot[\mathrm{x}]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{4 \sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}}{4 \sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}-3} \mathrm{dx}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \cdot\left[\frac{\pi}{4}\right]+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{4 \sec ^{2} x}{4\left(1+\tan ^{2} x\right)-3} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=-\frac{\pi}{12}+\frac{2}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{2 \sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}}{1+4 \tan ^{2} \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=-\frac{\pi}{12}+\mathrm{I}_{1}{/tex} .....(ii) First solve for I 1 : {tex}I_{1}=\frac{2}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{2 \sec ^{2} x}{1+4 \tan ^{2} x} d x{/tex} Let 2 tan x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 sec 2 x dx dt When x = 0 then t = 0 and when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} then t = {tex}\infty{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{2 \sec ^{2} x}{1+4 \tan ^{2} x} d x=\frac{2}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{1} \frac{1}{1+t^{2}} d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{1}=\frac{2}{3}\left[\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{t}\right]_{0}^{1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{3}\left[\tan ^{-1} 1-\tan ^{-1} 0\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{1}=1 \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{1}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} Put this value in equation (ii) {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=-\frac{\pi}{12}+\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\frac{5\pi}{12}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\cos ^{2} x+4 \sin ^{2} x} d x{/tex} 
Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\cos ^{2} x+4 \sin ^{2} x} d x{/tex} ....(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos ^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\cos ^{2} \mathrm{x}+4\left(1-\cos ^{2} \mathrm{x}\right)} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} 
{tex}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\cos ^{2} x+4(1)-\left(4 \cos ^{2} x\right)} d x{/tex} 
= {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\cos ^{2} x}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} 
= {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\frac{1}{3} \cdot 3 \cos ^{2} x}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} 
= {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{-3 \cos ^{2} x+4-4}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} 
= {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{4-3 \cos ^{2} x}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{4}{4-3 \cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} 
= {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}(1) d x+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{4}{4-3\left(\frac{1}{\sec ^{2} x}\right)} d x{/tex} 
= {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \cdot[\mathrm{x}]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{4 \sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}}{4 \sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}-3} \mathrm{dx}{/tex} 
= {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \cdot\left[\frac{\pi}{4}\right]+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{4 \sec ^{2} x}{4\left(1+\tan ^{2} x\right)-3} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=-\frac{\pi}{12}+\frac{2}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{2 \sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}}{1+4 \tan ^{2} \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=-\frac{\pi}{12}+\mathrm{I}_{1}{/tex} .....(ii)
First solve for I1:
{tex}I_{1}=\frac{2}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{2 \sec ^{2} x}{1+4 \tan ^{2} x} d x{/tex} 
Let 2 tan x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 sec2x dx dt
When x = 0 then t = 0 and when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} then t = {tex}\infty{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{2 \sec ^{2} x}{1+4 \tan ^{2} x} d x=\frac{2}{3} \cdot \int_{0}^{1} \frac{1}{1+t^{2}} d t{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{1}=\frac{2}{3}\left[\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{t}\right]_{0}^{1}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{2}{3}\left[\tan ^{-1} 1-\tan ^{-1} 0\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{1}=1 \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{1}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} 
Put this value in equation (ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=-\frac{\pi}{12}+\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\frac{5\pi}{12}{/tex}

explanation: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{\left(x^{4}+1\right)} d x{/tex} , then {tex}I=\int \frac{\left(1+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)}{\left(x^{2}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)} d x{/tex} [dividing num. and denom. by x 2 ] {tex}=\int \frac{\left(1+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)}{\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)^{2}+2} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{d t}{\left[t^{2}+(\sqrt{2})^{2}\right]}{/tex} , where {tex}\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)=t{/tex} and {tex}\left(1+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right) d x=d t{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+C={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x-\frac{1}{x}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+C{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x^{2}-1}{\sqrt{2} x}\right)+C{/tex}
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{\left(x^{4}+1\right)} d x{/tex} , then {tex}I=\int \frac{\left(1+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)}{\left(x^{2}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)} d x{/tex} [dividing num. and denom. by x 2 ] {tex}=\int \frac{\left(1+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)}{\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)^{2}+2} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{d t}{\left[t^{2}+(\sqrt{2})^{2}\right]}{/tex} , where {tex}\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)=t{/tex} and {tex}\left(1+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right) d x=d t{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+C={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x-\frac{1}{x}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+C{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x^{2}-1}{\sqrt{2} x}\right)+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \frac{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{\left(x^{4}+1\right)} d x{/tex}, then 
{tex}I=\int \frac{\left(1+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)}{\left(x^{2}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)} d x{/tex}    [dividing num. and denom. by x2]
{tex}=\int \frac{\left(1+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)}{\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)^{2}+2} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int \frac{d t}{\left[t^{2}+(\sqrt{2})^{2}\right]}{/tex}, where {tex}\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)=t{/tex} and {tex}\left(1+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right) d x=d t{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+C={/tex}{tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x-\frac{1}{x}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+C{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x^{2}-1}{\sqrt{2} x}\right)+C{/tex}

explanation: Let y = {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{d x}{(1+\sqrt{\tan x})}{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{1}{1+\sqrt{\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}}} d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{(\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x})}{/tex} ........ (i) Using theorem of definite integral {tex}\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}}{(\sqrt{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}+\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)})} d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{(\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x})} d x{/tex} ....... (ii) Adding eq.(i) and eq.(ii), we get {tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{(\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x})} d x{/tex} {tex}+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{(\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x})} d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}}{(\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x})} d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} 1 d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=(x)_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex} {tex}y=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution: Let y = {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{d x}{(1+\sqrt{\tan x})}{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{1}{1+\sqrt{\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}}} d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{(\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x})}{/tex} ........ (i) Using theorem of definite integral {tex}\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}}{(\sqrt{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}+\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)})} d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{(\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x})} d x{/tex} ....... (ii) Adding eq.(i) and eq.(ii), we get {tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{(\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x})} d x{/tex} {tex}+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{(\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x})} d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}}{(\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x})} d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} 1 d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=(x)_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex} {tex}y=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let y = {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{d x}{(1+\sqrt{\tan x})}{/tex}
{tex}y=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{1}{1+\sqrt{\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}}} d x{/tex}
{tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{(\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x})}{/tex} ........ (i)
Using theorem of definite integral
{tex}\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex}
{tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}}{(\sqrt{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}+\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)})} d x{/tex}
{tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{(\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x})} d x{/tex} ....... (ii)
Adding eq.(i) and eq.(ii), we get
{tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{(\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x})} d x{/tex}{tex}+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{(\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x})} d x{/tex}
{tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}}{(\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x})} d x{/tex}
{tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} 1 d x{/tex}
{tex}2 y=(x)_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}y=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{4}+1}{/tex} dx. Then, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1+\frac{1}{x^{2}}}{x^{2}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}} d x{/tex} [Dividing the numerator and denominator by x 2 ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1+\frac{1}{x^{2}}}{\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)^{2}+2} d x{/tex} Let x - {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1+\frac{1}{x^2}\right)dx{/tex} = dt. {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{d t}{t^{2}+(\sqrt{2})^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} + C = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{x-1 / x}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} + C = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}-1}{\sqrt{2} x}\right){/tex} + C.
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{4}+1}{/tex} dx. Then, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1+\frac{1}{x^{2}}}{x^{2}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}} d x{/tex} [Dividing the numerator and denominator by x 2 ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1+\frac{1}{x^{2}}}{\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)^{2}+2} d x{/tex} Let x - {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1+\frac{1}{x^2}\right)dx{/tex} = dt. {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{d t}{t^{2}+(\sqrt{2})^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} + C = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{x-1 / x}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} + C = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}-1}{\sqrt{2} x}\right){/tex} + C.
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{4}+1}{/tex} dx. Then,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1+\frac{1}{x^{2}}}{x^{2}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}} d x{/tex} [Dividing the numerator and denominator by x2]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1+\frac{1}{x^{2}}}{\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)^{2}+2} d x{/tex}
Let x - {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  {tex}\left(1+\frac{1}{x^2}\right)dx{/tex} = dt.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  I = {tex}\int \frac{d t}{t^{2}+(\sqrt{2})^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} tan-1{tex}\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex}+ C 
= {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} tan-1 {tex}\left(\frac{x-1 / x}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} + C = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}tan-1{tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}-1}{\sqrt{2} x}\right){/tex}+ C.
