question_html: Evaluate the Integral:{tex}\int \frac{(\sin x+\cos x)}{\sqrt{\sin 2 x}} d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate the Integral: {tex}\int \frac{(\sin x+\cos x)}{\sqrt{\sin 2 x}} d x{/tex}
explanation: To find: Value of the given integral {tex}\int \frac{(\sin x+\cos x)}{\sqrt{\sin 2 x}} d x{/tex} Formula to be used: {tex}\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x=\sin ^{-1} x+c{/tex} We have, {tex}I=\int \frac{(\sin x+\cos x)}{\sqrt{\sin 2 x}} d x{/tex} … (i) Let (sinx – cosx) = t {tex}\Rightarrow(\cos x+\sin x)=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (cos x + sin x) dx=dt {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t 2 = sin 2 x - 2sinx. cosx + cos 2 x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t 2 = 1 - 2sinx.cosx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2sinx.cosx = 1 - t 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin2x = 1 - t 2 Putting this value in equation (i) {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{t}^{2}}}{/tex} I=sin -1 t I=sin -1 (sin x - cos x) Let sin -1 (sin x - cos x)= {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I=sin -1 (sin x - cos x)= {tex}\theta{/tex} … (ii) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin x - cos x Now if sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin x -cos x Then cosθ = {tex}\sqrt{1-(\sin x-\cos x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosθ = {tex}\sqrt{1-\left(\sin ^{2} x-2 \sin x \cdot \cos x+\cos ^{2} x\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosθ = {tex}\sqrt{1-(1-2 \sin x \cdot \cos x)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosθ = {tex}\sqrt{2 \sin x \cdot \cos x)}{/tex} Now tanθ = {tex}\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}{/tex} Now tanθ = {tex}\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{2 \sin x \cos x)}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{2 \sin x \cdot \cos x)}}\right){/tex} Comparing the value θ from eqn. (ii) {tex}I=\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{2 \sin x \cdot \cos x)}}\right.{/tex} Dividing Numerator and denominator from cosx {tex}I=\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\tan x-1}{\sqrt{2 \tan x})}\right.{/tex} Hence the value.
solution: To find: Value of the given integral {tex}\int \frac{(\sin x+\cos x)}{\sqrt{\sin 2 x}} d x{/tex} Formula to be used: {tex}\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x=\sin ^{-1} x+c{/tex} We have, {tex}I=\int \frac{(\sin x+\cos x)}{\sqrt{\sin 2 x}} d x{/tex} … (i) Let (sinx – cosx) = t {tex}\Rightarrow(\cos x+\sin x)=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (cos x + sin x) dx=dt {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t 2 = sin 2 x - 2sinx. cosx + cos 2 x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t 2 = 1 - 2sinx.cosx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2sinx.cosx = 1 - t 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin2x = 1 - t 2 Putting this value in equation (i) {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{t}^{2}}}{/tex} I=sin -1 t I=sin -1 (sin x - cos x) Let sin -1 (sin x - cos x)= {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I=sin -1 (sin x - cos x)= {tex}\theta{/tex} … (ii) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin x - cos x Now if sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin x -cos x Then cosθ = {tex}\sqrt{1-(\sin x-\cos x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosθ = {tex}\sqrt{1-\left(\sin ^{2} x-2 \sin x \cdot \cos x+\cos ^{2} x\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosθ = {tex}\sqrt{1-(1-2 \sin x \cdot \cos x)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosθ = {tex}\sqrt{2 \sin x \cdot \cos x)}{/tex} Now tanθ = {tex}\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}{/tex} Now tanθ = {tex}\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{2 \sin x \cos x)}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{2 \sin x \cdot \cos x)}}\right){/tex} Comparing the value θ from eqn. (ii) {tex}I=\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{2 \sin x \cdot \cos x)}}\right.{/tex} Dividing Numerator and denominator from cosx {tex}I=\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\tan x-1}{\sqrt{2 \tan x})}\right.{/tex} Hence the value.
solution_html:
To find: Value of the given integral {tex}\int \frac{(\sin x+\cos x)}{\sqrt{\sin 2 x}} d x{/tex}
Formula to be used: {tex}\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x=\sin ^{-1} x+c{/tex}
We have, {tex}I=\int \frac{(\sin x+\cos x)}{\sqrt{\sin 2 x}} d x{/tex}… (i)
Let (sinx – cosx) = t
{tex}\Rightarrow(\cos x+\sin x)=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}(cos x + sin x) dx=dt
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t2 = sin2x - 2sinx. cosx + cos2x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t2 = 1 - 2sinx.cosx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2sinx.cosx = 1 - t2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin2x = 1 - t2
Putting this value in equation (i)
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{t}^{2}}}{/tex}
I=sin-1 t
I=sin -1(sin x - cos x)
Let sin -1(sin x - cos x)={tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I=sin -1(sin x - cos x)={tex}\theta{/tex}… (ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin x - cos x
Now if sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin x -cos x
Then cosθ = {tex}\sqrt{1-(\sin x-\cos x)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}cosθ = {tex}\sqrt{1-\left(\sin ^{2} x-2 \sin x \cdot \cos x+\cos ^{2} x\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}cosθ = {tex}\sqrt{1-(1-2 \sin x \cdot \cos x)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosθ = {tex}\sqrt{2 \sin x \cdot \cos x)}{/tex}
Now tanθ = {tex}\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}{/tex}
Now tanθ = {tex}\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{2 \sin x \cos x)}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{2 \sin x \cdot \cos x)}}\right){/tex}
Comparing the value θ from eqn. (ii)
{tex}I=\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{\sqrt{2 \sin x \cdot \cos x)}}\right.{/tex}
Dividing Numerator and denominator from cosx
{tex}I=\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\tan x-1}{\sqrt{2 \tan x})}\right.{/tex}

Hence the value.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate {tex} \int \frac { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } { x ( 1 - 2 x ) } d x.{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate {tex} \int \frac { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } { x ( 1 - 2 x ) } d x.{/tex}
explanation: Accoring to the question, {tex} I = \int \frac { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } { x ( 1 - 2 x ) } d x{/tex} {tex} = \int \frac { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } { x - 2 x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} Above integral can be rewritten as {tex} I = \int \left[ \frac { 1 } { 2 } + \frac { 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x } { x ( 1 - 2 x ) } \right] d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad I = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int d x + \int \frac { 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x } { x ( 1 - 2 x ) } d x{/tex} Using partial fraction, let {tex} \frac { \left( 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x \right) } { x ( 1 - 2 x ) } = \frac { A } { x } + \frac { B } { 1 - 2 x }{/tex} ...(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x = A ( 1 - 2 x ) + B x{/tex} ...(iii) Putting x = 0 and x = {tex} \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} in Equation (iii), {tex}\Rightarrow 1 - 0 = A(1 - 0) + 0 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} A = 1 and {tex} 1 - \frac { 1 } { 4 } = A ( 1 - 1 ) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } B{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 3 } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } B{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad B = \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex} Putting the values of A and B in equation (ii), {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x } { x ( 1 - 2 x ) } = \frac { 1 } { x } + \frac { 3 / 2 } { 1 - 2 x }{/tex} From Eq. (i), we get {tex} I = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int d x + \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x + \int \frac { 3 / 2 } { 1 - 2 x } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } x + \log | x | + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { \log | 1 - 2 x | } { - 2 } + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { 1 } { 1 - a x } d x = - \frac { 1 } { a } \log | 1 - a x | + C \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } x + \log | x | - \frac { 3 } { 4 } \log | 1 - 2 x | + C{/tex}
solution: Accoring to the question, {tex} I = \int \frac { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } { x ( 1 - 2 x ) } d x{/tex} {tex} = \int \frac { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } { x - 2 x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} Above integral can be rewritten as {tex} I = \int \left[ \frac { 1 } { 2 } + \frac { 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x } { x ( 1 - 2 x ) } \right] d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad I = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int d x + \int \frac { 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x } { x ( 1 - 2 x ) } d x{/tex} Using partial fraction, let {tex} \frac { \left( 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x \right) } { x ( 1 - 2 x ) } = \frac { A } { x } + \frac { B } { 1 - 2 x }{/tex} ...(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x = A ( 1 - 2 x ) + B x{/tex} ...(iii) Putting x = 0 and x = {tex} \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} in Equation (iii), {tex}\Rightarrow 1 - 0 = A(1 - 0) + 0 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} A = 1 and {tex} 1 - \frac { 1 } { 4 } = A ( 1 - 1 ) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } B{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 3 } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } B{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad B = \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex} Putting the values of A and B in equation (ii), {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x } { x ( 1 - 2 x ) } = \frac { 1 } { x } + \frac { 3 / 2 } { 1 - 2 x }{/tex} From Eq. (i), we get {tex} I = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int d x + \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x + \int \frac { 3 / 2 } { 1 - 2 x } d x{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } x + \log | x | + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { \log | 1 - 2 x | } { - 2 } + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { 1 } { 1 - a x } d x = - \frac { 1 } { a } \log | 1 - a x | + C \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } x + \log | x | - \frac { 3 } { 4 } \log | 1 - 2 x | + C{/tex}
solution_html:
Accoring to the question, {tex} I = \int \frac { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } { x ( 1 - 2 x ) } d x{/tex}
{tex} = \int \frac { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } { x - 2 x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
Above integral can be rewritten as
{tex} I = \int \left[ \frac { 1 } { 2 } + \frac { 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x } { x ( 1 - 2 x ) } \right] d x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad I = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int d x + \int \frac { 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x } { x ( 1 - 2 x ) } d x{/tex}
Using partial fraction,
let {tex} \frac { \left( 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x \right) } { x ( 1 - 2 x ) } = \frac { A } { x } + \frac { B } { 1 - 2 x }{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x = A ( 1 - 2 x ) + B x{/tex} ...(iii)
Putting x = 0 and x = {tex} \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} in Equation (iii), 
{tex}\Rightarrow 1 - 0 = A(1 - 0) + 0 {/tex}{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} A = 1
and {tex} 1 - \frac { 1 } { 4 } = A ( 1 - 1 ) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } B{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 3 } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } B{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad B = \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex}
Putting the values of A and B in equation (ii),
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x } { x ( 1 - 2 x ) } = \frac { 1 } { x } + \frac { 3 / 2 } { 1 - 2 x }{/tex}
From Eq. (i), we get
{tex} I = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int d x + \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x + \int \frac { 3 / 2 } { 1 - 2 x } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } x + \log | x | + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { \log | 1 - 2 x | } { - 2 } + C{/tex}
{tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { 1 } { 1 - a x } d x = - \frac { 1 } { a } \log | 1 - a x | + C \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } x + \log | x | - \frac { 3 } { 4 } \log | 1 - 2 x | + C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let {tex}I = \int \frac { x ^ { 3 } - 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } d x = \int \left( \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { x ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int x d x - \int \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - \frac { x ^ { - 1 } } { - 1 } + C{/tex} {tex}= \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { x } + C{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I = \int \frac { x ^ { 3 } - 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } d x = \int \left( \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { x ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int x d x - \int \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - \frac { x ^ { - 1 } } { - 1 } + C{/tex} {tex}= \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { x } + C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let  {tex}I = \int \frac { x ^ { 3 } - 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } d x = \int \left( \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { x ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int x d x - \int \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - \frac { x ^ { - 1 } } { - 1 } + C{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { x } + C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { \sin 4 x - 4 } { 1 - \cos 4 x } \right) d x.{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { \sin 4 x - 4 } { 1 - \cos 4 x } \right) d x.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, {tex}I=\int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { \sin 4 x - 4 } { 1 - \cos 4 x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { 2 \sin 2 x \cos 2 x - 4 } { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } 2 x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c } { \because \sin 2 x = 2 \sin x \cos x \text { and } } \\ { \cos 2 x = 1 - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } x } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { 2 \sin 2 x \cos 2 x } { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } 2 x } - \frac { 4 } { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } 2 x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \cot 2 x - 2 \ cosec ^ { 2 } 2 x \right) d x{/tex} We know that, {tex}\int e ^ { x } \left[ f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) \right] d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C{/tex} Here, {tex}f(x) = cot 2x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} f'(x) = -2 cosec^2 2x{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I = e^x cot 2x + C{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}I=\int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { \sin 4 x - 4 } { 1 - \cos 4 x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { 2 \sin 2 x \cos 2 x - 4 } { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } 2 x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c } { \because \sin 2 x = 2 \sin x \cos x \text { and } } \\ { \cos 2 x = 1 - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } x } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { 2 \sin 2 x \cos 2 x } { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } 2 x } - \frac { 4 } { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } 2 x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \cot 2 x - 2 \ cosec ^ { 2 } 2 x \right) d x{/tex} We know that, {tex}\int e ^ { x } \left[ f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) \right] d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C{/tex} Here, {tex}f(x) = cot 2x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} f'(x) = -2 cosec^2 2x{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I = e^x cot 2x + C{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}I=\int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { \sin 4 x - 4 } { 1 - \cos 4 x } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { 2 \sin 2 x \cos 2 x - 4 } { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } 2 x } \right) d x{/tex}{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c } { \because \sin 2 x = 2 \sin x \cos x \text { and } } \\ { \cos 2 x = 1 - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } x } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { 2 \sin 2 x \cos 2 x } { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } 2 x } - \frac { 4 } { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } 2 x } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \cot 2 x - 2 \ cosec ^ { 2 } 2 x \right) d x{/tex}
We know that,
{tex}\int e ^ { x } \left[ f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) \right] d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C{/tex}
Here, {tex}f(x) = cot 2x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex} f'(x) = -2 cosec^2 2x{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}I = e^x cot 2x + C{/tex}

explanation: Let x 2 = y Then {tex}\frac{1}{(y+1)(y+2)}=\frac{A}{y+1}+\frac{B}{y+2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A(y + 2) + B(y + 1) = (A + B)y + (2A + B) Equating similar terms, we get, A + B = 0, and 2A + B = 1 Solving, we get, A = 1, and B = -1, Thus, I = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+1}-\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+2}{/tex} = {tex}\tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}+c{/tex}
solution: Let x 2 = y Then {tex}\frac{1}{(y+1)(y+2)}=\frac{A}{y+1}+\frac{B}{y+2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A(y + 2) + B(y + 1) = (A + B)y + (2A + B) Equating similar terms, we get, A + B = 0, and 2A + B = 1 Solving, we get, A = 1, and B = -1, Thus, I = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+1}-\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+2}{/tex} = {tex}\tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}+c{/tex}

Thus,

= {tex}\tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Here we have, {tex}I=\int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\{\sin |x|+\cos |x|\} d x{/tex} {tex}\text { Let } f(x)=\sin |x|+\cos |x|{/tex} Then, f(x) = f(-x) {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is an even function. so, {tex}I=\int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\left\{\sin |x|+\cos |x| d x=2 \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}(\sin x+\cos x) d x\right.{/tex} {tex}=2[-\cos x+\sin x]_{0}^{\frac{x}{2}}=4{/tex} {tex}\therefore \int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\{\sin |x|+\cos |x| \} d x=4{/tex}
solution: Here we have, {tex}I=\int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\{\sin |x|+\cos |x|\} d x{/tex} {tex}\text { Let } f(x)=\sin |x|+\cos |x|{/tex} Then, f(x) = f(-x) {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is an even function. so, {tex}I=\int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\left\{\sin |x|+\cos |x| d x=2 \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}(\sin x+\cos x) d x\right.{/tex} {tex}=2[-\cos x+\sin x]_{0}^{\frac{x}{2}}=4{/tex} {tex}\therefore \int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\{\sin |x|+\cos |x| \} d x=4{/tex}
solution_html:
Here we have,
{tex}I=\int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\{\sin |x|+\cos |x|\} d x{/tex}
{tex}\text { Let } f(x)=\sin |x|+\cos |x|{/tex}
 Then, f(x) = f(-x)
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  f(x) is an even function.
so, {tex}I=\int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\left\{\sin |x|+\cos |x| d x=2 \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}(\sin x+\cos x) d x\right.{/tex}{tex}=2[-\cos x+\sin x]_{0}^{\frac{x}{2}}=4{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Evaluate: {tex}\int e^{2 x}\left(\frac{1+\sin 2 x}{1+\cos 2 x}\right){/tex} dx
explanation: Let I = {tex}\int e^{2 x}\left(\frac{1+\sin 2 x}{1+\cos 2 x}\right){/tex} dx, then we have {tex} I=\int e^{2 x}\left\{\frac{1+2 \sin x \cos x}{2 \cos ^{2} x}\right\}{/tex} dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int{/tex} e 2x { {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} sec 2 x + tan x} dx = {tex}\int{/tex} e 2x {tex}\left\{2\left(\frac12\underset f\tan x\right)+\frac12\underset{f'}\sec^2x\right\}{/tex} dx {tex}\Rightarrow I=\int e_\underset{II}{}^{2x}\cdot\underset I\tan xdx+\frac12\int{/tex} e 2x {tex}\cdot{/tex} sec 2 x dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = (tan x) {tex}\frac{e^{2 x}}{2}-\int{/tex} sec 2 x {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{e^{2 x}}{2}{/tex} dx + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} e 2x sec 2 x dx + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} e 2x tan x {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} e 2x sec 2 x dx + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} e 2x sec 2 x + C {tex}\Rightarrow I{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} e 2x tan x + C
solution: Let I = {tex}\int e^{2 x}\left(\frac{1+\sin 2 x}{1+\cos 2 x}\right){/tex} dx, then we have {tex} I=\int e^{2 x}\left\{\frac{1+2 \sin x \cos x}{2 \cos ^{2} x}\right\}{/tex} dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int{/tex} e 2x { {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} sec 2 x + tan x} dx = {tex}\int{/tex} e 2x {tex}\left\{2\left(\frac12\underset f\tan x\right)+\frac12\underset{f'}\sec^2x\right\}{/tex} dx {tex}\Rightarrow I=\int e_\underset{II}{}^{2x}\cdot\underset I\tan xdx+\frac12\int{/tex} e 2x {tex}\cdot{/tex} sec 2 x dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = (tan x) {tex}\frac{e^{2 x}}{2}-\int{/tex} sec 2 x {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{e^{2 x}}{2}{/tex} dx + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} e 2x sec 2 x dx + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} e 2x tan x {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} e 2x sec 2 x dx + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} e 2x sec 2 x + C {tex}\Rightarrow I{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} e 2x tan x + C
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int e^{2 x}\left(\frac{1+\sin 2 x}{1+\cos 2 x}\right){/tex}dx, then we have
{tex} I=\int e^{2 x}\left\{\frac{1+2 \sin x \cos x}{2 \cos ^{2} x}\right\}{/tex} dx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int{/tex} e2x {{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} sec2 x + tan x} dx = {tex}\int{/tex} e2x {tex}\left\{2\left(\frac12\underset f\tan x\right)+\frac12\underset{f'}\sec^2x\right\}{/tex}dx
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\int e_\underset{II}{}^{2x}\cdot\underset I\tan xdx+\frac12\int{/tex} e2x {tex}\cdot{/tex} sec2 x dx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = (tan x) {tex}\frac{e^{2 x}}{2}-\int{/tex} sec2 x {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}\frac{e^{2 x}}{2}{/tex} dx + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} e2x sec2 x dx + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} e2x tan x {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} e2x sec2 x dx + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} e2x sec2 x + C
{tex}\Rightarrow I{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} e2x tan x + C

subject: Mathematics
explanation: let the given integral be, I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\sin x-\sin 2 x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{(\sin x-2 \sin x \cos x)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\sin x(1-2 \cos x)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{\sin ^{2} x(1-2 \cos x)} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{\left(1-\cos ^{2} x\right)(1-2 \cos x)} d x{/tex} Putting cos x = t, and - sin x dx = dt or, sin x dx = - dt, we get I = {tex}\int \frac{-d t}{\left(1-t^{2}\right)(1-2 t)}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{-1}{(1-t)(1+t)(1-2 t)} d t{/tex} Let {tex}\frac{-1}{(1-t)(1+t)(1-2 t)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{1-t}+\frac{B}{1+t}+\frac{C}{1-2 t}{/tex} . Then, - 1 = A ( l + t) (1 - 2t) + B (1 - t) (1 - 2t) + C (1 - t) (1 + t) .....(i) Putting t + 1 = 0 or, t = - 1 in (i), we get -1 = 6B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = - {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} Putting 1 - t = 0 or, t = 1 in (i), we get -1 = -2A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Putting 1 - 2t = 0 or, t = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} in (i), we get -1 = {tex}C\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \Rightarrow C=-\frac{4}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{-1}{(1-t)(1+t)(1-2 t)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1-t}-\frac{1}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{1+t}-\frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{1-2 t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{-d t}{(1-t)(1+t)(1-2 t)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{1-t} d t{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{6} \int \frac{1}{1+t} d t{/tex} - {tex}\frac{4}{3} \int \frac{1}{1-2 t} d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log |1-t|{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} log| 1 + t| - {tex}\frac{4}{3} \times-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log|1 - 2t| + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log |1-\cos x|{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{6} \log |1+\cos x|{/tex} + {tex}\frac{2}{3} \log |1-2 \cos x|{/tex} + C Hence the result
solution: let the given integral be, I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\sin x-\sin 2 x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{(\sin x-2 \sin x \cos x)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\sin x(1-2 \cos x)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{\sin ^{2} x(1-2 \cos x)} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{\left(1-\cos ^{2} x\right)(1-2 \cos x)} d x{/tex} Putting cos x = t, and - sin x dx = dt or, sin x dx = - dt, we get I = {tex}\int \frac{-d t}{\left(1-t^{2}\right)(1-2 t)}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{-1}{(1-t)(1+t)(1-2 t)} d t{/tex} Let {tex}\frac{-1}{(1-t)(1+t)(1-2 t)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{1-t}+\frac{B}{1+t}+\frac{C}{1-2 t}{/tex} . Then, - 1 = A ( l + t) (1 - 2t) + B (1 - t) (1 - 2t) + C (1 - t) (1 + t) .....(i) Putting t + 1 = 0 or, t = - 1 in (i), we get -1 = 6B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = - {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} Putting 1 - t = 0 or, t = 1 in (i), we get -1 = -2A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Putting 1 - 2t = 0 or, t = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} in (i), we get -1 = {tex}C\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \Rightarrow C=-\frac{4}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{-1}{(1-t)(1+t)(1-2 t)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1-t}-\frac{1}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{1+t}-\frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{1-2 t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{-d t}{(1-t)(1+t)(1-2 t)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{1-t} d t{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{6} \int \frac{1}{1+t} d t{/tex} - {tex}\frac{4}{3} \int \frac{1}{1-2 t} d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log |1-t|{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} log| 1 + t| - {tex}\frac{4}{3} \times-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log|1 - 2t| + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log |1-\cos x|{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{6} \log |1+\cos x|{/tex} + {tex}\frac{2}{3} \log |1-2 \cos x|{/tex} + C Hence the result
solution_html:
let the given integral be,
I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\sin x-\sin 2 x} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{1}{(\sin x-2 \sin x \cos x)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\sin x(1-2 \cos x)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{\sin ^{2} x(1-2 \cos x)} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{\left(1-\cos ^{2} x\right)(1-2 \cos x)} d x{/tex} 
Putting cos x = t, and - sin x dx = dt or, sin x dx = - dt, we get
I = {tex}\int \frac{-d t}{\left(1-t^{2}\right)(1-2 t)}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{-1}{(1-t)(1+t)(1-2 t)} d t{/tex} 
Let {tex}\frac{-1}{(1-t)(1+t)(1-2 t)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{1-t}+\frac{B}{1+t}+\frac{C}{1-2 t}{/tex}. Then, 
- 1 = A ( l + t) (1 - 2t) + B (1 - t) (1 - 2t) + C (1 - t) (1 + t) .....(i)
Putting t + 1 = 0 or, t = - 1 in (i), we get
-1 = 6B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = -{tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} 
Putting 1 - t = 0 or, t = 1 in (i), we get
-1 = -2A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
Putting 1 - 2t = 0 or, t = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} in (i), we get
-1 = {tex}C\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \Rightarrow C=-\frac{4}{3}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{-1}{(1-t)(1+t)(1-2 t)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1-t}-\frac{1}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{1+t}-\frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{1-2 t}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int \frac{-d t}{(1-t)(1+t)(1-2 t)}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{1-t} d t{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{6} \int \frac{1}{1+t} d t{/tex} - {tex}\frac{4}{3} \int \frac{1}{1-2 t} d t{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log |1-t|{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex}log| 1 + t| - {tex}\frac{4}{3} \times-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log|1 - 2t| + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log |1-\cos x|{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{6} \log |1+\cos x|{/tex} + {tex}\frac{2}{3} \log |1-2 \cos x|{/tex} + C

Hence the result

(i) Let {tex}\frac{1}{(t-1)(t+1)(1-2 t)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A}{(t-1)}+\frac{B}{(t+1)}+\frac{C}{(1-2 t)}{/tex} Then, 1 = A(t + 1)(1 - 2t) + B(t - 1)(1 - 21) + C(t - 1)(t + 1) ....... (ii) Putting t = 1 in (ii), we get A {tex}=\frac{-1}{2}{/tex} Putting t = -1 in (ii), we get B {tex}=\frac{-1}{6}{/tex} Putting t {tex}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} in (ii), we get C {tex}=\frac{-4}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore I=-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{(t-1)}-\frac{1}{6} \cdot \int \frac{d t}{(t+1)}{/tex} {tex}-\frac{4}{3} \cdot \int \frac{d t}{(1-2 t)}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \log |t-1|-\frac{1}{6} \log{/tex} {tex}|t+1|+\frac{2}{3} \cdot \int \frac{-2 d t}{(1-2 t)}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \log |t-1|-\frac{1}{6} \log |t+1|{/tex} {tex}+\frac{2}{3} \log |1-2 t|+C{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \log |\cos x-1|-\frac{1}{6} \log |\cos x+1|{/tex} {tex}+\frac{2}{3} \log |1-2 \cos x|+C{/tex}
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{(\sin x-\sin 2 x)}{/tex} , then {tex}I=\int \frac{d x}{(\sin x-2 \sin x \cos x)}{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{d x}{\sin x(1-2 \cos x)}{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{\sin x}{\sin ^{2} x(1-2 \cos x)} d x{/tex} {tex}=-\int \frac{d t}{\left(1-t^{2}\right)(1-2 t)}{/tex} , where cos x = t {tex}=\int \frac{d t}{(t-1)(t+1)(1-2 t)}{/tex} ....... (i) Let {tex}\frac{1}{(t-1)(t+1)(1-2 t)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A}{(t-1)}+\frac{B}{(t+1)}+\frac{C}{(1-2 t)}{/tex} Then, 1 = A(t + 1)(1 - 2t) + B(t - 1)(1 - 21) + C(t - 1)(t + 1) ....... (ii) Putting t = 1 in (ii), we get A {tex}=\frac{-1}{2}{/tex} Putting t = -1 in (ii), we get B {tex}=\frac{-1}{6}{/tex} Putting t {tex}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} in (ii), we get C {tex}=\frac{-4}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore I=-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{(t-1)}-\frac{1}{6} \cdot \int \frac{d t}{(t+1)}{/tex} {tex}-\frac{4}{3} \cdot \int \frac{d t}{(1-2 t)}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \log |t-1|-\frac{1}{6} \log{/tex} {tex}|t+1|+\frac{2}{3} \cdot \int \frac{-2 d t}{(1-2 t)}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \log |t-1|-\frac{1}{6} \log |t+1|{/tex} {tex}+\frac{2}{3} \log |1-2 t|+C{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{2} \log |\cos x-1|-\frac{1}{6} \log |\cos x+1|{/tex} {tex}+\frac{2}{3} \log |1-2 \cos x|+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{(\sin x-\sin 2 x)}{/tex}, then

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let the given integral be, {tex}I=\int \frac{\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{x(2 x-1)} d x\\=\int\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{\left(\frac{1}{2} x-1\right)}{x(2 x-1)}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{1}{2} d x+\int \frac{x}{x(2 x-1)} d x-\int \frac{1}{x(2 x-1)} d x{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{1}{2} x+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\log |2 x-1|}{2}-I_{1}{/tex} .... (1) Where {tex}I_{1}=\int \frac{1}{x(2 x-1)} d x{/tex} .... (2) Now by partial fractions putting, {tex}\frac{1}{x(2 x-1)}=\frac{A}{x}+\frac{B}{2 x-1}{/tex} A(2x - 1) + Bx = 1 Putting 2x - 1 = 0 {tex}x=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}A(0)+B\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=1{/tex} B = 2 Putting x = 0, A(0 - 1) + B(0) = 1 A = -1 From equation (2), we get, {tex}\frac{1}{x(2 x-1)}=-\frac{1}{x}+\frac{2}{2 x-1}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{1}{x(2 x-1)} d x{/tex} {tex}=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x+2 \int \frac{1}{2 x-1} d x{/tex} {tex}=-\log |x|+\frac{2 \log |2 x-1|}{2}+c{/tex} From equation (1) {tex}I=\frac{1}{2} x+\frac{1}{4} \log |2 x-1|{/tex} {tex}2 x-1 |+\log x+c{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} x-\frac{3}{4} \log |1-2 x|+\log |x|+c{/tex}
solution: Let the given integral be, {tex}I=\int \frac{\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{x(2 x-1)} d x\\=\int\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{\left(\frac{1}{2} x-1\right)}{x(2 x-1)}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{1}{2} d x+\int \frac{x}{x(2 x-1)} d x-\int \frac{1}{x(2 x-1)} d x{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{1}{2} x+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\log |2 x-1|}{2}-I_{1}{/tex} .... (1) Where {tex}I_{1}=\int \frac{1}{x(2 x-1)} d x{/tex} .... (2) Now by partial fractions putting, {tex}\frac{1}{x(2 x-1)}=\frac{A}{x}+\frac{B}{2 x-1}{/tex} A(2x - 1) + Bx = 1 Putting 2x - 1 = 0 {tex}x=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}A(0)+B\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=1{/tex} B = 2 Putting x = 0, A(0 - 1) + B(0) = 1 A = -1 From equation (2), we get, {tex}\frac{1}{x(2 x-1)}=-\frac{1}{x}+\frac{2}{2 x-1}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{1}{x(2 x-1)} d x{/tex} {tex}=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x+2 \int \frac{1}{2 x-1} d x{/tex} {tex}=-\log |x|+\frac{2 \log |2 x-1|}{2}+c{/tex} From equation (1) {tex}I=\frac{1}{2} x+\frac{1}{4} \log |2 x-1|{/tex} {tex}2 x-1 |+\log x+c{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} x-\frac{3}{4} \log |1-2 x|+\log |x|+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the given integral be,
{tex}I=\int \frac{\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{x(2 x-1)} d x\\=\int\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{\left(\frac{1}{2} x-1\right)}{x(2 x-1)}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int \frac{1}{2} d x+\int \frac{x}{x(2 x-1)} d x-\int \frac{1}{x(2 x-1)} d x{/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{1}{2} x+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\log |2 x-1|}{2}-I_{1}{/tex} .... (1)
Where {tex}I_{1}=\int \frac{1}{x(2 x-1)} d x{/tex} .... (2)
Now by partial fractions  putting, {tex}\frac{1}{x(2 x-1)}=\frac{A}{x}+\frac{B}{2 x-1}{/tex}
A(2x - 1) + Bx = 1
Putting 2x - 1 = 0
{tex}x=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}A(0)+B\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=1{/tex}
B = 2
Putting x = 0,
A(0 - 1) + B(0) = 1
A = -1
From equation (2), we get,
{tex}\frac{1}{x(2 x-1)}=-\frac{1}{x}+\frac{2}{2 x-1}{/tex}
{tex}\int \frac{1}{x(2 x-1)} d x{/tex}{tex}=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x+2 \int \frac{1}{2 x-1} d x{/tex}
{tex}=-\log |x|+\frac{2 \log |2 x-1|}{2}+c{/tex}
From equation (1)
{tex}I=\frac{1}{2} x+\frac{1}{4} \log |2 x-1|{/tex}{tex}2 x-1 |+\log x+c{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} x-\frac{3}{4} \log |1-2 x|+\log |x|+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int \frac { ( x - 4 ) e ^ { x } } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } } d x.{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int \frac { ( x - 4 ) e ^ { x } } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } } d x.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, {tex}I=\int e ^ { x } \frac { ( x - 4 ) } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } } d x = \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x - 2 - 2 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left[ \frac { x - 2 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 2 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { 1 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } } + \frac { ( - 2 ) } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } } \right) d x{/tex} Consider f(x) = {tex}\frac { 1 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } }.{/tex} Then {tex}f'(x)=\frac { - 2 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } }.{/tex} Thus, the above integrant is of the form e x [f(x) + f'(x)]. As, {tex}\int e ^ { x } \left[ f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) \right] d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore I = e ^ { x } \frac { 1 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } } + C{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}I=\int e ^ { x } \frac { ( x - 4 ) } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } } d x = \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x - 2 - 2 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left[ \frac { x - 2 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 2 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { 1 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } } + \frac { ( - 2 ) } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } } \right) d x{/tex} Consider f(x) = {tex}\frac { 1 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } }.{/tex} Then {tex}f'(x)=\frac { - 2 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } }.{/tex} Thus, the above integrant is of the form e x [f(x) + f'(x)]. As, {tex}\int e ^ { x } \left[ f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) \right] d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore I = e ^ { x } \frac { 1 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } } + C{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,  {tex}I=\int e ^ { x } \frac { ( x - 4 ) } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } } d x = \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x - 2 - 2 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left[ \frac { x - 2 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 2 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { 1 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } } + \frac { ( - 2 ) } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } } \right) d x{/tex}
Consider f(x) = {tex}\frac { 1 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } }.{/tex} Then {tex}f'(x)=\frac { - 2 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 3 } }.{/tex}
Thus, the above integrant is of the form
ex [f(x) + f'(x)].
As, {tex}\int e ^ { x } \left[ f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) \right] d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore I = e ^ { x } \frac { 1 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } } + C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let I = {tex} \int \frac { \log | \sin x | } { \tan x } d x{/tex} Put log|sin x| = t {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \cdot \cos x d x = d t{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} cot x dx = dt {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { \tan x } d x = d t{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \cot x = \frac { 1 } { \tan x } \right]{/tex} {tex} \therefore I = \int \frac { \log | \sin x | } { \tan x } d x = \int t d t{/tex} {tex} = \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C = \frac { ( \log | \sin x | ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C{/tex} [put t = log |sinx|]
solution: Let I = {tex} \int \frac { \log | \sin x | } { \tan x } d x{/tex} Put log|sin x| = t {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \cdot \cos x d x = d t{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} cot x dx = dt {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { \tan x } d x = d t{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \cot x = \frac { 1 } { \tan x } \right]{/tex} {tex} \therefore I = \int \frac { \log | \sin x | } { \tan x } d x = \int t d t{/tex} {tex} = \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C = \frac { ( \log | \sin x | ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C{/tex} [put t = log |sinx|]
solution_html:
Let I = {tex} \int \frac { \log | \sin x | } { \tan x } d x{/tex}
Put log|sin x| = t
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \cdot \cos x d x = d t{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} cot x dx = dt
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { \tan x } d x = d t{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \cot x = \frac { 1 } { \tan x } \right]{/tex}
{tex} \therefore I = \int \frac { \log | \sin x | } { \tan x } d x = \int t d t{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C = \frac { ( \log | \sin x | ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C{/tex}    [put t = log |sinx|]

solution: Let I = {tex}\int \left( cosec ^ { 2 } x - \cot x \right) e ^ { x } d x{/tex} {tex}= - \int \left[ \cot x + \left( - cosec ^ { 2 } x \right) \right] e ^ { x } d x{/tex} = -e x cot x + C {tex}\left[ \because \int e ^ { x } \left\{ f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) \right\} d x = e ^ { x } \cdot f ( x ) + C \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let {tex} I = \int \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } x } { 3 + \tan ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex} Put tan x = t {tex} \Rightarrow \sec ^ { 2 } x d x = d t{/tex} {tex} \therefore I = \int \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } x } { 3 + \tan ^ { 2 } x } d x = \int \frac { d t } { 3 + t ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \int \frac { d t } { ( \sqrt { 3 } ) ^ { 2 } + t ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { t } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) + C{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { a ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { a } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { x } { a } + C \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \tan x } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) + C{/tex}
solution: Let {tex} I = \int \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } x } { 3 + \tan ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex} Put tan x = t {tex} \Rightarrow \sec ^ { 2 } x d x = d t{/tex} {tex} \therefore I = \int \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } x } { 3 + \tan ^ { 2 } x } d x = \int \frac { d t } { 3 + t ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \int \frac { d t } { ( \sqrt { 3 } ) ^ { 2 } + t ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { t } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) + C{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { a ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { a } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { x } { a } + C \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \tan x } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) + C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex} I = \int \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } x } { 3 + \tan ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex}
Put tan x = t
{tex} \Rightarrow \sec ^ { 2 } x d x = d t{/tex}
{tex} \therefore I = \int \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } x } { 3 + \tan ^ { 2 } x } d x = \int \frac { d t } { 3 + t ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \int \frac { d t } { ( \sqrt { 3 } ) ^ { 2 } + t ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { t } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) + C{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { a ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { a } \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { x } { a } + C \right]{/tex}

{tex} = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \tan x } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) + C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let {tex}I = \int \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 1 + x ^ { 3 } } d x{/tex} Put {tex}1 + x ^ { 3 } = t \Rightarrow 3 x ^ { 2 } d x = d t \Rightarrow x ^ { 2 } d x = \frac { d t } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = \int \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 1 + x ^ { 3 } } d x = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \int \frac { d t } { t }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 3 } \log | t | + C \quad \left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { x } = \log | x | + C \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 3 } \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 3 } \right| + C \quad \left[ \text { put } t = 1 + x ^ { 3 } \right].{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I = \int \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 1 + x ^ { 3 } } d x{/tex} Put {tex}1 + x ^ { 3 } = t \Rightarrow 3 x ^ { 2 } d x = d t \Rightarrow x ^ { 2 } d x = \frac { d t } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = \int \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 1 + x ^ { 3 } } d x = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \int \frac { d t } { t }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 3 } \log | t | + C \quad \left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { x } = \log | x | + C \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 3 } \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 3 } \right| + C \quad \left[ \text { put } t = 1 + x ^ { 3 } \right].{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I = \int \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 1 + x ^ { 3 } } d x{/tex}
Put {tex}1 + x ^ { 3 } = t \Rightarrow 3 x ^ { 2 } d x = d t \Rightarrow x ^ { 2 } d x = \frac { d t } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I = \int \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 1 + x ^ { 3 } } d x = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \int \frac { d t } { t }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 3 } \log | t | + C \quad \left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { x } = \log | x | + C \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 3 } \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 3 } \right| + C \quad \left[ \text { put } t = 1 + x ^ { 3 } \right].{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 }{/tex} (3x2 + 2x + k) dx = 0, then find the value of k.
question_text: If {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 }{/tex} (3x 2 + 2x + k) dx = 0, then find the value of k.
explanation: Given, {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \left( 3 x ^ { 2 } + 2 x + k \right) d x = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left[ \frac { 3 x ^ { 3 } } { 3 } + \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + k x \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left[ x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } + k x \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } = 0 \Rightarrow 1 + 1 + k = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k +2 = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} k = -2

According to the question ,  {tex}I = \int \frac { \tan x + \tan ^ { 3 } x } { 1 + \tan ^ { 3 } x } d x{/tex}

{tex} = \int \frac { \tan x \sec ^ { 2 } x } { 1 + \tan ^ { 3 } x } d x [\because sec^2x=1+tan^2x]{/tex}
Put , tan x = t

{tex} \Rightarrow \quad I = \int \frac { t d t } { 1 + t ^ { 3 } } {/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { t d t } { ( 1 + t ) \left( 1 + t ^ { 2 } - t \right) }{/tex} ....(i) [{tex}\because{/tex} (a3 + b3) =(a + b) (a2 - ab + b2)]
By using partial fractions ,
Let {tex}\frac { t } { ( 1 + t ) \left( 1 + t ^ { 2 } - t \right) } = \frac { A } { 1 + t } + \frac { B t + C } { t ^ { 2 } - t + 1 }{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = A(t2 - t + 1) + (Bt + C) (1 + t)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = (A + B)t2 + (-A + B + C)t + (A  + C)...(iii)
On putting t = -1 in Eq. (iii), we get
 {tex}- 1 = A +A+A {/tex}
{tex}3A=-1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A = \frac { - 1 } { 3 }{/tex}
On equating the coefficients of t2 , t and constant terms on both sides in Eq. (iii), we get 
A + B = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { - 1 } { 3 } + B = 0 {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow B = \frac { 1 } { 3 } {/tex}
{tex} A + C = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \frac { 1 } { 3 } + C = 0 {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow C = \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex}
From equation (i) and (ii) , we get
{tex}\therefore \quad I = \int \frac { ( - 1 / 3 ) } { 1 + t } d t + \int \frac { \frac { 1 } { 3 } t + \frac { 1 } { 3 } } { t ^ { 2 } - t + 1 } d t{/tex}

In second integral , Put z = t2 - t +1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dz = (2t - 1) dt, we get

{tex}\Rightarrow I = - \frac { 1 } { 3 } \log | 1 + t | + \frac { 1 } { 6 } \log | z |+ \ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d t } { \left( t - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 3 } \log | 1 + t | + \frac { 1 } { 6 } \log \left| t ^ { 2 } - t + 1 \right| + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cdot \frac { 1 } { \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { t - \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { \sqrt { 3 } / 2 } \right) + C{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { x ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { a } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x } { a } \right) + C \right]{/tex}

question_text: By using the properties of definite integral, evaluate the integral {tex}\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}(2 \log \sin x-\log \sin 2 x) d x{/tex}
explanation: According to the question , {tex} I=\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } ( 2 \log | \sin x | - \log | \sin 2 x | ) d x{/tex} From {tex}0 \ to \ {\pi\over2} , sinx \ and \ cosx{/tex} are positive. so, {tex}|sinx| = sinx , |cosx| = cosx{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } [ 2 \log ( \sin x ) - \log ( 2 \sin x \cos x ) ] d x{/tex} {tex}[\because sin2x = 2sinx cosx]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } [ 2 \log ( \sin x ) - ( \log 2 + \log ( \sin x ){/tex} {tex} + \log ( \cos x ) ) ] d x{/tex} {tex}[\because log(ABC)= logA+logB+logC]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } [ 2 \log ( \sin x ) - \log 2 - \log ( \sin x ){/tex} {tex} - \log ( \cos x ) ) ] d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } ( \log ( \sin x ) - \log 2 - \log ( \cos x ) ) d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log ( \sin x ) d x - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log 2 d x{/tex} {tex} - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log ( \cos x ) d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - x \right) d x - \log 2 [ x ] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 }{/tex} {tex} - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log \cos x d x{/tex} {tex}[\because \int_b^axdx= \int_b^a(a+b-x)dx]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log \cos x d x - \log 2 \left[ \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 0 \right]{/tex} {tex} - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log \cos x d x{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad I = - \frac { \pi } { 2 } \log 2{/tex}
solution: According to the question , {tex} I=\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } ( 2 \log | \sin x | - \log | \sin 2 x | ) d x{/tex} From {tex}0 \ to \ {\pi\over2} , sinx \ and \ cosx{/tex} are positive. so, {tex}|sinx| = sinx , |cosx| = cosx{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } [ 2 \log ( \sin x ) - \log ( 2 \sin x \cos x ) ] d x{/tex} {tex}[\because sin2x = 2sinx cosx]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } [ 2 \log ( \sin x ) - ( \log 2 + \log ( \sin x ){/tex} {tex} + \log ( \cos x ) ) ] d x{/tex} {tex}[\because log(ABC)= logA+logB+logC]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } [ 2 \log ( \sin x ) - \log 2 - \log ( \sin x ){/tex} {tex} - \log ( \cos x ) ) ] d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } ( \log ( \sin x ) - \log 2 - \log ( \cos x ) ) d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log ( \sin x ) d x - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log 2 d x{/tex} {tex} - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log ( \cos x ) d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - x \right) d x - \log 2 [ x ] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 }{/tex} {tex} - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log \cos x d x{/tex} {tex}[\because \int_b^axdx= \int_b^a(a+b-x)dx]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log \cos x d x - \log 2 \left[ \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 0 \right]{/tex} {tex} - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log \cos x d x{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad I = - \frac { \pi } { 2 } \log 2{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question , {tex} I=\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } ( 2 \log | \sin x | - \log | \sin 2 x | ) d x{/tex}
From {tex}0 \ to \ {\pi\over2} , sinx \ and \ cosx{/tex} are positive. so, {tex}|sinx| = sinx , |cosx| = cosx{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } [ 2 \log ( \sin x ) - \log ( 2 \sin x \cos x ) ] d x{/tex}{tex}[\because sin2x = 2sinx cosx]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } [ 2 \log ( \sin x ) - ( \log 2 + \log ( \sin x ){/tex}{tex} + \log ( \cos x ) ) ] d x{/tex}{tex}[\because log(ABC)= logA+logB+logC]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } [ 2 \log ( \sin x ) - \log 2 - \log ( \sin x ){/tex}{tex} - \log ( \cos x ) ) ] d x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } ( \log ( \sin x ) - \log 2 - \log ( \cos x ) ) d x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log ( \sin x ) d x - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log 2 d x{/tex}{tex} - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log ( \cos x ) d x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - x \right) d x - \log 2 [ x ] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 }{/tex}{tex} - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log \cos x d x{/tex} {tex}[\because \int_b^axdx= \int_b^a(a+b-x)dx]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log \cos x d x - \log 2 \left[ \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 0 \right]{/tex}{tex} - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \log \cos x d x{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad I = - \frac { \pi } { 2 } \log 2{/tex}

question_text: Evaluate {tex} \int \frac { 2 x + 5 } { \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } } d x.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, {tex} I = \int \frac { 2 x + 5 } { \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex} Above integral can be written as : {tex} I = \int \frac { 2 x + 5+1-1 } { \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex} {tex} I = \int \frac { 2 x + 6-1 } { \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex} {tex} I = \int \frac { - ( - 2 x - 6 ) - 1 } { \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex} {tex} = - \int \frac { - 2 x - 6 } { \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } } d x - \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} let \ I = - I _ { 1 } - I _ { 2 }{/tex} ...(i) {tex} I _ { 1 } = \int \frac { - 2 x - 6 } { \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex} Put {tex}7 - 6x - x^2 = t{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(-6 - 2x) dx = dt{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad I _ { 1 } = \int \frac { d t } { \sqrt { t } } = \int t ^ { - 1 / 2 } d t= 2 \sqrt { t } + C_1{/tex} {tex} t = 7-6x - x^2{/tex} {tex} = 2 \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } + C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex} I _ { 2 } = \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { -(-7 + 6 x + x ^ { 2 } )} }{/tex} {tex} = \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { - \left( - 7 + 6 x + x ^ { 2 } + 9 - 9 \right) } }{/tex} {tex} = \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { - \left( 6 x + x ^ { 2 } + 9 - 16 \right) } }{/tex} {tex} = \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { - \left[ ( x + 3 ) ^ { 2 } - 16 \right] } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad I _ { 2 } = \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { ( 4 ) ^ { 2 } - ( x + 3 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x + 3 } { 4 } \right) + C _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } = \sin ^ { - 1 } \frac { x } { a } + c \right]{/tex} Putting the values of I 1 and I 2 in Equation (i), we get {tex}I= - 2 \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } }-C_1- \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x + 3 } { 4 } \right) -C_2{/tex} {tex}I= - C_2]{/tex}

16 \right] } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad I _ { 2 } = \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { ( 4 ) ^ { 2 } - ( x + 3 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x + 3 } { 4 } \right) + C _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } = \sin ^ { - 1 } \frac { x } { a } + c \right]{/tex} Putting the values of I 1 and I 2 in Equation (i), we get {tex}I= - 2 \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } }-C_1- \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x + 3 } { 4 } \right) -C_2{/tex} {tex}I= - 2 \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } - \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x + 3 } { 4 } \right) -C_1-C_2{/tex} {tex}I= - 2 \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } - \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x + 3 } { 4 } \right) + C \ [\because C = -C_1 - solution_html:
According to the question,  {tex} I = \int \frac { 2 x + 5 } { \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex}
Above integral can be written as :

{tex} I = \int \frac { 2 x + 5+1-1 } { \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex}

{tex} I = \int \frac { 2 x + 6-1 } { \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex}
{tex} I = \int \frac { - ( - 2 x - 6 ) - 1 } { \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex}
{tex} = - \int \frac { - 2 x - 6 } { \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } } d x - \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
{tex} let \ I = - I _ { 1 } - I _ { 2 }{/tex} ...(i)
 {tex} I _ { 1 } = \int \frac { - 2 x - 6 } { \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } } d x{/tex}
Put {tex}7 - 6x - x^2 = t{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(-6 - 2x) dx = dt{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad I _ { 1 } = \int \frac { d t } { \sqrt { t } } = \int t ^ { - 1 / 2 } d t= 2 \sqrt { t } + C_1{/tex}

{tex} t = 7-6x - x^2{/tex}
{tex} = 2 \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } + C _ { 1 }{/tex}
 {tex} I _ { 2 } = \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}

{tex}=\int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { -(-7 + 6 x + x ^ { 2 } )} }{/tex}
{tex} = \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { - \left( - 7 + 6 x + x ^ { 2 } + 9 - 9 \right) } }{/tex}

{tex} = \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { - \left( 6 x + x ^ { 2 } + 9 - 16 \right) } }{/tex}
{tex} = \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { - \left[ ( x + 3 ) ^ { 2 } - 16 \right] } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad I _ { 2 } = \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { ( 4 ) ^ { 2 } - ( x + 3 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
{tex} = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x + 3 } { 4 } \right) + C _ { 2 }{/tex}{tex} \left[ \because \int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } } = \sin ^ { - 1 } \frac { x } { a } + c \right]{/tex}
Putting the values of I1 and I2 in Equation (i),
we get

{tex}I= - 2 \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } }-C_1- \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x + 3 } { 4 } \right) -C_2{/tex}
{tex}I= - 2 \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } - \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x + 3 } { 4 } \right) -C_1-C_2{/tex}

{tex}I= - 2 \sqrt { 7 - 6 x - x ^ { 2 } } - \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { x + 3 } { 4 } \right) + C \ [\because C = -C_1 - C_2]{/tex}

explanation: Let I = {tex}\int\;\underset{II}x\;\underset I\sin^{-1}x\;dx{/tex} . Then, we have I = (Sin -1 x) {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} \times \frac{x^{2}}{2} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} sin -1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{-x^{2}}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} sin - 1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1-x^{2}-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} sin -1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left\{\int \frac{1-x^{2}}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x\right\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} sin -1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left\{\int \sqrt{1-x^{2}} d x-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} sin -1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\left\{\frac{1}{2} x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1} x\right\}-\sin ^{-1} x\right]+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} sin -1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{4} x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}-\frac{1}{4}{/tex} sin -1 x + C
solution: Let I = {tex}\int\;\underset{II}x\;\underset I\sin^{-1}x\;dx{/tex} . Then, we have I = (Sin -1 x) {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} \times \frac{x^{2}}{2} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} sin -1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{-x^{2}}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} sin - 1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1-x^{2}-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} sin -1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left\{\int \frac{1-x^{2}}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x\right\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} sin -1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left\{\int \sqrt{1-x^{2}} d x-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} sin -1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\left\{\frac{1}{2} x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1} x\right\}-\sin ^{-1} x\right]+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} sin -1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{4} x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}-\frac{1}{4}{/tex} sin -1 x + C
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int\;\underset{II}x\;\underset I\sin^{-1}x\;dx{/tex}. Then, we have
I = (Sin-1 x) {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} \times \frac{x^{2}}{2} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} sin-1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{-x^{2}}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex}sin-1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1-x^{2}-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} sin-1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left\{\int \frac{1-x^{2}}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x\right\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} sin-1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left\{\int \sqrt{1-x^{2}} d x-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x\right.{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} sin-1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\left\{\frac{1}{2} x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1} x\right\}-\sin ^{-1} x\right]+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} sin-1 x + {tex}\frac{1}{4} x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}-\frac{1}{4}{/tex} sin-1 x + C

subject: Mathematics
explanation: I = {tex}\int \frac{e^{x}(x-4)}{(x-2)^{3}}{/tex} dx {tex}\int e^{x}\left\{\frac{(x-2)-2}{(x-2)^{3}}\right\}{/tex} dx = {tex}\int e^{x}\left\{\frac{1}{(x-2)^{2}}-\frac{2}{(x-2)^{3}}\right\}{/tex} dx Here, f(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{(x-2)^{2}}{/tex} and f'(x) {tex}\frac{-2}{(x-2)^{3}}{/tex} and we know that, {tex}\int{/tex} e x [f(x) + f'(x)) ]dx = e x f(x) + c {tex}\therefore \int e^{x}\left\{\frac{1}{(x-2)^{2}}-\frac{2}{(x-2)^{3}}\right\} d x=\frac{e^{x}}{(x-2)^{2}}+c{/tex} {tex}\therefore I=\frac{e^{x}}{(x-2)^{2}}+c{/tex}
solution: I = {tex}\int \frac{e^{x}(x-4)}{(x-2)^{3}}{/tex} dx {tex}\int e^{x}\left\{\frac{(x-2)-2}{(x-2)^{3}}\right\}{/tex} dx = {tex}\int e^{x}\left\{\frac{1}{(x-2)^{2}}-\frac{2}{(x-2)^{3}}\right\}{/tex} dx Here, f(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{(x-2)^{2}}{/tex} and f'(x) {tex}\frac{-2}{(x-2)^{3}}{/tex} and we know that, {tex}\int{/tex} e x [f(x) + f'(x)) ]dx = e x f(x) + c {tex}\therefore \int e^{x}\left\{\frac{1}{(x-2)^{2}}-\frac{2}{(x-2)^{3}}\right\} d x=\frac{e^{x}}{(x-2)^{2}}+c{/tex} {tex}\therefore I=\frac{e^{x}}{(x-2)^{2}}+c{/tex}
solution_html:
I = {tex}\int \frac{e^{x}(x-4)}{(x-2)^{3}}{/tex} dx
{tex}\int e^{x}\left\{\frac{(x-2)-2}{(x-2)^{3}}\right\}{/tex} dx
={tex}\int e^{x}\left\{\frac{1}{(x-2)^{2}}-\frac{2}{(x-2)^{3}}\right\}{/tex} dx
Here, f(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{(x-2)^{2}}{/tex} and f'(x) {tex}\frac{-2}{(x-2)^{3}}{/tex}
and we know that,
{tex}\int{/tex} ex [f(x) + f'(x)) ]dx = ex f(x) + c
{tex}\therefore \int e^{x}\left\{\frac{1}{(x-2)^{2}}-\frac{2}{(x-2)^{3}}\right\} d x=\frac{e^{x}}{(x-2)^{2}}+c{/tex}
{tex}\therefore I=\frac{e^{x}}{(x-2)^{2}}+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{\sec ^{2} \sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x}} d x{/tex} ...(i) Also let {tex}\sqrt x{/tex} = t then, we have d( {tex}\sqrt x{/tex} ) = dt {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}} d x=d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad d x=2 \sqrt{x} d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = 2t dt [ {tex}\therefore \sqrt{x}{/tex} = t] Putting {tex}\sqrt x{/tex} = t and dx = 2t dt in equation (i) we get {tex}I=\int \frac{\sec ^{2} t}{t} \times 2 t d t{/tex} = 2 {tex}\int{/tex} sec 2 t dt = 2 tan t + c = 2 tan {tex}\sqrt x{/tex} + c {tex}\therefore {/tex} I = 2 tan {tex}\sqrt x{/tex} + c
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{\sec ^{2} \sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x}} d x{/tex} ...(i) Also let {tex}\sqrt x{/tex} = t then, we have d( {tex}\sqrt x{/tex} ) = dt {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}} d x=d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad d x=2 \sqrt{x} d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = 2t dt [ {tex}\therefore \sqrt{x}{/tex} = t] Putting {tex}\sqrt x{/tex} = t and dx = 2t dt in equation (i) we get {tex}I=\int \frac{\sec ^{2} t}{t} \times 2 t d t{/tex} = 2 {tex}\int{/tex} sec 2 t dt = 2 tan t + c = 2 tan {tex}\sqrt x{/tex} + c {tex}\therefore {/tex} I = 2 tan {tex}\sqrt x{/tex} + c
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \frac{\sec ^{2} \sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x}} d x{/tex} ...(i)
Also let {tex}\sqrt x{/tex} = t then, we have
d({tex}\sqrt x{/tex}) = dt
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}} d x=d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad d x=2 \sqrt{x} d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = 2t dt [{tex}\therefore \sqrt{x}{/tex} = t]
Putting  {tex}\sqrt x{/tex} = t and dx = 2t dt in equation (i) we get
{tex}I=\int \frac{\sec ^{2} t}{t} \times 2 t d t{/tex}
= 2{tex}\int{/tex} sec2 t dt
= 2 tan t + c
= 2 tan {tex}\sqrt x{/tex} + c
{tex}\therefore {/tex} I = 2 tan {tex}\sqrt x{/tex} + c

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate: {tex}\int \limits_{\pi / 5}^{3 \pi / 10} \frac{\sin x}{(\sin x+\cos x)} d x{/tex}.
question_text: Evaluate: {tex}\int \limits_{\pi / 5}^{3 \pi / 10} \frac{\sin x}{(\sin x+\cos x)} d x{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}I=\int \limits_{\pi / 5}^{3 \pi / 10} \frac{\sin x}{(\sin x+\cos x)} d x{/tex} ............ (i) Here, {tex}a=\frac{\pi}{5}{/tex} and {tex}b=\frac{3 \pi}{10}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(a+b)=\left(\frac{\pi}{5}+\frac{3 \pi}{10}\right)=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} Using {tex}\int \limits_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int \limits_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex} , we have {tex}I=\int \limits_{\pi / 5}^{3 \pi / 10} \frac{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right) d x}{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)+\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\int \limits_{\pi / 5}^{3 \pi / 10} \frac{\cos x}{(\sin x+\cos x)} d x{/tex} ......... (ii) Adding (i) and (ii), we get {tex}2 I=\int \limits_{\pi / 5}^{3 \pi / 10} d x=[x]_{\pi / 5}^{3 \pi / 10}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{3 \pi}{10}-\frac{\pi}{5}\right)=\frac{\pi}{10}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{\pi}{20}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I=\int \limits_{\pi / 5}^{3 \pi / 10} \frac{\sin x}{(\sin x+\cos x)} d x{/tex} ............ (i) Here, {tex}a=\frac{\pi}{5}{/tex} and {tex}b=\frac{3 \pi}{10}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(a+b)=\left(\frac{\pi}{5}+\frac{3 \pi}{10}\right)=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} Using {tex}\int \limits_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int \limits_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex} , we have {tex}I=\int \limits_{\pi / 5}^{3 \pi / 10} \frac{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right) d x}{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)+\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\int \limits_{\pi / 5}^{3 \pi / 10} \frac{\cos x}{(\sin x+\cos x)} d x{/tex} ......... (ii) Adding (i) and (ii), we get {tex}2 I=\int \limits_{\pi / 5}^{3 \pi / 10} d x=[x]_{\pi / 5}^{3 \pi / 10}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{3 \pi}{10}-\frac{\pi}{5}\right)=\frac{\pi}{10}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{\pi}{20}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I=\int \limits_{\pi / 5}^{3 \pi / 10} \frac{\sin x}{(\sin x+\cos x)} d x{/tex} ............ (i)
Here, {tex}a=\frac{\pi}{5}{/tex} and {tex}b=\frac{3 \pi}{10}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(a+b)=\left(\frac{\pi}{5}+\frac{3 \pi}{10}\right)=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
Using {tex}\int \limits_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int \limits_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex}, we have
{tex}I=\int \limits_{\pi / 5}^{3 \pi / 10} \frac{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right) d x}{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)+\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\int \limits_{\pi / 5}^{3 \pi / 10} \frac{\cos x}{(\sin x+\cos x)} d x{/tex} ......... (ii)
Adding (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}2 I=\int \limits_{\pi / 5}^{3 \pi / 10} d x=[x]_{\pi / 5}^{3 \pi / 10}{/tex}{tex}=\left(\frac{3 \pi}{10}-\frac{\pi}{5}\right)=\frac{\pi}{10}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{\pi}{20}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} x+b^{2} \sin ^{2} x\right)} d x{/tex} ........... (i) Then, {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x)}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2}(\pi-x)+b^{2} \sin ^{2}(\pi-x)\right]} d x{/tex} or {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x)}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} x+b^{2} \sin ^{2} x\right)} d x{/tex} ............ (ii) Adding (i) and (ii), we get {tex}2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(x+\pi-x)}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} x+b^{2} \sin ^{2} x\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{d x}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} x+b^{2} \sin ^{2} x\right)}{/tex} [dividing num. and denim. by cos 2 x] {tex}=2 \pi \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{d t}{\left(a^{2}+b^{2} t^{2}\right)}{/tex} , where tan x = t {tex}=\frac{2 \pi}{b^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{d t}{\left(\frac{a^{2}}{b^{2}}+t^{2}\right)}=\left[\frac{2 \pi}{b^{2}} \cdot \frac{b}{a} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{b t}{a}\right)\right]_{0}^{\infty}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \pi}{a b}\left[\tan ^{-1}(\infty)-\tan ^{-1}(0)\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \pi}{a b}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-0\right)=\left(\frac{2 \pi}{a b} \times \frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\frac{\pi^{2}}{a b}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I=\frac{\pi^{2}}{2 a b}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} x+b^{2} \sin ^{2} x\right)} d x=\frac{\pi^{2}}{2 a b}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} x+b^{2} \sin ^{2} x\right)} d x{/tex} ........... (i) Then, {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x)}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2}(\pi-x)+b^{2} \sin ^{2}(\pi-x)\right]} d x{/tex} or {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x)}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} x+b^{2} \sin ^{2} x\right)} d x{/tex} ............ (ii) Adding (i) and (ii), we get {tex}2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(x+\pi-x)}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} x+b^{2} \sin ^{2} x\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{d x}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} x+b^{2} \sin ^{2} x\right)}{/tex} [dividing num. and denim. by cos 2 x] {tex}=2 \pi \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{d t}{\left(a^{2}+b^{2} t^{2}\right)}{/tex} , where tan x = t {tex}=\frac{2 \pi}{b^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{d t}{\left(\frac{a^{2}}{b^{2}}+t^{2}\right)}=\left[\frac{2 \pi}{b^{2}} \cdot \frac{b}{a} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{b t}{a}\right)\right]_{0}^{\infty}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \pi}{a b}\left[\tan ^{-1}(\infty)-\tan ^{-1}(0)\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \pi}{a b}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-0\right)=\left(\frac{2 \pi}{a b} \times \frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\frac{\pi^{2}}{a b}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I=\frac{\pi^{2}}{2 a b}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} x+b^{2} \sin ^{2} x\right)} d x=\frac{\pi^{2}}{2 a b}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} x+b^{2} \sin ^{2} x\right)} d x{/tex} ........... (i)
Then, {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x)}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2}(\pi-x)+b^{2} \sin ^{2}(\pi-x)\right]} d x{/tex}
or {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x)}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} x+b^{2} \sin ^{2} x\right)} d x{/tex} ............ (ii)
Adding (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(x+\pi-x)}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} x+b^{2} \sin ^{2} x\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{d x}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} x+b^{2} \sin ^{2} x\right)}{/tex} [dividing num. and denim. by cos2x]
{tex}=2 \pi \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{d t}{\left(a^{2}+b^{2} t^{2}\right)}{/tex}, where tan x = t
{tex}=\frac{2 \pi}{b^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{d t}{\left(\frac{a^{2}}{b^{2}}+t^{2}\right)}=\left[\frac{2 \pi}{b^{2}} \cdot \frac{b}{a} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{b t}{a}\right)\right]_{0}^{\infty}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 \pi}{a b}\left[\tan ^{-1}(\infty)-\tan ^{-1}(0)\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \pi}{a b}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-0\right)=\left(\frac{2 \pi}{a b} \times \frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\frac{\pi^{2}}{a b}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I=\frac{\pi^{2}}{2 a b}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x}{\left(a^{2} \cos ^{2} x+b^{2} \sin ^{2} x\right)} d x=\frac{\pi^{2}}{2 a b}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} Now using partial fractions we have A(x 2 + 1) + (Bx + C)(x + 2) = x 2 + x + 1 Ax 2 + A + Bx 2 + Cx + 2Bx + 2C = x 2 + x + 1 (A + B)x 2 + (C + 2B)x + (A + 2C) = x 2 + x + 1 Equating coefficients A + B = 1 ….(i) A + 2C = 1 A = 1 - 2C ……(ii) 2B + C = 1 2B = 1 - C {tex}B=\frac{1-C}{2}{/tex} ..... (iii) {tex}(1-2 C)+\frac{1-C}{2}=1{/tex} 2 - 4C + 1 - C = 2 3 - 5C = 2 -5C = -1 {tex}C=\frac{1}{5}{/tex} And, 2B {tex}=1-\frac{1}{5}=\frac{4}{5}{/tex} {tex}B=\frac{2}{5}{/tex} {tex}A=1-2 \times \frac{1}{5}{/tex} {tex}=1-\frac{2}{5}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{5}{/tex} {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{A}{(x+2)} d x+\int \frac{B x+C}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{5} \times \int \frac{1}{(x+2)} d x+\frac{1}{5} \times \int \frac{2 x+1}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{5} \log |x+2|+\frac{1}{5} I_{1}+C_{1}{/tex} {tex}I_{1}=\int \frac{2 x+1}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{2 x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x+\int \frac{1}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\log \left|x^{2}+1\right|+\tan ^{-1} x+C_{2}{/tex} {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{5} \log |x+2|+\frac{1}{5} \log \left|x^{2}+1\right|{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{5} \tan ^{-1} x+C{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} Now using partial fractions we have A(x 2 + 1) + (Bx + C)(x + 2) = x 2 + x + 1 Ax 2 + A + Bx 2 + Cx + 2Bx + 2C = x 2 + x + 1 (A + B)x 2 + (C + 2B)x + (A + 2C) = x 2 + x + 1 Equating coefficients A + B = 1 ….(i) A + 2C = 1 A = 1 - 2C ……(ii) 2B + C = 1 2B = 1 - C {tex}B=\frac{1-C}{2}{/tex} ..... (iii) {tex}(1-2 C)+\frac{1-C}{2}=1{/tex} 2 - 4C + 1 - C = 2 3 - 5C = 2 -5C = -1 {tex}C=\frac{1}{5}{/tex} And, 2B {tex}=1-\frac{1}{5}=\frac{4}{5}{/tex} {tex}B=\frac{2}{5}{/tex} {tex}A=1-2 \times \frac{1}{5}{/tex} {tex}=1-\frac{2}{5}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{5}{/tex} {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{A}{(x+2)} d x+\int \frac{B x+C}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{5} \times \int \frac{1}{(x+2)} d x+\frac{1}{5} \times \int \frac{2 x+1}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{5} \log |x+2|+\frac{1}{5} I_{1}+C_{1}{/tex} {tex}I_{1}=\int \frac{2 x+1}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{2 x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x+\int \frac{1}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\log \left|x^{2}+1\right|+\tan ^{-1} x+C_{2}{/tex} {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{5} \log |x+2|+\frac{1}{5} \log \left|x^{2}+1\right|{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{5} \tan ^{-1} x+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex}
Now using partial fractions we have A(x2 + 1) + (Bx + C)(x + 2) = x2 + x + 1
Ax2 + A + Bx2 + Cx + 2Bx + 2C = x2 + x + 1
(A + B)x2 + (C + 2B)x + (A + 2C) = x2 + x + 1
Equating coefficients A + B = 1 ….(i)
A + 2C = 1
A = 1 - 2C ……(ii)
2B + C = 1
2B = 1 - C
{tex}B=\frac{1-C}{2}{/tex} ..... (iii)
{tex}(1-2 C)+\frac{1-C}{2}=1{/tex}
2 - 4C + 1 - C = 2
3 - 5C = 2
-5C = -1
{tex}C=\frac{1}{5}{/tex}
And, 2B {tex}=1-\frac{1}{5}=\frac{4}{5}{/tex}
{tex}B=\frac{2}{5}{/tex}
{tex}A=1-2 \times \frac{1}{5}{/tex}
{tex}=1-\frac{2}{5}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}
{tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex}{tex}=\int \frac{A}{(x+2)} d x+\int \frac{B x+C}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3}{5} \times \int \frac{1}{(x+2)} d x+\frac{1}{5} \times \int \frac{2 x+1}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3}{5} \log |x+2|+\frac{1}{5} I_{1}+C_{1}{/tex}
{tex}I_{1}=\int \frac{2 x+1}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex}{tex}=\int \frac{2 x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x+\int \frac{1}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\log \left|x^{2}+1\right|+\tan ^{-1} x+C_{2}{/tex}
{tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex}{tex}=\frac{3}{5} \log |x+2|+\frac{1}{5} \log \left|x^{2}+1\right|{/tex}{tex}+\frac{1}{5} \tan ^{-1} x+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Prove that {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos x d x=-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex}
question_text: Prove that {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos x d x=-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex}
explanation: Let the given integral be, {tex}\text { } I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x{/tex} ...(i) Then, {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos x d x{/tex} ...(ii) Adding (i) and (ii), we get {tex}2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos x d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}(\log \sin x+\log \cos x) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log (\sin x \cos x) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \left(\frac{2 \sin x \cos x}{2}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \left(\frac{\sin 2 x}{2}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 x d x-\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log 2 d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 x d x-\frac{\pi}{2}(\log 2){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 x d x-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\text { Let } \quad I_{1}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 x d x{/tex} Putting 2 x=t, we get {tex}I_{1}=\int_{0}^{\pi} \log \sin t \frac{d t}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{1}=\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi} \log \sin t d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{1}=\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin t d t{/tex} [Using property X] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{1}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x=I{/tex} [Using Property I] Putting 1 1 =I in (iii), we get {tex}2 I=I-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2 \Rightarrow I=-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex} Hence, {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos x d x=-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex}
solution: Let the given integral be, {tex}\text { } I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x{/tex} ...(i) Then, {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos x d x{/tex} ...(ii) Adding (i) and (ii), we get {tex}2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos x d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}(\log \sin x+\log \cos x) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log (\sin x \cos x) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \left(\frac{2 \sin x \cos x}{2}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \left(\frac{\sin 2 x}{2}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 x d x-\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log 2 d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 x d x-\frac{\pi}{2}(\log 2){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 x d x-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\text { Let } \quad I_{1}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 x d x{/tex} Putting 2 x=t, we get {tex}I_{1}=\int_{0}^{\pi} \log \sin t \frac{d t}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{1}=\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi} \log \sin t d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{1}=\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin t d t{/tex} [Using property X] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{1}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x=I{/tex} [Using Property I] Putting 1 1 =I in (iii), we get {tex}2 I=I-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2 \Rightarrow I=-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex} Hence, {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos x d x=-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the given integral be,{tex}\text { } I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x{/tex} ...(i)
Then, {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos x d x{/tex}...(ii)
Adding (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos x d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}(\log \sin x+\log \cos x) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log (\sin x \cos x) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \left(\frac{2 \sin x \cos x}{2}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \left(\frac{\sin 2 x}{2}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 x d x-\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log 2 d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 x d x-\frac{\pi}{2}(\log 2){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 x d x-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex} ...(iii)
{tex}\text { Let } \quad I_{1}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin 2 x d x{/tex}
Putting 2 x=t, we get
{tex}I_{1}=\int_{0}^{\pi} \log \sin t \frac{d t}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{1}=\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi} \log \sin t d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{1}=\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin t d t{/tex}  [Using property X]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I_{1}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x=I{/tex} [Using Property I]
Putting 11=I in (iii), we get
{tex}2 I=I-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2 \Rightarrow I=-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \sin x d x=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cos x d x=-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex}

explanation: {tex}\text { Let } I=\int_{0}^{1} \cot ^{-1}\left(1-x+x^{2}\right) d x \text { . Then, }{/tex} we have {tex}I=\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{1-x+x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \cot ^{-1} x=\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{x}, x>0\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{1}{1-x(1-x)}\right\} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{x+(1-x)}{1-x(1-x)}\right\} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{0}^{1}\{\tan ^{-1} x+\tan ^{-1}(1-x) \}d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1} x d x+\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1}(1-x) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1} x d x+\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1}\{1-(1-x)\} d x{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1} x d x+\int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1} x d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=2 \int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1} x d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=2 \int_{0}^{1} \tan ^{-1} x \cdot {1} ~d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=2\left[x \tan ^{-1} x\right]_{0}^{1}-2 \int_{0}^{1} \frac{x}{1+x^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=2\left[x \tan ^{-1} x\right]_{0}^{1}-\int_{0}^{1} \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=2\left[x \tan ^{-1} x\right]_{0}^{1}-\left[\log \left(1+x^{2}\right)\right]_{0}^{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I= 2\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-0\right)-(\log 2-\log 1){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{\pi}{2}-\log 2{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: {tex}I=\int \frac{(1+\sin x)}{(1-\sin x)} \times \frac{(1+\sin x)}{(1+\sin x)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{(1+\sin x)^{2}}{\left(1-\sin ^{2} x\right)} d x=\int \frac{\left(1+\sin ^{2} x+2 \sin x\right)}{\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int\left(\frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x}+\frac{\sin ^{2} x}{\cos ^{2} x}+\frac{2 \sin x}{\cos ^{2} x}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int\left(\sec ^{2} x+\tan ^{2} x+2 \sec x \tan x\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int\left(2 \sec ^{2} x-1+2 \sec x \tan x\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=2 \int \sec ^{2} x d x-\int d x+2 \int \sec x \tan x d x{/tex} = 2tan x - x + 2sec x + C.
solution: {tex}I=\int \frac{(1+\sin x)}{(1-\sin x)} \times \frac{(1+\sin x)}{(1+\sin x)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{(1+\sin x)^{2}}{\left(1-\sin ^{2} x\right)} d x=\int \frac{\left(1+\sin ^{2} x+2 \sin x\right)}{\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int\left(\frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x}+\frac{\sin ^{2} x}{\cos ^{2} x}+\frac{2 \sin x}{\cos ^{2} x}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int\left(\sec ^{2} x+\tan ^{2} x+2 \sec x \tan x\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int\left(2 \sec ^{2} x-1+2 \sec x \tan x\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=2 \int \sec ^{2} x d x-\int d x+2 \int \sec x \tan x d x{/tex} = 2tan x - x + 2sec x + C.
solution_html:
{tex}I=\int \frac{(1+\sin x)}{(1-\sin x)} \times \frac{(1+\sin x)}{(1+\sin x)} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int \frac{(1+\sin x)^{2}}{\left(1-\sin ^{2} x\right)} d x=\int \frac{\left(1+\sin ^{2} x+2 \sin x\right)}{\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int\left(\frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x}+\frac{\sin ^{2} x}{\cos ^{2} x}+\frac{2 \sin x}{\cos ^{2} x}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int\left(\sec ^{2} x+\tan ^{2} x+2 \sec x \tan x\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int\left(2 \sec ^{2} x-1+2 \sec x \tan x\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}=2 \int \sec ^{2} x d x-\int d x+2 \int \sec x \tan x d x{/tex}
= 2tan x - x + 2sec x + C.

explanation: It is given that {tex}I=\int \frac{2 \cos x}{3 \sin ^{2} x} d x{/tex} We can write it as {tex}I=\frac{2}{3} \int \cot x \ cosec x \, d x{/tex} By integrating w.r.t. x {tex}I=\frac{2}{3}(-\ cosec \ x)+c{/tex} So we get {tex}I=-\frac{2}{3} \ cosec\ x+c{/tex} .
solution: It is given that {tex}I=\int \frac{2 \cos x}{3 \sin ^{2} x} d x{/tex} We can write it as {tex}I=\frac{2}{3} \int \cot x \ cosec x \, d x{/tex} By integrating w.r.t. x {tex}I=\frac{2}{3}(-\ cosec \ x)+c{/tex} So we get {tex}I=-\frac{2}{3} \ cosec\ x+c{/tex} .

It is given that
{tex}I=\int \frac{2 \cos x}{3 \sin ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
We can write it as
{tex}I=\frac{2}{3} \int \cot x \ cosec x \, d x{/tex}
By integrating w.r.t. x
{tex}I=\frac{2}{3}(-\ cosec \ x)+c{/tex}
So we get
{tex}I=-\frac{2}{3} \ cosec\ x+c{/tex} .
