subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let f(x) {tex}=\log \left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right){/tex} Then, f(-x) {tex}=\log \left(\frac{2+x}{2-x}\right)=\log \left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right)^{-1}{/tex} {tex}=-\log \left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right)=-f(x){/tex} f(-x) = -f(x) {tex}\therefore {/tex} f(x) is an odd function of x. But, we know that {tex}\int \limits_{-a}^{a} f(x) d x=0{/tex} , when f(x) is an odd function of x. {tex}\therefore \int \limits_{-1}^{1} \log \left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right) d x=0{/tex}
solution: Let f(x) {tex}=\log \left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right){/tex} Then, f(-x) {tex}=\log \left(\frac{2+x}{2-x}\right)=\log \left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right)^{-1}{/tex} {tex}=-\log \left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right)=-f(x){/tex} f(-x) = -f(x) {tex}\therefore {/tex} f(x) is an odd function of x. But, we know that {tex}\int \limits_{-a}^{a} f(x) d x=0{/tex} , when f(x) is an odd function of x. {tex}\therefore \int \limits_{-1}^{1} \log \left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right) d x=0{/tex}
solution_html:
Let f(x) {tex}=\log \left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right){/tex}
Then, f(-x) {tex}=\log \left(\frac{2+x}{2-x}\right)=\log \left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right)^{-1}{/tex} {tex}=-\log \left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right)=-f(x){/tex}

f(-x) = -f(x)
{tex}\therefore {/tex} f(x) is an odd function of x.
But, we know that {tex}\int \limits_{-a}^{a} f(x) d x=0{/tex}, when f(x) is an odd function of x.
{tex}\therefore \int \limits_{-1}^{1} \log \left(\frac{2-x}{2+x}\right) d x=0{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: {tex}y=\int_{0}^{2} x \sqrt{2-x} d x{/tex} Using theorem of definite integral {tex}\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{0}^{2}(2-x) \sqrt{x} d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{0}^{2} 2 x^{\frac{1}{2}}-x^{\frac{3}{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}y=\left(2 \frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}-\frac{x^{\frac{5}{2}}}{\frac{5}{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}y=\frac{8 \sqrt{2}}{3}-\frac{8 \sqrt{2}}{5}=\frac{16 \sqrt{2}}{15}{/tex} .
solution: {tex}y=\int_{0}^{2} x \sqrt{2-x} d x{/tex} Using theorem of definite integral {tex}\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{0}^{2}(2-x) \sqrt{x} d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{0}^{2} 2 x^{\frac{1}{2}}-x^{\frac{3}{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}y=\left(2 \frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}-\frac{x^{\frac{5}{2}}}{\frac{5}{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}y=\frac{8 \sqrt{2}}{3}-\frac{8 \sqrt{2}}{5}=\frac{16 \sqrt{2}}{15}{/tex} .
solution_html:
{tex}y=\int_{0}^{2} x \sqrt{2-x} d x{/tex}
Using theorem of definite integral
{tex}\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex}
{tex}y=\int_{0}^{2}(2-x) \sqrt{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}y=\int_{0}^{2} 2 x^{\frac{1}{2}}-x^{\frac{3}{2}} d x{/tex}
{tex}y=\left(2 \frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}-\frac{x^{\frac{5}{2}}}{\frac{5}{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}y=\frac{8 \sqrt{2}}{3}-\frac{8 \sqrt{2}}{5}=\frac{16 \sqrt{2}}{15}{/tex}  .

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let I, {tex}=\int \frac{(2 x-1)}{(x-1)(x+2)(x-3)} d x{/tex} Using partial fractions we have, {tex}\frac{(2 x-1)}{(x-1)(x+2)(x-3)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A}{x-1}+\frac{B}{x+2}+\frac{C}{x-3}{/tex} .... (i) Which implies, A(x + 2)(x - 2) + B(x - 1)(x - 3) + C(x - 1)(x + 2) = 2x - 1 Now put x + 2 = 0 Therefore, x = -2 A(0) + B(-2 - 1)(-2 - 3) + C(0) = 2x - 2 - 1 B(-3)(-5) = -5 {tex}B=-\frac{1}{3}{/tex} Now put x - 3 = 0 Therefore, x = 3 A(0) + B(0) + C(2) (5) = 5 {tex}C=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Now put x - 1 = 0 Therefore, x = 1 A(3) (-2) + B(0) + C(0) = 1 {tex}A=-\frac{1}{6}{/tex} Now From equation (i) we get, {tex}\frac{(2 x-1)}{(x-1)(x+2)(x-3)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{6} \times \frac{1}{x-1}-\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{x+2}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{x-3}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{(2 x-1)}{(x-1)(x+2)(x-3)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{6} \int \frac{1}{x-1} d x-\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{1}{x+2} d x{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x-3} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{6} \log |x-1|-\frac{1}{3} \log |x+2|{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2} \log |x-3|+c{/tex}
solution: Let I, {tex}=\int \frac{(2 x-1)}{(x-1)(x+2)(x-3)} d x{/tex} Using partial fractions we have, {tex}\frac{(2 x-1)}{(x-1)(x+2)(x-3)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{A}{x-1}+\frac{B}{x+2}+\frac{C}{x-3}{/tex} .... (i) Which implies, A(x + 2)(x - 2) + B(x - 1)(x - 3) + C(x - 1)(x + 2) = 2x - 1 Now put x + 2 = 0 Therefore, x = -2 A(0) + B(-2 - 1)(-2 - 3) + C(0) = 2x - 2 - 1 B(-3)(-5) = -5 {tex}B=-\frac{1}{3}{/tex} Now put x - 3 = 0 Therefore, x = 3 A(0) + B(0) + C(2) (5) = 5 {tex}C=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Now put x - 1 = 0 Therefore, x = 1 A(3) (-2) + B(0) + C(0) = 1 {tex}A=-\frac{1}{6}{/tex} Now From equation (i) we get, {tex}\frac{(2 x-1)}{(x-1)(x+2)(x-3)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{6} \times \frac{1}{x-1}-\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{x+2}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{x-3}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{(2 x-1)}{(x-1)(x+2)(x-3)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{6} \int \frac{1}{x-1} d x-\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{1}{x+2} d x{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x-3} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{6} \log |x-1|-\frac{1}{3} \log |x+2|{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2} \log |x-3|+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I, {tex}=\int \frac{(2 x-1)}{(x-1)(x+2)(x-3)} d x{/tex}
Using partial fractions we have,
 {tex}\frac{(2 x-1)}{(x-1)(x+2)(x-3)}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{A}{x-1}+\frac{B}{x+2}+\frac{C}{x-3}{/tex} .... (i)
Which implies,
A(x + 2)(x - 2) + B(x - 1)(x - 3) + C(x - 1)(x + 2) = 2x - 1
Now put x + 2 = 0
Therefore, x = -2
A(0) + B(-2 - 1)(-2 - 3) + C(0) = 2x - 2 - 1
B(-3)(-5) = -5
{tex}B=-\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
Now put x - 3 = 0
Therefore, x = 3
A(0) + B(0) + C(2) (5) = 5
{tex}C=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Now put x - 1 = 0
Therefore, x = 1
A(3) (-2) + B(0) + C(0) = 1
{tex}A=-\frac{1}{6}{/tex}
Now From equation (i) we get,
{tex}\frac{(2 x-1)}{(x-1)(x+2)(x-3)}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{-1}{6} \times \frac{1}{x-1}-\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{x+2}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{x-3}{/tex}
{tex}\int \frac{(2 x-1)}{(x-1)(x+2)(x-3)} d x{/tex}{tex}=\frac{-1}{6} \int \frac{1}{x-1} d x-\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{1}{x+2} d x{/tex}{tex}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x-3} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-1}{6} \log |x-1|-\frac{1}{3} \log |x+2|{/tex}{tex}+\frac{1}{2} \log |x-3|+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate the Integral:{tex}\int \frac{\sin 2 x}{(a+b \cos x)^{2}} d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate the Integral: {tex}\int \frac{\sin 2 x}{(a+b \cos x)^{2}} d x{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}I=\int \frac{\sin 2 x}{(a+b \cos x)^{2}} d x{/tex} …..... (i) {tex}I=\int \frac{2 \sin x \cos x}{(a+b \cos x)^{2}} d x{/tex} Also et (a + bcos x) = t {tex}\Rightarrow(\cos x)=\frac{t-a}{b}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(\sin x) d x=\frac{d t}{-b}{/tex} Putting this value in equation (i), we get {tex}I=\frac{2}{-b^{2}} \int \frac{t-a}{t^{2}} d t{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{2}{-b^{2}}\left[\int \frac{t}{t^{2}} d t-\int \frac{a}{t^{2}} d t\right]{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{2}{-b^{2}}\left[\int \frac{1}{t} d t-a \int \frac{1}{t^{2}} d t\right]{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{2}{-b^{2}}\left[\log |t|-a\left(-\frac{1}{t}\right)+c\right]{/tex} {tex}I=-\frac{2}{b^{2}}\left[\log |a+b \cos x|+\left(\frac{a}{a+b \cos x}\right)\right]+c{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}I=\int \frac{\sin 2 x}{(a+b \cos x)^{2}} d x{/tex} …..... (i) {tex}I=\int \frac{2 \sin x \cos x}{(a+b \cos x)^{2}} d x{/tex} Also et (a + bcos x) = t {tex}\Rightarrow(\cos x)=\frac{t-a}{b}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(\sin x) d x=\frac{d t}{-b}{/tex} Putting this value in equation (i), we get {tex}I=\frac{2}{-b^{2}} \int \frac{t-a}{t^{2}} d t{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{2}{-b^{2}}\left[\int \frac{t}{t^{2}} d t-\int \frac{a}{t^{2}} d t\right]{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{2}{-b^{2}}\left[\int \frac{1}{t} d t-a \int \frac{1}{t^{2}} d t\right]{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{2}{-b^{2}}\left[\log |t|-a\left(-\frac{1}{t}\right)+c\right]{/tex} {tex}I=-\frac{2}{b^{2}}\left[\log |a+b \cos x|+\left(\frac{a}{a+b \cos x}\right)\right]+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let  {tex}I=\int \frac{\sin 2 x}{(a+b \cos x)^{2}} d x{/tex}…..... (i)
{tex}I=\int \frac{2 \sin x \cos x}{(a+b \cos x)^{2}} d x{/tex}
Also et (a + bcos x) = t
{tex}\Rightarrow(\cos x)=\frac{t-a}{b}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(\sin x) d x=\frac{d t}{-b}{/tex}
Putting this value in equation (i), we get
{tex}I=\frac{2}{-b^{2}} \int \frac{t-a}{t^{2}} d t{/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{2}{-b^{2}}\left[\int \frac{t}{t^{2}} d t-\int \frac{a}{t^{2}} d t\right]{/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{2}{-b^{2}}\left[\int \frac{1}{t} d t-a \int \frac{1}{t^{2}} d t\right]{/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{2}{-b^{2}}\left[\log |t|-a\left(-\frac{1}{t}\right)+c\right]{/tex}
{tex}I=-\frac{2}{b^{2}}\left[\log |a+b \cos x|+\left(\frac{a}{a+b \cos x}\right)\right]+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate: {tex}\int \frac{\sin 2 x}{(1+\sin x)(2+\sin x)} d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate: {tex}\int \frac{\sin 2 x}{(1+\sin x)(2+\sin x)} d x{/tex}
explanation: Let the given integral be written as, {tex}\int \frac{\sin 2 x}{(1+\sin x)(2+\sin x)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{1+\sin x}+\frac{B}{2+\sin x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin2x = A(2 + sin x) + B (1 + sinx) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 sinx cosx = (2A + B) + (A + B) sinx Equating similar terms, we get, 2A + B = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = - 2A and A + B = 2 cosx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -A = 2cos x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = - 2 cosx and B = 4 cosx Thus, I = {tex}\int-\frac{2 \cos x}{1+\sin x} d x{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{4 \cos x}{2+\sin x} d x{/tex} = -2 log {tex}|1+\sin x|+4 \log |2+\sin x|+c{/tex} = {tex}\log \left|\frac{(2+\sin x)^{4}}{(1+\sin x)^{2}}\right|+c{/tex}
solution: Let the given integral be written as, {tex}\int \frac{\sin 2 x}{(1+\sin x)(2+\sin x)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{1+\sin x}+\frac{B}{2+\sin x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin2x = A(2 + sin x) + B (1 + sinx) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 sinx cosx = (2A + B) + (A + B) sinx Equating similar terms, we get, 2A + B = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = - 2A and A + B = 2 cosx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -A = 2cos x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = - 2 cosx and B = 4 cosx Thus, I = {tex}\int-\frac{2 \cos x}{1+\sin x} d x{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{4 \cos x}{2+\sin x} d x{/tex} = -2 log {tex}|1+\sin x|+4 \log |2+\sin x|+c{/tex} = {tex}\log \left|\frac{(2+\sin x)^{4}}{(1+\sin x)^{2}}\right|+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the given integral be written as,
{tex}\int \frac{\sin 2 x}{(1+\sin x)(2+\sin x)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A}{1+\sin x}+\frac{B}{2+\sin x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin2x = A(2 + sin x) + B (1 + sinx)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 sinx  cosx = (2A + B) + (A + B) sinx
Equating similar terms, we get,
2A + B = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = - 2A and
A + B = 2 cosx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -A = 2cos x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = - 2 cosx
and B = 4 cosx
Thus,
I = {tex}\int-\frac{2 \cos x}{1+\sin x} d x{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{4 \cos x}{2+\sin x} d x{/tex}
= -2 log {tex}|1+\sin x|+4 \log |2+\sin x|+c{/tex}
= {tex}\log \left|\frac{(2+\sin x)^{4}}{(1+\sin x)^{2}}\right|+c{/tex}

explanation: Let I = {tex}\int{/tex} (sin -1 x) 2 dx Also let sin -1 x = t Then, x = sin t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = cos t dt {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int{/tex} (sin -1 x) 2 dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int\underset I{t^2}\;\underset{II}\cos\;t\;dt{/tex} = t 2 (sin t) - {tex}\int{/tex} 2t sin t dt = t sin t - 2 {tex}\int\underset It\;\underset{II}sin\;t\;dt{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = t 2 sin t - 2 {t (- cos t) - {tex}\int{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} (- cos t) dt} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = t 2 sin t - 2 {- t cos t + {tex}\int{/tex} cos t dt} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}x\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^{2}+2 \sqrt{1-x^{2}} \sin ^{-1} x-2 x+C{/tex}
solution: Let I = {tex}\int{/tex} (sin -1 x) 2 dx Also let sin -1 x = t Then, x = sin t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = cos t dt {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\int{/tex} (sin -1 x) 2 dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int\underset I{t^2}\;\underset{II}\cos\;t\;dt{/tex} = t 2 (sin t) - {tex}\int{/tex} 2t sin t dt = t sin t - 2 {tex}\int\underset It\;\underset{II}sin\;t\;dt{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = t 2 sin t - 2 {t (- cos t) - {tex}\int{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} (- cos t) dt} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = t 2 sin t - 2 {- t cos t + {tex}\int{/tex} cos t dt} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}x\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^{2}+2 \sqrt{1-x^{2}} \sin ^{-1} x-2 x+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int{/tex} (sin-1 x)2 dx
Also let sin-1 x = t
Then, x = sin t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = cos t dt
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  I = {tex}\int{/tex} (sin-1 x)2 dx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\int\underset I{t^2}\;\underset{II}\cos\;t\;dt{/tex} = t2 (sin t) - {tex}\int{/tex} 2t sin t dt = t sin t - 2 {tex}\int\underset It\;\underset{II}sin\;t\;dt{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = t2 sin t - 2 {t (- cos t) - {tex}\int{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} (- cos t) dt}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = t2 sin t - 2 {- t cos t + {tex}\int{/tex}cos t dt}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}x\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^{2}+2 \sqrt{1-x^{2}} \sin ^{-1} x-2 x+C{/tex}

question_html: Evaluate the Integral:{tex}\int\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^{2} d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate the Integral: {tex}\int\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^{2} d x{/tex}
explanation: To find: Value of {tex}\int{/tex} (sin -1 x) 2 dx Formula used: {tex}\int{/tex} sin x , dx = cos x+c We have, I = {tex}\int{/tex} (sin -1 x) 2 dx …(i) Let sin -1 x = t, x = sint, {tex}\Rightarrow \operatorname{cost}=\sqrt{1-x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{1-x^{2}} d t=d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{1-(\sin t)^{2}} d t=d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} t} d t=d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cost dt = dx Putting this value in equation (i).Hence the given integral becomes, I= {tex}\int {/tex} t 2 cos tdt {tex}I= t^{2} \int\cos t d t-\int\left[\frac{d\left(t^{2}\right)}{d t} \int \cos t \, d t\right] d t{/tex} I=t 2 sin t- {tex}\int{/tex} [2 tsin t]dt Again by parts we have, {tex}I=t^{2} \sin t-2\left[-t \operatorname{cost}+\int 1 . \operatorname{cost} d t\right]{/tex} I=t 2 sin t+2 t cost-2 sin t+c {tex}I=\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^{2} x+2\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right) \sqrt{1-x^{2}}-2 x+c{/tex}
solution: To find: Value of {tex}\int{/tex} (sin -1 x) 2 dx Formula used: {tex}\int{/tex} sin x , dx = cos x+c We have, I = {tex}\int{/tex} (sin -1 x) 2 dx …(i) Let sin -1 x = t, x = sint, {tex}\Rightarrow \operatorname{cost}=\sqrt{1-x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{1-x^{2}} d t=d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{1-(\sin t)^{2}} d t=d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} t} d t=d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cost dt = dx Putting this value in equation (i).Hence the given integral becomes, I= {tex}\int {/tex} t 2 cos tdt {tex}I= t^{2} \int\cos t d t-\int\left[\frac{d\left(t^{2}\right)}{d t} \int \cos t \, d t\right] d t{/tex} I=t 2 sin t- {tex}\int{/tex} [2 tsin t]dt Again by parts we have, {tex}I=t^{2} \sin t-2\left[-t \operatorname{cost}+\int 1 . \operatorname{cost} d t\right]{/tex} I=t 2 sin t+2 t cost-2 sin t+c {tex}I=\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^{2} x+2\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right) \sqrt{1-x^{2}}-2 x+c{/tex}
solution_html:
To find: Value of {tex}\int{/tex}(sin -1 x)2 dx
Formula used: {tex}\int{/tex} sin x , dx = cos x+c
We have, I ={tex}\int{/tex}(sin -1 x)2 dx …(i)
Let  sin-1x = t, x = sint,
{tex}\Rightarrow \operatorname{cost}=\sqrt{1-x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{1-x^{2}} d t=d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{1-(\sin t)^{2}} d t=d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} t} d t=d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cost dt = dx
Putting this value in equation (i).Hence the given integral becomes, 
I={tex}\int {/tex} t2 cos tdt
{tex}I= t^{2} \int\cos t d t-\int\left[\frac{d\left(t^{2}\right)}{d t} \int \cos t \, d t\right] d t{/tex}
I=t2 sin t-{tex}\int{/tex}[2 tsin t]dt

Again by parts we have, 
{tex}I=t^{2} \sin t-2\left[-t \operatorname{cost}+\int 1 . \operatorname{cost} d t\right]{/tex}
I=t2 sin t+2 t cost-2 sin t+c
{tex}I=\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^{2} x+2\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right) \sqrt{1-x^{2}}-2 x+c{/tex}

explanation: Let I = {tex}\int {/tex} sin 4 x dx, then I = {tex}\frac{1}{4} {/tex} {tex}\int {/tex} (2 sin 2 x) 2 dx = {tex}\frac{1}{4} \int{/tex} (1 - cos 2x ) 2 dx = {tex}\frac{1}{4} \int{/tex} (1 + cos 2 2x - 2 cos 2x) dx = {tex}\frac{1}{8} \int{/tex} (2 + 2 cos 2 2x - 4cos 2x) dx = {tex}\frac{1}{8} \int{/tex} [2 + (1 + cos 4x) - 4cos 2x] dx = {tex}\frac{3}{8} \int d x+\frac{1}{8} \int \cos 4 x \, d x-\frac{1}{2} \int \cos 2 x \, d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{8} x+\frac{\sin 4 x}{32}-\frac{\sin 2 x}{4}+C{/tex}
solution: Let I = {tex}\int {/tex} sin 4 x dx, then I = {tex}\frac{1}{4} {/tex} {tex}\int {/tex} (2 sin 2 x) 2 dx = {tex}\frac{1}{4} \int{/tex} (1 - cos 2x ) 2 dx = {tex}\frac{1}{4} \int{/tex} (1 + cos 2 2x - 2 cos 2x) dx = {tex}\frac{1}{8} \int{/tex} (2 + 2 cos 2 2x - 4cos 2x) dx = {tex}\frac{1}{8} \int{/tex} [2 + (1 + cos 4x) - 4cos 2x] dx = {tex}\frac{3}{8} \int d x+\frac{1}{8} \int \cos 4 x \, d x-\frac{1}{2} \int \cos 2 x \, d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{8} x+\frac{\sin 4 x}{32}-\frac{\sin 2 x}{4}+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I =  {tex}\int {/tex}sin 4 x dx, then
I = {tex}\frac{1}{4} {/tex}{tex}\int {/tex}(2 sin 2 x)2 dx
= {tex}\frac{1}{4} \int{/tex}(1 - cos 2x )2 dx = {tex}\frac{1}{4} \int{/tex}(1 + cos22x - 2 cos 2x) dx
= {tex}\frac{1}{8} \int{/tex}(2 + 2 cos2 2x - 4cos 2x) dx
= {tex}\frac{1}{8} \int{/tex}[2 + (1 + cos 4x) - 4cos 2x] dx
= {tex}\frac{3}{8} \int d x+\frac{1}{8} \int \cos 4 x \, d x-\frac{1}{2} \int \cos 2 x \, d x{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3}{8} x+\frac{\sin 4 x}{32}-\frac{\sin 2 x}{4}+C{/tex}

question_html: Prove that: {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{3} x}{\left(\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x\right)} d x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}.
question_text: Prove that: {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{3} x}{\left(\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x\right)} d x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} .
explanation: Let the given integral be, {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex} ... (i) Use King theorem of definite integral we have, {tex}\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}{\sin ^{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)+\cos ^{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex} .... (ii) Adding eq. (i) and eq (ii) {tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex} {tex}+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} 1 d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=(x)_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex} {tex}y=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution: Let the given integral be, {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex} ... (i) Use King theorem of definite integral we have, {tex}\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}{\sin ^{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)+\cos ^{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex} .... (ii) Adding eq. (i) and eq (ii) {tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex} {tex}+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} 1 d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=(x)_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex} {tex}y=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the given integral be,{tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex} ... (i)
Use King theorem of definite integral we have, 
{tex}\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex}
{tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}{\sin ^{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)+\cos ^{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)} d x{/tex}
{tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex} .... (ii)
Adding eq. (i) and eq (ii)
{tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex}{tex}+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} 1 d x{/tex}
{tex}2 y=(x)_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}y=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Prove that: {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3} x d x}{\left(\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x\right)}=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}.
question_text: Prove that: {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3} x d x}{\left(\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x\right)}=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} dx{/tex} ........ (i) Use King theorem of definite integral {tex}\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}{\sin ^{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)+\cos ^{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)} d x{/tex} ...... (ii) Adding eq. (i) and eq. (ii), we get {tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex} {tex}+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3} x+\sin ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} 1 d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=(x)_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex} {tex}y=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} dx{/tex} ........ (i) Use King theorem of definite integral {tex}\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex} {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}{\sin ^{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)+\cos ^{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)} d x{/tex} ...... (ii) Adding eq. (i) and eq. (ii), we get {tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex} {tex}+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3} x+\sin ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} 1 d x{/tex} {tex}2 y=(x)_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex} {tex}y=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} dx{/tex} ........ (i)
Use King theorem of definite integral
{tex}\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{a}^{b} f(a+b-x) d x{/tex}
{tex}y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}{\sin ^{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)+\cos ^{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)} d x{/tex} ...... (ii)
Adding eq. (i) and eq. (ii), we get
{tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex}{tex}+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex}
{tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{3} x+\sin ^{3} x}{\sin ^{3} x+\cos ^{3} x} d x{/tex}
{tex}2 y=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} 1 d x{/tex}
{tex}2 y=(x)_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}y=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Putting x 3 = t and x 2 dx {tex}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} dt, we get {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}}{\left(1+x^{3}\right)\left(2+x^{3}\right)} d x=\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int \frac{d t}{(1+t)(2+t)}{/tex} Using partial fractions, we have, {tex}\frac{1}{(1+t)(2+t)}=\frac{A}{(1+t)}+\frac{B}{(2+t)}{/tex} . Then, 1 = A(2 + t) + B(1 + t) .... (i) Putting t = 1 in (i), we get A = 1 Putting t = -2 in (i), we get B = -1 {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{(1+t)(2+t)}=\frac{1}{(1+t)}-\frac{1}{(2+t)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\int \frac{d t}{(1+t)(2+t)}{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{d t}{(1+t)}-\int \frac{d t}{(2+t)}{/tex} {tex}=\log |1+t|-\log |2+t|+C{/tex} {tex}=\log \left|\frac{1+t}{2+t}\right|+C{/tex} {tex}=\log \left|\frac{1+x^{3}}{2+x^{3}}\right|+C{/tex}
solution: Putting x 3 = t and x 2 dx {tex}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} dt, we get {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}}{\left(1+x^{3}\right)\left(2+x^{3}\right)} d x=\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int \frac{d t}{(1+t)(2+t)}{/tex} Using partial fractions, we have, {tex}\frac{1}{(1+t)(2+t)}=\frac{A}{(1+t)}+\frac{B}{(2+t)}{/tex} . Then, 1 = A(2 + t) + B(1 + t) .... (i) Putting t = 1 in (i), we get A = 1 Putting t = -2 in (i), we get B = -1 {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{(1+t)(2+t)}=\frac{1}{(1+t)}-\frac{1}{(2+t)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\int \frac{d t}{(1+t)(2+t)}{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{d t}{(1+t)}-\int \frac{d t}{(2+t)}{/tex} {tex}=\log |1+t|-\log |2+t|+C{/tex} {tex}=\log \left|\frac{1+t}{2+t}\right|+C{/tex} {tex}=\log \left|\frac{1+x^{3}}{2+x^{3}}\right|+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Putting x3 = t and x2dx {tex}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}dt, we get
{tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}}{\left(1+x^{3}\right)\left(2+x^{3}\right)} d x=\frac{1}{3} \cdot \int \frac{d t}{(1+t)(2+t)}{/tex}
Using partial fractions, we have,

{tex}\frac{1}{(1+t)(2+t)}=\frac{A}{(1+t)}+\frac{B}{(2+t)}{/tex}. Then,
1 = A(2 + t) + B(1 + t) .... (i)
Putting t = 1 in (i), we get A = 1
Putting t = -2 in (i), we get B = -1
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{(1+t)(2+t)}=\frac{1}{(1+t)}-\frac{1}{(2+t)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\int \frac{d t}{(1+t)(2+t)}{/tex}{tex}=\int \frac{d t}{(1+t)}-\int \frac{d t}{(2+t)}{/tex}
{tex}=\log |1+t|-\log |2+t|+C{/tex}
{tex}=\log \left|\frac{1+t}{2+t}\right|+C{/tex}
{tex}=\log \left|\frac{1+x^{3}}{2+x^{3}}\right|+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Evaluate: {tex}\int{/tex} e ax cos bx dx
explanation: Let the given integral be, I = {tex}\int{/tex} e ax cos (bx) dx Now solving this by integral as parts Considering cos (bx) as first function and e ax as second function I = cos (bx) {tex}\frac{e^{a x}}{a}-\int{/tex} - sin (bx) {tex}\times{/tex} b {tex}\times \frac{e^{a x}}{a}{/tex} dx {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{e^{a x} \cos (b x)}{a}+\frac{b}{a} \int{/tex} sin (bx) e ax dx {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{e^{a x}}{a}{/tex} cos (bx) + {tex}\frac{b}{a} \int{/tex} e ax {tex}\times{/tex} sin (bx) dx {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{e^{a x}}{a}{/tex} cos (bx) + {tex}\frac{b}{a}{/tex} I 1 ...(i) where I 1 = {tex}\int{/tex} e ax sin (bx) dx Now, I 1 = {tex}\int{/tex} e ax sin (bx) dx Considering sin (bx) as first function e ax as second function I 1 = sin (bx) {tex}\frac{e^{a x}}{a}-\int{/tex} cos (bx) {tex}b \frac{e^{a x}}{a}{/tex} dx {tex}\Rightarrow I_{1}=\frac{\sin (b x) e^{a x}}{a}-\frac{b}{a} \int{/tex} e ax cos (bx) dx {tex}\Rightarrow I_{1}=\frac{e^{a x} \sin (b x)}{a}-\frac{b}{a} I{/tex} ...(ii) from (i) and (ii) {tex}I=\frac{e^{a x}}{a}{/tex} cos (bx) + {tex}\frac{b}{a}\left[\frac{e^{a x} \sin (b x)}{a}-\frac{b}{a} I\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{e^{a x} \cos (b x)}{a}+\frac{b e^{a x} \sin (b x)}{a^{2}}-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}} I{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I\left(1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)=e^{a x}\left[\frac{a \cos (b x)+b \sin (b x)}{a^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore I=\frac{e^{a x}[a \cos b x+b \sin (b x)]}{\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right)}+C{/tex}
solution: Let the given integral be, I = {tex}\int{/tex} e ax cos (bx) dx Now solving this by integral as parts Considering cos (bx) as first function and e ax as second function I = cos (bx) {tex}\frac{e^{a x}}{a}-\int{/tex} - sin (bx) {tex}\times{/tex} b {tex}\times \frac{e^{a x}}{a}{/tex} dx {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{e^{a x} \cos (b x)}{a}+\frac{b}{a} \int{/tex} sin (bx) e ax dx {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{e^{a x}}{a}{/tex} cos (bx) + {tex}\frac{b}{a} \int{/tex} e ax {tex}\times{/tex} sin (bx) dx {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{e^{a x}}{a}{/tex} cos (bx) + {tex}\frac{b}{a}{/tex} I 1 ...(i) where I 1 = {tex}\int{/tex} e ax sin (bx) dx Now, I 1 = {tex}\int{/tex} e ax sin (bx) dx Considering sin (bx) as first function e ax as second function I 1 = sin (bx) {tex}\frac{e^{a x}}{a}-\int{/tex} cos (bx) {tex}b \frac{e^{a x}}{a}{/tex} dx {tex}\Rightarrow I_{1}=\frac{\sin (b x) e^{a x}}{a}-\frac{b}{a} \int{/tex} e ax cos (bx) dx {tex}\Rightarrow I_{1}=\frac{e^{a x} \sin (b x)}{a}-\frac{b}{a} I{/tex} ...(ii) from (i) and (ii) {tex}I=\frac{e^{a x}}{a}{/tex} cos (bx) + {tex}\frac{b}{a}\left[\frac{e^{a x} \sin (b x)}{a}-\frac{b}{a} I\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{e^{a x} \cos (b x)}{a}+\frac{b e^{a x} \sin (b x)}{a^{2}}-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}} I{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I\left(1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)=e^{a x}\left[\frac{a \cos (b x)+b \sin (b x)}{a^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore I=\frac{e^{a x}[a \cos b x+b \sin (b x)]}{\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right)}+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the given integral be,

I = {tex}\int{/tex} eax cos (bx) dx

Now solving this by integral as parts
Considering cos (bx) as first function and eax as second function
I = cos (bx) {tex}\frac{e^{a x}}{a}-\int{/tex} - sin (bx) {tex}\times{/tex} b {tex}\times \frac{e^{a x}}{a}{/tex} dx
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{e^{a x} \cos (b x)}{a}+\frac{b}{a} \int{/tex} sin (bx) eax dx
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{e^{a x}}{a}{/tex} cos (bx) + {tex}\frac{b}{a} \int{/tex} eax {tex}\times{/tex} sin (bx) dx
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{e^{a x}}{a}{/tex} cos (bx) + {tex}\frac{b}{a}{/tex} I1 ...(i)
where I1 = {tex}\int{/tex} eax sin (bx) dx
Now, I1 = {tex}\int{/tex} eax sin (bx) dx
Considering sin (bx) as first function eax as second function
I1 = sin (bx) {tex}\frac{e^{a x}}{a}-\int{/tex} cos (bx) {tex}b \frac{e^{a x}}{a}{/tex} dx
{tex}\Rightarrow I_{1}=\frac{\sin (b x) e^{a x}}{a}-\frac{b}{a} \int{/tex} eax cos (bx) dx
{tex}\Rightarrow I_{1}=\frac{e^{a x} \sin (b x)}{a}-\frac{b}{a} I{/tex} ...(ii)
from (i) and (ii)
{tex}I=\frac{e^{a x}}{a}{/tex} cos (bx) + {tex}\frac{b}{a}\left[\frac{e^{a x} \sin (b x)}{a}-\frac{b}{a} I\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{e^{a x} \cos (b x)}{a}+\frac{b e^{a x} \sin (b x)}{a^{2}}-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}} I{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I\left(1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)=e^{a x}\left[\frac{a \cos (b x)+b \sin (b x)}{a^{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore I=\frac{e^{a x}[a \cos b x+b \sin (b x)]}{\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right)}+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Let I = {tex}\int {/tex} (x + 1) log x dx, then we have I = log x {tex}\int {/tex} (x + 1) dx - {tex}\left.\int\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)(x+1) d x\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x\right) \log x-\int \frac{1}{x}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x\right){/tex} log x - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int x d x-\int d x{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x\right){/tex} log x - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} - x + C I = {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x\right){/tex} log x - {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{4}+x\right){/tex} + C
solution: Let I = {tex}\int {/tex} (x + 1) log x dx, then we have I = log x {tex}\int {/tex} (x + 1) dx - {tex}\left.\int\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)(x+1) d x\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x\right) \log x-\int \frac{1}{x}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x\right){/tex} log x - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int x d x-\int d x{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x\right){/tex} log x - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} - x + C I = {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x\right){/tex} log x - {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{4}+x\right){/tex} + C
solution_html:
Let I =  {tex}\int {/tex}(x + 1) log x dx, then we have
 I = log x {tex}\int {/tex}(x + 1) dx - {tex}\left.\int\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)(x+1) d x\right) d x{/tex}
= {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x\right) \log x-\int \frac{1}{x}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x\right) d x{/tex}
= {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x\right){/tex}log x - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int x d x-\int d x{/tex}
= {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x\right){/tex}log x - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex}- x + C
I =  {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x\right){/tex}log x -  {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{4}+x\right){/tex}+ C

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate the integral:{tex}\int \frac{\sin \left(2 \tan ^{-1} x\right)}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)} d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate the integral: {tex}\int \frac{\sin \left(2 \tan ^{-1} x\right)}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)} d x{/tex}
explanation: Take tan -1 x = t So we get {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} d x=d t{/tex} By integrating w.r.t. t {tex}\int \sin 2 t d t=\frac{-\cos 2 t}{2}+c{/tex} By substituting the value of t {tex}=-\frac{\cos \left(2 \tan ^{-1} x\right)}{2}+c{/tex}
solution: Take tan -1 x = t So we get {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} d x=d t{/tex} By integrating w.r.t. t {tex}\int \sin 2 t d t=\frac{-\cos 2 t}{2}+c{/tex} By substituting the value of t {tex}=-\frac{\cos \left(2 \tan ^{-1} x\right)}{2}+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Take tan-1 x = t
So we get
{tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} d x=d t{/tex}
By integrating w.r.t. t
{tex}\int \sin 2 t d t=\frac{-\cos 2 t}{2}+c{/tex}
By substituting the value of t
{tex}=-\frac{\cos \left(2 \tan ^{-1} x\right)}{2}+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Evaluate the integral:{tex}\int \frac{(x+1)(x+\log x)^{2}}{x} d x{/tex}
question_text: Evaluate the integral: {tex}\int \frac{(x+1)(x+\log x)^{2}}{x} d x{/tex}
explanation: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{(x+1)(x+\log x)^{2}}{x} d x{/tex} , then {tex}I=\int \frac{x+1}{x} \times \frac{(x+\log x)^{2}}{1} d x{/tex} On further simplification, we have {tex}I=\int\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right) \times \frac{(x+\log x)^{2}}{1} d x{/tex} Take x + log x = t So we get {tex}\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right) d x=d t{/tex} By integrating w.r.t. t {tex}\int t^{2} d t=\frac{t^{3}}{3}+c{/tex} By substituting the value of t {tex}I=\frac{(x+\log x)^{3}}{3}+c{/tex}
solution: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{(x+1)(x+\log x)^{2}}{x} d x{/tex} , then {tex}I=\int \frac{x+1}{x} \times \frac{(x+\log x)^{2}}{1} d x{/tex} On further simplification, we have {tex}I=\int\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right) \times \frac{(x+\log x)^{2}}{1} d x{/tex} Take x + log x = t So we get {tex}\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right) d x=d t{/tex} By integrating w.r.t. t {tex}\int t^{2} d t=\frac{t^{3}}{3}+c{/tex} By substituting the value of t {tex}I=\frac{(x+\log x)^{3}}{3}+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I =  {tex}\int \frac{(x+1)(x+\log x)^{2}}{x} d x{/tex}, then 
{tex}I=\int \frac{x+1}{x} \times \frac{(x+\log x)^{2}}{1} d x{/tex}
On further simplification, we have
{tex}I=\int\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right) \times \frac{(x+\log x)^{2}}{1} d x{/tex}
Take x + log x = t
So we get
{tex}\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right) d x=d t{/tex}
By integrating w.r.t. t
{tex}\int t^{2} d t=\frac{t^{3}}{3}+c{/tex}
By substituting the value of t
{tex}I=\frac{(x+\log x)^{3}}{3}+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
explanation: Given: {tex}\int \frac{d x}{(\sqrt{x+3}-\sqrt{x+2})}{/tex} Multiply and divide by {tex}\sqrt{(x+3)}+\sqrt{(x+2)}{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{(\sqrt{(x+3)}+\sqrt{(x+2)})}{x+3-x-2} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \sqrt{(x+3)}+\sqrt{(x+2)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{3}(x+3)^{\frac{3}{2}}+\frac{2}{3}(x+2)^{\frac{3}{2}}+c{/tex}
solution: Given: {tex}\int \frac{d x}{(\sqrt{x+3}-\sqrt{x+2})}{/tex} Multiply and divide by {tex}\sqrt{(x+3)}+\sqrt{(x+2)}{/tex} {tex}=\int \frac{(\sqrt{(x+3)}+\sqrt{(x+2)})}{x+3-x-2} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \sqrt{(x+3)}+\sqrt{(x+2)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{3}(x+3)^{\frac{3}{2}}+\frac{2}{3}(x+2)^{\frac{3}{2}}+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\int \frac{d x}{(\sqrt{x+3}-\sqrt{x+2})}{/tex}
Multiply and divide by {tex}\sqrt{(x+3)}+\sqrt{(x+2)}{/tex}
{tex}=\int \frac{(\sqrt{(x+3)}+\sqrt{(x+2)})}{x+3-x-2} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int \sqrt{(x+3)}+\sqrt{(x+2)} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{3}(x+3)^{\frac{3}{2}}+\frac{2}{3}(x+2)^{\frac{3}{2}}+c{/tex}
